body:
3K-5
Winter
Indicate: Indoor
Indicate: Year 1


New Years Food Traditions 

Summary: Students will learn about different New Year’s food traditions from around the world, think about their own food traditions, and do a New Year’s tasting (12 Grapes)

Objectives: Students will be able to identify what a food tradition is, describe a New Year’s food tradition from somewhere in the world, and be able to make connections to their own lives.

Core Competencies: kitchen skills and related academic content, multicultural education, social-emotional learning.

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson promotes positive perspectives on parents and families by asking students to share about their family/culture’s New Year’s food tradition(s). This lesson communicates high expectations for upper grades by asking them to read and complete a worksheet without constant supervision. This lesson encourages learning within the context of culture by building understanding about other cultures and allowing students to connect to and reflect on their own heritage and cultural experiences.

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson encourages students to show self confidence when they share a story about their family’s food culture. This lesson improves relationship skills by making connections to outside life. This lesson improves self awareness by giving opportunities for students to display a sense of pride or ownership over their family’s food traditions.

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual with SEL component:
Welcome students to class, ask how their winter break was, ask what holidays students celebrated - encourage answers to NYE if nobody brings it up. Tell students that today we will be learning about some different Food traditions that people participate in in different parts of the world. 
Literacy check: “Food Traditions”
If need be (especially for younger grades) explain that a food tradition is a tradition that involves food in some way, and that a tradition is something that you do on certain special events, every time that event or day happens, i.e. on your birthday you might always have a certain kind of sweet, or on Thanksgiving many families’ food tradition is to eat turkey or ham.

Engaging activity:
For upper grades/classes that can read(30 mins): Pass out books to each table about different food traditions. My classroom has a copy of “A song of frutas” (which is about a Cuban family eating 12 grapes at midnight), and I made copies of “Shante keys and the New Years Peas” (about Black eyed Peas/Hoppin John (although never specifically says Hoppin John in the story) and “Bringing in the New Year” (which is about a chinese family making “get rich dumplings” on NYE). Along with the books, pass out the worksheets attached below, one to each person. Tell students to read the book assigned to their table, and to answer the questions about the book and about their own traditions on their papers. Walk around and assist as necessary. When all the tables are done ask someone at each table to share the answers to the first 3 questions about their book (title, food tradition, and where that tradition is practiced). After every table has shared, ask students if any of them practice any of the traditions that were mentioned, then ask if there are different food traditions that they practice (take 3 or 4 answers). Share your own NYE food tradition with students! I.e. I shared with my classes that my mom was born in a country called Finland so every NYE my family makes rice pudding and hides a peeled almond in one of the bowls and then we all eat it together and whoever finds the peeled almond in their bowl will get good luck for the following year.
For lower grades: Read “A Song of Frutas” to students, explaining things to them on each page and checking for comprehension. Make sure they all understand at the end of the story, what foods the characters ate, what holiday they ate it on, where the book took place, and why they specifically ate 12 grapes. Then, share your own family’s NYE tradition with the class, and ask if anybody wants to share theirs (3K pre-K will likely have a hard time recalling), you can also ask students if they have other favorite holidays that are celebrated by eating specific foods. 
Pass out coloring sheets with empty plates on them (I googled and found a simple one with a big plate in the middle and a fork and a knife), tell students we are going to draw our NYE food traditions (or whatever they ate on New Years) If they can’t remember, you can tell them they can draw the twelve grapes from the story you just read.

Closing ritual with SEL component:
Do a grape tasting! Put a big bowl of rinsed grapes in the center of the table, have students wash/sanitize their hands if they haven’t already. For older grades, they should have written 12 wishes on the backside of their worksheet. For younger students, tell them every time they take a grape from the bowl they have to say a wish outloud before they can eat it! Older students can choose to share their wishes or keep them in their heads but I did not pass grapes out until everyone had completed the back side.
Before students leave, ask what new traditions they learned about? How did learning about different food traditions make them feel? Etc. Give shout outs of relevant. Dismiss them.

Materials: 
Books (got one of these by screenshotting a youtube read along and the other with my virtual library card and then screenshotted on my iPad)
Worksheet

Prep work and setup:
Arrange classroom/chairs for younger grades to read to them.

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:


Back pocket activities:
If younger grade classes fly by you can read a second book at the end of class. The Shante Keys book is a great one because it includes many different traditions in it.

Photos, if applicable:

Copyright Edible Schoolyard NYC. Attribute before sharing.
truth: Promotes positive perspectives on parents and families
Encourages learning within the context of culture

6th GradeSpring
Indoor


Green Room Party


Summary: Students will make three-cheese mashed potatoes. 

Objectives: Students will be able to work together to complete a recipe.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: In math class you all did an assignment where you took a recipe for two people and changed it to be enough for your whole class.  Today we will be preparing the winning recipe, which is three-cheese mashed potatoes! 

Engaging activity:
Students will be broken up into smaller groups to complete the recipe:
Mash potatoes
Grate cheese
Measure/combine other ingredients

Closing ritual:
Clean up
Say thank yous
Eat together
Share thoughts about the meal. What was your favorite part? How could we make it better? 

Materials: 
Potato mashers
Graters
Measuring cups
Measuring spoons
Cutting boards
Rubber spatula
Food processor
Knive
Prep work and setup:
Cut cheddar (225g) and parmesan cheese (150 g) into smaller chunks
Pre-boil 8-10 potatoes (add salt/bay leaf to the water)

Anticipated problems: none

Background information for instructor:
Students made this dish as a part of a math lesson; details below 
Credit to Allison Holton - Evergreen MS
Class 603 is having a party!  But Ms. Holton, Ms. Reiss, Mr. Brown and Ms. Kennelly can’t decide what to serve.  We need your help.  You will find a recipe you think the whole class will enjoy (No meat! No peanuts!), calculate how much money the ingredients will cost, including taxes, and ensure there will be enough ingredients to cook for the class (17 people!).  In order to test the recipe, you must also include the recipe and cost for only 2 people.  You will then create a short presentation with an argument as to why your recipe should be chosen for the party.  The winning work will be chosen to be made in the Green Room for the party!  

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities: none

body:
2nd-3rd GradeAll Seasons
Indoor


Seed Dispersal

Summary: Students build models of seeds that can float in water, float through the air, or attach to animals.

Objectives: Students will be able to identify different ways seeds have adapted to aid their own dispersal.

Core Competencies: Garden skills and related academic content

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles by giving students a creative, kinesthetic project to help them understand the concept of seed dispersal. 

Social-Emotional Skills: Students practice self-management by working creatively on their own project, while also practicing relationship skills to share materials in small groups. 

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Explain that we are going to continue learning more deeply about plants and animals. “The more we know about plants and animals, the better we can care for them in our garden and our community.”
Write “adaptation” on the white board. Ask for volunteers to explain what it means for plants or animals to “adapt.”
Explain that plants are also adapted to their environment. As an example, ask students to think about how a cactus is adapted to its environment. 
Explain that plants are also adapted in how they have to spread out their seeds. Ask students to explain why a plant needs its seeds to be spread out and can’t be all in one place. 
“One way that plants survive is by making seeds that spread out in different ways. What happens if I plant all my seeds together in one area? They won’t have enough space to grow. In garden class, we’ll be learning about the ways that plants spread their seeds out in nature. But another way that plants are adapted is that they make fruits and seeds that are delicious. Because we like their fruits and seeds, we plant their seeds for them!” 
Show pictures of seeds dispersing in different ways, or physical examples if you have them: a coconut floating on the water, an oak seed and/or dandelions flying through the air. “How has each of these adapted for better dispersal?”

Engaging activity:
While the students are still in the circle, explain that they are going to play Mother Nature, and help seeds adapt to a specific challenge. Tell them they will be given pretend “seeds” and materials they can use to help the seeds adapt. Their job is to help their seed travel.
Divide students into three groups. Students will build seeds that float, fly, or stick to an animal, depending on their group.
Give each student a metal washer to represent their “seed.” Give each group building materials and written instructions. Have one student in each group read the challenge for the rest of the group.
Give students time to build their individual designs and test them for success. 

Closing ritual:
Let students share how they built their seed to adapt to either float, fly, or stick to an animal.
“A final way that seeds can disperse is by us, as people and gardeners. There are so many seeds and fruits and vegetables that we like to eat. When we plant seeds in the garden, we are helping the parent plant to spread out its seeds and have enough space. And then when those plants grow up, we can eat the fruits or vegetables and seeds that they make, too.
“In nature, plants have adapted clever ways to distribute their seeds. One of those ways is by forming a relationship with human beings. The plants give us something to eat, and in return, we plant the seeds over and over again so they can survive.” 
End with a tasting.


Materials: 
Variety of seeds for demonstration purposes or seed dispersal visuals
Visuals of animal adaptations
Materials for seed-building activity (i.e. rubber bands, toothpicks, balloons, scissors, pencils, plastic bags, corks, cotton, feathers, tacks, metal springs, wire)
For the floating group: tape, popsicle sticks, corks, balloons, paper, trays with water to test their constructions
For the flying group: paper, scissors, string, tape, a fan to test their constructions
For the sticking to an animal group: paper clips, pipe cleaners, rubber bands, clothespins, piece of felt or other fuzzy fabric for testing their constructions 
Bolts or washers to represent seeds
Sets of instructions for each small group
Tasting 

Prep work and setup:
Set up stations for students to build and test their constructions. Depending on the layout of your space, you may have to make more than three groups.  

Anticipated problems:
Students may want to take their constructions home. Decide ahead of time and tell students whether or not they will be allowed to take their constructions home. 

Background information for instructor: None. 

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities: None. 

Task cards or handouts used: 
Seed Dispersal Materials packet

Photos: See handouts.

Copyright Edible Schoolyard NYC. Attribute before sharing.

body:
3K/PK
Winter/Rain
Indicate: Indoor
Indicate: Year A

I spy…in the garden!

Summary: This is part four in a series about the role of gardeners.  Students will explore plant parts in the garden on trays, and use their senses to identify plant parts.  

Objectives: Students will build confidence in exploring the natural world using their senses, and learn about the different plant parts.  Students will be able to correctly identify each plant part.  

Core Competencies: List all that apply: Environmental and community stewardship, garden skills and related academic content

Cultural Responsiveness: Communicates high expectations by expecting students to complete a matching plant parts activity independently.  

Social-Emotional Skills: Students will practice self-management by taking small risks and safely exploring nature items from the garden using their senses.

Garden Connection: This lesson uses both fresh and dried ingredients from the garden.  

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
For all lessons: winter, rainy day

garden theme: seeds, flowers, roots, leaves, plant parts, exploration, senses
For 216: Indicate if lesson is co-tauaght and/or double period

Agenda/Class Flow: (45 mins)
Welcome students back to class.  Last time, we learned what a gardener wears.  What does a gardener wear in the garden? (10 minutes) 
Today, we are going to explore some plant parts from the garden. Can anyone name a plant part?  Prompt by showing a few items from the trays and ask for answers for what it is.  Help students identify a seed, a leaf, a root, and a flower.  
Go find your table seats!  Let’s get started with our activity.  
Engaging activity (15 minutes):
Hand out trays with some items from the garden on it.  Choose a theme for each tray: a seeds tray, a leaf tray, a flower tray, and/or a root tray.  
Hand out placemat worksheets to each student.  Instruct students to look at their nature trays and use their eyes to see.  When they see one of the items from their placemat, they can pick up one item and put it on top of the correct photo on their placemat.  
Circulate to help students identify the items on their tray.  If a table finishes finding everything on their tray, hand out a set of new placemats and a nature tray to that table.  You can also swap trays with another finished table.  

Closing ritual with SEL component (10 minutes):
Sanitize hands and distribute a tasting. “Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks!”
Bring students back to the circle or rug.  Ask students: who here found a root today (what root?) who here found a leaf today? Who here found a flower (what color was it?)
Finish with a goodbye song.

Grade Level Variations–REQUIRED for COOKING LESSONS

Site Based Variations, if applicable 
Site name: 

Back pocket activity–REQUIRED: 
If students finish with all trays, distribute a seasonal coloring sheet.  


Materials: 
https://www.canva.com/design/DAGN8q0QqOg/E3kgPMuD-RUiMhMkBSLEPg/edit
Sheet protectors for placemats
Plastic trays
Items from the garden or bought: 
carrots
watermelon radish, sliced 
turnips
ginger
cilantro or a garden weed with root attached
sorrel leaves 
kale leaves
Maple leaf
Clover
Okra
Milkweed
Acorns
Pumpkin seeds
Coriander seeds
Beans in pods
  NOTE: You can always edit the canva page above to meet the needs of your school and garden!  Make sure you have enough of each item for all students at a table to have one (at least 4-5 of each item, i.e. 4 okra pods, 4 milkweed seeds, 4 acorns, etc).  
Prep work and setup:
Collect ingredients for trays from garden and/or store.  Print placemats and insert into sheet protectors.

7th Grade
Fall/Winter
Indoor


 	Microbiome

Summary: Students will learn about some foods that promote good bacteria for the gut microbiome

Objectives: Students will be able to name some bacteria that is good for the microbiome.
                    Students will be able to identify yogurt as a food that is good for the microbiome.
                    Students will be able to follow a recipe and make tzatziki dip. 

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: Today we are going to discuss the case study you all worked on in science and then make a recipe that is related to the case. 
Ask for a volunteer to summarize the case study they worked on in science class.  
Did the patient in the case have good or bad bacteria in their body? (Bad)
What did contact with the bad bacteria do to their body? (stomachache, fever, nausea, vomiting)
Do you know of any good bacteria? 
One type of good bacteria is lactobacillus. Based on the word can anyone guess what types of food you would find that bacteria? (Dairy - especially yogurt)
Lactobacillus helps promote other good bacteria in our guts and keeps the microbiome healthy. This is an example of something called a probiotic. 
We are going to make a recipe using yogurt, which is very rich in this good type of probiotic bacteria!

Engaging activity: Go over rules for the day - safety, cleaning up after ourselves, being respectful
Students will work together in small groups to prepare the recipe for tzatziki dip. 

Closing ritual: Students will clean up their space, say thank yous and eat together. 
Discussion: Did you enjoy the recipe? How could we make it better? 

Materials: Recipe task cards, cutting board, bowls/small cups, measuring spoons/cups, scissors, spoons, citrus reamer, grater 

Prep work and setup (include photos if possible):
Anticipated problems: 
For large classes prepare two recipes - chips & dip
Not enough tools for large classes - teacher may need to precut some ingredients

Tips and tricks:

Questions for students/extension activities: none

Back pocket activities: none

Tzatziki

6th Grade
Fall - September
Indicate: Indoor/Outdoor
Indicate: Year A/Year B



Community Gardens 
& Food Justice 

Summary: Students will be introduced to food justice by learning about community gardens and work in the school garden. 

Objectives: Students will be able to explore their school garden to learn about food justice.

Core Competencies: Garden skills and related academic content, Social-emotional learning, Environmental and Community Stewardship, social justice

Cultural Responsiveness: 
Communicates high expectations: Conveying to students your faith in their abilities, Expects students to complete various tasks within a certain time frame 
Reshapes curriculum: incorporating movement, Incorporating group work

Social-Emotional Skills:
Self-management: Take small risks in using new tools.
Relationship skills: Working well in groups, Display calm, caution, or care for animals and plants
Responsible decision-making: Putting materials back where they belong, Using tools safely
Self-awareness: Display a verbal or physical action of joy

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
For all lessons: Garden, garden jobs, urban gardening, community gardening, community, SEL, socioemotional health, food justice

Agenda/Class Flow:
Introduction (10 minutes): Welcome back to the Green Room! So far this year we have already talked about gardening and how it can positively impact our health. Gardens can also be beneficial for communities. Does anyone know what a community is? (A community is a group of people who work together and share the same things) What are some examples of a community? (neighborhood, culture, family, school, class)  There are many community gardens in NYC, including our own school community garden! Community gardens help make things beautiful, get people to help each other and share resources, and lastly help achieve something called food justice, which is the work to make sure everyone has fresh, affordable, and healthy food. You can achieve many of these same things by working in our school garden!

Engaging activity (25 minutes): 
Go over garden rules:
Stay in the garden area
No running in the garden
Don’t pick/eat anything without permission
No throwing dirt or watering our friends.
Try new things & have fun!

Today we are going to work in the garden to help our school community look its best and help provide food we can share with our community. 
Garden jobs.


Closing ritual with SEL component (10 minutes):
Say thank yous and eat a tasting together as a class community
Speed round around the room: use one POSITIVE  word for how you would describe our school/class community. 

Back pocket activity: Draw your own community garden
Have students draw their own community garden
Name your garden
Where is your garden located?
What are you growing in your garden? (Try to have biodiversity: flowers, fruits, vegetables, herbs, trees, etc.) 
How does it impact your community?

Materials: 
Garden tools: watering cans, seeds, trowels, garden gloves, buckets for compost, scissors

Prep work and setup:
Make signs for garden job stations
Set up garden stations
Prep tasting - in season fruit

5th Grade 
All Seasons
No Heat


Foods From Around the World: Ukraine

Summary: Students will do a reading and watch a video about the culture, geography, and typical ingredients from Ukraine, as well as how this food culture is present in New York City. Students then prepare and taste a quick recipe that is from this country.

Objectives: Students will be able to make connections between themselves, their culture, and other cultures. 

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Tell everyone that we are learning about Ukraine and the Ukrainian American community in New York City.
Hand out the reading. Read it aloud to students as they follow along. Take student questions and comments about what surprised them or what they connected to.
Watch the video. Again, have students share their questions or comments. 
Explain the recipe of the day. Use hand sanitizer and remind students about norms for health and safety while cooking. 

Engaging activity:
Prepare the recipe with students divided into small groups at tables. Each table will be following the same recipe. 
Fill tasting cups and sit down back as a large group.

Closing ritual: 
Finish by tasting what students have made together.
Have students share tasting words about the tasting.

Materials: 
Projector, Smartboard, or other means of showing video
Copies of Ukraine reading
Video (watch from 15:25-19:00)
Take-home copies of olivier salad recipe
Hand sanitizer
Trays per each table with the following:
Ingredients from recipe
Cutting board and knives for cutting cucumbers
Scissors and plates for cutting herbs
Measuring cups
Bowls for ingredients
Stirring spoon
Recipe copy
Cups, plates, and utensils for tasting

Prep work and setup:
Boil the carrots ahead of time. Boil and peel the potatoes ahead of time. 
Hard boil the eggs ahead of time. 
Set up trays with materials and ingredients. The recipe is enough for one whole class. Divide the materials for the recipe evenly between your number of groups. 

Anticipated problems:
It is likely that you will not have an adult to lead each small group. Assign one student to be the table leader for each group. The table leader can give out jobs, pass out materials, and ensure the recipe is followed. 

Background information for instructor:
This lesson is the first in a larger unit about the food culture of New York City. The unit sequence is as follows:
Introductory lesson
Cooking and culture lessons (Uzbekistan, Mexico, Pakistan, China, Caribbean Islands, Ukraine)
Recipe design lesson
Voting lesson
An alternate recipe to make for this lesson is Ukranian vareniki, although this recipe requires heat and pre-work. 

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities: None.

Task cards or handouts used: See above.

Photos: None. 



Copyright Edible Schoolyard NYC. Attribute before sharing.

   Types of Energy

Summary: Students will discuss the different types of energy and prepare a recipe. 

Objectives: Students will be able to identify what types of energy are used while preparing a recipe
                    Students will be able to work together to make a recipe. 

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: Today we are going to discuss what you know about the different types of energy. Can you all shout out what the 7 types of energy you have learned about in science (chemical, thermal, nuclear, electrical, mechanical, sounds, light)are? We are going to play a game where you must identify the different types of energy that are used for the recipe we will be making today. 

Engaging activity: 
Part 1: Give students energy flash cards. When I read out a part of the recipe, raise the card(s) of which types of energy are being used.
Ask students to explain why they chose the cards
Part 2: Students will work together to prepare an agua fresca recipe 

Closing ritual: Students will clean up their space, say our thank yous and try our recipe.
Did you like the recipe? What would you change about it if you could?  

Materials:
Energy flash cards
Pitchers
Strainer
Cutting boards
Knives
Grater/Microplane
Scissors
Citrus Reamers

Prep work and setup (include photos if possible):
Freeze ice cubes
Boil water
Cut out and sort flash cards

   Healthy Eating

Summary: Students learn about how food affects our bodies and prepare a recipe for a healthy snack.

Objectives: Students will be able to discuss and understand how the food they eat affects their bodies. 
Students will be able to work together to prepare empanadas. 

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: Ask students about what kind of food they like to eat. After hearing a few answers, ask why they like those foods - try to get responses about taste/texture (ex. Crunchy, salty, sweet…). Now ask students to think about what effect they think their favorite foods have on their body. What foods make you feel energetic/tired? What do our bodies need to grow and function - what kind of foods help us do that.

Engaging activity: Students will work in groups to prepare empanada filling and fill their own empanadas. 

Closing ritual: After preparing their food, students will say their thank yous and eat together.
Discussion Questions: How do you feel after eating your empanada? Do you think this is something you would make/eat again?


Materials: recipe cards, cutting boards, knives, measuring cups/spoons

body:
Middle School
Any Season
Indoor


  Comparative Writing

Summary: Students will prepare two different recipes to practice comparative writing. 

Objectives: Students will be able to write a convincing argument regarding a food related topic.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: Today we are going to be practicing writing an argument.  A good argument can convince or prevent someone from trying something. A very important part of writing a convincing argument is using descriptive language. Descriptive language includes words that helps someone imagine something using their minds without seeing or tasting something for themselves. In class today you all are going to split into groups to make two different recipes, which we all try. After tasting our recipes you all will have to write an argument trying to convince a friend to try the recipe you liked best. 

Engaging activity: Students will be split into groups to prepare recipes. The recipes will be different in some way. After making their dish students will try both recipes and take notes about what they liked/disliked about each one. Students will then have to write a short argument explaining why someone should eat one recipe over the other. 

Closing ritual: Ask students to read what they wrote aloud. Take a vote to see which recipe students would prefer. 

Materials: chart paper, tasting words poster, recipes (see below for different options), paper, pencils

Prep work and setup (include photos if possible):
Anticipated problems: May be hard to do just 2 recipes with a large class

Tips and tricks: 
Pre-select groups so that students can not come in with a preference for certain ingredients
Do a blind taste test (so students won’t just choose the recipe they made)

Background information for instructor: none

Questions for students/extension activities: none

Back pocket activities: none

Task cards or handouts used: Different recipes for different arguments
Guacamole - With/Without Tomatoes
Vegan vs. Vegetarian - Broccoli Cheddar Soup, Queso Dip, Banana Bread
Dairy vs. Non-Dairy - Oatmeal
Traditional vs. Non-Traditional - Brownies
Sweet vs. White Potatoes - Baked French Fries
Plant Based Burgers - Recipe & Worksheet
Pizza - Worksheet

Primavera Pasta & Citrus Lemonade 

Summary: Students will learn how to make pasta from scratch , how to properly cook fresh pasta , cook vegetables or sauce  and make seasonal citrus lemonade . 

Objectives: During the month of march we will be teaching the importance of water in life  , by teaching pasta we will be incorporating water to make the pasta , to boil pasta , and make fresh lemonade.

Cultural Responsiveness: 
Communicates high expectations:
expect students to read and complete a recipe on their own
Conveying to students your faith in their abilities
Expects students to complete various tasks within a certain time frame 
Reshapes curriculum:
Incorporating group work
incorporating movement

Social-Emotional Skills: 
Self-management:
Take small risks in trying new foods and using new tools.
Regulate one’s emotions, thoughts, behaviors (ie: managing stress, controlling impulses, self-motivation)
Waiting to eat until everyone is served 
Asking for the recipe and trying it at home 
Relationship skills:
Show support to classmates
Willingly take turns and/or share tools
Working well in groups	
Setting the table and helping to serve each other
Responsible decision-making:
Cleaning up messes
Putting materials back where they belong
Using tools safely
Considers safety and well-being of self and other


Agenda/Class Flow: (include estimated time)

Food Workers Rights


Summary: Each student will read a true life story of a food worker and complete a first-person narrative template of the story. The stories will be diverse and representative of the members of the food system. Students will form groups according to the story they have been assigned, and one student per group will volunteer to present their first-person narrative to the class. While listening to their fellow classmates present, students will complete an audience matrix. 

Objectives: Students understand the injustices that food workers face and will be able to identify solutions.

Core Competencies: social justice, social-emotional learning.

Cultural Responsiveness: Reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: Social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual with SEL component:
In a circle, ask students: Who knows someone who works in the food industry? Raise your hand if you know someone who works in a grocery store, a fast food restaurant or is a cook or a farmer.
These are all food workers. They are members of our food system who encounter a specific set of workplace issues and injustices. Today we’ll be reading real life stories of a diverse set of food workers to witness their struggles and their successes.
Today you’ll get to understand the perspective of a food worker and learn ways to make their workplace better and fairer.

Engaging activity:
Divide students into five groups. Each group gets the story of one food worker and the accompanying guide to explain that worker to their classmates.
Students read their story together and take notes on their guide. They decide on a person to present their worker’s story to the class.
Each group presents their story in the first person while the rest of the class takes notes on their matrix.

Closing ritual with SEL component:
What’s something that surprised you? Upset you? Inspired you?
What are some ways to make things better and fairer for workers in the food system?


Shantel Walker

Shantel Walker lives in Bed-Stuy Brooklyn where you might see her riding her bike: it’s decorated with a Black Lives Matter bandana and she plays music from speakers she has strapped to it.

Shantel, who is 35, has worked for Papa John’s Pizza for nearly 20 years. When she started working there, she assumed it was temporary and that she would soon have a career working with computers. But when Shantel was a teen ager, she had a difficult  family life, and that made it difficult for her to continue her education and find better-paid work.

The average age of a fast food worker is 29, and there are many people like Shantel who end up working in fast food as a career. Fast food jobs usually pay minimum wage, and there are many other challenges for these workers.

One challenge is that 87% of fast food workers do not receive health insurance. In fact, fast food restaurants avoid giving workers full-time work, because full-time workers have to receive health insurance. This causes two problems for fast food workers—no health insurance and not enough hours of work to make ends meet.

Fast food jobs also give their workers difficult schedules. Shantel sometimes leaves work at 5 a.m and returns at 9 a.m. the same day, which makes sleep almost impossible.

Some people might become depressed working a job with so many challenges, but Shantel has chosen a different approach: she has become an activist, fighting to make the industry better for herself and all fast food workers.  

In 2015, Shantel began working with a labor union called Service Employees International Union. She knew that most of her co-workers were making only $7.25 an hour and that this was not enough to live on in New York City. So, she joined the “Fight for 15,” working to get the minimum wage raised to $15/hour.

The good news is that Shantel and her union won that fight: the minimum wage in New York City rose to $15/hour at the end of 2018. The bad news is that Papa John’s fired Shantel for being in a union. But Shantel knew that she had a right to be in a union, so she fought back. And she won! Papa John’s had to hire Shantel back and pay her $6000 in lost wages.

Even though Shantel sometimes hopes for a better-paying job, she knows that her work as an activist is important. “Of course I’m proud of my job,” she explains. “I’m a fast food worker, a freedom fighter.”





















Shantel Walker

La‘Ray Reed

You may know that labor unions help workers get higher pay and better working conditions. But did you know that they also help workers fight against discrimination? That’s what La‘Ray Reed realized when she decided to fight for her rights as a transgender woman of color.

La’Ray worked at a McDonald’s in Michigan. Like all fast food workers, she struggled with low wages, lack of healthcare, and a difficult schedule. But La’Ray faced additional challenges as well: horrible harassment from her manager for being transgender.

One time, her manager encouraged a co-worker to grab her, referring to La’Ray as “it.” He also refused to let her use either the men’s or women’s bathroom, and constantly made rude comments about her appearance.

At first La’Ray felt defeated—she needed the job and she could not imagine things getting better. But then she decided to fight back. She complained to her manager, who then cut her wages. So she went above his head to the owner of her McDonald’s, but he fired her for speaking up.

But, La’Ray kept fighting. She joined the Fight for 15, an organization working to raise the minimum wage to $15/hour. With the support of Fight for 15, La’Ray filed a lawsuit against McDonald’s for discrimination.

You might be wondering what the connection is between workers’ rights and transgender rights. Well, as La’Ray realized, workers need protection from all kinds of things. And the best way for workers to protect themselves is to form a union. Why? Because if there is one worker speaking up, like La’Ray, it’s very easy for McDonald’s to fire that worker. But what if all the workers joined together to defend La’Ray? McDonald’s can’t fire all of its workers or it would go out of business.

As La’Ray realized, unions bring workers together, making them more powerful. All fast food workers want better pay, access to health care, and good schedules. And all fast food workers want to feel safe and respected at work. La’Ray was harassed for being transgender, but other workers might be harassed for their race, their religion, or their sexual orientation. Unions help workers come together across all their differences to fight for better working conditions for everyone.

Ulzii Hoyle

Have you ever noticed an unfair attitude about cooking? A lot of people think that when it comes to making food for a family at home, cooking is women’s work. At the same time, however, most famous chefs are men, and restaurants can be very sexist places to work.

Ulzii Hoyle is a woman who experienced that sexism first-hand. Ulzii went to culinary school because she loved to cook and wanted to be a chef. In culinary school, she was taught that women should never complain about how they are treated in a restaurant, because if they do, they will be perceived as weak.  

This is a problem because the hotel and food business has more instances of sexual harassment than any other industry. One study found that two-thirds of female restaurant workers have been harassed by management, 80 percent by their co-workers, and 78 percent by their customers. 

Ulzii was one of these workers. While attending culinary school, she supported herself by working in a pizza restaurant. One day, Ulzii says, a man she worked with grabbed her and poked her. So, she explains, “I elbowed him.”

A manager was aware of the incident and asked Ulzii whether she was going to report it. Remembering what she learned in culinary school, Ulzii said no.  Believe it or not, the manager fired Ulzii, not the man who grabbed her, because he felt that having her there was causing too many problems.

The good news is that this attitude is beginning to change. Women all across the country in all kinds of jobs are speaking up about sexual harassment, in what is being called the “Me Too” movement. Ulzii, for example, decided to ditch the advice she got in culinary school and speak out about her experience. Her story appeared in the Washington Post and has helped raise awareness about harassment in the restaurant industry.

Another restaurant worker with a story like Ulzii’s founded a group called Medusa (named after the Greek goddess who turned men to stone), to make restaurants treat all of their employees with decency and respect. As a result of women speaking out and taking action, many sexual harassers have lost their jobs, making their restaurants safer places to work. 


Ulzii Hoyle

Faith Florez

The people who grow your food work very hard. Faith Florez knows this because she spent her early childhood in the Central Valley of California, where nearly half of the fruits, vegetables and nuts in the United States are grown. Many of her relatives were farm workers. “They would work 10 hours or more a day, through 100-degree heat, sometimes without breaks, water, or shade.” 

Even after Faith moved to a different part of California, she thought a lot about her relatives and other farm workers. When Faith was in 10th grade, she had an idea for an app that could help them, and she began working on it.

Faith’s project is called CalorApp, and one of the things it does is warn farm workers when the heat in the fields gets above 95 degrees. (“Calor” means “heat” in Spanish, which is the language that many farmworkers in California speak). 

95 degrees is very hot. It’s dangerous to do hours of hard physical work in very hot temperatures. In fact, farmworkers have died from working in extreme heat. So California passed a law that says farmworkers are allowed to take breaks whenever the temperature reaches 95 degrees or above. Specifically, when it is that hot, workers are allowed to take a 10 minute water break, in a shaded location, every two hours.

The law was a good first step, but Faith knew that it wasn’t enough to protect workers. She knew that the owners of the fields weren’t telling their workers when the temperature got too hot, and they weren’t enforcing the water breaks. Faith knew that with her app, workers could see for themselves when they should be taking a break.

In addition to showing the temperature, CalorApp will show workers how to report unsafe conditions on their jobs, and it will remind them of other rights they have. Faith is very excited that 50 farmworkers will soon be giving her app a try, and they will be able to offer her ideas for how to improve it.

Not all of us have relatives who work on farms, but all of us depend on farmworkers so we can eat every day. Faith used her imagination and her skills to help a group of people who she knows help us all. 
Faith Florez

Hernan
Have you ever had a churro? It’s a Mexican dessert that tastes similar to a cinnamon doughnut. Believe it or not, when Donald Trump became president, it became more difficult to find churros and other delicious street foods in New York City. Many food vendors are immigrants, and things became very difficult for immigrants when Trump became president.

Hernan immigrated to the US from Ecuador 17 years ago. When he first arrived, he earned $30 a day working at a car wash—a lot compared to Ecuador, but not enough to make it in New York. When Hernan traveled to his job, he noticed many food vendors near subway stations. It looked like they were making a lot of money, so he decided to give it a try.

Hernan asked a Mexican baker to show him how to make churros. Now, Hernan runs a business that makes 4,000 churros a day. When Donald Trump became president, however, Hernan lost half of his customers.

To understand why, you need to understand a few things about immigration. Some immigrants have official permission to live in the United States. But others are “undocumented,” or here without official permission, which means they don’t have the right documents to stay in the country.

It has never been easy to be an undocumented immigrant, but before Trump became president, undocumented immigrants were mostly left alone by the police, unless they committed a crime. But, in just the first 100 days of Trump’s presidency, more than 41,000 undocumented immigrants were arrested, with a risk of being deported, or sent back to the countries where they came from.

Some food vendors, including some who sell Hernan’s churros, are undocumented. Because of Trump’s policies, undocumented immigrants have become more afraid to be out on the street where they might be arrested. Hernan lost a lot of money because he had fewer people who could buy and sell his churros.

Undocumented immigrants are fighting back, though. Jonathan Jayes-Green is an undocumented immigrant and an activist, and he says: "This fight is linked to the greater fight against white supremacy that deems our communities as disposable, and that's a lie. We as undocumented people know we are powerful, resilient, and we are not going anywhere."
Hernan

Making Potting Soil with 3K-K 

Summary: Students work in small groups to make potting soil. 

Objectives: Students will be able to see, feel, and smell the ingredients that make up potting soil.

Cultural Responsiveness: Reshapes curriculum by allowing students to use their senses while making potting soil.

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Read a book about soil
What did we learn from the book? 
Ask students what is soil? What is in it? 
Let students know, “Today, we’ll be making our own soil. This is a special soil that is good for plants growing in pots, so it’s a little different from the soil you find outside.” 

Engaging activity: 
Work with small groups to make a batch of potting soil. 
Introduce soil ingredients to students. Make comparisons between the ingredients for potting mix and the things they usually see in soil. 
Pass around soil ingredients in cups so students can see, touch, and smell them before beginning to make potting soil. 
Write up the recipe on the board:
one cup/bucket vermiculite
one cup/bucket perlite 
6 cups/buckets coir
3 cups/buckets worm compost
Put students into small groups. Call out each ingredient and have students take turns putting the ingredient into the bucket or tray. Let students stir all the ingredients together at the end using their hands.
At the end, combine all the groups’ soil into one large bucket. Show it to the whole group. Congratulations! You made a lot of soil today!
Soil Lasagna (if time allows or instead of #1)
In nature, soil is usually found in layers. Show picture of soil layers.

Layers of Soil: demonstrate or have students build layers step-by-step in clear plastic cups with edible ingredients
Bedrock →  cup
Parent rock → granola, Cheerios, marshmallows, or nuts
Subsoil →  seeds, coconut flakes, rice crispies
Topsoil →  chia seeds, cocoa powder, chocolate syrup, or cinnamon
Soil surface → fresh or dried fruit
Air + Water →  milk or yogurt

Closing ritual:
Tasting: “Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks!”

Materials: 
Potting Soil- For each group of 5 students:
one cup/bucket vermiculite
one cup/bucket perlite 
6 cups/buckets coir
3 cups/buckets worm compost
Large tray, bucket, or tub for mixing all ingredients together

 Soil Lasagna
Bedrock →  clear cups (1 for each student)
Parent rock → granola, Cheerios, marshmallows, or nuts
Subsoil →  seeds or rice crispies
Topsoil →  chia seeds, cocoa powder, chocolate syrup, or cinnamon
Soil surface → fresh or dried fruit
Air + Water →  milk or yogurt
Spoons for tasting

Prep work and setup:
Prep potting soil materials
Prep Soil Lasagna ingredients and tools

Anticipated problems:
Younger students may not be familiar with all of the components of soil. Adjust dialogue according to age/grade.
Give younger students plenty of time to see, touch, and smell the soil ingredients. Have each student have a cup of soil media to interact with to prevent any fighting or arguing.
Don’t put out all the soil ingredients at once. Only give out each ingredient right as you are about to have students put it into the bucket. Take away each ingredient as soon as students are done, so that students don’t add the wrong ingredient accidentally.

Education.com Soil Layers
What is Soil?
Soil Facts for Kids

  Temperature and Tea Making

Summary: Students will make their own tea bags to make tea using hot and cold water.

Objectives: Students will be able to observe the differences between hot and cold water.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: Students will review definitions of temperature and molecules from their vocabulary trackers. Ask one student to give a definition in their own words for both temperature and molecule. Tell students that today we will be making tea using hot water and cold water so that they can visualize molecules at different temperatures. 

Engaging activity: Students will be making tea bags. Students will be sitting in 3 groups 
Each student will be giving two tea bags - one for the hot water and one for cold water
Students will measure 1-2 teaspoons of hibiscus leaves and add to each of their tea bags
Students will then put one tea bag in each cup of water and observe what happens

Closing ritual: Ask students the following discussion questions: 
What do you notice?
How does this connect to what you already know about why molecules move faster at higher temperatures?

Materials: 
Clear plastic measuring cups
Tea bags
Hibiscus leaves
Water
Measuring spoons

Prep work and setup (include photos if possible):
Boil water
Refrigerate water
At each table:
2 measuring cups with water
1-2 sets of measuring spoons
Bowl with hibiscus leaves 
Tea bags

Anticipated problems: none

Tips and tricks: If available use herbs from the garden

Background information for instructor: Based on lesson created by Cheily Zhune & Stephanie Nuñez 

Questions for students/extension activities: none

Back pocket activities: none

Task cards or handouts used: 

HOW TO MAKE TEA
Count 6 pieces of hibiscus flowers, put them into one tea bag 
With a new tea bag repeat step 1 
Put one tea bag in the hot water 
Put one tea bag in the cold water

HOW TO MAKE TEA
Use hands or scissors to break the hibiscus flowers into small pieces
Put small pieces into the empty bowl
Measure 1 tablespoon of crushed flowers. Put into your tea bag
Make 2 tea bags
Put one of your tea bags in the hot water 
Put one of your tea bags in the cold water

Mystery Recipes 

Summary: Students will solve a mystery in the Green Room by answering riddles to figure out what they are going to make. 

Objectives: Students will be able to use context clues to solve riddles.
                    Students will be able to work together to solve riddles and to prepare a recipe. 

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, 

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Today in the Green Room you will all have to solve a mystery. Instead of telling you all what you will be making today, you will have to work in your groups to figure that out. I am going to give you all a set of riddles, the answer to each one will tell you one of the ingredients of our recipe.  Once you solve all the riddles and find out what all the ingredients you will work in your groups to prepare the recipe.

Engaging activity:
Every group will get a kitchen riddle worksheet and solve the ingredient riddles and find the answer to the mystery recipe.  One person should be responsible for writing the group’s answers on their worksheet. 
Once all groups have solved the mystery as a class review the answers to the riddles and reveal what they will be preparing. Give each group a full recipe guacamole/mangu.

Closing ritual:
Clean up
Say thank yous
Eat together
Share thoughts about the meal. What was your favorite part? How could we make it better?


Materials: 
Kitchen riddles sheet *remove answers*
Recipe (Mangu/Guacamole/Mini Frittata)
Ingredients and cooking tools - different based on which mystery recipe is used

For mangu - peel and boil plantains before class
For guacamole - cut avocados in half before class 
For frittata - wash greens (kale/spinach)
Set up trays for each group with ingredients and full recipe

  The Applesauce Claim

Summary: Students will make a claim stating whether applesauce made with sugar or honey will be better and then prepare apple sauce.

Objectives: Students will be able to use their knowledge about scientific claims to write their own claims about applesauce. 

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: Give students a worksheet, have them do the do-now. Explain that today we will be making 2 types of applesauce, one using sugar and one using honey. Students will then write a claim about which applesauce will be best and explain why. 

Engaging activity: Students will work in groups to prepare an applesauce recipe. Group 1 will be making the apple sauce with honey and Group 2 will be using sugar. 

Closing ritual: Let students know that we will be sampling both applesauces in class next week. 

Materials: applesauce claim worksheet, pencils

Prep work and setup (include photos if possible): pots, wooden spoons, apples, measuring cups/spoons, lemon, cinnamon, honey, sugar

Anticipated problems: Not enough time to prepare applesauce and do worksheet

Tips and tricks: 
Core/peel apples before hand
Use different color cutting boards for each group

Background information for instructor: Two-part lesson - part 1-make applesauce; part 2 - eat applesauce 
Questions for students/extension activities:
Part 2: Students will sample each applesauce and answer the last questions on the worksheet. Start class by going over the claims that they wrote the prior week. On chart paper keep a tally of which applesauce students said would be better.

Food Roots Lesson


Keywords: food roots, heritage, culture, community

Summary: Students will listen to a food roots story read aloud by the lead garden teacher and then will come up with their own food roots narrative that they will share with the class.

Objectives: Students will see how our relationship to food connects and influences our relationships to our identities, culture and each other.

Cultural Responsiveness: Encourages learning within the context of culture, communicates high expectations and reshapes curriculum.

Social-Emotional Skills: Self-awareness, relationship skills, self-management

Agenda/Class Flow:

Welcome back! Today we will be talking about food roots. Thinking of the roots of a plant, what do they help a plant do? (Take suggestions from students)
They give plants water.
Deliver nutrients to the plant. 
Keep the plant grounded! (*DING DING* This is the answer we are looking for)

So when someone asks you what your roots are, what do you think they are asking?
They are asking us about our background, our heritage, where we are from or where our family is from.
Like plant roots, our roots help to keep us grounded by ensuring that we stay connected to our culture, community and to our identities.
Today we will be discussing food roots and how foods from our cultures and/or the communities in which we live shape who we are and the way we self identify.
Food Root: the origins of  a food that is associated with a particular culture and/or community. 
Today’s discussions will take place in the form of a story. Listen while I read you my food roots story. 
Student’s will listen to teacher’s story
Grandad’s Mangoes

Activity:
Now that you’ve listened to my food roots story, you will become the author of your own story and you will use  this root cutout as a guide.
In the middle of the root, write the food that will be the focus of your story.
Pick 5 questions from this worksheet that you will answer. 
Be sure to put the answer to each question on its own individual root branch. Please write in full sentences. 
Once you have answered 5 questions in full sentences, use the piece of paper on your clipboard to develop your food roots story, being sure to give your story a title. In the story be sure to include the name of the food, its origin, how it tastes, what fond memories it brings and any other information you think will create a fun and engaging read.

Students will read aloud their food stories.

2nd GradeSpring

Expert’s Guide to Gardening: Watering and Weeding 

  
Summary: Students will weed and water in the garden and then fill in the corresponding pages in their Expert’s Guide to Gardening books. 

Objectives: Students will demonstrate their ability and knowledge about how to care for plants.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Welcome students. Ask them to share out what they remember from the Expert’s Guide project so far. Ask students, now that our plants are out in the garden what other things could we do to take care of them. 
Engaging activity:
Tell the students that there are weeds growing next to our plants. If there are other plants growing right next to ours, why could that be bad for our plants? Discuss. Give students instructions on weeding, being sure to tell students to be careful not to pull out the plants we want. 
Demonstrate watering with a watering can in the garden. Discuss with students where the water should go (does the top of the plant drink the water) and how to pour slowly and carefully. 
Split students into two groups. Have one group independently watering around the garden while you lead in the other group in weeding the garden beds. After ten minutes have the groups switch jobs. 
After students have completed both gardening jobs bring them back to their seats and hand out the Expert’s Guide to Gardening books. Have students work on the watering and weeding pages. 
Circulate to help students and discuss the answers. 
Closing ritual:
Clean up the Expert’s Guide to Gardening books and other materials. 
Finish with a tasting. 

Materials: 
Dry erase board and markers
Buckets to put weeds in
Expert’s Guide to Gardening workbooks with watering and weeding pages 
Watering cans
Water
Pencils
Colored Pencils
Seasonal tasting

Prep work and setup:
Print out watering and weeding pages and attach them to the students’ Expert Guide books.
Set out buckets for weeding and fill watering cans. 

Anticipated problems:
Since this lesson plan includes two different garden jobs it may take more than a 45 minute period. This lesson could be split into two separate classes, one about watering and one about weeding. 

Background information for instructor:
This lesson is part of a series where students write books called the Expert’s Guide to Gardening. Students complete various garden jobs and then write about them in their books to demonstrate their knowledge. This lesson comes after seed starting, transplanting, and composting. 

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities: None.

Objectives: Students will be able to complete a garden scavenger hunt

In Seedfolks, the characters come together as a community through a community garden. Our school community is very lucky to have our own garden. 
What do you think are some benefits of having a community garden? 
Brings people together
Good for the environment
Beautifies the community

3K-2nd GradeFall/Winter
Indoor/Outdoor


Hummus


Summary: Students will read a story about being kind, then work in teams to follow a recipe for hummus. 

Objectives: Students will be able to work together to follow a recipe.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills, responsible decision-making, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Read The Sandwich Swap & discuss what the story means to students. Reminder about the importance of trying new foods and not yucking anybody’s yum, different families eating different foods, etc.
Ask if anyone eats hummus or knows what it is (they serve it at lunch sometimes!)
Describe hummus and introduce ingredients and directions. 
Choose group roles (upper grades)

Engaging activity:
Students work together to prepare the hummus at their table (Add pinches of salt, garlic, cumin and tahini/lemon juice, mash it)  
*K-2 students: Pass out supplies to each table group and introduce each ingredient one at a time, having each student take turns adding each ingredient to the bowl. At the end, everyone takes a turn mashing while singing the “mash it” song
Students slice cucumbers and carrots to dip in the hummus 

Closing ritual:
Students try the hummus with chopped vegetables and pita bread
Vote on how it tasted (loved/liked/tried it)
Presenters in each group share reflections about how their team worked together, what they did well and what was challenging.
Reflection questions: 
Where else can you get hummus or chickpeas to eat? (at lunch!)
What are other types of dips that people have made or tried?
Why are chickpeas and other beans good for our bodies? 

Materials: 
Tray Prep List:
Bowl of chickpeas 
Mini masher
Pincher cups of tahini, olive oil, salt, garlic, cumin
Lemon (and lemon squeezer ideally)
Hand sanitizer
Recipe sheets

Prep work and setup:
Pre-portion the tahini, olive oil, seasonings
Slice lemons in halves or wedges
Put chickpeas in bowls 
Write recipe and voting chart on board 

Anticipated problems:
Students might disagree on how much seasonings to put in and end up using too much or too little for other team members’ taste. Remind students to use “pincher fingers” and not pour the whole bowl of spices in. They should taste test as they work to know if they need more seasoning
Students get confused that the vegetables are for dipping and try to put them inside the hummus

Background information for instructor:

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities:
Slamwich matching game (goes with Sandwich Swap book)

Task cards or handouts used: 
Group recipe sheet
Take-home recipes

Food Miles

Summary: Students will learn about food miles and why eating locally is good for the environment.

Objectives: Students will be able to follow a recipe to make applesauce
Students will be able to understand the concept of food miles and how where our food comes from affects our environment.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: Can anyone tell us where the food we eat comes from? While for most of us food comes from the supermarket, did you know that many of the foods we eat come from near and far before they get to the supermarket and eventually our plates? Food miles is the distance our food had to travel to get to us. 

Engaging activity: 
In groups students will cut apples and bring them to the pot for the teacher to cook.
While applesauce is cooking, students will complete the food miles worksheet and activity.
Each group will be given an ingredient to calculate the food miles of and measure a string representing how far their ingredient traveled.
All groups will compare their strings to see which ingredient traveled the farthest

Closing ritual: Students will say thank yous and eat together as a class. And talk about the following discussion questions: 
What kind of things do we need to get ingredients from far away? How does that impact the environment?
Do you think it is better to have ingredients that come from far away or locally (city/state/country)? Why?

Apples
Cutting boards
Knives
Measuring cups
Measuring spoons

All GradesFall/Winter
Indoor/Remote

 

Crunchy Noodle and Tofu Salad

Summary: Students will learn to follow a recipe for crunchy noodle and tofu salad.

Objectives: Students will be able to work together to follow a recipe to make a healthy and nutritious salad, and learn some descriptive words to discuss the variety of textures in the dish.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Welcome students
Introduce yourself
Explain that we are going to be cooking something delicious and healthy.
Go over class expectations to be safe, responsible, respectful and adventurous. Give all students hand sanitizer and remind them how to be clean and safe with their bodies and the tools.

Go over plan for the day  (5 minutes)
Today we are going to cook a yummy recipe with noodles, tofu and vegetables. Is anyone here a vegetarian or know someone who is a vegetarian? Why do you/they not eat meat? Even if you are not a vegetarian, have you had tofu before/do you like it? Explain that everyone can make their own choices about what they eat and it’s okay to eat meat or prefer not to, but it’s kind and inclusive to learn how to cook yummy food for vegetarians, in case you ever have a friend over or a family member who is veg.
This is a delicious and healthy recipe because it has multiple food groups - noodles are carbohydrates which give your body energy and help you with digestion, vegetables which protect you from diseases, keep your heart healthy and also help with digestion, and tofu which is a protein that keeps our skin healthy and bones and muscles strong. It also has multiple textures and flavors - spicy, sweet, salty, crunchy, and soft. 
We are going to be making crunchy noodle and tofu salad. Show students pictures of crunchy noodle and tofu salad.

Engaging activity:
Ingredients (5 minutes)
Show crunchy noodle and tofu salad task card picture instructions to review ingredients and instructions.
Safety and Materials (5 minutes)
Do a demo of handling knives and measuring cups and spoons.
Go over safety and the materials we will be using.
Pass out cutting boards, knives and measuring cups and spoons.
Follow the step by step instructions to prepare all the ingredients and assemble the noodle and tofu salad and the sauce to dress it with. 
Demonstrate each step first and then allow the students to each take a turn. 
Use the task cards!
You can also assign one task to each student or pair of students so they can all do different tasks at the same time.
Serve a serving of the salad to each student.

Closing ritual:
Have them wait for everyone to get food before they eat (5 minutes)
Before they taste it, have them say “Thank you gardeners, Thank you Cooks” 
As they’re eating, ask students to describe what they’re tasting. Is there anything else you would add to make it better? If you eat meat, what type of meat would you add? Remember how we talked about how this recipe has a lot of textures, flavors, and colors? What textures do you feel? (crunchy, smooth, etc.) What colors do you see? What flavors do you taste? Thank students for sharing and remind them that it helps us enjoy our food more when we slow down and take time to notice and appreciate the flavors, textures and colors.
You did a great job trying new things, taking turns and working together! Without everyone’s help it wouldn’t have tasted so delicious! Did you have fun working together? 
Pass out recipes to take home.

Materials: 
Carrots
Cucumbers
Cabbage (Napa or Green)
Baked Tofu
Scallions
Rice Vinegar
Maple Syrup
Olive Oil
Sesame Oil 
Soy Sauce
Salt
Pepper
Fried Noodles (Chow Mein Noodles)
Sliced or Slivered Almonds
Mixing Bowl
Mixing Spoon
Knives
Spoons
Cutting Boards
Plates
Bowls
Measuring cups and spoons
Hand Sanitizer
Recipe Sheets
Coloring Pages
Word Searches
Task Card Picture Instructions
Crunchy Noodle and Tofu Salad Pictures

Prep work and setup:
Open up tofu packages and cut into manageable pieces.
Cut up vegetables into manageable pieces.
Measure out sauces and spices into bowls.
Print out recipes, pictures, task card picture instructions, handouts, coloring sheets and word searches 

Anticipated problems:
Make sure that students don’t have allergies to any ingredients, especially the sauces (soy and sesame allergies are common). If students have allergies, make them a separate dish and make sure not to cross contaminate ingredients.

Background information for instructor:

Questions for students/extension activities:

body:
2nd-3rd Grades
Spring
Indoor/Outdoor
Year 1



Plants as Medicine

Summary: Students will make a tea bag of aromatic herbs to understand how plants have been used as medicine.

Objectives: Students will understand that plants, specifically herbs, have healing properties and can be used as medicine.

Core Competencies: Kitchen skills and related academic content, multicultural education 

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson encourages learning within the context of culture by having students share their personal experiences with tea and its cultural significance. 

Social-Emotional Skills: In this lesson, students will practice responsible decision-making and relationship skills by sharing materials, distributing resources evenly, and caring for their personal work space. 

Agenda/Class Flow: 
Opening ritual (10 minutes):
“We’ve been talking in garden class about all the ways that nature helps us as people. Today we are going to learn about one more special way that plants help us.”
“We haven’t always had doctors and hospitals. How did people heal or get better from being sick without doctors and hospitals? They used plants as medicine. People have used plants as medicine in cultures around the world for thousands of years. One way this is done is by making teas.”
Have students turn and talk to a neighbor: “Do people drink tea in your family? Do they do it for any reason, or for special occasions, like a holiday?” Share out to the whole group, if time allows.

Engaging activity (25 minutes):
Write the names of the herbs you are using on the board, along with a quick summary of their use. 
Pass around premade herb bags so students can smell and explore the materials. 
Demonstrate to students the steps to make their own herbal tea bag: 
Use the spoon to scoop the herbs into the tea bag. Give students a limit on how many spoonfuls can fit in one tea bag. 
Use a string or stapler to close the tea bag tightly.
Use cardstock and colored pencils to make a decorated label for the tea bag. Students can even give their tea blend a name.

Closing ritual (10 minutes):
Have students share with a partner about their tea blend, why they made it, and how they want to use it. 
Share a seasonal tasting, if time allows, such as premade tea. 


Materials:
Various dried aromatic herbs (such as lavender, rose, mint, lemon balm) placed in bowls or ramekins with labels
Sample herbs to smell, labeled in tea bag
Tea bags
String or stapler, to close tea bags 
Cardstock
Colored pencils
Small measuring spoons
Tasting (optional)

Prep work and setup:
Set up ramekins or cups with labels and herbs.
Pre-cut cardstock for tea bag labels. 
Prepare an optional tasting. 

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
A Kid’s Herb Book is a great resource with more information about herbs and their uses. It includes pages that can be used as information sheets for this lesson. 
Some example herbs that can be used for tea:
Lavender is used to promote calm and sleep.
Mint is used for alertness and memory. 
Lemon balm helps you feel better when you have a cold.
Ginger helps when your stomach feels bad. 
Rose hips help you fight off sickness.

Back pocket activities:
Students can plant herbs to take home in small pots. 
Students can harvest herbs from the garden, either to use in their tea or to dry for future use. 

Photos, if applicable: None.

African American Foodways:   Black-Eyed Pea Salad 

Summary: Students will read an introductory text about the influence of African Americans on American farming and food traditions, moving from the era of slavery to the modern era. Then students will cook and eat black-eyed pea salad, a recipe created that uses an ingredient that is Native to Northern Africa and that was brought to the United States.

Objectives: Students will be able to understand some of the ways that African American farmers and chefs have contributed to American food. They will be able to list ingredients, dishes, and cooking techniques that were innovated by African American farmers and cooks that have become an essential part of American cuisine. Students will be able to dice vegetables.

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Explain that we are making food from America today, specifically highlighting foods and traditions invented and made popular by African American chefs.
Explain that a lot of American food originates with Black Americans, just as a lot of American culture originates with Black Americans.
Introduce today’s recipes- black-eyed pea salad. Explain that this is no-cook version of a dish called Hoppin’ John.

Engaging activity:
RECIPE
Chop: celery, red onion, bell pepper.
Make dressing.
Toss together.

Closing ritual:
Clean up, set the table, eat together, clear tables.
Ask for suggestions on how students would make the recipe different next time.
Review artifacts and/or modern day Black food and farming heroes that they learned about.
Share shout-outs before lining up.

Modern Black food and farming heros and worksheet

body:
3rd Grade 
All Seasons
Indoor


Ancient China Bingo


Summary: Students will do a reading and then play a game to learn about some of the foods, ideas, and inventions that came from the ancient Chinese empires.  

Objectives: Students will be able to describe the ancient Chinese empires’ contributions in terms of tools, inventions, and agricultural products, as well as some of the major geographical features of China.  

Cultural Responsiveness: encourages learning within the context of culture, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Ask students to tell you what they already know about the ancient Chinese empire. Explain that today we will be learning about the foods, ideas, and inventions that came from ancient China. 
Create definitions on the board of “ancient,” “empire,” and “emperor,” using previous student knowledge to inform definitions. Explain that these definitions will be important for our first reading. 
Look at the map of Chinese geography together. Have students describe the major features that they see on the map: rivers, mountains, oceans, etc. 
Hand out reading. Read aloud as students follow along. Emphasize that the foods, ideas, and inventions that came from ancient China only reached the rest of the world after a long time, due to their geographic isolation. Explain that now, we will be playing a game to learn about those foods, ideas, and inventions.

Engaging activity:
Model how to play the bingo game.
Put on a necklace and model reading the name of the food, idea, or invention, as well as the description on the back.
Model with another student or adult how to share with each other what food, idea, and invention you are. Model filling out the bingo chart, not with that other person’s name but with the name of the food, idea, or invention that matches the description on the bingo board.
Hand out bingo boards and necklaces to students. Have students read their own necklaces. They can do a practice round by writing their own item on their bingo board.
Play the game, circulating to help students as needed. 

Closing ritual: 
Discuss what surprised them or what they learned in the game about the foods, ideas, and inventions that came from ancient China.
End with a tasting, preferably of an ingredient from ancient China. 

Materials:
Ancient China reading 
Large map of China 
Bingo necklaces 
Bingo board worksheets 
Clipboards
Pencils

Prep work and setup:
Print and assemble necklaces. Assemble them double-sided so the picture is on the front of the necklace, and the description of the item that matches with the bingo board is on the back of the necklace. 

Anticipated Problems:
This game does require a fair amount of reading and writing.  The pictures on the necklaces are meant to help new English Language Learners or emergent readers with the game. Pay special attention and give assistance to these students during the bingo game. 
Circulate especially through the first couple minutes of the game with them to make sure students understand how to sign each other’s bingo boards.  Emphasize to students that they should be signing boards with their item (i.e. compass) rather than their actual name.
The bingo activity requires students to work together and help each other to complete it. You may want to highlight this before beginning the activity and have students offer their own suggestions about behaviors that will help them to work together during this activity. 

Tips and tricks:
Students will have enough time to get more than one bingo. Pose a challenge to the class to see how many different times they can get bingo.  Challenge students to fill up their entire board.

Background information for instructor:
This lesson is meant to accompany the 3rd grade social studies world cultures case studies. It is also meant to be followed by this lesson on growing rice. 

Questions for students/extension activities:
Ask students about the geography they notice by looking at the map of China.  Remind students that we are learning about ancient China, so this was a time before airplanes, cars, computers, etc.  Ask students prompting questions to help them think about why people settled where they did:
Where do you think would be a good place to live?  
Where do you think would be a good place to have a farm? Why?
Is there somewhere that you think it would be difficult to live or have a farm?  

Back pocket activities: None. 

Task cards or handouts used: See above.

Photos: None.
