body:
Grade: MSWinter 2022
Indoor - Kitchen


Seaweed - Cooking 
 
Summary: Students will learn about nori and aquatic ecosystems, then make a sushi hand roll.  

Objectives: Students will make connections between aquatic plants and their dinner plate. This lesson will lead into a follow up lesson, using seaweed-based agar to create bioplastic.

Cultural Responsiveness: 
Encourages learning within the context of culture
Promotes student-centered instruction
Reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: 
Self-management
Take small risks in trying new foods and using new tools.
Relationship skills
Willingly take turns and/or share tools
Working well in groups
Make connections to outside life
Responsible decision-making
Using tools safely
Self-awareness
Displays sense of pride or ownership	

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual (20 minutes):
Thank students for joining us. 
Quickly introduce ourselves (10 seconds each teacher)
Introduce the theme of the semester - biomaterials (1 minute)
Community Agreements - student led (5 minutes)
What are ways we can share this space in a way that is fun & respectful for everyone?
Jot down student input on post-it paper.
Icebreaker game - Hold a ball of yarn and toss to each other whenever students make a connection.
Students all stand in a circle. 
Student A holds the ball of yarn and begins by sharing something about themselves. Ex: “My favorite ice cream is chocolate.”
Students all raise their hands if they share that trait or interest. 
Student A chooses one person with their hands raised to toss the yarn ball to, but not letting go of their end of yarn.
The game continues, and by the end we have formed a spider web/ interconnected chain.
Remind students to say their names during their turns.
Wash hands for the next part of the class.

Engaging activity (10 minutes):
Introduce the lesson: Ask if any students have had seaweed before. Sushi? 
Pass out nori.
Have students look, touch, and smell. Can they recognize what it is? Make a guess based on their observations? 
Show students kombu in miso soup, or seaweed salad - there are lots of kinds of seaweed that we eat!
Explain that nori is made from seaweed, dried, and roasted. Today, we’ll use it to wrap up our sushi. 
Provide images or video (if time allows) of how seaweed is grown/ processed. 
Each student will make their own hand roll. Students will:
Slice veggies.
Add rice and whichever veggie toppings they like.
Make a spicy mayo dip.
Dip their hand rolls in soy sauce or spicy mayo.
Put in containers to take home.
While students are cooking, chat about any of the following: 
environmental impact/ sustainability of nori
ecological role
nutritional value
cultural aspects/ other recipes they might find nori in (or other types of seaweed)

Closing ritual (10 minutes):
Have students tidy up their space.
Thank students for their time, and let them know what they can look forward to at the next session.
Next week, we’re using a seaweed-based ingredient, agar agar, to make bioplastic. You’ll have a chance to make your own project to take home. 
Show example photos.

Materials: 
Roasted seaweed
Cooked rice - prepare ahead of time
Mirin
Veggies - cucumber, carrots, avocado
Soy sauce
Spoons
Plates
Post it notes & markers
Ball of yarn


Prep work and setup:
Cook rice.
Set up ingredients on trays before class. 

Anticipated problems:
Timing - meeting students for the first time & allowing time for introductions.
Trying new foods - could be unappealing to some students.

Background information for instructor:
Recipe is similar to the one found here.
Background info on nori as a sustainable resource: here and here.
How nori is made: here.

Questions for students/extension activities: 
Check in and see if students are excited about trying out the recipe. 

Back pocket activities:
Alexis Nikole - Black forager video. This can help expand the idea of using seaweed in our foods, and have students start thinking about how versatile it is as an ingredient, or even as a material.

PK-5th
Any Season
Indicate: Indoor
Year B



Berry Rosehip Bars


Summary:  Students bake bars with berries and rosehips and learn about foods native to North America.

Objectives: Students will be able to measure dry and wet ingredients and identify fruits that come from North America. 

Core Competencies: Kitchen skills and related academic content, multicultural education

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson encourages learning within the context of culture by deepening students’ knowledge about Native American foodways.

Social-Emotional Skills: Students practice relationship skills by taking turns to mix batter and to measure ingredients.

Tags: Native American, baking, mixing, measuring, fruit, berries, native foods, oven, stove, vegan

Agenda/Class Flow: 
Opening ritual (5 minutes):
“We have been learning this month about Native American people, their cultures, and their food traditions.” Remind students that Native American people were the first people on the American continent, arriving thousands of years before European colonists. Have students share what they remember from previous lessons.
“Native American people grew food on their farms, and they also found foods that grew wild in nature to eat. Some of the foods that were growing wild in North America are foods we still eat today. Because of Native American knowledge, we have access and can enjoy these same foods today.”

Engaging activity (35 minutes):
Have students work in small groups to make the batter for the bars. Student jobs can include:
Measuring oats, canola oil, raisins, and sunflower butter
Straining rose hips 
Greasing the baking tray 
Mixing the batter
Pouring the batter into the baking tray
While the bars are baking, choose one of the grade level variation activities, described below, depending on your students’ age. Take out bars and give them about five minutes to cool before cutting. You can invert the pan to tip out the bars, then cut them into pieces to serve.

Closing ritual (5 minutes): 
Eat the bars together. 
Ask students to share in small groups or partners about any recipes they eat at home that contain any of the fruits we used today. Emphasize that these fruits are available to us because of the environmental stewardship of Native American people over time.


Grade level variations:
Grades 4-5: While the bars are baking, read the quotes from Valerie Segrest, a Native American writer and food expert from the Muckleshoot Tribe. Have each table, pair, or small group pull a discussion question from a grab bag. If students finish, they can choose another question. 
Grades 2-3: Explain that several ingredients that we used today are originally from North America. Native Americans across the continent used these wild foods. Have students color in the coloring sheet. 
Grades PK-1: Offer two fresh berries to compare, such as blackberries or raspberries. Have students taste and share tasting words. Students can also vote on their favorite. Use a whiteboard or chart paper to track votes for favorite berry. Students can practice adding the sums together or practice using math symbols like <, >, and = with the results. 

Back pocket activity: 
Watch either the video about harvesting nettles or harvesting camas from the Seattle Times’ project on preserving indigenous foodways in the Pacific Northwest. Discuss with students:
What surprised you about what you saw in the video? What’s something new that you learned?
What did the people in the video say about why these foods are important to them? 
What questions do you have?
Are there foods in your family or your culture that are very special, like how these foods are special for Native American people?

Materials: 
8x8 baking pan
Bowls for mixing
Measuring cups and spoons 
Spatula for spreading
Plates or napkins for serving
Knife
Small pot for cooking berries
Parchment paper (optional)
Ingredients for recipe:
Frozen fruit, such as blueberries, blackberries, or raspberries 
Oats
Rosehips
Canola oil
Raisins
Sunflower butter

Prep work and setup:
Preheat the oven to 350 degrees.
Pre-soak rosehips in hot water. 
Begin cooking berries over the stove so they can be cool enough for students to mix.
Assemble trays for small groups. 
Prepare materials for secondary activity.
For 4th-5th grades, cut out discussion questions. Put in brown paper bags, cups, or bowls for your grab bag. Make copies of the reading.
For 2nd-3rd grades, make photocopies of the coloring sheet. 

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
This recipe was developed through a partnership with the Indigenous Food Lab and the USDA. It combines native ingredients that can be foraged, along with pantry items available through the USDA’s Food Distribution Program on Indian Reservations (FDPIR). 
Chef Sean Sherman (Oglala Lakota) demonstrates the recipe here. The recipe is available here. Replace peanut butter with sunflower butter, and replace butter with canola oil when cooking with students. 
Food writer and nutritionist Valerie Segrest (Muckleshoot) writes here about the importance of native berries and rosehips. The reading for 4th-5th grade has excerpted quotes from these essays. 

Photos, if applicable: None.

2nd and 3rd GradeAll year



Blue Corn Cookies


Summary: Students will explore the history and cultural significance of corn by cooking up blue corn cookies. Students will be able to relate the importance of corn to many Native American groups.

Objectives: Students will be able:
To utilize a culturally significant food to make something out of it (a cookie)
To understand corn is significant to Native American Culture 
To connect with the history of the American West 
To understand the significance of plants and animals for the Plains Indians 

Cultural Responsiveness: Encourages learning within the context of culture by learning about Native American crop, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum through hands on activities

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, self awareness, responsible decision-making, social awareness, Take small risks in trying new foods and using new tools, Willingly take turns and/or share tool, Working well in groups, display joy and pride or ownership, carefully uses tools. 

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: (15-20 minutes)
Wash hands
Opening circle/discussion:
Today you are going to learn about a food Native Americans/American Indians eat called CORN. Give me a thumbs up if you have ever eaten corn.
Corn is a very important food group because it has so many uses! You can make flour from corn, eat it fresh, and use the corn husks to create dolls, baskets, mats, and more!. Corn is also central to a way of life. It is important in culture and religion as well as a primary food source.
Today we are going to practice gratitude, being thankful for our food, as we make a delicious recipe with corn!

Engaging activity: (15-20 minutes)
Grind Corn 
This is Corn, did you know corn can be different colors? 
This is Blue Corn, blue is utilized a lot by some Tribes of the American West like the Hopi and Navajos. What color of corn do you eat? 
Native Americans used a stone to grind their corn (provide picture & if possible a video of grinding corn with a stone) 
Today people use a machine called a grain grinder which is a lot faster. We bought our corn already ground up and ready to put in our cookies!
Prep/Cook
Blue Corn cookies:
(for 3rd grade) Students help mix together pre-measured wet and dry ingredients
(2nd graders just roll dough) Students help roll out dough 
Students help use corn cookie cutters to cut out cookie (dip the cookie cutter in flour then press onto cookie dough)
Bake (10-15)
Students clean their tables and look through photos (included in this document)
Closing ritual: (5-10 minutes)
Eat, discuss, shout-outs, and clean up
“Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks!”
Students name something they are thankful for.

Materials: 
Grain Grinder  
Corn Cookie Cutters  (Or any cookie cutters) 
Video of Dine women grinding corn (Can start at 9:52) 
Blue corn cookie ingredients
Blue corn cookie steps  
Take home recipe
Blue Corn & Juniper Ash 
Parchment paper or Foil 
Mixing Bowls 
Whisks or Mixing Spoons 
Measuring spoons and cups 
Photo book

Prep work and setup:
Trays with cooking materials for each table
Have pictures ready to show if using it 
Make cookie dough before hand and place it in fridge and take it out before class. It helps to have the dough cold for cookie cutters to work nicely. 
Print take home recipe 

Anticipated problems:
May think cookie dough mixture looks gross 

Background information for instructor:
An alternative fun activity when it is warm: Grinding corn in sidewalk 
Grinding Corn for Pueblos 
The heart of the Hopi 
Corn grinding story 
Corn symbolism 
The amazing Journey of corn (A good article for Thanksgiving) 

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities:
Corn Coloring Page 
Navajo Women with sheep coloring page 
Navajo Hogan coloring page

Task cards or handouts used: None

Navajo (Recent)

body:
K-8th GradeFall/Winter
Indoor


The Water Cycle 
  
Summary: Students will create a model of the water cycle by making terrariums.

Objectives: Students will understand the phases of the water cycle and how an enclosed terrarium depicts the water cycle.

Cultural Responsiveness: Communicates high expectations by having students follow step-by- step instructions to create terrariums. Incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles and reshapes curriculum by allowing students to create and observe their own self-made water cycles.

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual/circle:
Ask students what are ways we use water? List all on the board. Can we survive without water? Where does water come from?
Introduce the water cycle. Draw each step of the water cycle on board. Evaporation, condensation, precipitation.
Has anyone ever heard of a rainforest? What is a rainforest? In a forest there are lots of plants of all different sizes and a rainforest is a forest where there’s lots of water. Rainforests are typically found in the tropics or near the equator.
In a forest, plants closest to the ground are called the herb layer. Plants that are bigger are called the shrub layer. The tallest trees are part of the canopy. There are also vines that creep on the ground or crawl up trees.
Draw each plant type on the same board with the water cycle drawing.
Draw a box around the water cycle/rainforest and tell students they will each make their own rainforest in a clear box or container to take home. These are called terrariums.

Engaging activity:
Create terrariums: Explain to students that they will have to follow step-by-step instructions to make their terrariums along with you.
First, add a layer of perlite (¼-½” depending on container depth). This will act as a water reservoir. In nature, this is called an aquifer. (May want to demonstrate this on the drawing on board.)
Then add a layer of soil, at least an inch (more if container allows).
Add plants one at a time depicting herb, shrub, vine, and canopy layers. Use less or more depending on how much room is available in containers. Example plants:
Herb: moss
Shrub: spider plant, cuban oregano, pink splash
Canopy: asparagus fern, rosemary cuttings
Vine: creeping fig, zebrina
Add pebbles, mini animal figures, etc for decoration.
Spritz each plant or add 3 Tbsps of water to each plant then close container.
Explain to students that mist will form on the container surface which depicts condensation. This shows that the water cycle is working and they do not have to ever add water. (The only time they will have to add water is if they don’t see condensation. This could be because it’s in direct light or gets too much heat.) 
Put terrariums in bags and carry home, taking care not to shake up. Terrariums can be placed in indirect sunlight.

Closing ritual:
NYC Water Cycle Rain Dance

Materials: 
Terrariums
Bowls of: perlite, potting soil, and pebbles
Individual terrariums (deli containers, corsage boxes, recycle jars, etc.)
3 different types of plants that can fit in terrarium containers (depicting 3 levels of a rainforest: herb, shrub, and canopy layers)

Prep work and setup:
Set up terrarium stations (2-4 students per stations)

Anticipated problems:
Students may need help getting plants in the terrarium

Background information for instructor:
What is the Water Cycle?

Questions for students/extension activities: 


Back pocket activities:
None.

Task cards or handouts used: 
None.

Mural Painting 101: Beautifying the Garden

Summary: Students will learn about the importance of using art to take ownership of and take care of public spaces, and begin the process of painting a mural in their garden.

Objectives: Students will be able to recognize the garden as a community and public space, and share their ideas and dreams for an artistic vision that represents them & their community.

Core Competencies: Environmental and community stewardship, social justice, cultural diversity 

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson encourages learning within the context of culture, because it invites students to reflect on their heritage and cultural experiences, and represent family, community, and neighborhood symbols and values in a mural. It also promotes student-centered instruction, because it encourages students to generate ideas for the mural and work together to combine ideas and create an image and message that they all relate to and support.

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson encourages self-awareness, because it encourages students to feel pride and ownership over their garden space, as well as social awareness, because it creates physical space in the garden to discuss and represent diverse backgrounds and cultures in the school community.

Garden Connection: This lesson utilizes physical garden space (on a wall, shed or other big surface), for the students to create their mural. This draws a connection between a collaborative art project, and the collaborative work that they do to take care of the garden throughout the year.

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
For all lessons: Spring garden job, Spring harvest festival/holiday, Any heritage month
If gardening: Stewardship, community

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual: (10 min)
Welcome students back to garden and kitchen class and review class expectations, “Be kind, be safe, be respectful, be brave.” 
“Today we are going to be working on a mural project. Raise your hand if you know what a mural is. Does anyone want to share what a mural is? What is your favorite mural you have seen in your neighborhood?”
Give students a chance to share. Explain that a mural is a big painting on a wall, sometimes inside and sometimes outside, that people work on together to represent their culture, history, dreams and ideas. Emphasize that even if you are not an artist, there are many jobs that you can help to make this project happen - thinking of ideas for the mural, thinking of a slogan/message for the mural, helping set up and clean up, etc. The most important part of this project is that everyone works together and it's an art project that is made by a big group of people, like a class or a school, so that you can make your shared public space, like your garden space, extra special and beautiful, and a little piece of all of you will always be here even when new students come to the school.
“What have you worked on in the garden to take care of this space? How did you work together with your classmates and teachers to do this?”
Give students a chance to share about garden jobs, especially ones that needed more than one person (ex: picking up trash together so that the garden is clean and there are places to sit, turning compost to help make soil that will fertilize the plants).
Remind students that this garden is a shared community space that we all take care of so we can enjoy spending time here and eating fruits, vegetables and herbs that grow here. Tell them that adding art to the garden is another way for us to take care of this space, and feel ownership over it.

Engaging activity: (30 min)
Read the book, Hey Wall: A Story of Art and Community by Susan Verde. You can also show this video of the book being read aloud.
After the video, ask students why they think that the kids in this story wanted to paint a mural in their neighborhood. Ask them why they worked together on painting together instead of just having one artist paint it by themselves. Ask what they liked about this story.
Emphasize that this project is an opportunity for us to beautify our garden space, since this is a garden space that we all share, take care of and spend time in. “When we work together on projects in public spaces, it helps us all feel more connected to the space and make sure that it is clean, beautiful and taken care of.” Remind students what a “steward” is and that art projects are another way to be stewards of our garden and school.
Show students examples of murals in NYC, and ask them to reflect on what parts of each mural they like and do not like. 
What colors/images/symbols/messages do you see? What is the message of this mural and how did they make this clear? (simple short clear messages, dark color on light background to make it pop, using symbols to represent hope/love/peace instead of writing it out like an essay, etc.). You could pass these images around one by one or have them set up around the room like a gallery walk and have students use post-it notes to write their reflections on each image. You can use pictures of murals in their neighborhood/school, especially youth made murals. Here are links to Public Assistants, Groundswell and Thrive Collective, NYC organizations that paint murals in the city with youth. You can also show some of these murals that are celebrating community gardeners and farmers in the Bronx. If you want to make the mural more relevant to nature, you could use this forest mural in the Bronx or this ecosystem restoration mural in Manhattan as examples. You can also use these murals about police brutality/BLM or gun violence if they are relevant to your students.
Tell students we are going to use the inspiration from all these murals to start thinking about our own mural. Make a collaborative list on a piece of chart paper of: Who is in your community? What cultures and communities are in your community? Are there important symbols/places/landmarks in this community? (kids, adults, teachers, ice cream lady, dog walker, Puerto Rican, Dominican, train station, etc. This can be broad or specific!)
Tell students we are going to do some drawing to brainstorm images for our mural. Remind them how we can use this art project to represent our community and all the people and cultures in it. 
Pass out paper and colored pencils/markers and give students 5 min to work independently to pick 3 things from this list, and draw a picture representing these 3 things. It could be a scene in a school, a home, a community, a garden, a park, etc. 
When they are done, give them partners and tell them to combine their individual pictures into one big picture with their partner. Hand out butcher/chart paper. You can repeat this again in bigger groups and have students share what they drew.
If you have time, vote on what images/text/symbols/colors you want to include in your mural and start sketching a final sketch. You can have students help write notes to the school/community to tell them about the mural and ask for their help in painting it (pick a date/time). You also could start discussing/voting on where in the garden to put the mural, and discuss if you want it to be connected to garden imagery.

Closing ritual with SEL component: (5 min)
Show students a video/picture from this powerpoint about social activism and youth activism through art.
Ask students what they think about this project. Why did students do this? Why did they work together? Why do you think they chose art as the way to represent their ideas and voices?
Congratulate students on working together well and listening to each other and combining ideas for this project.

Grade Level Variations
If you are working with older students, you can get into the history of Mexican Muralists. 
You can also discuss the differences between murals and graffiti. You can research the graffiti artists in the neighborhood you are teaching, show students tags, etc. 

Site Based Variations, if applicable 

Back pocket activity–REQUIRED: 
Paint rocks to decorate the garden (bees, lady bugs, butterflies, or just colors/designs)
Draw/paint signage for the garden
Have students lay down on a piece of chart paper, trace each other, and cut out these shapes. Start mapping out your mural with these cut outs and paper cut outs of other shapes.

Materials: 
Book or Video of reading book aloud
Paper 
chart/butcher paper
Colored pencils/markers
Pictures of murals (linked above)
Sticky notes
Pens/pencils
Scissors
Tape

Prep work and setup:
If you are doing a gallery walk to show students examples of murals, print and hang these up around the classroom or garden space.
You can write questions on posters/butcher paper for the brainstorming section if you want students to brainstorm that way - walk around and answer questions on each paper.

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
Powerpoint on social activism
Powerpoint on social action/youth activism through art
Powerpoint on public art
Powerpoint on symbols/elements of art, helpful for the brainstorming section of the lesson
Powerpoint on your social message, helpful for brainstorming what message/slogan to write on the mural
Powerpoint on turning mural ideas/sketches into a mural

Photos, if applicable:

Chilled Cucumber and Tahini Soup with Spicy Pumpkin Seeds

Summary:  Students make a cold cucumber soup using summer vegetables and herbs while learning about Palestinian culture. 

Objectives: Students will practice kitchen skills such as cutting with a knife, cutting with scissors, and measuring. Students will make a recipe featuring ingredients used in Palestinian cooking. 

Core Competencies: Kitchen skills and related academic content, multicultural education

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson encourages learning within the context of culture by deepening students’ knowledge about Palestinian food and ingredients. 

Social-Emotional Skills: Students practice self-management by using kitchen tools, such as knives and scissors, in a safe manner. Students practice relationship skills by drawing connections between their own cultural traditions and Palestinian culture.

Tags: Palestine, tahini, soup, tomato, cucumber, summer harvest, raw, vegan, blender

Agenda/Class Flow: 
Opening ritual (10 minutes):
“Today we are making a recipe using some ingredients that grow in our summer garden.” Show cucumber, tomato, and herbs. 
“We are also going to be using another ingredient, called sesame.” Pass around cups of sesame seeds at each small group. Ask students to discuss in small groups if they have seen or eaten sesame seeds before. 
“Sesame seeds are a very ancient food. People have been eating sesame seeds for thousands of years. Sesame probably first started growing in southern Africa and in India.” Show these places on the map as you go along. “But sesame also became very popular in the Middle East. Sesame plants can grow even in very hot and dry places, even in the desert where very few other plants can grow!” 
Show Palestine on the map. “Today we are making a recipe from Palestine, which is a nation in the Middle East. We are going to be combining foods that grow in our garden, like the cucumbers and tomatoes, along with sesame, a traditional Palestinian ingredient, to make a soup.”
“We are going to watch a video about Chef Ayat, who is Palestinian and has her own restaurant in Brooklyn. She’s going to show us how to make one of her favorite dishes.” Watch the video from 0:12 to 1:09, then 4:29 to 6:49. Before watching, ask students to watch for what other ingredients Chef Ayat uses in Palestinian cooking. 
After watching, write up on the board some of the ingredients that students saw in the video. Have students discuss in small groups or pairs: “Chef Ayat says that Palestinian food is all about bringing family together. Do you have a connection to any of the ingredients she mentioned? Do you eat any of these at home with your family?” 

Engaging activity (30 minutes):
In small groups, students each are assigned a cooking task to prepare for the recipe. Student jobs can include:
Zesting the lemon
Juicing the lemon
Dicing cucumber
Dicing tomato
Measuring tahini
Cutting herbs with scissors
Measuring chili flakes, salt, and pepper
Blend the ingredients, as directed by the recipe. You will likely need to do this in multiple batches. Set aside ingredients for toppings.

Closing ritual (5 minutes): 
Serve soup to students. Allow students to choose their toppings to put on top. Share tasting words and feedback. 
“Chef Ayat says that Palestinian food is served in big portions so that lots of people can come together as a family to share. Are there any traditions in your family that you use to bring your family all together?” Have students share in small groups or pairs at their tables. 


Grade level variations:
For grades PK-1, you can simplify the activity by having the lemon zested ahead of time and/or the lemon squeezed ahead of time. 
For grades 2-5, while the recipe is blending, students can help to set up toppings at their tables. Have students put remaining herbs, tomato, and the pre-roasted pumpkin seeds in small plates or bowls at their table. While waiting, they can set the table with plates and spoons and begin to add toppings to their own soup. 

Back pocket activity: 
Watch the full video on Palestinian Chef Ayat and her restaurant in Bay Ridge. Have students discuss the following questions:
How was Chef Ayat influenced by her mother to open her restaurant?
Why does Chef Ayat’s husband, Abdul, raise his own animals for the restaurant?
What new things did you learn about Palestine or Palestinian culture from the video?
What other connections did you make to the video?

Materials: 
Map
Smartboard, or projector and screen to watch video
Blender
Take home recipe
Ingredients for recipe
Scissors
Knives
Cutting boards
Measuring spoons
Spoons and bowls for serving
Microplane or box grater for zesting
Citrus juicer
Bowls

Prep work and setup:
Make the pumpkin seeds ahead of time, according to the recipe. 
Cut cucumbers and tomatoes in half to make for easier cutting.
Wash herbs and produce. 
Set trays with tools needed for each small group: measuring cups and spoons, knives, cutting boards, microplane or box grater, citrus juicer, scissors, and bowls for ingredients. 

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable: None. 

Photos, if applicable: None.

 Plant Based Eating

Summary: Students will learn about different types of diets with a focus on plant based eating.

Objectives: Students will be able to identify and describe characteristics of different types of diets.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: You have been talking in class about different connections between us and the food we eat. Can someone volunteer to tell the class what the following words mean:
Carnivore: eats meat
Herbivore: eats plants
Omnivore: eats both meat and plants. 
Many humans are omnivores but there are also a lot of humans that are herbivores! Does anyone know the difference between a vegetarian and a vegan? Vegans do not eat any foods that come from animals, so not just meat but also eggs/dairy. These are some examples of plant based diets, which means that all (or almost all) of the foods people eat are made up of plants. Today we are going to make a recipe that is completely plant based. 

Engaging activity: Students will work together in small groups to prepare a plant based recipe. 

Closing ritual: Students will clean up their space, say thank yous and eat together. 
Discussion: Do you think it would be easy for you to include more plant based meals into your diet? Are there some things you already eat that are plant based? How do you think plant-based eating impacts the environment? 

Materials: Recipe task cards, cutting board, bowls, measuring spoons/cups, scissors, wooden spoon, citrus reamer, grater

Seed & Date Balls 

Summary: Students will make seed and/or date balls. 

Objectives: Students will be able to learn a technique of rolling seeds into clay balls and also practice with a date ball recipe.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: 
Discuss seasons and signs of Spring (10-15 min)
Review change of seasons and the vernal equinox (March 20 - first day of Spring)
Springtime brings flowers and today we will be planting some in the form of seed balls

Engaging activity:
Make Date Balls(10-15 min)
Soak about 15 pitted dates in hot water for 10 minutes, then place in food processor and process 1-2 minutes until a paste has formed. Wet hands, roll into balls then roll in coconut flakes. Set in fridge while doing next activity. 
Make Seed Balls (10-15 min)
What are seed balls? [A seed or seeds that have been wrapped in soil materials, usually a mixture of clay and compost, and then dried.]
Why make seed balls? 
Seed balls may have been used by the Ancient Egyptians to seed the receding banks of The Nile after annual floods. 
They have been used in Asia and elsewhere, especially in arid regions, because of their ability to keep the seeds safe until conditions are favorable for germination.
In the Carolinas in the 1700’s, West African slaves, predominantly women, were brought in to cultivate rice using a seed ball technique that was used in Africa. Rice seeds were coated in clay, dried, and pressed into the mud flats with the heel of the foot. This served two purposes, protecting the seed from the birds, and also preventing it from floating off when the fields were flooded.
Today, seed balls are an important tool of the guerrilla gardening movement: the conscious choice to plant flowers where they don’t belong, to bring beauty to ugly, old spaces – and to breathe new life into community spaces that had fallen into disrepair as a result of civic neglect.
How to make seed balls?
Materials:
Clay 
Compost or potting soil
Seeds 
Location: Indoor or outdoor
Instructions:
1. Divide your materials so you have:
1 part clay1-2 part compost/potting soil4-10 seeds 
2. Combine the clay and compost. Add a little water if your mixture is dry. The mixture should be moist but not dripping wet; similar to the consistency of cookie dough.
3. Add the seeds to the clay and compost. Thoroughly work the materials together with your hands.
4. Shape the mixture into balls about the size of a golf ball or smaller.
5. You can plant the seed balls while they're still moist, or allow them to air dry.
6. Find areas in your yard and surrounding community that could use a little green. Toss or place your new treasures directly onto bare soil. As long as they are watered (either manually or by rain), the clay will break down and the seeds will grow.

Closing ritual: 
Tasting (5 min)
Have date balls as post-gardening snack 

Materials: 
Bags for date balls
Food processor or blender
Seed ball materials:
Clay
Compost/potting soil
Cups of water
Cardboard for drying or paper bags

Prep work and setup:
Soak and process dates
Set up 2-3 seed ball stations

Anticipated problems:
Students may not enjoy texture/feel of making balls

Background information for instructor:
What are Seed Balls?
Everything You Need to Know About Guerilla Gardening

   Leaf Related Art Activities


Summary: Students will learn about/review leaves as a plant part. Students will engage in leaf related art projects. 

Objectives: Students will have an opportunity to be creative. Students will be able to use art supplies safely. Students will know a bit about leaves.

Core Competencies: garden skills and related academic content, social-emotional learning.

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson reshapes curriculum by incorporating various different educational modalities. This lesson communicates high expectations by expecting students to complete various tasks within a time frame.

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson promotes self confidence by expecting students to work on their own art projects without high levels of support. This lesson promotes self management by expecting students to use new tools or participate in a new type of project. This lesson promotes relationship skills by expecting students to willingly share and take turns with tools. This lesson promotes responsible decision making by expecting students to use tools safely and to put materials back where they belong. This lesson promotes self awareness by hoping that students will display a sense of pride or ownership over what they’ve made and/or a verbal or physical action of joy.

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
For all lessons: Plant parts, leaves, fall
If gardening: Plants, Plant parts, Leaves

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual with SEL component AND with emphasis on yearly theme/connection to previous lesson.
Welcome students to class (3-8 minutes)
How is everyone feeling? Today we’re going to learn a little bit about leaves and do some art projects with leaves, how does that sound? 
Raise your hands if you’ve seen leaves before - what color leaves have you seen? Has anyone ever seen a leaf that is smaller than your hand? Has anyone ever seen a leaf bigger than your head? Leaves come in all sorts of colors, and sizes and shapes (pass around some example pictures). Are these leaves all the same color? Same size? Same shape? 
Explain that leaves are the part of the plant that absorb sunlight (literacy check: absorb). All plants need to absorb sunlight in order to grow, so leaves are very important. All plants have leaves. Check for comprehension.
Explain activity: Today I have some different art projects that you can choose from that all have to do with leaves. Explain the options that you have chosen. Most of them will first require students to walk around the garden and collect leaves. Remind them to only pick leaves up off the ground and not to break leaves off plants. If you have leaves that you want to pick off plants, pick them yourself. If you are doing this lesson indoors make sure you have collected enough leaves prior to class.

Engaging activity options:
Leaf Rubbing
Leaf Collecting
Leaf Coloring Sheets (very easy to find - just google “leaf coloring sheets”
Making Confetti (hole punching leaves)
Making Leaf Collages (preserved leaves in wax?)

Closing ritual with SEL component:
Initiate clean up time. Make sure students know how they can help clean up and where everything should be returned to.
Gather students, see if anyone wants to share what they made today.
Raise your hand if you feel proud of what you made today. Raise your hand if you used your tools safely. Raise your hand if you shared tools with a friend. Kiss your brain
Dismiss

Back pocket activity–REQUIRED: 
Prepare for more activities than you actually plan to do, Or have leaf related book to read if there is extra time.

example pictures of leaves of different sizes, shapes, colors, can add your own as well.
Leaf coloring sheets
Glue or Tape
Crayons
Hole puncher and little baggies
clipboards

Healthy Breakfast / Instant Oatmeal Packets

Summary: 
Students will learn about the importance of breakfast and make their own instant oatmeal packets

Objectives: 
Students will be able to: 
Explain the importance of breakfast
identify what makes a strong breakfast
make their own oatmeal

Core Competencies: 
Kitchen skills and related academic content: students will practice knife skills and follow the steps of a recipe.

Cultural Responsiveness: 
Communicates high expectations - students will complete a recipe
Promotes student-centered instruction - includes student choice by letting them choose their own toppings
Reshapes curriculum - students will work together in groups 

Social-emotional skills: 
Self-awareness - students will express joy in making their own healthy breakfast
Relationship skills: students will work together well in small groups and share tools/supplies
Responsible decision-making - students will clean up after themselves and practice safe knife skills

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Welcome to the Green Room! Today we are talking about eating a healthy breakfast.
In groups or pairs, students discuss: “What do you like to eat for breakfast, and why?” How do you feel when you skip breakfast?
As a class, discuss: “What makes a breakfast healthy?” 
A healthy breakfast should include some go, grow, and glow elements that keeps you feeling full and gives you energy to get through the day
Give some examples: these can be laminated pictures, or on a chart paper
Go: whole wheat toast/bagel, oatmeal, granola
Grow: eggs, peanut butter, lean proteins
Glow: fruit, veg, dried fruits in oatmeal etc
Briefly discuss high-sugar cereals- they do not give you as much sustained energy
Introduce recipe: oatmeal
Oatmeal is a quick and easy breakfast that can be customized to suit your tastes

Engaging activity:
Demo making a batch of oatmeal for the class. Use trivia questions to call volunteers up to add oats, milk/water, cinnamon, vanilla, dried fruit
Explain that while oatmeal cooks, students will make their own “instant oatmeal” packets to take home. Give directions before transitioning:
Groups will get a shared bowls of coconut flakes, dried fruits, cinnamon
Each student gets a snack-size ziploc bag and adds (x) cup of dried oats + 1 tbsp of toppings of their choice (review measurements if needed)
Remind students to seal their bag tight with no air inside when they are finished
Recipe writing (optional)
Review the main components of a recipe (title, servings, ingredients, directions)
Have students write a recipe for oatmeal on an index card
A recipe template that can be filled in may be best for younger grades

Closing ritual:
Clean up
Taste cooked oatmeal together 
Reflection questions:
What toppings would you like to try with oatmeal?
If you don’t like oatmeal, what are some other Go foods you could try for breakfast?
Why is breakfast important? 

Task cards or handouts used: 
Oatmeal Take-home recipe

Oats - 1.25 cups per student
Ziplocs bags - 2 per student
Craisins/raisins
Dried fruit
Coconut flakes
Apples
Cinnamon
Vanilla
Hot plate
Large pot
Mixing spoon
Each group tray: zip loc bags, set of measuring cups and spoons, bowl of oats, dried fruit, seeds, coconut, cinnamon

Set up trays: ziplocs, measuring utensils, oats, bowl of oats, dried fruit, coconut
Cut fried fruit into small pieces
Prepare hot plate with water/dairy-free milk

Flower Anatomy & Broccoli Salad 

Summary: Students will dissect a flower and make broccoli salad.

Objectives: Students will learn the parts of a flower and that some flowers are edible.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: (5-10 min)
Review previous lesson & wash hands
Last week, we spoke about Spring
What are some of the signs of Spring that we discussed?
One of the signs of Spring are blooming flowers. Today we will dissect a flower to find out what's inside and also eat some flowers.

Engaging activity:
Flower Dissection (10-15min)
Provide each student with a flower diagram, magnifying lens, and a flower.
Each student should carefully remove parts of the flower and lay it on the diagram that matches that part.
Discuss the different parts of a flower and their jobs, highlighting pollination.
Broccoli salad (15-20 min)
INGREDIENTS
1 pound broccoli florets (from 1 ½ pounds broccoli stalks), thinly sliced and then roughly chopped
½ cup sunflower seeds 
½ cup finely chopped red onion
⅓ cup dried cranberries chopped
Honey mustard dressing
⅓ cup extra-virgin olive oil
2 tablespoons apple cider vinegar
1 tablespoon Dijon mustard
1 tablespoon honey
1 medium clove garlic, pressed or minced
¼ teaspoon fine sea salt
Have students chop ingredients and mix together in a bowl
Pour dressing on each and enjoy
Closing ritual:
Tasting: Thank you gardeners, Thank you cooks!

Materials: 
For each student: cutting board and knife
Bowl and cup
Flowers, magnifying lens, flower anatomy diagram

Prep work and setup:
Divide recipe ingredients into individual ramekins and plates according to the number of students per class.
Pre-chop some ingredients - onions?
Pre-make salad dressing.
Print out flower anatomy diagram.

Anticipated problems:
Students may tear parts of the flower during dissection
Not all grades will be able to perform all tasks; adjust accordingly.

Pests in the Garden

Summary: Students play a game to demonstrate how pesticides affect other animals in the food chain,

Objectives: Students will be able to explain how pesticide use adversely affects other living things within the garden community.  

Cultural Responsiveness: incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles

Social-Emotional Skills: social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Ask students to tell you about some of the bugs or insects that do important jobs for our garden: decomposers that break down compost, bees that pollinator flowers, etc. 
Explain that there are bugs called “pests” in our garden that eat our plants. It’s not their fault, because they need to eat, too, but we don’t want them in our garden. 
Pass out garden pest visuals and have students discuss, in pairs or small groups, whether or not they have seen any of these pests in the garden before. 

Engaging activity:
Explain that farmers and gardeners have different strategies for dealing with these bugs on their farms or gardens. One way is by spraying a chemical poison called a “pesticide.” Write the word “pesticide” on the board. Ask students if they have heard of this word before. A pesticide is a kind of poison that makes the bug die. 
Tell students that you are going to pass out some visuals of farmers using pesticides. Write prompting questions on the board:
Do you think the farmer is just killing the pest bugs, or other bugs, too?
What would happen if the farmer accidentally killed other bugs, like worms or bees? 
Explain that we are going to play a short game to demonstrate how pesticides can affect other animals, too.
Explain that in our game, our food chain is going to consist of caterpillars, sparrows, and hawks. Some caterpillars are a pest that eats the leaves of plants in the garden. Write the sequence of the food chain on the board. 
Assign half of the class to be caterpillars. Explain that they are going to go out in the garden or in the classroom and find some food. Show an example necklace with the word “food” written on it. Once they find the necklace with the food on it, they should stay in their spot and wiggle their bodies to show that they have eaten some food. 
Assign a smaller portion of students to be sparrows, about half as many students as were sparrows. Tell them to go out and find two caterpillars to eat. In order to “eat” the caterpillars, they should take their necklaces. Once they have eaten two caterpillars, they should stay in their spot and chirp like birds to show that they have eaten their food. The caterpillar students return to their seats.
Assign the remaining students to be hawks. They eat the sparrows. They are also pretty hungry, so they should find at least two sparrows to eat. In order to eat the sparrows, they should take the necklaces from two sparrows. Once they have taken the necklaces from two sparrows, they should stay in their spot and flap their wings to show they have eaten their food.
Call the whole group back to their seats. Point out that the hawks should each have four food necklaces: they ate the sparrows, and the sparrows ate the caterpillars, which ate the food in the garden. 
Explain that some of the food that was in the garden at the beginning of our game was poisoned with pesticides. If your food is on a blue lanyard, you’re safe. But if you’re wearing one with a green lanyard, that means you ate poisonous pesticides. Tell the hawks that if you have at least two green lanyards, the poisonous pesticides have killed that hawk. 
Ask students: how did the pesticides get into the hawks’ bodies, since hawks don’t eat plants? Solicit from students that, because the caterpillars ate the pesticides, the pesticides traveled into the sparrows and ultimately into the hawks.

Closing ritual:
Ask students if they can think of other things that farmers or gardeners could do to control pests instead of spraying pesticides.
Finish with a tasting.

Materials: 
Garden pest visuals
Pesticide visuals
Food necklaces
Tasting

Prep work and setup:
Assemble food necklaces, some with green lanyards and some with blue lanyards.
Hide food necklaces within the garden or classroom space.

Anticipated problems: None.

Background information for instructor: None.

Questions for students/extension activities: 
This lesson can exist as a jumping off point for learning about organic pest management practices or to do a deeper investigation about pesticide use and its effects.

Back pocket activities: None.

Task cards or handouts used: See above.

Rainbow Smoothies

Summary: Students will learn how eating a variety of colors is good for our bodies, then try a rainbow smoothie

Objectives: Students will be able to describe the benefits of different colored foods

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Read the book “Rainbow Stew,” pointing out different colored fruits and vegetables and having students do a silent signal to show when they hear a food they like
Ask if anyone has ever heard that carrots are good for our eyesight. Explain that fruits and vegetables that are different colors have vitamins in them that help our body in many ways
Show visual aid that displays the benefits of each color
Red: good for heart and blood
Orange: promotes vision in dark
Yellow: strong immune system 
Green: good for bones and teeth
Blue/Purple: helps memory/learning
Play a movement game: call out different fruits and vegetables and have students do a movement that represents the benefit of each color. For example:
Red: pumping heart movement or running in place
Orange: make circle around eyes like binoculars
Yellow: fight the germs with fists
Green: stretch to show bones growing or smile to show teeth
Blue/Purple: wiggle fingers by head to show brainpower 

Engaging activity:
Tell students you are going to make a smoothie that will give them all of the superpowers of the rainbow! Display whole ingredients one at a time and ask students to show the movement that goes with that color food
Optional: students can slice bananas/strawberries, tear greens, squeeze orange juice etc for a more hands-on lesson. 
Call up volunteers to help add ingredients to the blender (each table can take turns adding one fruit so everyone gets a turn) 
While serving and passing out smoothies, students can do the Eat the Rainbow coloring sheet

Closing ritual:
Taste the smoothie as a class and vote tried/liked/loved it
Reflection questions:
Why did we call this a superhero smoothie? Where do the superpowers come from?
Why is it a good idea to eat lots of different colored fruits and vegetables? 
What do red/green/blue etc colored foods do for our body?
What is one way to eat a rainbow at lunchtime? (salad bar!)

Strawberries (red)
Banana / pineapple (yellow)
Oranges (orange)
Spinach or kale (green)
Blueberries (blue/purple)
Coconut milk

Blender
Citrus juicer
Cups

All About Birds

Summary: Students play a game to learn about birds and create helpful habitat for birds in the garden. 

Objectives: Students will be able to identify features of birds.

Cultural Responsiveness: incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Tell students that we are going to learn all about birds today. Ask students to tell you what they already know about birds. If they tell you that birds have feathers, emphasize that feathers are what makes birds unique -- only birds have feathers.
Play a mini-game with students about how birds use their feathers. Give each student one large feather to use as a prop for the game.
Have students flap their feathers as if flying. Explain that feathers help birds fly.
Have students hold their feather over their heads, with the flat of the feather like an umbrella. Explain that feathers help birds to stay dry when it rains.
Have students wrap their arms around themselves, holding the feather in their hand. Explain that feathers keep a bird warm.
Have students put their feathers in front of their faces. Explain that a feather helps a bird to hide. It helps them to blend in with what’s around them. 
Once students have practiced the four movements with their feather, play a game of “Birdie Says,” following the rules for Simon Says. Have students practice flying, staying dry, staying warm, and hiding with their feather. 

Engaging activity:
Ask students if they know what birds like to eat: seeds, plants, bugs. Explain that we are going to make some food for birds that come to visit our garden. 
Show students how to make bird feeders. String Cheerios or other circular cereal onto pipe cleaners, then twist them closed so the cereal stays on. Students can also bend their pipe cleaners into different shapes to practice their knowledge of shapes.
If time allows, hang the finished bird feeders in the garden.

Closing ritual:
Finish with a tasting, ideally of something that birds also eat, like seeds or dried fruit.

Materials: 
Large feathers, one per student
Pipe cleaners for creating birdfeeders 
Circular cereal (like Cheerios)
Seed or dried fruit tasting, or other seasonal tasting

Unit Rates

Summary: Students will use a recipe to understand how to calculate unit rates.

Objectives: Students will be able to explain how to calculate an equivalent ratio. 
Students will be able to work together to complete a recipe that is enough for the whole class.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
You all have been working on ratios in math class. Can someone quickly share what a ratio is? All recipes have ratios because there are certain amounts of each ingredient needed to make sure the finished product comes out just right. 
The unit rate describes how much of something exists per item. Let’s pretend we have a recipe that makes 100 pancakes/cookies. How do we find how much of each ingredient there is per item? We are going to go over this worksheet to calculate the unit rate for our recipe. 

Engaging activity:
Students will work in groups to complete the recipe (for pancakes or cookies)
For pancakes each group will make the original recipe (not the scaled up version)

Closing ritual:
Clean up
Say thank yous
Eat together

Foods From Around the World: Recipe Design

Summary: Students will look through a slideshow of different recipe ideas, then create their own recipe ideas for their final cooking class. 

Objectives: Students will be able to create their own recipe, using their personal knowledge and their learning in this unit. 

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills, self-awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Explain that after learning all about foods from around the world and becoming cooking experts, students will have a chance to design their own recipe. Explain that we will be voting as a class on which recipe we will make for the end of the year.  
Explain that each recipe has to also include at least one thing that we can grow in the garden or in our classroom before the end of the school year. That will allow our final recipe to be a celebration of both our gardening and cooking skills. Tell students about any other limitations or requirements for their recipe ideas (equipment that will be available, time frame in which the recipe must be cooked, etc.). 

Engaging activity:
Go through the slideshow to help students start generating recipe ideas. 
Hand out recipe design worksheets. Read aloud questions on the worksheets with students to ensure understanding. 
Distribute pencils and colored pencils. Circulate to assist students as needed.

Closing ritual:
If time allows, invite students to share their recipe ideas and generate excitement. 
Explain that you will be narrowing down students’ ideas and that voting will take place during the next lesson. 
Finish with a tasting. 


Materials:
Projector, Smartboard, or other means of showing video
Recipe inspiration slideshow
Recipe creation worksheet  
Pencils
Colored pencils
Tasting

Prep work and setup:
Edit the example slideshow to include examples of foods that are connected to students’ home cultures, as well as foods mentioned in students’ food histories from the first lesson in this unit.
Edit the example slideshow to present vegetable options that are feasible for your garden or classroom. Herbs, some greens, and scallions are feasible to grow indoors, but other ingredients featured in the slideshow may not be. 

Anticipated Problems:
Students are very likely to nominate foods they’re familiar with or eat often, like pizza. Encourage students to think of nominating foods that they have never tried or cooked before. This will be more successful if you include more diverse examples in the slideshow.
Students may have ideas of what they want to make, but they may not know how to incorporate one of the garden ingredients into their recipe. For example, they may want to make ramen, but they may not know what ingredient they could include in their recipe from your list. Circulate to help offer suggestions. 

Background information for instructor:  
This lesson is the first in a larger unit about the food culture of New York City. The unit sequence is as follows:
Introductory lesson
Cooking and culture lessons (Uzbekistan, Mexico, Pakistan, China, Caribbean Islands, Ukraine)
Recipe design lesson
Voting lesson

body:
1st Grade
All Seasons
Indoor


Where Does Cheese Come From?
Summary: Students taste cheese, put visuals in sequence, and do a drawing about how cheese gets from the cow on the farm to the store in our neighborhood. 

Objectives: Students will be able to explain some of the steps that food takes to get on their plates.

Cultural Responsiveness: encourages learning within the context of culture, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills 

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Begin with a cheese tasting. Ask students to describe how it looks, feels, and smells. 
Tell students that today we are going to be learning about all the steps that food takes to get made and to get to us. Explain that you are going to show three pictures to students, and you want them to guess which picture shows where cheese comes from.
Show the three pictures to students: the garden, the ocean, and the cow. As you show each one, ask students if this is where cheese comes from. When they see the cow picture, explain that cheese does come from the milk of a cow, but we have to learn all the steps that make it into cheese and get the cheese to us.

Engaging activity:
Begin with a sequencing activity about the steps to make cheese.
Put students in small groups of 3-4 students. Give each group copies of the sequence pictures and the sequencing worksheet.
Hold up the worksheet and explain or model what you will do. Show students that they have to take out all the pictures, then discuss in their group which is the first step in making cheese. Whichever picture is the first step goes next to the number one on their paper. 
Circulate and help them get going. Work with them to understand what is happening in each picture. 
When students are done, ask them to share out the steps. Let different groups share different steps. Ask them to explain what is happening in each step. You can accompany the steps with hand movements that the entire class can do. Post pictures on the board in the right order. 
Give students the Before I Ate My Cheese worksheet, and tell them to draw and write about any step in the process of the life of cheese. Help students to practice writing a complete sentence about one of the steps in making cheese.
Assemble pictures into a book in order; you can use one or many pages of each step of the process.

Closing ritual:
Read the students’ book. 
Ask students to teach back to you what they learned about one of the steps in making cheese. 

Materials: 
Cheese tasting
Visuals of garden, ocean, and animal
Envelopes with visuals of steps in cheese-making process, one per small group
Sequencing worksheet, one per small group
Before I Ate My Cheese worksheets
Crayons or colored pencils

Prep work and setup: None. 

Anticipated problems:
The truck card can go either before or after the cheese-making card.  Let students put it where they want it.

Animal Defenses

Summary: Students will learn about three ways that animals defend themselves (spikes, shells, and camouflage), then will build models of animals that can defend themselves in the garden. 

Objectives: Students will be able to describe how and why animals defend themselves.

Core Competencies: Garden skills and related academic content

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles by using physical movement and kinesthetic play as learning modalities. 

Social-Emotional Skills: Students will practice relationship skills by working well in groups to share materials. 

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Explain that today we are learning about animals in the garden. “The more that we know about animals and how they survive, the more we can help them in our garden and in our community.”
Tell the students to imagine that there is a bird looking for food in the garden. Give students one of the three animal visuals in pairs or small groups. Ask them to talk with their group or partner about how that animal might protect itself from being eaten by the bird. 
Have students share out when finished, highlighting the three strategies: spikes, shells, and camouflage. Reinforce the three strategies by having the students do hand movements for each of the strategies:
Fingers pointing out to represent spikes
Hands cupped overhead to make a shell
Hands over the face to represent camouflage
Tell students that they will be building their own animal that can protect itself in the garden or in nature using one of the three strategies. Explain that during our building activity, we need to practice sharing the building materials with each other. Ask students to give examples of what sharing behavior looks like. 

Engaging activity:
Brainstorm with students about what animals they might build. Have students share with you what animals they already know about that have spikes, shells, or camouflage.
Give each student a ball of Play-doh and materials for three strategies: sticks for spines, sea shells for shells, and leaves for camouflage. 
Circulate to help students model building their animals. If time allows, students can share out their designs.

Closing ritual:
Share a seasonal tasting.
Highlight student behavior that exemplified kind sharing during the building activity. 

Materials:
Animal visuals
Play-doh
Sticks, leaves, shells for building
Tasting

Prep work and setup:
Make or purchase Play-doh. If making, follow the recipe here.
Sort materials into bins for each table or small group when they are assembling their model animals. 

Anticipated problems: None.

Background information for instructor: None. 

Questions for students/extension activities:
This lesson can serve as part of a larger unit on food webs, animal needs, or predator-prey relationships.

Agar Jelly - Cooking 
 
Summary: Students will learn about the lunar new year and create an agar jelly recipe.  

Objectives: Students will make connections between the seaweed they ate last week & the agar powder they are using this week. This lesson will lead into a follow up lesson, using agar to create bioplastic.

Cultural Responsiveness: 
Encourages learning within the context of culture
Promotes student-centered instruction
Reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: 
Self-management
Take small risks in trying new foods and using new tools.
Relationship skills
Willingly take turns and/or share tools
Working well in groups
Make connections to outside life
Responsible decision-making
Using tools safely
Self-awareness
Displays sense of pride or ownership	

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual (5 minutes):
Wash hands.
Thank students for joining us. 
Introduce the activity. 
We are making a dessert that many people enjoy during the lunar new year. 
Note: some students may know this holiday as Chinese new year, but it is celebrated in many countries throughout Asia.
The main ingredient, agar, comes from seaweed - similar to what we used to make sushi last week.

Engaging activity (30 minutes):
Agar Jelly
Each table will mix their own set of ingredients.
While one of the teachers heats up the mixture, the other will facilitate the next step.
Students can pick their favorite color and add a drop of food coloring to their mixture, using a paintbrush to move it around.
Let students know that it will take a long time for the mixture to cook & then cool down, so we have already prepared a batch for them.
Have students clean up their space.
Give out premade jellies for students to try.
Once the mixture is cooled to lukewarm, pass out cups for students to pour into silicone molds.

Closing ritual (5 minutes):
How did it taste?
Next week, we’re using the same ingredients to make a plastic cup or bowl (but we can’t eat it this time!). We will make your own project to take home. 
Show example, pass around to feel texture & break apart.

Materials: 
Lunar new year storybook
Silicone molds
Agar agar powder
Coconut milk
Sugar
Paintbrushes

Prep work and setup:
Pre-make a batch the day before, for students to try. 

Anticipated problems:
Hot mixture - make sure to cool enough for students to use.
Student disinterest - have alternative activities prepared or offer individuals to help teacher heat up the mixture.

Compost Relay & Stew


Summary: Students will learn about compost, participate in a compost relay, and make a compost “stew”.

Objectives: Students will understand what happens to their kitchen scraps when they put them in the compost bins. They will recognize that a compost pile needs greens, browns, air and moisture. 

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
SEL jars - how are we feeling today
Opening Circle 
Hello everyone! Today we will be talking about compost. What is compost? What are some things we put in the compost? Point out whether the things they suggest are greens or browns.
Watch video. 
Ask students, “What are the four main components of compost?” → Greens, browns, air, and moisture. Hold up four signs with the words, greens on green paper, browns on brown paper, air on white paper, moisture on blue paper. 
(For 4th-8th Grades) Why do we compost?
Are there landfills in NYC? Where does our waste go? What are the issues transporting waste?
NYC calculations waste calculations:
9 million people x 4 lbs. waste each daily = 36,000,000 lbs. daily
⅓ of waste is compostable = ~10,000,000 lbs daily 


Engaging activity:
(20 min) Compost relay - Students are invited to practice their knowledge of compost "ingredients" by participating in a compost relay race. Teams of students work together to add greens, browns and water to their compost bucket, then aerate using a cultivator. Relay Lesson Plan
Start With Sticks (on a paper plate)
Scoop Some Soil (in a paper cup)
Add 3 handfuls browns (in a tub trug)
Add 1 handful greens (in a tub trug)
Add 3 handfuls browns (in a tub trug)
Add 1 handful greens (in a tub trug)
Keep It Moist (watering can)
Turn, Turn, Turn (cultivators)
( 20 min) Compost stew - Make Banana Nice Cream compost pile
Start with shredded coconut “sticks”
Scoop some nice cream soil
Add browns (dried figs)
Add greens (pumpkin seeds or pears)
Layer browns and greens again
Turn with a “cultivator” (fork)
Say “Thank You Gardeners, Thank You Cooks” and taste together. 

Closing ritual:
Tasting - Thank you gardners, thank you cooks!  
How do we feel now that class is over? Same, better?

Materials: 
Compost relay
2 empty tub trugs
Sticks on a paper plate
Soil in a paper cup
Browns in a tub trug
Greens in a tub trug
2x Watering can, pre-filled
2x Cultivator
Paper slips with steps, pre-written (below)
Compost stew in individual tasting cups per student
Blue cup of Nice cream “soil” 
Shredded coconut “sticks” 
Chopped fig “browns”
Pumpkin seed “greens” 
Fork per student

Prep work and setup:
Compost relay
Rearrange greenhouse tables and benches
Compost pile steps written on a whiteboard
Pre-cut student relay jobs
Compost stew - prep all ingredients and place into bowls
Make nice cream
Set up tasting cups of nice cream
Set up tasting cups of figs, shredded coconut, pumpkin seeds


Anticipated problems:
There may be an odd or low number of students for the relay. Adjust relay groups accordingly.

Composting at Home
Video: The Perfect Compost Recipe - How to Get Your Compost Heap Cooking!

Word searches

Vegetable Dumplings 

  
Summary: Students will learn how to make dumplings using different cooking methods. 

Objectives: Students will be able to:
 understand the difference between pan fried and steamed dumplings.
discuss the different types of dumplings made throughout various cultures.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual (10-15 min): 
Hand washing
Pulse check: How’s the day going? Thumbs up (good) or sideways (can get better)
Opening Circle/Discussion: 
Has anyone ever heard of or tried a dumpling before?
Dumplings are small pieces of dough wrapped around a filling of veggies, sweets, meats or fish. Dumplings can be made by boiling, steaming, simmering or frying. 
What types of different dumplings have you had or heard of?
Carribean/Latin America: empanada 
Puerto Rico: pasteles
Poland: pierogi 
Italy: ravioli and tortellini
Today we will be making Asian style dumplings, which are often made and eaten by Chinese families on the eve of the Lunar New Year which falls at the end of January or early February. 

Engaging activity (15-20 min): 
Cooking demonstration: show ingredients, tools, and how to safely use tools to prep vegetables
Dumpling Prep 
Tables 1 & 2: Filling 
Finely chop or shred carrots, ginger, garlic, cabbage, mushrooms, and scallions. Put all together in a bowl, except for garlic and ginger.
Heat oil in a pan and saute garlic and ginger (~2-3 minutes). 
Add the veggie mixture and saute until hot and slightly soft (about 5 minutes).
Remove from heat and add the soy sauce and pepper to the mixture.
Tables 3 & 4: Dumpling
Take one wonton wrapper and place ~1 tsp of filling in the center of the dough. 
Wet edges of wrapper and fold in half around the filling. Pinch and seal the edges.
While 2-3 student chefs are steaming or frying (adult-supervised), other students can prepare dipping sauces.

Closing ritual (10-15):
Clean and set tables
Eat, Discuss, Shout-outs & Clean up 

Materials: 
Tray set-up:
Knives & cutting boards
Box graters & shredders
Garlic press
Measuring spoons & cups
Frying pans
Steam baskets
Tongs

Prep work and setup:
Create a batch of dumpling filling before class so a group of students can stuff dumpling wrappers

Anticipated problems:
Have students use extreme caution with box graters and shredders 
Younger grades may need help with tools and stuffing/sealing dumplings

Background information for instructor:
Symbolic foods eaten during the Lunar New Year
Dumplings are made all over the world.
Puerto Rican dumplings pasteles.
Italian dumplings: ravioli & tortellini.

None.

Grade

Worm Breakfast Recipe


Summary: Students will learn about a basic definition for compost, and what materials can go into the compost bin.  Then, they will follow a recipe for a worm breakfast dish.  

Objectives: Students will be able to identify what materials can go into a compost bin and follow a written recipe for a healthy compost.  

Core Competencies: Environmental and community stewardship, garden skills and related academic content, kitchen skills and related academic content.

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson communicates high expectations and faith in students’ abilities by expecting students to follow a written recipe and/or measure ingredients independently.  

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson builds relationship skills by having students take turns, share tools, and work well together in a group.  

Garden Connection: This lesson uses materials found in garden compost.  

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
For all lessons: 
If cooking: 
If gardening: measuring skills, recipe reading, compost, worms, mixing skills, sharing skills

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual with SEL component AND with emphasis on yearly theme/connection to previous lesson.
Welcome students back to class.  Go over the 4 rules for garden class, and point out which petal point they will be trying to get today (or whatever other classroom management technique you have!)  This lesson works especially well for students to practice being kind to one another while sharing.
Have students sit at tables.  (If indoors, I recommend disposable tablecloths for this activity!)  There should be no more than 5 students per table.  
Explain that today, we are going to make a recipe for worm breakfast.  Ask: what do you think worms might eat for breakfast?  Help students out with prompts if needed: do worms eat cereal? Tin foil? Takis? Yogurt? Leaves?
Hold up a copy of the Worm Breakfast recipe so everyone can see.  Show them a few more things worms might eat: sticks, soil, leaves, rotten fruit, and water.  
The worms are getting hungry!  Time for us to make them a big pot of worm breakfast.  

Engaging activity (15 minutes):
Today, we are going to measure ingredients using cup measures.  This will help us practice the skills we will need for cooking class this year.  
Hand out 3 measuring cups and a copy of the recipe to each table.  Ask students to work together to match the fractions on the cups to the fractions in the recipe.  
Once most students seem confident enough to to start measuring, model how to measure correctly.  Show an example of a cup measure that is underfilled, and ask the class “Is this full?”.  No, it is not full.  Show students an example of a cup that is overfilled.  “Is this full?”  If students respond yes, agree but show them how it is a little too full.  Show how to carefully scrape the top off back into the bowl so that it is perfectly flat at the top. 
Model how to add the ingredient to the bowl, mix it in, and then pass it to the next person to measure and add another ingredient.  
Hand out compost materials and ask one student to start at each table, and show the direction to pass the bowl once they finish measuring. 

Closing ritual with SEL component (10 minutes):
Clean up–pack up materials and wipe tables. 
Ask the class: what do worms eat for breakfast?  What goes in the compost? 
Who here was kind today in class? Congratulate teams or students that were especially kind to eachother in class.  
Give out hand sanitizer and a fruit tasting. “Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks!”

Grade Level Variations–REQUIRED for COOKING LESSONS
This lesson can be adapted for many ages!  Here are some ideas:
K/1: put a large tray of compostable materials on each table, and explain that everyone is going to get a turn to pick one thing to add to the breakfast bowl, mix it in, and then pass it to the next person at their table.  Add water last!  For kindergarten, I found that no recipe is needed, but 1st grade can benefit from a simple ingredient list.  
Site Based Variations, if applicable 
Site name: 

Back pocket activity–REQUIRED: 
If students finish early, bring out the worm bins and have students look for worms.  Show them how to gently feed them the worm breakfast.  Emphasizes kindness to others!!
Materials: 
4-5 old kitchen bowls or other large vessels for mixing
Spades for mixing
worm breakfast recipe, one per table
3 measuring cups (I suggest 1 cup, ½ cup, ¼ cup to start)
Sheet protector for recipes
Disposable bowls for recipe ingredients
Watering can/s
Plastic trays (optional, but useful for setup!)

Prep work and setup:
Setup trays with compost ingredients bowls.  Set aside watering cans with water.

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
Note: students may not have worked with fractions yet!  Walk around and help them identify which cup to use for each ingredient, and repeat often that “1-2” is also known as one half, etc, etc.

3rd GradeAll Seasons
