Climate Change 

Summary: Students will read case studies about how climate change is affecting different nations around the world.

Objectives: Students will be able to explain how climate change is affecting people and the environment.

Cultural Responsiveness: positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Ask students to explain what they already know about climate change. 
What is climate change?
What is happening in nature because of climate change?
What causes climate change?

Engaging activity:
Explain that today, we are going to pretend that we are going to a meeting of all the world leaders. The meeting is all about climate change. Each leader is going to tell the rest of the world leaders about how climate change is affecting their country. 
Tell students that their job as the representatives is to read about how climate change is affecting their country, then report back.
Put students into small groups and assign each group one of the four case studies. Give each student a worksheet and a case study. As students work, circulate to help students with comprehension or group work.
First, have students read the case study and write down what they learn on their worksheets.
Next, have students discuss in their small group about what they learned.
Finally, have students decide who will present what information to the larger group. 
After all groups are prepared, tell students that the meeting of world leaders is ready to begin. Have each group present their findings about how climate change is affecting their country. After, ask students to explain what they learned from the other groups.
Tell the students that perhaps the world leaders can work together to come up with solutions to climate change. Ask students what they think world leaders can do to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and prevent climate change. Write students’ ideas on the board.

Closing ritual:
Ask students what stuck out to them or what new information they learned today during the activity.
Finish with a tasting.

Materials: 
Climate change case studies
Case study worksheets
Pencils
Tasting

Prep work and setup: None.

Anticipated problems:
Students may come up with unrealistic ideas about how to combat climate change. This lesson can be a jumping off point to talk about examples of modern and historical environmental activists whose work combats climate change and environmental destruction.

Background information for instructor:
This lesson is intended to exist as part of a larger unit about climate change. It does not teach specifically about what climate change is or how it happens. 

Questions for students/extension activities:

3rd GradeAll Seasons
Indoor


Food Webs

Summary: Students will reenact a garden food web to better understand the role of producers and consumers as well as the interconnectedness between animals, plants, soil and sun in a garden.

Objectives: Students will be able to describe how organisms are interconnected in a garden food web and what happens when the food web is disrupted. 

Cultural Responsiveness: incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Ask students what they already know about a food web. Write up what students already know.
Explain that in a food web, we make connections based on who is eating what and who is getting eaten. Ask students to think of an example of something in our garden that gets eaten, or something in our garden that eats something else. 
Ask students to remind you: since plants can’t eat, where do they get their energy? Emphasize that the sun is actually the beginning of any food chain.

Engaging activity:
Have students build a food web.
Divide class into small groups or conduct the activity as one large group in a seated circle. Give each student a food chain card necklace. Have students look at their cards. Explain that this is who they will represent in the food chain. Ask students to read who eats them and who they eat, then to put on the necklace when they are finished reading. 
Model with another student how to read what is on the card and then decide on who else in the food web they are connected to. Model rolling the ball of yarn across to make that connection. Model how to hold the string and then pass it to the next student in the food web. 
Start with one student, holding the string. Have the student name another animal or plant that they are connected to. Unwind the string and connect the next student, starting to form the web. Facilitate moving the ball of string in the group until all students are included in the web. 
Demonstrate what would happen with something removed from the food web. Have some of the plants (i.e. all the students with blackberry necklaces) drop their strings. Ask students: “What would happen if there wasn’t enough rain and the blackberries died in our garden? How does that weaken our food web? What happens then to the animals that eat the blackberries?” These secondary connections should then drop their strings.
Discuss what happens when one part of the food web is changed. Students can pose their own potential scenarios and discuss. Clean up materials. 
Write the words “producer,” “consumer,” “herbivore,” “carnivore,” “omnivore,” and “decomposer” on the board with space underneath each word. Define these terms in conjunction with students. Have students identify which plants or animals in the food web (or in the garden) are producers, consumers, herbivores, etc. 

Closing ritual:
Ask students to explain how we are connected to the garden food web.
End with a tasting.  

Materials: 
Balls of twine or string
Food Web necklaces
Tasting

Prep work and setup: None. 

Anticipated problems:
It works best to run the food web activity in two small groups. If available, have a second adult facilitate one of the food web groups. If that’s not possible, you can also choose one student to be the “webmaster” and facilitate the building of the food web.

Background information for instructor: None. 

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities: None.

Flies & Fruit

Summary: Students make a mango smoothie bowl, learning the history of the mango, and share ideas about the harms and benefits of flies.

Objectives: Students will be able to explain that although sometimes pests, flies are also an important pollinator.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: (15-20 min)
Quick Review: What was the pollinator we discussed in our last session?
Students should be able to name some Bee facts:
4,000 native bee species in the United States / 20,000 globally
75% of all food crops are pollinated by bees
Blueberries (an important crop in NY) are pollinated by bees.
Intro Lesson: Today’s pollinator is the fly. What are some things we know about flies? (For younger elementary students, read a book about flies and/or mangoes.)
Allow students to share/respond to what they think/know about flies. Prompt with questions:
Do flies hurt humans? They can be pests to humans, with a potential to spread disease.(House flies have been known to carry over 100 different kinds of disease causing germs.)
Where do we find flies? What are the conditions (temperature, moisture, etc.) when flies appear? Fly larvae are most common in damp habitats, and flies populations are largest in humid places with lots of moisture.
What do flies eat? What are they attracted to? Some species eat dead animals, and many eat dung. 
How many different types of flies are there? There are more than 120,000 species of flies worldwide with about 18,000 found in North America.
Do flies bite or sting? Flies bite, but they use their sharp mouth parts (not teeth). They have a long tongue called a proboscis, which sucks up food like a straw. Some flies drink nectar or blood. House flies like the food we eat. When a fly lands on your lunch, it vomits on the food. Acids in the vomit dissolve the food so the fly can suck it up. 
How long do flies live? Most flies live an average of 21 days and take on various shapes throughout their short lives. A female housefly can lay up to 600 eggs in her short lifetime.
Are adult flies different from baby flies? Baby flies are called larvae but they are also known as maggots.
How many wings do flies have? Most flying insects have four wings, but flies have two. They can fly up and down, side to side and even backwards.
Do they have any other special body parts? Flies have hairy, sticky feet and can stick to almost any surface. They can even walk across your ceiling.
Ask students to share if they know any benefits of flies: 
Many fly larvae are part of the natural 'clean-up squad', helping get rid of dung and dead animals. 
Flies are important food sources for many other animals.
Flies are the 2nd most important pollinators, next to bees.
Certain species of flowers, like skunk cabbage, the corpse flower and other voodoo lilies, give off the scent of rotting meat to attract flies for pollination.
Flies help pollinate more than 100 types of crops such as: avocado, cherry, cacao, coffee, mangoes, and pears.
Engaging activity: (15-20 min)
Today, we will be focusing on an ingredient pollinated by flies, the mango!
Mangos are indigenous to Southeastern Asia: India and Myanmar. Tropical areas that are also common to flies.
They are considered a sacred fruit in the Buddhist religion and the spread of Buddhism across Southeast Asia resulted in the spread of mangoes.
Persians carried mangoes across western Asia and planted seeds in east Africa in the 10th century.
Portuguese explorers introduced mangoes to Brazil in the 16th century and from Brazil mangoes spread throughout the Americas.
Today most mangoes found in grocery stores were grown in Florida, Mexico, Haiti, and South America. However, Asia grows 75 percent of all mangoes in the world.
Mangoes are a rich source of vitamins A, C, and D.
Wash hands and prep for recipe
Recipe: Banana Mango Smoothie Bowl

Closing ritual: (5 min)
Tasting: “Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks, thank you flies.”
If time allows, review important fly/mango facts.

Materials: 
Recipe cards and globe (or map)
Tray set-up for each group of 3-4 students:
Blender/Immersion blender
Medium Bowl and measuring tools for Ingredients:
3/4 cup Pacific Foods Unsweetened Coconut Milk 
1 cup frozen mango
1 small frozen banana
1 teaspoon chia seeds
Spatula
Bowls & spoons for tasting
Optional toppings: Sliced Banana, Granola, Berries, Coconut Flakes, Pepitas, Chia Seeds, Shaved Chocolate Chunks, Dried Fruit

Prep work and setup:
Open up mobile kitchen
Bring folding table(s) down
Wipe down tables/chairs
Dirty dish tub
Cleaning spray, wipes, paper towels, hand soap & sanitizer
Garbage pail & compost bin
3 blenders
Tray set-up for students (see above)

Anticipated problems:
Discussion will have to be adjusted per age/grade.
Lead students step-by-step through recipe measurements (demonstrate different measuring spoons/cups)

Mango Fact Sheet
How Much do Flies help with Pollination
USDA Fly Pollination
Pest World for Kids
Different Types of Flies
Original Recipe

Questions for students/extension activities: None.

None

Breakfast around the World / Israeli Salad (Intro to Global Unit)

Summary: Students will read Pancakes to Parathas as an introduction to the global unit exploring foods from around the world. They will learn about the country Israel and make an Israeli salad. They will start filling in their Food Passports. 

Objectives: Students will be able to describe how people eat breakfasts around the world. Students will be able to make Israeli salad.

Cultural Responsiveness: encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Review previous lesson (whole wheat pancakes or plant part stir fry)
Read Pancakes to Parathas, highlighting global foods and pointing out countries on the map
Pause during the page about Israel to read the description of Israeli foods.
Introduce new global unit: We will be learning about different countries and trying foods from all around the world. 
Ask, Has anyone ever visited another country? What do you need to visit a new country? A passport! Show passports and explain that after they finish cooking, they will set up their passports and fill in the page for Israel.
Intro to Israel - show on map, discuss culture, highlight traditional foods
Israel is a small country in the Middle East (point it out on map) about the same size as New Jersey
The capital is Jerusalem
It has snowy mountains in the north, and hot deserts in the south
Most people speak either Arabic or Hebrew. Let’s practice some Hebrew! To say hello, you say “Shalom.” 
Have students turn to their neighbors to greet them in Hebrew.
Show pictures of popular foods: hummus, falafel, pita bread, and Israeli salad
Introduce recipe
In Israel, it is commonly referred to as “salat katzutz” (chopped salad). 
Group role cards and transition to cooking

Engaging activity:
Students make israeli salad in groups:
Dice cucumbers, tomatoes, onions, bell peppers
Cut parsley with kid scissors
Squeeze lemon juice & pour olive oil on top 
As students start to finish, pause to give directions for cleaning up and filling in the first page of food passports. Pass out passports and markers/crayons.
Younger students can do a worksheet instead of passports (Israel worksheet 1 or Israel worksheet 2)
Serve with pita bread. Teach students how to say “Thank you” in Hebrew (“Toda”)

Closing ritual:
Groups present on how they worked together as a team to follow the recipe
Reflection questions:
What is something new you learned about Israel today?
Why do you think we should learn about foods from around the world?
How would you describe the Israeli salad? 
Teach students how to say “Goodbye” (“Lehit’raot”)

Materials: 
Cucumbers
Bell peppers
Tomatoes
Parsley
Lemons
Olive oil in pincher
Salt & pepper
Bowl
Mixing spoon
Knives
Cutting boards
1 pair kid scissors (for parsley)

Prep work and setup:
Print and put together passports
Print take home recipes
Prep vegetables 
Set up trays
Cut pita bread
Make a chart paper with info about Israel, including food pictures

Soil Components and Layers 

Summary: Students will learn about the different components and layers of soil. They will conduct a soil filtration experiment and create an edible Soil Lasagna recipe.

Objectives: Students will be able to explain the different components and layers of soil. They will also gain an understanding of how soil acts as a filter.

Cultural Responsiveness: Promotes student-centered instruction by having students create and test their own soil filters. Incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles and reshapes curriculum by using hands-on scientific exploration/experimentation as well as a food tasting to visualize soil layers. 

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: Opening Circle (10-15 minutes)
Previous class review: 
What did we talk about last class? Compost
How is compost made? Food waste, recycling organic resources, worms, greens/browns, etc.
What are some benefits of composting? Mitigate climate change, less pollution transporting waste to landfills, supports plant growth, etc.
Compost is only a very small part of the soil and today, we’ll be discussing soil as a whole. 
What else is in soil? What is it made of? Elicit student responses and list on board. Draw pie chart on board and draw line from listed student responses to respective components in pie chart:

Compost is part of the soil called Organic Matter. Organic Matter contains both living or decomposing materials that provide plants with nutrients to thrive and grow. Nutrients are things that help us grow and keep us strong. (For example, eating an apple gives us nutrients that keep us healthy; the apple core that we don’t eat still has nutrients and can go into a compost bin. It then gets eaten by other smaller organisms like worms, insects, and bacteria. Those nutrients help animals grow and then pass through the animals back into the soil, then back into the plants growing in the soil.) It’s a cycle of nutrients.
Soil is also made up of Minerals. Minerals come from rocks, sand (which are like tiny rocks), clay, and even shells. Minerals are also nutrients. Have you heard of Calcium from milk that helps your bones stay strong? Calcium is a mineral! 
Soil is also made up of at least half water and air! 

Engaging activity: 
(10-15 min)Exploration: Soil Layering - In nature, soil isn’t found in a circle pie chart, it actually falls into layers.
Hand out Soil Layers Worksheet and give students a minute to guess at the layers of soil
Layers of Soil: demonstrate or have students build layers step-by-step in clear plastic cups (with pre-drilled holes in bottom)
Bedrock→ cup 
Weathered/Parent Rock → gravel, rocks, perlite
Subsoil → sand/sandy soil
Topsoil → potting soil
Surface → plant material (leaves, grass, moss, etc.)

(15-20 min)Experiment: Soil as a Filter  
Once all layers are assembled, place layered cup in smaller clear cup
Ask students: 
What is a filter?  (Show a coffee filter or a tea bag to aid discussion.)
Can soil be a filter?
Do all soils work the same?
What would happen if I poured dirty water into the soil?
Have students make hypothesis: 
What will happen to the dirty water "floaties" (colored sprinkles)? 
What color will come out the bottom? 
Have students slowly pour pre-made “dirty” water through soil cup and make observations on water that flows into smaller cups
Prompts for observation:
What happens to the things that were floating in the dirty water? 
What color is the water that collects in the bottom cup?
Is the water in the bottom cup the same color that it was?
If we had more sand or rocks or grass/leaves in one of our cups, would that change the color of the water in the bottom cup?
So what?  (Application)
Soil naturally filters water that falls as rain and goes into rivers.
Soil filters many chemicals out of water just like it did some of the food color.
(15-20min)Make Soil Lasagna: If time allows or instead of filter experiment, make edible Soil Lasagna in clear cups.
Bedrock →  cup
Parent rock → granola, Cheerios, marshmallows, or nuts
Subsoil →  seeds or rice crispies
Topsoil →  chia seeds, cocoa powder, chocolate syrup, or cinnamon
Soil surface → fresh or dried fruit
Air + Water →  milk or yogurt

Closing ritual:
Tasting: Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks! 
Taste Soil Lasagna
1 thumb up = Like, 2 thumbs up = love, Sideways thumbs = make suggestions to make it better

Materials: 
Soil Layers
Bedrock→ 5oz cup with pre-drilled holes in bottom (1 for each student or group of students) 
Weathered/Parent Rock → gravel, rocks, perlite
Subsoil → sand/sandy soil
Topsoil → potting soil
Surface → plant material (leaves, grass, moss, etc.)
Soil Filtration
3oz cup (1 for each student or group of students)
“Dirty” water: a combination of all or some of the the following - 2 Tbsp coffee grinds, 1 Tbsp cooking oil, colored sprinkles, food coloring or kool aid mixed in 1 cup water
Soil Lasagna
Bedrock →  clear cups (1 for each student)
Parent rock → granola, Cheerios, marshmallows, or nuts
Subsoil →  seeds or rice crispies
Topsoil →  chia seeds, cocoa powder, chocolate syrup, or cinnamon
Soil surface → fresh or dried fruit
Air + Water →  milk or yogurt
Spoons for tasting

Prep work and setup:
Edible soil: Set up bowls/ramekins of ingredients for each student
Sol Layering: Set up bowls of each type of soil media; poke holes in larger 5oz cups
Soil filter: make dirty water mix 

Anticipated problems:
Even if the filtered water looks clean - advise students to NOT drink the water!
Different levels of comprehension for each age/grade level - adjust verbiage accordingly

Background information for instructor:
Education.com Soil Layers
What is Soil?
Soil Facts for Kids

Questions for students/extension activities: Same as back pocket activities. 

Back pocket activities:
What is Soil?
Layers of Soil
Dr. Dirt Soil Filter

Task cards or handouts used: 
Soil Layers Worksheet

Sourdough, Petri Dishes, Smoothies
 
Summary: Students will make sourdough starter, petri dish cultures, and smoothies.

Objectives: Students will learn about microorganisms, and their role in cooking.

Cultural Responsiveness: 
Promotes student-centered instruction
have students offer suggestions for recipes - students will build their own pasta bowl
Reshapes curriculum
Incorporating group work

Social-Emotional Skills: 
Self-management
Take small risks in trying new foods and using new tools.
Relationship skills
Willingly take turns and/or share tools
Working well in groups
Make connections to outside life
Responsible decision-making
Cleaning up messes
Putting materials back where they belong
Self-awareness
Displays sense of pride or ownership	


Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual (10 minutes):
Thank students for joining, and have them wash hands before taking a seat. (5 minutes)
Introduce the activity - we’ll start with making sourdough to make bread next week, then talk about the bacteria that make it taste great.

Engaging activity - Sourdough starter (5 minutes):
Explain that sourdough includes the ingredient yeast, which are suuuper tiny living things. This living ingredient helps our dough rise.
Each instructor will make one sourdough starter with each table.
Show slides with photos of yeast and examples in cooking.

Engaging activity - Petri dishes (10 minutes):
Display prepared petri dishes to students. Although we can’t see much of the microorganisms that exists around us, we can use petri dishes to help us grow visible colonies of these living beings. 
Demo: Wet the cotton swab, swipe it on your sample of interest, and gently swipe the surface of the agar. The agar is a nutritious gelatinous substance that will feed our bacteria. Do you remember using agar in our recipes earlier this year?
Cover up the plate and place it in a ziploc bag.
What are some of the areas of our classroom we might expect to find lots of bacteria? What are we curious to explore?
We’ll allow a week for growth, and will set up an area in the warm greenhouse to facilitate growth.

Engaging activity - Smoothies (15 minutes):
Have students read the recipe.
Measure out ingredients & blend.
Pour into cups to enjoy. 

Closing ritual (10 minutes):
Have students tidy up their space.
Stack materials into the dishwasher tray, wipe tables, and wash hands.
Thank students for their time, and remind students that we’ll be making our very own bread next week. 

Materials: 
Sourdough
Flour & water
Jars, cheesecloth
Petri dishes & swabs
Smoothies
Kale, spinach, frozen fruits, oat milk
Blenders, cups
Measuring cups (1 cup, ½ cup)

Anticipated problems:
Sourdough - contamination
Student disinterest

School Lunch Heroes

Summary: Students learn about school food workers, what a day in the life of a school food worker is like, complete a coloring and writing worksheets on school food workers to display in the school, and decorate aprons to give to their school cafeteria staff.

Objectives: Students will be able to:
Identify the responsibilities of school cafeteria workers.
Demonstrate appreciation for the hard work and dedication of school cafeteria workers in their school community.

Core Competencies:
Community stewardship
Social justice

Cultural Responsiveness: Promotes student-centered instruction (encourages student activism)

Social-Emotional Skills: Relationship skills (working well in groups)

Garden Connection: Staff and/or students could cut flowers from the garden to make bouquets for their school food workers.

Tags: school lunch hero day, cafeteria, community, activism

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual (with SEL component, emphasis on yearly theme):
Welcome the students and ask them to give you a thumbs up/down to show how they are feeling. (5 minutes)
Today we’re going to learn about a very special group of people in this school. I like to call them school lunch heroes because they are the workers in the cafeteria who make lunch for all <insert school enrollment> students each and every day! Isn’t that amazing?
Show a video that gives students an overview of what a day in the life of a school food worker is like, then check for understanding by having students answer reflection questions. (10 minutes)
Why do you think it’s important that every student gets lunch?
How might you feel if you didn’t eat lunch? 
Would you have the energy to do all the things you love to do? 
Would you be able to focus if you felt hungry?

Engaging activity (25 minutes):
The job that our school lunch heroes do is so important that there’s a day celebrated all across America in honor of workers just like the ones in our school cafeteria. It’s called School Lunch Hero Day and it’s coming up in May!
To show our appreciation for our cafeteria workers and celebrate School Lunch Hero Day, we’re going to make signs to hang up in the cafeteria and decorate aprons to give to them.
Walk through the two activities - sign making and apron decoration. Show examples of a sign you have made and a decoration you have added to an apron (see Materials below).
Depending on your class size and space, you could have an area to pull students to decorate the aprons while everyone works on their signs, or you could have everyone work on their signs then transition everyone to work on aprons (1 apron per table). 
Note: if doing this with multiple classes/grades, ensure students leave space on the aprons for all students to add their own decoration.
For the signs, you might write prompts on the board that they can use (see Materials).
If students need more time beyond the class period to finish their signs, ask the classroom teacher if they can take them back to their room to do so then put them in your mailbox when complete.

Closing ritual (with SEL component) (5 minutes):
Bring everyone back to their seats if you have separated them into groups. 
Raise your hand and tell me 1 thing you learned about school lunch heroes.
Show me thumbs up/thumbs down if you think our cafeteria workers will like your signs.
What is 1 thing you wrote on your sign?
If there’s time, you could hand out a tasting of a fruit they serve on the tray line, like apples or clementines.

Back pocket activity:
If students finish their signs early, have print-outs of relevant coloring sheets and/or word games they can do.

Materials: 
Signs and possible back pocket sheets (at the end)
Pgs. 1-11 are signs
Alternatively, pg. 12 is a fill in the blank letter
You could also make your own in Canva that include the official graphics
Markers, crayons, or colored pencils
Pencils
Apron decoration guide
Blank aprons
​​Fabric markers or permanent markers

Plant Adaptations


Summary: Students will do a gallery walk, observing how different plants are built in special ways that allow them to survive.  

Objectives: Students will be able to explain what adaptation is and give some examples of how plants are adapted. Students will be able to notice how a plant is adapted by observing it.  

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles by providing a kinesthetic, sensory method of learning about plant adaptations. 

Social-Emotional Skills: Students practice self-management by working independently or in small groups on the activity.

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
“This year in garden class, we are going to be learning all about how plants and animals survive. The more we know about plants and animals, the better we will be at taking care of them in our garden and in our neighborhood. We’re going to be learning about plants and animals, then practice what we learn when we take care of the garden.” 
Write the word “adaptation” on the board. Ask the students if they know this word, or if this is a new word for them.  Have students share what they already know. Write on the board that “adaptation” means that a plant or an animal is built in a special way that helps it survive and live in its natural habitat. 
Walk around a picture of a polar bear and as students to think about how this animal is adapted to its environment.  Ask: “How is its body built in a way that is helping it survive in the Arctic?”
Allow students to turn and talk with a partner to share their answers, or allow students to share their answers in the full group. 
“This year in garden class we are going to be learning about how plants and animals adapt in their environments. When we understand plants and animals more, we better know how to help and protect them.”
Engaging activity:
Tell the students that just like this animal is adapted to its environment, plants can also be built in special ways to help them survive and grow. Explain that we will be walking around the room and looking at different plants to try to figure out how they are adapted.  
Hand out plant adaptation worksheets to the students.  Have students read the adaptation descriptions out loud.  Show the students the duckweed floating on water as the example.  Read its adaptation description on the paper so students will see how each plant on their paper will match with one description. Take any questions about the worksheet.  Tell the students that there will also be little clues next to each plant to help them with the matching game. 
Hand out pencils. Walk around with students, making sure they are noticing the clues next to each plant, helping them read the worksheet, and asking them prompting questions about the activity. 

Closing ritual:
Ask a few of them to share out loud one of the matches they made, and why they thought that plant matched that description. 
Ask students whether humans can adapt too, like plants and animals. Have students turn to a partner and share a time that they adapted. Reiterate that when we share things about ourselves, we can understand each other better, just like we understand plants and animals more when we learn about them.
End with a tasting. 



Materials:
Plants (such as aloe plant, a geranium, a citrus tree, a flowering plant, a banana tree, and duckweed)
Plant adaptation worksheet 
Plant adaptation clues 
Photo of an adapted animal such as polar bear, to show during the opening circle
Clipboards
Pencils
Tasting

Prep work and setup:
If doing this lesson outdoors, determine what plants you will be using. Ensure they are spaced far apart so no area gets too crowded.
If doing this lesson indoors, arrange plants for the gallery walk so they are evenly spaced around the room so no area gets too crowded.  
Put out signs that help give students clues about what they should be noticing about a plant at each station.
Anticipated problems:
A simplified version of the worksheet for younger students or students with less writing proficiency is available here.
Don’t use a cactus plant to show spikes as a form of adaptation; students will touch it!  Aloe plants have small spikes that still illustrate the point, while also not being sharp enough to hurt students. 
Students may not get to each plant. The teacher can tell them before the activity that during the gallery walk we will get to as many plants as they can, but it is not required for them to get to every plant.  
Some students may mismatch the plants to different adaptations on their worksheet, but as long as they are thinking about the adaptations and have an explanation for the answer, it is okay to have different answers. For example, one could argue that multiple plants have means to get a lot of sunlight: tall branches, many leaves, wide leaves, etc.
As you send students to start the gallery walk, have a group of about 5-6 students start at each of the different plants, so that no one plant is too crowded.  Tell students to go in order, starting with whichever plant they were assigned to by the teacher and proceeding numerically so students are evenly dispersed throughout the space.

Background information for instructor:
This lesson is meant to line up with the 3rd grade science curriculum in New York City about animal adaptations.  Try to match this lesson up to the correct time of year so it connects to the students’ current curriculum.

Questions for students/extension activities:
Students can continue to talk about adaptations and connecting these plant adaptations to how animals are adapted as well.  You could also use the seed adaptation lesson for an additional day to continue talking about adaptations.  

Back pocket activities:
If students are struggling with the worksheet or activity, try to engage them in the gallery walk and plant observation in other ways, perhaps giving them a magnifying glass, by allowing them to draw the plants, or by doing a leaf rubbing instead.  

Task cards or handouts used: See above.

Photos: None.

Pupusas & Curtido

Summary: Students will make this stuffed flatbread while discussing the Spanish diaspora throughout Latin America, as witnessed by the use of similar recipes in various countries and cultures.

Objectives: Students will be able to make dough and understand the differences and similarities of the El Salvadoran pupusa, Venezualan and Columbian arepa, and Mexican gordita.

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Hand washing
Pulse check
Opening Circle/Discussion
Today we are making a dish from El Salvador called Pupusas, similar to Arepas from Venezuela and Columbia and Gorditas from Mexico.
Where are these countries on the map? (Optional: Have students find/point out countries on a map.) Despite being different countries, why/how do they have similar dishes? 
Review the word Diaspora? The spread of cultures known as the Spanish Diaspora happened during Spain’s exploration efforts beginning in the 1400’s. What we now know as Latin America was a result of the Spanish empire’s largest emigration through the colonization of the Americas.
Discuss and demonstrate the commonality of Maseca masa/dough
3K Adaptation: Ingredient Identification (beans, carrots, cabbage, etc.) and/or sorting pinto beans from a variety of different beans

Engaging activity:
Prep/Cook
2-4 tables preparing and cooking pupusas
Making Masa
Stuffing with beans and cheese
Flattening and cooking
1-2 tables preparing filling 
shredding mozzarella cheese
dicing onions and peppers
mincing garlic
cooking and mashing beans
1-2 tables preparing Curtido
Grating carrots
Shredding cabbage
Slicing onion
Combining vinegar mixture

Closing ritual:
Eat/ Discuss, Shout-outs & Clean up

Materials: 
Large mixing bowl 
Cutting board/mat for each student
Measuring cups/spoons
Whisk
Griddle or large pan
Graters/shredders or scissors


Prep work and setup:
Prep dough into individual balls before students arrive (allotting 1-3 golf ball size balls per student).
Prep filling ingredients (onions, peppers, garlic, beans).
Shred cheese for stuffing. 
Pre-make pupusas so a group of students can get started on cooking while others assemble more pupusas.

Anticipated problems:
For 3K-PreK: Ingredients should be pre-prepped to be more manageable. Utilize scissors and lettuce knives, but also have ingredients that can be ripped.
Trouble rolling, stuffing, and flattening dough.
Grater safety.

What Was The Age Of Exploration Or The Age Of Discovery?
Arepa, Pupusa, Gordita comparison

Indoor/Outdoor


Haudenosaunee      
Thanksgiving Address


Summary: Students will read/listen to the Haudenosaunee Thanksgiving Address, partake in a garden job related to Native teachings, and do a gratitude activity related to the Thanksgiving address. 

Objectives: Students will learn about Indigenous peoples’ reciprocal relationship to the environment 

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson reshapes curriculum by positioning Native American people and cultures as leaders and models for environmental caretaking. 

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson promotes social awareness by helping students to understand the leadership of Native Americans on current environmental and social justice issues.

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening: 15mins
Welcome back to class, 
The teacher introduces that today’s class will be about Native Americans, or Indigenous People. Ask what students know about how NYC looked like a few hundred years ago vs. now.  New York City looked very different. “There were no big buildings or subways or parks, but instead hills and forests. The land of New York City belonged to the Lenni Lenape people, and for thousands of years they cared for and honored this land.” Show image (p.1) from the Welikia Project.
What forces do you know of that caused this change? 
Colonization/ what effects do we see, what effects on indigenous people.
What do you know about colonization
“All across North America there were other tribes and Nations of Indigenous people. Today I want to talk about the Haudenosaunee People, an alliance of six tribes. The Haudenosaunee People’s traditional lands are in other parts of New York State.” Show image (p.2) of map of New York or the image from p.17 of this reading. “Each tribe has their own language and culture, but all live by The Great Law of Peace. This tells people how to treat others, the world, and how to live in community together.”
Distinguish bt Haudenosaunee/ Iroquois and Lenape- show map
“Every year, many Americans celebrate Thanksgiving and take time to share what they are thankful for. The Haudenosaunee People practiced gratitude everyday, not just on Thanksgiving, through their Thanksgiving Address. This address tells us to appreciate and acknowledge all things.”
What does peace look or feel like for you? Where are you when you feel at peace? 
Before reading, explain that Native American people understand that people and nature have an “interconnected” relationship. In this understanding, the land provides for people, and people have a responsibility to care for the land in return. 
Have printouts of Thanksgiving Address (Page 1) on each table. 1 volunteer from each table will read aloud while others read along. If no student wants to read the 5th paragraph aloud, the teacher will read for class along with the conclusion paragraph. 
What does peace look or feel like for you? Where are you when you feel at peace?
Has anyone been outside of the city to the countryside? How do you feel when you’re there?
What resources can we get from our environment, how do we take care of it while doing so
How did people stay warm, what do we need to make a fire, a house, food, water.. Etc
Ask how this connects to The Great Law of Peace- The Haudenosaunee understood a long time ago how important the peaceful feelings and resources are that come from our environment. That is why in their Thanksgiving Address, they gave thanks to all of the different things the earth can provide for us if we take care of it. (water, food, people..)
Discussion questions/prompts for after the reading:
What was your favorite part? 
What parts did you connect with most?
Why is it important to be thankful for nature’s gifts?
Do you think all people see themselves as interconnected with nature? Why or why not?

Engaging activity: 20 mins
Lead students in a seasonal garden job.
601- Harvest culantro and sweet potatoes from terrace beds
602 (Raining)- Plant marigold seeds and water 
701 (Raining)- Separate molokhia seeds from pods
702- Harvest tatsoi, kale, herbs if time
801- Sift compost bins
802- Sift compost bins
811- Collect herbs from the garden, put on music and make bouquets
Outside backup: 
Plant daffodil bulbs in perennial pollinator bed
Clear dead sunflowers, peppers, other annuals
Closing ritual: 10 mins
Wash Hands
Reference back to list in Thanksgiving Address, people, plants, water.. At the end, have students partake in their own gratitude activity, sharing something in the natural world that they are thankful for and how they can take care of that thing.
What do you like about working to take care of our garden
What emotions did you feel while gardening? 

Materials: 
Document with the excerpts we will be reading from the Haudenosaunee Thanksgiving Address
Photos
Materials for garden job
Seasonal tasting (potentially popcorn to relate to 3 sisters)

Prep work and setup:
Set up for garden jobs.
Set up for tasting. 

Anticipated problems:
Students may come with preconceived notions about Indigenous Peoples, the history behind Thanksgiving, and our general right to live on this land. Correct students and inform them of the truthful history and help them respect and appreciate Indigenous knowledge and tradition.

Background information for instructor:
A reading from the Museum of the American Indian provides much more context on the Haudenosaunee people and their lives.
An interactive map details ancestral homelands of different tribes and nations.

Photos: 
From the Welikia Project
Map of New York state that shows different tribal lands
Document with the two images to print before class

Back pocket activities: Make herb bouquets to hang in classroom and dry
	For Rainy Days: 
Worm bin, prepare newspaper for them 
Questions for students/extension activities: None.

Seeds

Summary: Students will learn a dance to show what seeds need to grow and remove seeds from pods or fruits to save them.

Objectives: Students will be able to explain what seeds are and what they need to grow.

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, self-awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Tell students that we are going to be learning about the special way that a plant starts its life. 
Read your chosen book relating to seeds. 

Engaging activity:
Teach the students a “seed dance.”
Show students how to crouch down low and curl up like a seed. 
Walk around and tap each student on the head and explain that you are planting them into the soil.
Mime pouring the watering can over each student’s head and explain that you are giving them water to grow.
Walk around over each student with arms overhead, like a shining sun. Explain that now that they have water and sun, the seeds can grow.
Demonstrate to students how to stand up and stretch out, miming like a plant growing out of the soil.
Lead students in seed saving.
Show students how to remove the bean from the pod, or how to remove another kind of seed from its pod or fruit. 
Show students where to place the seeds that they are saving, and where to place the compost. Distribute small piles of dried pods around the group so that all students have easy access, and replenish as students work. 
At the end, compile all the saved seeds into one container, and show it around the circle. 
Closing ritual:
Recap the seed dance, and ask students to tell you what seeds need to grow. 
Finish with a tasting.

Materials: 
Book for reading about seeds (such as “Planting a Rainbow” by Lois Ehlert or “The Tiny Seed” by Eric Carle)
Watering can
Baskets or bins for compost
Small cups for saved seeds
Seeds to save (i.e. beans, sunflower seeds, corn)
Seed tasting (or other tasting)

Prep work and setup:
Collect bean pods or other seeds to save. 

Anticipated problems:
Larger seeds such as beans, corn, peas, and certain flower seeds are ideal for younger students. Students can also save seeds from inside fruits or vegetables, like sweet peppers.

Background information for instructor: None.

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities:
If students finish early or there are not many seeds to save, students can also decorate envelopes to store their seeds in. 

Task cards or handouts used: None.

Photos: None.

                  Orientation Lesson
Summary: Students will be reoriented to the garden and kitchen classrooms. 

Objectives: Students will re-learn the rules and norms of the garden and kitchen classrooms and will be reintroduced to the space. Students will also do garden tasks and play ice breaker games to rebuild classroom culture and comradery. 

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness, renews connection

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Opening Circle
“Good Morning/Afternoon class. Welcome back to school! I am very excited to be with you all this year! Take a moment and think about one fun thing you did this summer. Then we will share what we discussed in our partner groups with the class.” 

Take responses from students. 

“Show of hands, who wants to have a fun and successful time in garden and kitchen class this year? I know I do. Turn to your neighbor and discuss two things our class will need in order to have a fun and successful year in garden and kitchen class. Then once everyone has discussed with their partner, I will ask a few students to share what they discussed in their groups with the rest of class.”

Take responses from students
Reaffirm student responses with RULES OF THE GARDEN SIGN
Briefly review garden rules and highlight how the rules are also the same for the kitchen classroom


ICEBREAKERS/NAME GAMES/INITIATIVES:

Name Toss
Gather students in a circle and explain the objective and instruction

“We have this ball, this ball has to make its way around the circle one time. Everybody in the circle has to touch the ball once. However, you may not pass the ball to the person standing to the right or to the left of you. Everybody take a look and see who's standing next to you. DO NOT pass the ball to either person”

“You must only pass the ball to someone standing across from you. Then you pass the ball, make sure you say Here you go (insert person’s name) and once they catch it they will say Thank you (insert person’s name) before they proceed to throw it to someone else. If the ball drops, we will start over in the same order that we began with. Does everyone understand these rules?”

As the ball drops and students cycle through rounds, instruct students to strategize ways that they can prevent the ball from dropping (making the circle closer, tossing the ball gently, saying the person’s name loud and clear etc.)

As students get the first ball successfully around the circle without dropping it, introduce a new ball, keeping the previous ball(s) in the game. This challenges students to focus and work more collaboratively.

2nd ball: Have students toss the ball in the reverse order of the first
3rd ball: Allow students to toss pass this ball to the person standing left of them 
4th ball: Have students toss the to the person standing to the right of them

GARDEN TASKS:
Compost sifting 
Harvesting cherry tomatoes for a tasting
Watering
Weeding

Closing ritual:
Review Rules of the Garden and Kitchen

g
The Garden in the Fall 

Summary: Students will learn about different signs of the Fall season that can be seen in the school garden and some things that grow in the garden in the Fall.

Objectives: Students will be able to identify signs of fall in the garden.

Core Competencies: garden skills and related academic content, social-emotional learning.

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson reshapes curriculum by incorporating movement and group work. This lesson positions teacher as facilitator by executing students to work mostly independently.

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson promotes relationship skills by expecting students to work well in groups, Display calm, caution, or care for animals and plants, and Make connections to outside life. 

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
For all lessons: Seasons, Fall
If gardening: Signs of Fall

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)

Welcome students to the garden (5-8 mins) 
How is everyone feeling today?
Review rules/expectations for existing in garden space: raise you’re hand if you feel ready to be safe in the garden today? Who’s excited to be in the garden today? 
Last time we were here, we learned about leaves in the garden. Leaves are a very important part of the season we are in right now!
Introduce activity: Does anyone know what season we are in right now? If no responses: is it winter? … is it Summer? It’s Fall! Today we’re going to explore signs of Fall in the garden. How do we know that we are in Fall right now? What is different about Fall? (take answers)
Introduce activity and remind students to move around the garden safely and not to pick anything without asking!

Engaging activity: (25-35 mins)
Talk quickly about signs of fall! (maybe some were already shared by students)
Signs: Leaves changing color! Leaves fall off the trees, Weather is getting cooler, Acorns falling, May be more squirrel activity.
Ask students if it feels cooler than summer? Are they wearing any clothes that they wouldn’t have worn in the summer?
Three activities that you can choose one or both of depending on time/ class functioning:
Pass out outlines of leaf shape outlines on clipboards of leaves that can be found in your garden - have red, orange, yellow, and brown crayons available for students. Tell students they are going to have to explore the garden in search of a leaf that matches the shape(s) on their clipboard. Once they find a matching leaf they are going to do their best to color in the outline that they have with the colors/pattern they see on the leaf they found. You can either have papers with different shapes on each paper (maybe 3 or 4 different shapes and a bunch of copies of each) or one sheet of paper with each leaf shape on it so that students have the opportunity to find and draw different types of leaves. 
Have students make leaf crowns. You’ll need to have bands of paper that will fit around students' heads. Students will find leaves that they think are interesting or beautiful looking and glue them to their paper band. You and teachers/paras could also staple or help tape them to the paper. Students can color the paper bands if they want to. You and other adults should carefully measure and staple the bands to fit each student's head. 
Make a coloring sheet of different crops that are growing in your garden (this will be different at every school) in the fall, take students on a guided tour to show them where each are growing before they start coloring. These are things that grow best in the fall because of how the sun and temperature are in the fall. 

Closing ritual with SEL component: (5-10 mins)
Gather students back together
Make sure materials are all gathered and put away in the correct places
Ask if anyone wants to share their drawings with the class
Tell them all how beautiful and amazing and detailed their drawings/crowns are!
Raise your hand if you moved around the garden safely today 
Who can remind us what season we’re in right now? How do we know?
Raise your hand if you found a really beautiful leaf today
Who had a nice time in the garden today?
Dismiss!

Back pocket activity–REQUIRED: 
Students can look for insects, water the garden, or draw other garden observations on blank paper.

Materials: 
Clipboards, Paper, Papers with Leaf outlines, Crayons or Markers (if you have magical pre-k kids that don’t lose all the caps in the garden)

World Religions - Hinduism

Summary: Students will recall what they know about Hinduism and prepare a recipe.

Objectives: Students will be able to use teamwork to complete a recipe.
                    Students will be able to understand the dietary customs related to Hinduism. 

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
In social studies you all have begun a unit about the different religions people practice around the world.  Can anyone remind the class what the first religion you have been studying is? (Hinduism) 
Can one volunteer from each table share one fact about Hinduism that you have learned so far? (Wait for responses → move on after ~3-5). 
Those who practice Hinduism believe that cows are a sacred animal, so they don’t eat beef.  But many hindus don’t eat meat at all! Someone who does not eat meat is a vegetarian.
Today we are going to make a vegetarian dish called biryani. Biryani is a rice dish filled with vegetables that is popular in India where a lot of people who practice Hinduism live.

Engaging activity: 
Each table will have a task:
Table 1: Work at the stove top. 
Table 2: Vegetable prep
Table 3: Seasonings and rice

Closing ritual:
Clean up
Say thank yous
Eat together
Share thoughts about the meal. What was your favorite part? How could we make it better?

Wash cilantro and mint

Black Bean Burgers




Summary: Students will read an article, then cook a recipe.  
	

Objectives: Students will be able to use teamwork to make a recipe
                    Students will be able to explain what vegetarians do/don’t eat.
                    Students will be able to identify fast foods from around the world. 
	

Cultural Responsiveness:communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, reshapes curriculum 
	

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, social awareness
	

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
1. You all have read the article about fast food around the world. Can anyone recall some of the different fast foods available in other countries that you read about? (Wait for responses) One of the countries mentioned in the article was India. Many people in India are vegetarians (can someone tell us what a vegetarian is? - Explain that vegetarians don’t eat any meat) because their religion considers cows a sacred animal. Does anyone know what kind of popular fast food item comes from cows? (Wait for responses - move one when someone answers burger).  So instead of eating the hamburgers made of meat that you are used to, they eat veggie burgers instead.  Today we are going to make our own veggie burgers using black beans.


Engaging activity:
1. Students will split up into small groups to prepare the recipe

   2. Slaw (1-2 students)
   3. Optional: sauce (1 student)


Closing ritual:
1. Clean up
2. Say thank yous
3. Eat together
4. Share thoughts about the meal. What was your favorite part? How could we make it better?

5th-8nd GradeFall/Winter
Indoor/Outdoor





The NYC Watershed and Lemonade 

Summary: Students will create a watershed model and learn about the NYC watershed, from source to sink. They will also create and taste a refreshing strawberry lemonade.

Objectives: Students will be able to explain:
- what is a watershed.
- the difference between salt versus freshwater.
- understand natural and human effects on our waterways.
- learn to make strawberry lemonade.

Cultural Responsiveness: Communicates high expectations in following a recipe. Promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, and reshapes curriculum by having students create their own watershed models and explore the different effects of natural and human elements on a watershed.

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: (10-20 min)
Globe Beach Ball Toss 
How much water is on the planet (refer to beach ball toss)? 2/3 of the planet is covered in water
How much of it is fresh water or water that we can drink? 3% of the earth's water is fresh. 2.5% of the earth's freshwater is unavailable: locked up in glaciers, polar ice caps, atmosphere, and soil. Only .5% of water on the planet is available to humans.
As a prompt for next activity, ask where does our drinking water come from in NYC? How does it get here?

Engaging activity: (30-40 min)
Watershed Models; What is a Watershed? (20-30 min)
Have students work in pairs or groups (depending on class size) to create and discuss their watershed models.
Apply different food coloring or other “pollution” examples (glitter, sand, etc.) and have students make observations on what happens to pollution in their watersheds and how does rain affect the movement of those pollutants?
Add a layer of green felt or paper towel over a part of the watershed model to depict a forest. Pour water and pollutants over felt areas and have students make observations. Ask students if/how the “forest” affected the movement of water and pollutants through the watershed. [Forests slow down water and pollutants from flowing into our waterways.]
Show a map of NYC watershed and trace our water route from the Catskills to NYC. 
Celebrate water by making and tasting Strawberry Lemonade! (10 min)
Place chopped 1-2 strawberries in cups
Have students use forks, spoons, or pestles (if available) to crush or muddle strawberries
Add 1 teaspoon of sugar (explain that students should limit their daily sugar intake to a maximum of 6 teaspoons per day)
Ask students how many teaspoons of sugar do they have left for the day after they have the lemonade?
If available, compare the amount of sugar in the store-bought bottle of juice versus lemonade.
Stir sugar and strawberry mixture until sugar is dissolved (add a bit of hot water if necessary to dissolve sugar)
Squeeze lemon sliver over strawberry/sugar mixture and stir (being careful to remove any lemon seeds)
Fill remainder of cup with fresh water, stir, and enjoy

Closing ritual:
Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks. Cheers to freshwater!

Materials: 
Opening Ritual: 
Inflatable beach ball globe 
Chalk/dry erase board, overhead or large paper and appropriate writing utensil 
Open space in a room for students to form a circle 
Watershed models for each pair or group:
A plastic or metal tray (at least 4" deep) ∙
A spray bottle filled with water 
Six sheets of newspaper 
One large sheet of plastic  
Two paper towels 
Food coloring, sand, or glitter
Felt squares or paper towel
Strawberry Lemonade: (for 1 pitcher)
8 large strawberries, halved
2 tablespoons white sugar 
7 cups water, divided
1 cup white sugar 
2 cups freshly squeezed lemon juice
Cups and pitcher for serving
Pestles, small potato mashers, or forks for crushing strawberries

Prep work and setup:
Prep lemonade ingredients 
Fill spray bottles and gather materials for sets of watershed models

Anticipated problems:
Adjust activities and verbiage according to age/grade level. 

Background information for instructor:
How Much Water is there on Earth?

Questions for students/extension activities: See back pocket activities.

Back pocket activities: Watershed Activities

Task cards or handouts used: None

3rd GradeFall/Spring
Outdoor


Sensory Poetry

Summary: Students make sensory observations in the garden and turn their observations into descriptive poetry.

Objectives: Students will be able to practice using descriptive language.

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes student-centered instruction, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills:  self-awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Explain to students that you are going to read a poem to them called “April Rain Song” by Langston Hughes. Ask them to listen for examples of the senses used in the poem: sight, sound, smell, touch, and taste.
Ask them to describe the mood of the poem. Explain that mood is how the poem makes you feel, what emotions it makes you feel. 

Engaging activity:
Explain to students that we will start by gathering sensory information in the garden, using sight, sound, smell, and touch. Later, we will use what we observed to write a poem, like Langston Hughes did.
Show students the sensory observation worksheet and model how to write their notes. 
Pass out sensory observation worksheets on clipboards and pencils. Give students time to walk around the garden, making observations on their worksheets. Circulate to help students as needed.
Return students to the large group. Ask students to share out what they observed.
Explain to students that there are multiple ways to write a poem. Ask students to share their knowledge with you about what a poem is. Emphasize elements that they might mention: rhyme, rhythm, simile, metaphor. You can also give them examples of poem structures that they could use, such as an acrostic poem or a haiku. 
Pass out blank paper. Circulate to help students write their poems. 

Closing ritual:
Give students an opportunity to read out their poems.
Finish with a tasting.

Materials: 
Copies of Langston Hughes’ poem, “April Rain Song” 
Sensory Observations worksheet
Lined paper
Clipboards
Pencils
Tasting

Prep work and setup:
Put papers on clipboards.

Anticipated problems:
Your students may need some guidance or structure in writing their poems. If so, give them a worksheet with sentence starters or a poem structure for them to fill in. 

Background information for instructor: None.

Questions for students/extension activities: 
This lesson can exist as part of a larger unit on poetry, studying elements used in poetry and different poetic structures.

Back pocket activities: None.

Task cards or handouts used: See above.

6-8 GradeFall
Heat & No Heat




Middle School Three Sisters Soup 
Summary: Students will explore the food culture of Indigenous Peoples of the Americas by creating this Three Sisters soup. The soup also highlights the agricultural method of companion planting

Objectives: Students will be able to:
Create a soup by combining and pureeing the three sisters: corn, beans, and squash
Understand the difference between agriculture and foraging as food sources
Discover some foods that grow in NY state

Cultural Responsiveness: encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: 20 mins 
First 10 mins:
Welcome back to kitchen class! Hand washing
Pulse check: How is everyone today? 
Welcome back to kitchen class! 
Today we’re going to learn about the Three Sisters and make a soup using them!!
Intro Indigenous Peoples month  (October)
Does anyone know who’s heritage month it is right now?
Who were the first people to ever live here? What does the word Indigenous mean
Is anyone here Indigenous, do you have friends or family who are? 
The reason we’re making this recipe today is because Indigenous people were the first people to create this growing practice using the Three Sisters. This system of companion planting or planting different vegetables to work together ensured that there was enough food for everyone in their community. 
Recipe for today is a Three Sisters Soup
What are the three sisters- does anyone here have siblings?
Explain ingredients being used today. What are the three sisters, what vegetables, has anyone cooked with them before?
Ask students if they remember the different roles of the three sisters (use pictures or live plants if available):
Corn grows tall and allows beans to trellis around it
Beans have nitrogen-fixing nodules in their roots that add nitrogen to the soil
Squash leaves cover the soil to keep it moist
Ask students, “What do plants need to grow?” Soil, sun, air, compost, etc
Ask students, “What do humans need to grow?”
Accept all answers. Write student responses on the board. Direct the discussion to elicit air, water, and food.
Let’s get started using the Three Sisters to make our soup!
Engaging activity:
Prepare soup 
Peel and dice garlic
Dice onion
Dice bell peppers
Measure spices
Saute squash (and other ingredients)
Once cooked, add to blender and puree
Add corn and beans to squash puree and serve
Closing ritual:
“Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks.”
Eat and discuss: how many thumbs up? Changes/improvements?
Was this your first time having squash in a soup? 
What is something your proud of yourself or your table for doing today?
Clean up

Materials: 
Graters and microplanes
Knives or scissors and cutting boards
Measuring spoons and cups
Immersion blender

Prep work and setup:
Prepare and distribute tools and ingredients on trays 
Prep for younger grades that may need help:
Cut bell pepper into chunks
Cut onion into chunks
Combine and saute ingredients then blend
Core apples
Measure and combine dressing ingredients

Anticipated problems:
Finishing all tasks at the same time
Younger groups may need help finishing the entire recipe; adjust by pre-prepping ingredients or only doing one of the recipes
Different levels of understanding/interest in regards to companion planting and nitrogen/protein → provide visuals!

Background information for instructor:
Nitrogen Cycle
Nitrogen Fixation in Legumes
The Chemical Reaction that Feeds the World
In Search of Essential Nutrients
Feed The World - General Briefing Sheet

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities:
Coloring pages of three sister crops
Reading different food labels to find protein sources and amounts

Task cards or handouts used: 
Three sisters puppet show
Recipe cards
Task cards
Three Sisters coloring page

K-2nd GradeAll seasons
Indoor


ABCs of Heart Health 


Summary: Students will make an art project, play a game, and taste a smoothie while learning about heart health.

Objectives: Students will be able to explain what makes a healthy heart.

Cultural Responsiveness: reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Go over what we’re eating today, ask if there are any allergies.

Engaging activity:
Today we are going to talk about your heart and how to keep it healthy! Ask students to make a fist with their right hand (which is about the size of your heart) and place it over the left side of their chest. The heart’s job is to pump blood through your body - a healthy heart leads to a healthy body.
Eating healthy foods gives you a healthy heart! Reference the Go, Grow, Glow board and say that the foods we talk about in this class help keep your heart healthy and strong. Red foods are especially good for your heart. Call on students to name all the red foods they can think of, and challenge them not to repeat any (reference “Edible Colors” book if students get stuck).
Students will use beet juice (or any red juice) to paint a picture of an anatomical heart. This can also be a coloring sheet. If you do choose to paint, mix juice with a small amount of cornstarch, and put into tasting cups. Use q tips as paint brushes. Tell students not to use too much paint or else the paper might rip! Put aside finished pictures to dry.
Remind students that even though red foods in particular are great for our heart, all healthy foods are also good for our hearts and our bodies. Play bingo and tell students that all the foods on their bingo cards are heart healthy foods!
Demonstrate making a smoothie (this lesson was done right before schools closed due to COVID-19, but in other circumstances there could be opportunities for students to help make the smoothie - peeling bananas, cutting berries, measuring juice, etc)

Closing ritual:
Hand out tasting cups of smoothies. Remind students to wait before they taste, but they can look and smell. What do they think it will taste like? (Sweet, like bananas, tart, etc)
Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks, bon appetit, let’s eat! Students vote with thumbs to show how they liked it.
Before they leave, ask students what red foods are good for (your heart!)

Materials: 
Coloring sheets, crayons/colored pencils OR beet juice paint (juice, cornstarch), tasting cups, Q tips
Bingo cards, bowls with markers for each table (can be squares of construction paper, beans, seeds, etc)
Blender, ingredients for smoothie, tasting cups, cutting board and knife/apple corer

Prep work and setup:
Make sure there are bananas in the freezer
Prep berries, and any other fruit for the smoothies
Make the “paint,” portion into tasting cups
Have markers in bowls for each table

Anticipated problems:
Students might use too much paint and break the paper - remind them not to go over one area too many times (note: after I realized this and told students beforehand not to use too much paint, this wasn’t a problem. It was only an issue when I didn't say anything. Adding cornstarch to the juice also helps thicken it a bit which I believe helps avoid it being to liquidy)

Background information for instructor: None.


Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities:
Play bingo as many times as the kids want/you have time for. Let students come to the front of the room and read out the bingo cards to the class.

https://docs.google.com/document/d/1jLrzPzDV4Iopc4Z7kp7l1pv4XmU8w4HKUrDdA5UitTE/edit

Fall
Outdoor


Garden Intro: Bingo


Summary: Students will practice being respectful while exploring the garden with a Bingo game.

Objectives: Students will be able to follow class expectations for being in the garden. Students will be able to identify important parts of the garden.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Ask if anyone has been out to the school garden before. What did they see or do in the garden?
Ask, why is good to have a garden? (we can grow food, be in nature, learn/explore etc)
Today we are going outside to the garden! We will be getting to know the space and learning what’s out there by playing a game of garden bingo. 
The garden is home to a lot of living things. How can we practice respect in the garden today - to plants, animals, and each other? How can we be safe? 
Take notes on whiteboard. These will be the rules that students agree to (walk instead of running, don’t hurt anything or pick plants, be calm and kind, etc)
Review rules of bingo
Transition out to garden. Once outside, remind students of rules for being respectful. Show them the cowbell and that when they hear this bell, it’s time to gather at benches.

Engaging activity:
Distribute clipboards with bingo sheets and dry erase markers. Students may work in pairs or alone. If it is a very large or young class, it may help to have half do bingo with classroom teacher while another half does a calm garden task (watering, planting seeds, coloring etc)
Assist students with bingo. Give stickers out to students as they finish. Early finishers can play All-Star Bingo (try to find everything)
Extension for 4th/5th graders who finish very early
Ring the bell and give clean up directions: put gloves away, collect markers, wipe off clipboards & put in bin
Gather at picnic tables/benches to reflect:
Ask yourself: was I safe and respectful in the garden today? What do I want to do differently/the same next time?
What are you excited to learn/do in the garden? 
Did you see anything interesting or surprising in the garden?
How do you think the garden might change as we move into fall and winter?
Why do you think gardens are important / how can they benefit communities?

Closing ritual:
Do something here.
Add more details with a bullet like this. 
Do something else. 

Materials: 
Clipboards
Garden bingo laminated sheets 
Dry erase markers and erasers
Garden gloves


Prep work and setup:
Put bingo sheets on clipboards
Put dry erase markers and gloves on picnic table

body:
3K-8th GradeAny Season
Indoor/Outdoor


Insects: 
Pests, Pollinators, & Proteins

Summary: Students become junior entomologists, examining insect parts and making clay insect models.

Objectives: Students will be able to explain insect characteristics.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Review garden norms/ “Respect in the Garden.”
See what students know about the roles of insects in the ecosystem, food web, etc. You can discuss:
Insects as pollinators & beneficial insects
Insects as urban & agricultural pests
Insects as a source of protein
Ask students to describe characteristics of insects and draw those on the board.
3 body parts - head, abdomen, thorax
6 legs
Antennae
Wings (for most insects)

Engaging activity #1:
Using clay and objects found in the garden, have students create insect models (following basic insect characteristics, but adding whatever additions they would like to their personal insect). 
Demonstrate to students how to shape the three body parts using clay. Then, use some found objects from nature to create the rest of the body. Ex: leaves for wings, sticks for antennae, etc.
Have students share about their insect, for example: name, habitat, superpower?
Label brown paper bags with names. Place insects into bags for take home.

Engaging activity #2:
We discussed that some insects are pollinators. Now, let's do something special for the insect pollinators that visit our garden. We are going to plant flowers, and pollinators will be able to drink the nectar from the flowers that we grow.  
Give each student some wildflower seed mix. Demonstrate how to plant them in the bed.

Engaging activity #3:
Insect trivia! Arrange students into teams.
Show a photo of a creepy crawly critter– Is this an insect?
Go through each card and see which group racks up the most points.
Wild card/ final round! Is a caterpillar or a chrysalis an insect? Why or why not? Have everyone decide as a group and present their argument. Bonus point to everyone who gets it right!

Closing ritual:
Thank you, gardeners; thank you, cooks; thank you, insects!

Materials: 
Wildflower seed mix
Individually packaged air-dry clay
Paper bags
Markers
Cricket snacks
Insect trivia cards

Anticipated problems:
Students may need help collecting nature materials for their insect parts.

6th GradeFall/Winter
Indoor


Green Room Store

Summary: Students will practice using greetings by modeling going to a store to get ingredients for their recipe. 

Objectives: Students will be able to ask for the ingredients they need to make mashed potatoes. 

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
We are going to practice using greetings and talking to people when you are outside the house. One important thing to learn is how to buy things at the store. So what we are going to do today is set up the Green Room store!
You all are going to make mashed potatoes but to do that you will have to buy your ingredients at the Green Room store. Make sure that you are greeting the store worker and asking them to purchase your ingredients.  When you are done, make sure you thank them for helping you. 
Once you have all your ingredients, then you can start your recipe. 

Engaging activity:
Students will break up into two smaller groups
In their groups students will take turns going to the “Green Room” store to ask for the ingredients they need for their recipe
When at the store students should be:
Greeting the person at the store
Asking for the ingredient 
Paying the correct amount
Saying thank you when they are finished
After they have all their ingredients students will be making a quick mashed potatoes recipe.

Closing ritual:
Clean up
Say thank yous
Eat together!

Materials: 
Chart paper/markers
Fake money (Everbucks)
Pots
Wooden spoon
Potato masher

Prep work and setup:
Peel and boil potatoes
Measure out ingredients:
Milk (1 cup)
Butter (2 tablespoons)
Salt (¼ teaspoon)
Pepper (½ teaspoon)
Cheddar cheese (¼ cup grated)

Anticipated problems:
Students may want to respond in Spanish - urge them to practice using English (teacher should create word bank/sentence starters)

Background information for instructor:
This lesson was done with a small 6th grade ENL class. 

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities: None.

Task cards or handouts used: 
Mashed Potatoes Recipe: 
Cut 5 boiled potatoes into small cubes and add to a pot
Use the potato masher to mash the potatoes until they are smooth
Put the pot on the stovetop on level 1 or 2 
Add 1 cup of milk to the potatoes
Add 2 tablespoons of butter to the potatoes
Add ¼ teaspoon of salt to the potatoes
Add ¼ teaspoon of black pepper to the potatoes
Mix everything together until smooth and creamy
Add ¼ cup of grated cheddar cheese to the potatoes
Mix until the cheese melts
Enjoy!!
