5th Grade
All Seasons
Indoor


Who Grew Your Lunch?

Summary: Students will read narratives to learn more about the farmers and workers who grow our food. 

Objectives: Students will be able to explain some of the challenges that farmers and workers face in growing our food.

Cultural Responsiveness: reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Explain that almost three-quarters of our food is grown on farms that are larger than 1,000 acres. Give students some sort of scale to understand how big an acre is. (For example, a city block is usually about 5 acres.)
Explain that if a farmer owns a farm that is 1,000 acres or larger, they cannot do all the farm work themselves. A person who works on a farm, but who doesn’t own a farm, is called a “farm worker.”  
Discuss other differences between small farms and large farms.  

Engaging activity:
Explain that every group is going to get a paper lunch bag. In the paper lunch bag are some pretend “food items.” Explain that these food items are ordinary things that we might eat for lunch. On the back of each one, students will read a story about someone who grew the food or otherwise helped to get the food to us. Show students an example. 
Tell students that they should read all of the narratives in their lunch bag and then answer, in their group, the questions on the board.
Write the following questions on the board for students to discuss:
What surprised you about these stories?
What are some of the things that the farmers and farm workers liked about farming?
What are some of the challenges the farmers and farm workers faced in these stories?
Do all of the farmers and farm workers face the same challenges? 

Closing ritual:
Discuss students’ responses as a whole group. 
End with a tasting. 

Materials: 
Paper bags
Food Items Cards 
Seasonal tasting

Prep work and setup:
Assemble one paper bag per small group, each with a Grape card, Tomato card, and Lettuce card.

Anticipated problems: None. 

Background information for instructor:
The materials from this lesson are adapted from the following articles and resources:
Injustice on Our Plates
American Dirt, Mexican Grit: New York's Newest Farmers
Field of Dreams

Questions for students/extension activities: 
The Injustice on Our Plates lesson guide from Teaching Tolerance offers suggestions for furthering this realm of study, including advocacy work that students can participate in. 

Back pocket activities: None.

Plant Part Pizza



Summary: Students will make pesto and dough from scratch. They will then assemble the pizza with given ingredients and make 6 pizza’s per class.  The class will also talk about Italy, the Gravesend community and the food from Italy


Objectives: Students will be able to distinguish certain parts of the plant, learn about the ingredients needed to make dough, and be able to knead dough and roll it into a flat circle-like shape. 


Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening Ritual
Class will be opened with talking about the parts of a plant, older students that have been exposed to this information previously will be asked to recall any information they may remember about the different plant parts. Students will also then recognize what plant parts will go into the recipe for the pizza being made in class. Teachers will also mention Italy and that pizza comes from Italy but is a staple in New York city as well. 

Engaging Activity
Cucumber Table: Students will use task cards to make the pesto, they find their ingredients, add it to the mortar and pestle, then they mash it. 
Corn Table: Students at the middle table will also work with task cards, find their ingredients and add them to the bowl. They will then mix all ingredients and make observations about the dough
Carrot Table: Students at the assembly table are kneading the dough: older students get a chance to roll out two pizzas per group while younger students will each get a small piece of dough to manipulate as they please. Older students will then assemble the pizza working together, younger students will be assisted by the teacher in assembling the pizza. 
Pizzas are put into the oven during the third rotation on hot pizza stones. These pizzas will cook for only 5-7 minutes. 6 pizzas were made for 1-5th grades, and only 4-5 for kindergarten. 

Closing Ritual
Eat, “thank you gardners thank you cooks,” cleaning jobs, shout outs

Recipe ingredients
Mortar and pestle
Food processor or blender
Rolling pins
Scissors
Oven
Pizza stones

Anticipate the pizzas taking some time to cook, so preheat ovens to about 500 degrees.
Younger students might have difficulty completely mashing the pesto and assembling 2 pizzas per group.

body:
KindergartenFall/Winter/Spring
Indoor


Plant Life Cycle 

Summary: Students engage in a kinesthetic re-enactment of the plant life cycle, then illustrate the plant life cycle.

Objectives: Students will understand that plant life goes in a cycle. Students will be able to identify different parts of the plant life cycle.

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson reshapes curriculum by incorporating movement and art. 

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson allows students to practice self-management as they practice not calling out an answer during opening circle and controlling their movement in a way that is safe in a classroom during the life cycle dance. 

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Welcome students. Tell the students that you are going to show them something that we are going to be talking about in class today. If they know what it is, don’t say it out loud! Keep it a secret until everyone has a chance to see. Walk around the circle with a handful of seeds, showing each student. Once everyone has seen the seeds ask the students if anyone has a guess about what they were. They’re seeds! Today we’ll be talking about seeds and what happens to seeds in the garden. 
Engaging activity:
Do the plant life cycle dance.
Tell the students: Become seeds! Get on the ground, balled up as small as you can be, but keep your eyes on me.
Walk around and wiggle your fingers over each child, explaining that you are the spring rain.
Now your seed is going to sprout. Copy me being a seedling. Slowly raise one finger and eventually your whole hand upward over your head.
Send your other hand upward and explain: Now we are getting older, or more mature.
Wiggle your feet outward and explain: Now we are sending our roots into the ground at the same time as we grow above ground.
Grow up to the sun and stand as tall as you can, spreading out your leaves. Now we are growing up to the sun. Stand and stretch as tall as you can be.
Grow a flower bud with one hand and open it up so a bee can pollinate the flower. Narrate: Now we are growing a flower for a bee to pollinate.
Tell them: Now I am going to be a bee and I will pollinate each of your flowers.  Walk from child to child buzzing, and tickling each student’s palm, explaining that you are pollinating their flowers.
Mime the flower closing and growing into a fruit. Explain:  Now our flowers are turning into fruits. Fall on the ground. Explain: Now we are fruits. We have fallen on the ground, and eventually we will rot and mix into the soil.
Curl back up and become the seed again: When our fruit mixes into soil, it leaves seeds! And we can begin the life cycle again.
If desired, you can repeat the dance, or you can just remind students that in the life cycle the process repeats itself. If you don’t repeat the dance, ask: What happens next, now that there is a new seed in the soil?
Next draw a plant life cycle up on the board, or hang up the photos attached below in order. Show the students that it starts with a seed just like we did in our dance, then it becomes a plant and makes its own seeds. It’s like a circle, it keeps going and starting over. 
Now we’re going to make our own pictures to show the life cycle. Hand out plant life cycle worksheets to students and explain how they will draw each step in one of the boxes. Give students crayons or colored pencils to make their drawings. 
Closing ritual:
Clean up colored pencils and clipboards. Have students show their neighbor their drawing. 
Finish with a tasting, ideally a seed or a sprout to connect it to the plant life cycle dance. 

Materials: 
Dry erase board and markers
Blank paper
Crayons or colored pencils
Plant life cycle visuals and worksheet 
Seeds or sprouts, or other seasonal tasting

Prep work and setup:
Print plant life cycle visuals and worksheets.
Put worksheets on clipboards.
Put colored pencils in cups to pass out. 

Anticipated problems: None. 

Background information for instructor: None. 

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities:
If there is extra time have students start seeds or do a seed sorting activity. 

Task cards or handouts used: See above. 

Adapted from ESYNOLA, http://esynola.org/

Grade: 4-8
Season: Any?June for Pride?
Indicate: Indoor
Indicate: Year B



The Tradition of Lesbian 
Potlucks

Summary: Students learn about the significance of potlucks in LGBTQ history and prepare a mini potluck of their own

Objectives: Students will be able to identify how the potluck became an act of resistance by the LGBTQ community, will be able to make a dish to share with their classmates, and will experience the feeling of sharing food with one’s community.

Core Competencies: List all that apply: Social justice, kitchen skills and related academic content

Cultural Responsiveness: Encourages learning within the context of culture.

Social-Emotional Skills: working well in groups, learning about and discussing social justice issues

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
For all lessons: Pride
If cooking: Cooking skill(s), Main ingredient(s). Heating element: none, stove or oven.

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual with SEL component AND with emphasis on yearly theme/connection to previous lesson.
Ask if anyone knows what a “potluck” is. Have students explain, or if they don’t know, tell them it is a social gathering where everyone brings something to eat to share with others.
Have students turn and talk to one another about something they would like to bring to a potluck or something they would want to be served at a potluck.
Ask students to share out.
Ask students why a potluck might be a welcoming experience.
Tell students they will be learning about how potlucks played a role in LGBTQ history and doing a mini potluck of their own.

Engaging activity:
Explain to students that two parts of a person’s identity are their gender–are they a boy or a girl or neither? And also their sexual orientation–what kind of people they love or are attracted to.
Explain that while this is an important part of our diverse school and city and country, that there are still people who face discrimination based on their gender identity or their sexual identity, in particular, people who identify as gay or trans.
Explain that people who are gay or trans are sometimes referred to by the acronym LGBTQ.
Explain that things were especially bad in this regard 70 years ago in the 1950s when most LGBTQ people felt that they had to hide their identities and their true selves.
Give out the Lesbian Potluck Read-Along and have students read along as you read out loud.
Check for understanding.
Tell students that they are going to work in groups to make three simple dishes which they will share in a mini potluck.
Divide class into three groups to make three simple dishes. Possibilities could be guacamole, fruit salad, salsa, etc.
Make the dishes!

Closing ritual with SEL component:
Mix students up so that they are sitting with different students than those they cooked with. Make sure you have a student at each table who made each recipe.
Have students share the food they made one another.
You did a nice job today making and sharing food! How did it feel to prepare food for your classmates and eat food they prepared for you?

Grade Level Variations–REQUIRED for COOKING LESSONS

Site Based Variations, if applicable 
Site name:

Lesbian Potluck Read-Along
Recipes for the potluck dishes, possibly: guacamole, fruit salad, salsa

   Blind Cooking

Summary: Students will use sensory language to describe ingredients to a blindfolded group member to make a recipe. 

Objectives: Students will be able to use sensory language.
                    Students will be able to use teamwork to complete a recipe. 

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Agenda/Class Flow:

Engaging activity: One student in each group will be wearing a blindfold and the other group members have to describe what each ingredient is before adding it to their recipe.

K-3rd Grade
Fall/Winter
Indoor


Seed Saving


Summary: Students learn about the plant life cycle, do a seed dance, and save seeds. 

Objectives: Students will learn, through movement and a hands-on activity, about the life cycle of a seed. 

Core Competencies: Garden skills and related academic content

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles by giving students kinesthetic and hands-on methods of accessing content. 

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson reinforces students’ relationship skills by positioning students as integral actors in our garden community. 

Agenda/Class Flow: (45 minutes)
Opening ritual: (15 minutes)
Explain that today we are learning to tell the story of a seed. “The more we know about seeds and plants, then the better we can be at taking care of them.”
Draw a seed life cycle on the board in simplified terms: a planted seed, a growing plant, a plant with a flower, a flower fertilized by bees and butterflies, and then a flower making new seeds. Have students share their previous knowledge of what they see to help you build and describe the life cycle. 
Have students do a “dance” to represent the stages of the plant life cycle:
Start as seeds, curled up low to the ground. Pretend to bury them in soil, water them, and give them sun.
Grow up toward the sun, stretching arms like leaves. 
Make a flower with their fingers and their face. Pretend to be a bee or butterfly pollinating flowers.
Make a big circle with their arms to represent the seed pod, then lower back down and become a new seed. 
“Who wants to do a job to help the garden today? Today we are going to take some seeds out of the old flowers so we can plant them in our garden in the spring, when it’s warmer.”

Engaging activity: (20 minutes)
Show students the seeds they will be saving today, and demonstrate how to remove the seeds from the flower, seedpod, or fruit. If possible, show them the mature plant, or the part of the plant that we eat. Have students put their saved seeds in a cup and put extra plant matters into a bucket for compost. 
Give students envelopes and crayons or colored pencils. Write the name of the seed on the board so that students can label their envelopes. Students can decorate their envelopes as well. Have students put their seeds into the envelopes, or help them to do this. 

Closing ritual: (15 minutes)
Close with a tasting, such as sunflower seeds or another edible seed. 
“There are lots of seeds that we can eat, like corn, rice, and wheat in bread. We can help plants in the garden and in our world, but plants also help us by giving us food.” 
“How do you feel after doing a job today to help our garden?” 


Materials: 
Seeds, seedpods, or fruits to save
Small cups
Buckets
Envelopes
White board and markers
Crayons or colored pencils
Scissors (optional)

Prep work and setup:
Harvest seed pods from the garden. Good varieties for students include fennel, garlic chives, marigolds, dry beans, milkweed, and asters. 

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable: None. 

Back pocket activities:
If time allows, students can also gather seeds, seedpods, or fruit in the garden before saving seeds. Use scissors only if necessary. 

Photos, if applicable: None.

body:
PK-1
Fall
Outdoor


Leaf Collecting

Summary: Students talk about fall in the garden and collect leaves to put in compost or to use as mulch.

Objectives: Students will be able to identify how the garden changes with the changes in the seasons.

Core Competencies: Garden skills and related academic content

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles by incorporating physical movement as a learning modality. 

Social-Emotional Skills: Students practice self-awareness by reflecting on their feelings after spending time helping in the garden. 

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Ask students if they know what season it is. Ask them to observe how the garden has changed since summertime. Emphasize changes such as leaves changing color, leaves falling, temperatures cooling, and animals hibernating.
Explain to students that since the leaves are falling from the trees and plants, we will be doing an important garden job today. We will be collecting fallen leaves to give to the worms in the compost and help to care for our garden.

Engaging activity:
Help students put on garden gloves, to protect their hands when picking up leaves. Bring students to the place in the garden where they will be picking up leaves, and show them how to place the leaves in the bags or buckets. Students can also help one another by holding the bags open for each other, or pressing the leaves down into the bag or bucket. 
If possible, dump the leaves in the compost at the end. Explain to the students that these leaves are going to be food for worms, and the worms are going to turn it into soil. They may not fully understand this concept, but it’s okay for now to just briefly visit the compost.

Closing ritual:
Ask students to reflect on the work they did in the garden today. How did it make them feel to feed the worms and clean up the garden? Remind students how they helped take care of the garden today.
Finish with a tasting. If possible, explain that some leaves from the garden can also be food, and taste a leaf.

Materials: 
Children’s garden gloves
Large paper bags or buckets
Leaf tasting (i.e. kale, lemon sorrel, etc.) or other tasting

Prep work and setup: None.

Anticipated problems:
Supervise students to make sure they are only picking leaves off the ground, not picking green leaves from plants. Show them what colors of leaves they are collecting and which they are not.

Background information for instructor: None.

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities:
Read a fall book, such as Red Leaf, Yellow Leaf by Lois Ehlert; Time to Sleep by Denise Fleming; or When Winter Comes by Nancy Van Laan.
Another option is to mulch garden beds using the dried leaves. Move filled buckets and bags around a garden bed, and show students how to place the dried leaves around existing plants in the bed to protect the soil for winter.

All Seasons

Foods From Around the World: Uzbekistan

Summary: Students will do a reading and watch a video about the culture, geography, and typical ingredients from Uzbekistan, as well as how this food culture is present in New York City. Students then prepare and taste a quick recipe that is from this country.

Objectives: Students will be able to make connections between themselves, their culture, and other cultures. 

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Tell everyone that we are learning about Uzbekistan and the Uzbek community in New York City.
Hand out the reading. Read it aloud to students as they follow along. Take student questions and comments about what surprised them or what they connected to.
Watch the video. Again, have students share their questions or comments. 
Explain the recipe of the day. Use hand sanitizer and remind students about norms for health and safety while cooking. 

Engaging activity:
Prepare the recipe with students divided into small groups at tables, each table making the same recipe.
Fill tasting cups and sit down back as a large group.

Closing ritual: 
Finish by tasting what students have made together.
Have students share tasting words about the tasting.

Materials: 
Projector, Smartboard, or other means of showing video
Copies of Uzbekistan reading
Video
Take-home copies of Uzbek Korean carrot salad recipe
Hand sanitizer
Induction burner
Large pot with stirring spoon
Trays per each table with the following:
Ingredients from recipe
Box graters and gloves for grating carrots
Cutting boards
Scissors and plates for cutting herbs
Garlic press or grater
Measuring spoons
Bowl for ingredients
Spoon for stirring
Recipe copy
Cups, plates, and utensils for tasting

Prep work and setup:
Set up trays with materials and ingredients. This recipe should be tripled for a class of 32. If you have three groups, each group can make the recipe as written. If not, divide materials and ingredients between your groups appropriately. 
Pre-chop onion.

Anticipated problems:
It is likely that you will not have an adult to lead each small group. Assign one student to be the table leader for each group. The table leader can give out jobs, pass out materials, and ensure the recipe is followed. 
This recipe uses one induction burner. An adult should always be nearby and closely monitoring the induction burner. Ensure that the cooking area is away from other groups for safety purposes and that students are aware of the norms around the stove. 
The recipe states that the carrots should be mixed with salt and vinegar and rested for 20 minutes before proceeding. If time does not allow that, it is okay to leave less time for this step.
The onion is meant to be cooked and then added with the hot oil to the carrot salad as a dressing. A student or a small group of students can be responsible for this step under close supervision by the teacher, or the teacher can do it independently. 
Once the onion is cooked, have a student from each group bring over their group’s bowl to the induction burner to have the onion added safely. 

Background information for instructor:
This lesson is the first in a larger unit about the food culture of New York City. The unit sequence is as follows:
Introductory lesson
Cooking and culture lessons (Uzbekistan, Mexico, Pakistan, China, Caribbean Islands, Ukraine)
Recipe design lesson
Voting lesson
An alternate recipe to cook for this lesson is Uzbek plov. 

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities: None.

All grades
Summer
Indoor
A


Red Foods for Juneteenth


Summary: Students will discuss Juneteenth, the importance of red foods and drinks for Juneteenth celebrations, and work together in small groups to make their own red drink recipes.  

Objectives: Students will be able to explain why eating red foods or drinks is associated with Juneteenth. Students will learn about prosperity or “good luck” foods/symbols in relation to Black American culture. Students will make a red cabbage slaw.

Core Competencies: kitchen skills and related academic content, multicultural education, social-emotional learning. 

Cultural Responsiveness:  This lesson encourages learning within the context of culture because it is a lesson that gives historical context and that builds understanding of other cultures.

Social-Emotional Skills: Students practice relationships skills by working as a table to create their drink recipe.  This lesson promotes social awareness by discussing social justice issues.

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual with SEL component:
Welcome students to class. Engage them in a conversation about Juneteenth: Who knows what holiday is coming up? Who’s heard of Juneteenth? What does Juneteenth celebrate? Does anyone have any examples of what the color red symbolizes?
Juneteenth commemorates the day the last enslaved people in the United States (in Texas) were informed of their freedom. The practice of drinking red drinks on Juneteenth comes from the enslaved Yoruba (from West Africa) and Kongo (from central Africa) who were brought to Texas in the 1800s. For both of these and other West African cultures, red is a symbol of strength, power and resilience.  
Write the words strength, power and resilience on the board.  
Red drinks as we know them in the U.S. are a nod to red drinks from Africa. Bissap, which is from the hibiscus plant that is native to West Africa. And red kola nut tea has kola nuts that also come from West Africa. 
Write bissap and kola on the board.
Show pictures of hibiscus and kola nuts. 
For older grades, have students watch this video:
The Best Foods to Celebrate Juneteenth or this video: ​​
Since this video was made, Juneteenth has become a national holiday! That happened in 2021. What did you learn from this video?
When we make our red drink today, we are going to be doing our part to educate ourselves and others about this important holiday.


Engaging activity:

Closing ritual with SEL component:
 
What is significant about the color red? 

Materials: 
Ingredients
Cutting boards and knives
Mixing bowls, mixing spoons


Prep work and setup:
Write a message on the board or make a handout if you’d like.

body:
3rd Grade
Fall/Thanksgiving time
Indoor/Outdoor


Haudenosaunee      
Thanksgiving Address


Summary: Students will read/listen to the Haudenosaunee Thanksgiving Address, partake in a garden job related to Native teachings, and do a gratitude activity related to the Thanksgiving address. 

Objectives: Students will learn about Indigenous peoples’ reciprocal relationship to the environment 

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson reshapes curriculum by positioning Native American people and cultures as leaders and models for environmental caretaking. 

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson promotes social awareness by helping students to understand the leadership of Native Americans on current environmental and social justice issues.

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: 3k- pull tomato + okra rooftop 
Pk- marigold seed pods
KH- Plant seed pods + water greenhouse (raining)
K- pull 2nd tomato bed + break down, pulling leaves
101- pull tomatoes on terrace 
211- seed saving/ flower bouquets 
201 - Harvest garlic chives 
301- harvest collards, bok choy, kale
401- herb bouquet / compost?
511-  harvest seed pods molokhia, harvest leaves /compost? 
501 - prep bed & plant garlic 
(Distinguish bt Haudenosaunee/ Iroquois and Lenape- show map) (*intro, garden needs rest, segway for older kids

3k-2 Opening (15 mins) 
Start in the classroom, sing Goodmorning Gardeners, explain plan for day 
The last time we were in the garden, we talked about the seasons and how it's getting colder outside. Remember harvesting/ collecting lots of veggies from the garden? Now that it’s Winter and it’s really cold outside, it's time to help the garden rest. 
How do you feel after a long day working at school? You might be really tired and need to go to sleep. The garden has been working all year making lots of fruits and veggies for us to eat, so we need to help it rest too by (insert class garden activity here) today. 
Let's read about some of the work that the garden has done this year together- Start reading “In the Garden” for 3k+ K have them repeat “I pick it and eat it” 
For 1+2 read “Up, Down, and Around”
For both: Crouch on floor like carrot, reach up high to pick imaginary grapes, apples, green beans, stand tall like a cornstalk 
That’s a lot of work that we read about and acted out, growing all of those green beans, corn, and carrots! If you had to grow as tall as you could or as low into the ground as you could for a whole year would you be tired? 
Is anyone tired and need a nap after a long day at school? That’s how our garden feels after making lots of fruit and veggies for us to eat all year. 
Are you all excited to help the garden rest for the winter today? 
Explain class gardening activity
3-5 Opening (15 mins) 
Welcome back to class, 
The teacher introduces that today’s class will be about Native Americans, or Indigenous People. Have students raise their hands if they already knew that thousands of years ago, New York City looked very different and that, “There were no big buildings or subways or parks, but instead hills and forests. The land of New York City belonged to the Lenni Lenape people, and for thousands of years they cared for and honored this land.” Show image (p.1) from the Welikia Project.
“All across North America there were other tribes and Nations of Indigenous people. Today I want to talk about the Haudenosaunee People, an alliance of six tribes. The Haudenosaunee People’s traditional lands are in other parts of New York State.” Show image (p.2) of map of New York or the image from p.17 of this reading. “Each tribe has their own language and culture, but all live by The Great Law of Peace. This tells people how to treat others, the world, and how to live in community together.”
Distinguish bt Haudenosaunee/ Iroquois and Lenape- show map
Point out sections representing each tribe’s land
Have students point to where we are on the map of New York 
“Every year, many Americans celebrate Thanksgiving and take time to share what they are thankful for. The Haudenosaunee People practiced gratitude everyday, not just on Thanksgiving, through their Thanksgiving Address. This address tells us to appreciate and acknowledge all things.”
Before reading, explain that Native American people understand that people and nature have an “interconnected” relationship. In this understanding, the land provides for people, and people have a responsibility to care for the land in return. 
What does peace look or feel like for you? Where are you when you feel at peace?
Has anyone been outside of the city to the countryside? How do you feel when you’re there?
What resources can we get from our environment, how do we take care of it while doing so
How did people stay warm, what do we need to make a fire, a house, food, water.. Etc. 
Connect to The Great Law of Peace- The Haudenosaunee understood a long time ago how important the peaceful feelings and resources are that come from our environment. That is why in their Thanksgiving Address, they gave thanks to all of the different things the earth can provide for us if we take care of it. (water, food, people..)
Discussion questions/prompts for after the reading:
What was your favorite part? 
What parts did you connect with most?
Why is it important to be thankful for nature’s gifts?
Do you think all people see themselves as interconnected with nature? Why or why not?

Engaging activity:
Lead students in a seasonal garden job.

Closing ritual:
At the end, have students partake in their own gratitude activity, sharing something in the natural world that they are thankful for and how they can take care of that thing.

Materials: 
Document with the excerpts we will be reading from the Haudenosaunee Thanksgiving Address
Photos
Materials for garden job
Seasonal tasting (potentially popcorn to relate to 3 sisters)

Prep work and setup:
Set up for garden jobs.
Set up for tasting. 

Anticipated problems:
Students may come with preconceived notions about Indigenous Peoples, the history behind Thanksgiving, and our general right to live on this land. Correct students and inform them of the truthful history and help them respect and appreciate Indigenous knowledge and tradition.

A reading from the Museum of the American Indian provides much more context on the Haudenosaunee people and their lives.

Summary: Students will clear a garden bed to prepare it for spring. Students will then begin their Expert’s Guide workbook and corresponding page for clearing and weeding. 

Objectives: Students will begin a project that demonstrates their mastery of key garden knowledge.  

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Explain to your students that they will be starting a project together that they’ll be working on for their next several garden classes. They will be writing their own books called The Expert’s Guide to Gardening. Take student ideas about what it means to be an expert. 
Explain that they get to write this book to explain to other students, their families, and even the teachers about how to grow plants because they are the garden experts. 
Ask them what has been happening in the garden during winter. How do we get the garden ready for spring? 
Engaging activity:
Tell the students that sometimes at the end of the fall we don’t have time to take all the plants out of the ground so we leave them in the bed until spring. When spring comes, we need to make sure to clear the beds so they are ready for the next plants. Ask students why having a clear bed with no weeds is important for planting new seeds. “Why are weeds bad for plants?”
Demonstrate where and how we will be clearing a bed in the garden. Students will work as a team to fill a bucket with weeds.
After students have completed both gardening jobs bring them back to their seats and hand out the Expert’s Guide to Gardening books. Have students work on the cover page and weeding/clearing page.
Circulate to help students and discuss the answers. 
Closing ritual:
Clean up the Expert’s Guide to Gardening books and other materials. 
Finish with a tasting.

Insect Detectives

Summary: Students will build familiarity around insects by completing an insect scavenger hunt in the garden.  

Objectives: Students will be able to correctly identify insects that live in the garden and practice safety and curiosity around insects. 

Core Competencies: List all that apply: Environmental and community stewardship, social justice, garden skills and related academic content, social-emotional learning.

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson communicates high expectations by expecting students to self regulate their emotions (possible anxieties and fears) when interacting with insects.  

Social-Emotional Skills: Students will practice self-management  by regulating stress and anxieties that may come up around insects, and practice safety by managing impulses to touch insects in the garden.  

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
For all lessons: monthly theme, holiday or heritage month (if applicable), region of the world (if applicable)
If gardening: insects, stewardship
For 216: 

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Welcome students back to the garden.  Ask students if they have seen anything exciting growing in the garden yet this spring, or something they are excited to see growing.  Go over expectations for the garden: We are Kind, We are Safe, We are Brave, We are Respectful. Give students a petal point to focus on this period (this lesson works especially well for brave and safe).  (5 minutes) 
Today, we are going to learn about insect habitats. (10 minutes)
Can someone tell me what an insect is?  If needed, help prompt students by asking them some insects they know already and what they have in common. 
An insect is a small animal with SIX legs, THREE body parts, and TWO antennae.  Sometimes, they have wings too.  
Do we think a worm is an insect?  What about spiders? Ants? Grasshoppers?
What about habitat?  Has anyone heard that word before?
Habitat is the place where a plant, animal, or insect lives.  Where do you think insects live?
Prompt students with different insect suggestions as needed (i.e. where do you think butterflies might live? Ants? Wasps?)  
In the garden, we want to create habitat for insects that help our garden grow.  Today, we’re going to learn about some of these insects and the different habitats they live in.  

NOTE: Remind students that today, we are going to practice all our petal rules, but pay special attention to the brave petal.  Sometimes, insects can make us feel a little nervous!  If you’re feeling nervous today, stop what you are doing, take a deep breath, and hug yourself tight.  That’s how we are going to be brave and safe today around insects. 

Engaging activity (20 minutes):
Hand out worksheets and nature habitat trays with various plastic insects hidden in them. (time)
Explain how to complete worksheet–for younger grades, have students complete a simple bingo where they walk around to different areas of the garden looking for insects and circle when they find them.  For older students, have students complete the habitat worksheet where they write down observations about where each insect lives. 
Remind students not to pick up insects–these insects are made of plastic, but picking up real insects isn’t safe.  Explain that this is because some insects can sting, and we are practicing being safe today around insects.  We are insect detectives today–use your eyes!    
If students finish early, hand out trays or paper plates for students to make their own insect habitats to attract certain insects. (time)

Closing ritual with SEL component (10 minutes):
What is one thing you learned about insects today?  What is one thing you did to be brave today in class? (2 minutes)
Clean up, wash hands and give out a tasting. (8 minutes)

Grade Level Variations–REQUIRED for COOKING LESSONS
A version of this can be done with 3k-pk, just have fewer habitats (flowers, leaves, and compost).  Hand out magnifying glasses and poking sticks to students to explore.  Ask them to help count legs, observe colors, or identify insects.  This activity can also be paired with the for Pk-1st grade.  
For olders students, you can use real specimens or specimens in resin to make it more difficult. insects in resin butterflies in resin 12 insects resin have students identify good vs bad bugs? https://www.amazon.com/Macs-Field-Guide-Garden-Northeast/dp/0898867126






Site Based Variations:
If you have to bring this activity indoors, you can bring in trays or bins of nature stuff from the garden to make pretend habitats in.  Place the habitats around the classroom and hide the insects in different bins.  


Site name: 

Back pocket activity:
If students finish making their habitats, they can place their habitat in the garden somewhere and look for the insects they want to attract.  Alternatively, have students draw their habitat and/or insect on blank paper.  

Materials: 
1-2 sets of Plastic insects
Plastic trays x5 (enough for each ‘habitat’, leaves, flowers, compost, aquatic, rocks)
Worksheets
Dry erase markers for bingo or pencils for worksheet #2
Nature materials for habitat
Optional: popsicle sticks and magnifying glasses
Extra natural materials for students to make their own habitats

Prep work and setup:
Print worksheets and hide plastic insects around the garden.  

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
Students may struggle with anxiety around insects–give lots of reminders about how to be brave around insects, and how to take breaks when you need it!
Make sure you have enough growing in the garden for habitats (harder to do in early spring).

Spring Special Spot and Signs of Spring Scavenger Hunt 
 
Summary: Students will draw pictures of what they observe in the garden during springtime, and will then complete the Signs of Spring Sensory Scavenger Hunt. 

Objectives: Students will understand the concept of seasonal change and the different types of weather associated with each season, specifically recognizing the difference between fall and spring in the garden.  

Cultural Responsiveness: reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Welcome students. Ask if they know what season it is right now. Talk about the four seasons, make hand gestures for each season to have them act it out (i.e. hands falling like leaves in the fall, pretending to be a flower for spring).
Remind students about the special spot lessons they did in the fall and winter. Ask them about what things they saw in the garden during the fall and winter. 
Engaging activity:
Hand out the spring special spot papers to students. Discuss the rules for the garden and how we should be staying still with our bodies and drawing what we see during the special spot observation. When students are ready send them out into the garden to get started. Give students two minutes to find a special spot to sit and help them find a good spot if they are having a hard time settling in. 
Circulate to discuss with students what they are drawing in the garden and to pass out coloring supplies as needed. 
Bring kids back to get their Signs of Spring Sensory Scavenger Hunt sheets.
Send the kids out in the garden with the Sensory Scavenger worksheets. Have them make a checkmark for every sign of spring they see, hear, smell, and feel. You can give them examples so they know what to look for.
Closing ritual:
Have students share out what they see in the garden during the springtime. What was the best thing they saw?
Share a seasonal tasting. 

Materials: 
Clipboards
Coloring Materials
Pencils
Spring Scavenger Hunt and Special Spot papers

Print out special spot papers and put them on clipboards. Print the spring scavenger hunt papers and leave to the side until after the special spot observation.

This lesson is part of a series. Students are meant to choose a “special spot” in the garden and make observations and drawings from their special spot during each of the four seasons.

Growing Indoor Edible Sprouts 
Note: This is the first of three lessons, taking place on day one. Lesson two should be done on day five. Lesson three should be done on day ten. Sprouts need to be watered once a day between days five and ten. Teacher instructions here and here.

Summary: Students plant “soil sprouts” as a first step toward growing microgreens for their seed-to-table salad unit.

Objectives: Students will be able to plant seeds and know what plants need to survive.

Core Competencies: Garden skills and related academic content

Cultural Responsiveness: Promotes student-centered instruction by giving students a hands-on way to engage with life sciences.

Social-Emotional Skills: Students will practice self-management by learning a new garden skill and being careful with their planting supplies.

Agenda/Class Flow: 
Opening ritual with SEL component:
Tell students that they will be doing a special kind of planting today. This is a way to grow seeds quickly, in a small indoor space! You can go over all the steps ahead of time, or go through each step together as you do the planting.
Tell students that this is a way they can be gardeners, even if they don’t have any outdoor space to garden.
Tell students that they are going to grow and harvest sprouts that they will eat in a salad. They will be creating a salad from seed to table!

Engaging activity:
Explain to the students that the first step will be to put compost in the bottom of their pan. This will help their plants to grow strong. Each tray should get 1 tablespoon of compost, or a heaping spoonful. 
Then the pan gets a layer of kelp in the bottom of the tray. The kelp helps the plant to grow up quickly. Each pan should get ½ teaspoon of kelp, or a scant spoonful. 
Then the pan gets filled up almost to the top with the potting mix. Tell the students to be careful and not mix up their layers! They are layering all the good nutrition at the bottom, so the seeds will have to grow deep roots to get to the nutrition. 
The next thing is to spread the seeds out on top. Tell the students that today they should not cover their seeds up! This is from the usual way of planting, but today they are going to cover them up with something else. 
Before the students cover the seeds, have them complete “Day One” of their Soil Sprouts Observation Sheet.
After students complete their observations, they will cover their seeds with wet newspaper. Students can use spray bottles to dampen the newspaper, then cover the trays. The newspaper will keep the seeds damp. 
Once the pans are prepared, they should be kept away from light for four days. They will start to sprout and push up the newspaper. After that, the newspaper can be removed and the pans can be placed by a window for light. After about 10 days, the sprouts will be tall enough to eat! 

Closing ritual with SEL component:
Tell students they took the first steps toward growing their own food and that they made scientific predictions.
Ask students to share some of their predictions for what they will find on day five.

Materials: 
Small aluminum loaf pans–one per student
Spoons–one per student or they can share
Spray bottles–a few that can be shared.
Kelp
Compost
Potting mix 
½ teaspoon and tablespoon measures, or plastic spoons
Seeds for sprouting (such as peas or sunflower seeds), soaked
Newspaper
Spray bottles
Scissors
Soil Sprouts Observation Sheet.

Prep work and setup:
Soak seeds overnight, about one TB seeds per loaf pan.
If you don’t want students to cut newspaper, cut it in advance to fit on top of loaf pans




Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
Potting mix can be made by mixing 6 parts coconut coir to 1 part vermiculite and 1 part perlite. If you want to use pre-made potting mix, be sure that it doesn’t include fertilizers or silicon pellets that retain water. 
If students have allergies, kelp can be omitted. Including kelp, if you are able, will help the sprouts to grow more quickly.

Give me a thumbs up if you have ever eaten ramen.

Ramen is a Japanese dish (show Japan on Map). The word ramen means "pulled noodles". Noodles came from China 4000 years ago.

Objectives: Students will understand that even though they have different preferences for what sandwiches they like, they can put differences aside in order to create the possibility of learning something new about others and about themselves. They will learn to follow recipes to make two basic sandwiches.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills, responsible decision-making, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Welcome students
Introduce yourself
Explain that we are going to be talking about respect and sharing, and then cooking something delicious and healthy.
Go over class expectations to be safe, responsible, respectful and adventurous. Give all students hand sanitizer and remind them how to be clean and safe with their bodies and the tools.
Go over plan for the day  (5 minutes)
Look around the room. Is everyone the same, or are we all different? Do we all eat the same things, or do we eat different things? Why is it important that we all like different things?
Today we are going to be cooking two different types of sandwiches. Raise your hand if you like eating sandwiches. What is your favorite type of sandwich? What ingredients are in your favorite sandwich?
Many cultures have a unique type of sandwich. Does anyone have a type of bread or a sandwich that your family likes to eat at home, or on holidays? A sandwich can be anything that has a type of bread and a filling. (For example, I grew up in New York, and my family eats a lot of bagel sandwiches with cream cheese. In Mexican food culture, people might eat tacos, and in Harlem, people might eat chopped cheese).
Optional: Read “The Sandwich Swap” by Her Majesty Queen Rania Al Abdullah
Print out the pages of the book and pass them around as you read, or print enough copies for each student to have one and follow along.
Optional: Pass out “The Perfect Sandwich” handout. Explain that this is a guide for how to build your own healthy and yummy sandwich at home. Today we will be making sandwiches using just bread and condiment/proteins, which is a simple type of sandwich. Tell students that if they make this again at home, they can feel free to add more fillings or a side to make it a more filling meal.
We are going to be making sun butter + jelly and hummus + pita sandwiches. Show students pictures of these sandwiches.

Engaging activity:
Ingredients (5 minutes)
Show sandwich swap task card picture instructions to review ingredients and instructions.
Safety and Materials (5 minutes)
Do a demo of handling knives and spoons and using them to spread dips on bread. 
Go over safety and the materials we will be using.
Pass out cutting boards, knives and spoons.
Separate the class into two groups. One group will make sun butter and jelly sandwiches by spreading sun butter and jelly on two halves of the bread and then putting it together. The other group will make hummus and pita sandwiches by using a spoon to spread hummus on both sides of a split pita and putting it together. Then they will cut sandwiches in half. (10 minutes)
Have each group make enough sandwiches so that everyone can try a half of each type of sandwich.
Pass out ingredients and show them how to spread dips and cut the sandwiches in half.
Use the task cards!
Serve one half of each type of sandwich to each student. Tell students that even though they made one type of sandwich, they will get to try both sandwiches. 
Have each group thank the other for making sandwiches for them.
Remind students that even though they made different things, they can be open minded to trying something that their friends made for them. Tell them it’s just like giving a friend a present and getting one in return! Cooking for friends is a nice way to give them a present, and enjoy it with them too.

  Closing ritual:
Have them wait for everyone to get their sandwiches before they eat (5 minutes)
Before they taste it, have them say “Thank you gardeners, Thank you Cooks” 
As they’re eating, ask students to describe what they’re tasting.
Did you like the sun butter and jelly sandwich? Have you tried it before? Did you like the hummus and pita sandwich? Have you tried it before? Which one did you like better? If this was your first time trying one of these sandwiches, how did it feel to try something new? What would you do if you saw a friend eating something you have never tried? What’s a nice thing to say? (That looks yummy, what is it? What is in it? Tell me about it! Do you eat this a lot? Do you eat it with your family at home?)
You did a great job trying new things, taking turns and working together! Without everyone’s help it wouldn’t have tasted so delicious! Did you have fun working together? 
Pass out recipes and “The Perfect Sandwich” handouts to take home.

Materials: 
Sun butter
Jelly
Bread
Hummus
Pita
Knives
Spoons
Cutting Boards
Plates
Hand Sanitizer
Recipe Sheets
Coloring Pages
Word Searches
Task Card Picture Instructions
Sandwich pictures
“The Sandwich Swap” book

Prep work and setup:
Split up sandwich materials into two trays or onto two tables.
Print out recipes, pictures, task card picture instructions, handouts, coloring sheets and word searches 

Anticipated problems:
Students might be nervous to try new things, or might not want to try a food that another student touched/prepared for them. Encourage them to try new things, even just a little bit, but also don’t make students try something if they don’t want to. Tell them that even if they don’t try it, they got to learn something new and they can always try it another time or teach a friend or family member the new recipes that they learned.
Students might be resistant to trying sun butter, but assure them that it is a similar texture to peanut butter and is yummy.
Be careful to make sure students keep their hands clean and don’t touch their faces/the tables since they are cooking for each other and could possibly spread germs or cross contaminate.

Background information for instructor:

Questions for students/extension activities: 

Back pocket activities: 
Sandwich swap coloring pages
Sandwich swap word searches

Take-home recipes
Task card picture instructions

“The Perfect Sandwich” handouts

body:
8th Grade
Fall/Winter
Indoor


   Newly Freed Americans

Summary: Students learn about sharecropping and the importance of sweet potatoes following the Civil War

Objectives: Students will be able to discuss and understand why sweet potatoes were an important crop for newly freed Americans following the civil war. 
Students will be able to work together to prepare sweet potato hash. 

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: 
Ask students what they know about slavery being abolished following the Civil War. What did the newly freed Americans have to do to make money/support themselves and their families? Many farmed and grew crops, some notable crops were watermelons and sweet potatoes. Discuss sharecropping (Sharecropping is a system where the landlord/planter allows a tenant to use the land in exchange for a share of the crop.) Was sharecropping fair? 
Explain that despite the injustice of sharecropping, Black Americans maintained their culture, including their food culture.
Explain the historical significance of the sweet potato to Black Americans
Most Black Americans who lived in the South after the Civil War trace their ancestry to West Africa.
Yams were a very important crop to West African peoples. According to Modern Farmer magazine: “Celebrations of culture, community and life, yam festivals occur throughout West Africa. The festivals are essential events; they mark the passing of another season, honor religious offerings and provide a cultural focal point for a whole town to converge around.”
Meanwhile, sweet potatoes were cultivated in Europe and eventually North America.
Although the sweet potato and the yam are technically different foods, they are similar enough that sweet potatoes took on the role of yams for newly freed Black Americans.
Here is a picture of yams, and here is a picture of a sweet potatoes.
Today, many people use the word “yam” interchangeably with “sweet potato.”
We’re going to make a dish using sweet potatoes to acknowledge the history of newly freed Black Americans after the Civil War.

Engaging activity: Students will work together to prepare sweet potato hash

Closing ritual: Say thank yous and eat together as a class.
Discussion Questions: Did you like the recipe? How can we make it better? 


Materials: 
Recipes
cutting boards
Knives
Stovetops
Pans
Baking sheets
Oven
Mixing bowls
Wooden spoons
Graters
Prep work and setup (include photos if possible): Set up each table with recipes, tools and ingredients; pre-roast sweet potatoes


Anticipated problems: Too much down time while hash is cooking


Tips and tricks: none

Grade :6th-8th
Season:Winter
Indicate: Indoor



BIODIVERSITY

Summary: Students will hear the story of the 3 sisters and learn about biodiversity. 

Objectives: Students will learn and understand the importance of diversity amongst members of a social and ecological community and how that diversity increases the richness and resilience of that community. 

Cultural Responsiveness: 

Encourages learning within the context of culture by asking students to think of their own self identity as a contributing attribute to the diversity of their own community.


Social-Emotional Skills: 

Strengthens students self awareness by having students look closely at their role in their own community and their own sense of self.

Builds relationship skills by encouraging students to work together and think of their role in their classroom, school and wider community. 

 Requires students to self manage by having them take turns reading and working with their fellow classmates to complete a fun activity. 

Agenda/Class Flow: (10 minutes)
Opening ritual:
“Today we will discuss biodiversity! Would anyone like to help us define the term biodiversity?”
Take answers from students.
What does the term “Bio” mean/refer to?
What does the term “Diversity” mean/refer to?
Bio= life Diversity= a variety of
Biodiversity= A variety of life/ a variety living things
“Are we all the same? Do we all look the same? Like the same things? Have the same personality? Eat the same foods” 
Why not?
Because we are all very different. We come from different experiences and backgrounds and have different things to offer our community. 
What is a community?
Take answers from students
A community comes from the word “commons” which means a space that is shared by people, animals and other living and nonliving things.

What are some things we can find right here in our East Harlem Community?

“We are going to read the story of the Lenape who are a group of indigenous people who were the first people to live in Manhattan and the bronx. They understood the importance of biodiversity when it came to growing food. Let’s learn more!”

Engaging activity:

Read Story of the 3 Sisters
Have students take turns reading

Debrief story
Who are the 3 sisters?
Why were they planted together at the same time?
How did each sister help the other?
What does it mean to be independent?
Introduce students to the term Interdependent
A relationship of reciprocity where 2 or more things rely on each other for support. 
This relationship, like many relationships and connections in nature, is an interdependent one.
Can you think of a time you worked together with 1 or more people to solve an issue or to accomplish a goal. A time when you were interdependent. 
Were you able to accomplish the goal much easier with more people than you would’ve by yourself? Why?
Give an example of ecosystems that are biodiverse and resilient:
Rainforest
Coral Reefs
A Garden

Human Bingo 

“Have you ever played Bingo?
How do you play?
We are going to play human bingo to figure out how diverse our class is.
Each of you will get a bingo board.Your job is to find 1 person who fits the description in each box and have them write their name in that box .
You can only have 1 person sign per box. 
The first person to get a full row is the winner!

Closing ritual:
Do something here.(time)
Add more details with a bullet like this. 
Do something else. (time)
`
 

Materials: 
Lenape story
Human bingo boards
Clipboards
Pencils

Prep work and setup:
Print out stories and human bingo boards

Anticipated problems:
Too long?

Biodiversity, Ecology,

3rd-4th GradeSummer
Indoor/Outdoor


Summer @ 109: Worms & Compost

Summary: Students will examine the worm bin, discuss the importance of healthy soil, and complete a worm-related worksheet.  

Objectives: Students will demonstrate their knowledge of soil and compost.

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson communicates high expectations by having students explore their own live worm trays, positions teacher as facilitator as students work in small groups to complete their worm observations.  

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson promotes relationship skills as students practice displaying calm and care towards garden animals. 

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Welcome students to the garden and introduce or review expectations and norms.  
Today will be all about compost. Ask students what they already know about compost--where have they heard that word before?  What belongs in the compost bin?  What doesn’t belong?  Show or discuss examples.  
Explain that things that can go into the compost break down.  Show students compost from bins 1, 2, and 3.
Do you notice anything living in the compost?  There are lots of garden animals who live in the compost, and they help break down the big stuff in our compost bin into smaller and smaller pieces, eventually making compost that we can use in the garden to help our plants grow big and tall.
One of those garden animals is worms!  Tell students that they will be observing the compost and the worms as they make compost for our plants. 
Review the rules for observing the worms (students can use popsicle sticks to stir and look for worms, students may hold worms, be gentle and kind to the worms, don’t walk around with the worms and keep them over the tray). 
Engaging activity:
Hand out worksheets on clipboards.  Explain that we will be working in pairs today to observe worms and answer the questions on the worksheet.  
Hand out trays and popsicle sticks to each table.  Have students observe trays filled with material from the worm bins. Circulate and help students with worksheet as needed.  
If students finish worksheets early, hand out bins with materials to make a worm hotel.  What do worms need to live?  Have students create a habitat for their worms.  
When finished, wash hands and return to their seats to complete a compost worksheet.  
Closing ritual:
Last week, we discussed actions we can take as gardeners that can help our community.  Why do you think composting is important for our neighborhood community? (Provide leading questions if needed, i.e. what happens to our trash if it doesn’t get composted?  Where does it go?  What would our garden look like without compost?). 
Congratulate students on their good work today. 
Finish with a seasonal tasting. 

Materials: 
Page 33 of 
Trays
Worm bin
Popsicle sticks
Bin with compostable materials for ‘worm habitat’
Pencils
Colored pencils
Seasonal tasting

Prep work and setup:
Clip worksheets to clipboards.  Leave out worms and worm bin materials in trays for students to observe. 

Anticipated problems: Some students might be nervous around the worms. Tell students worms don’t have any teeth, they can’t hurt us. Try to encourage students to hold the worms. Make sure students hold worms over the trays, tell them not to walk around with the worms so they don’t drop them. 

Background information for instructor:

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities:
compost page

Chickpea & Spinach Garden Salad

Summary: Students will harvest spinach from the garden and make a chickpea spinach salad.

Objectives: Students will be able to experience a harvest-to-table recipe and the benefits of eating beans.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: (10-15 min)
Harvest & Rinse: ~5 leaves of spinach each 
Rinse spinach and wash hands
Lesson Introduction: It’s Spring and soon to be Summer in New York and the garden is now producing food we can harvest! This week we will make a spinach and chickpea salad, using the spinach we just harvested. 

Engaging activity: (20-25 min)
Review ingredients and measurements of Spinach and Chickpea Recipe
Have each group (3-5 students) work as a team to:
Chop cheese
Open can, rinse and drain garbanzos
Mix cheese with spinach and garbanzos 
Make dressing:
Measure ½ tbsp lemon juice
Mix with honey, olive oil, raisins, and spices

Closing ritual: (5 min)
Tasting: “Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks.”
Discussion:
Quick Review for MS: What were the main ideas behind eating Beyond Sausage last week?
Alternative protein that is more environmentally friendly
Pea protein was the main protein 
What is protein?
The building blocks of our body, found in muscle, bone, skin, hair, and virtually every other body part or tissue. Protein also builds hormones, enzymes, and immune cells.
How much you need: just over 7 grams for every 20 pounds of body weight.
Have students volunteer to do math for how much protein they need.
What is the main protein source in this week’s recipe? 
Certain plants can be excellent sources of protein, vitamins, and minerals, often with fewer calories and fewer potentially harmful effects than animal products (ex: heart disease, cancer, diabetes, bone health, and weight control).

Make a batch of dressing

Who lives in the garden? 

Summary: Students will be introduced to the plants and animals that live in the garden by participating in a garden tour, listening to a book read aloud or puppet show, and finishing with a short garden processing task.  

Objectives: Students will be able to list some of the plants, birds, and mammals that live in the school garden, and participate in a short garden task.  

Core Competencies: garden skills and related academic content

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson reshapes curriculum by incorporating movement and a variety of short activities to keep students engaged throughout the class period.  

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson promotes relationship skills by encouraging students to share tools, work as a group on a garden task, and showing care for living things in the garden.  

Garden Connection: This lesson takes place in the garden.  Students will complete a short garden task to help the garden. 

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
For all lessons: september, intro lesson, back to school
If gardening: garden theme (ie garden community, 3k/pk garden tasks, fall lesson
For 216: Indicate if lesson is co-taught and/or double period

Agenda/Class Flow: (45 mins)
Welcome students back to class.  If in PK, ask if they remember coming to the garden last year.  The garden was growing all summer, let’s go take a look and see what’s new! (5 minutes)
Remind students that in the garden, we use our walking feet.  Make sure you have both of your walking feet on!
In the garden, we use our eyes to see.  Show me how you use your eyes (demonstrate by holding your hands up to your eyes like binoculars).  Good!  We are going to use our eyes to see today, but we are not going to touch the plants just yet.  
If you see a bee or a bug today and you are feeling nervous, give yourself a hug tight–That’s how we are safe around bees in the garden.  
Walk students through the garden area.  Point out some things that are growing now, reminding students as needed about using our eyes, not our hands.  End in an area where they can sit in a circle (10 minutes) 
Explain that today, we are going to learn about who lives in the garden.  What animals live in a garden?  Give some silly examples, i.e. do dinosaurs live in a garden?  What about worms?  Pigeons? Do bananas grow in this garden?  Pepperoni? What about corn? Carrots?

Engaging activity (10 minutes):
Choose one option below to teach students about the plants and animals that live in the garden.
Option 1: Read a short book about the plants and animals that live in this garden.
Option 2: Stand up and pretend to be different plants and animals that live in the garden.  Show them pictures in the book and ask them to flap their wings to be a butterfly, meow to be a cat, stretch their arms out like a flower, etc etc.
Option 3: Introduce the animals and plants that live in a garden with finger puppets.  Introduce each one at a time and have them guess what it is (a squirrel, a cardinal, a roly poly, a bee).  Have them guess what each animal is, and what it eats in the garden.  Once they guess correctly, hand each student an item to “feed” to the puppet.  Acorns, seeds, dead leaves, and flowers.
     2.  End with a reflection: Who lives in the garden?  
Engaging activity pt 2 (10 minutes).  
Today, we are going to do something that will help our garden grow.  Put 2-3 large bowls and a compost bucket in the center of the circle.  Show students how to do a light processing task (i.e. shucking beans or corn, removing leaves from stems, topping carrots, or sorting seeds).  Demonstrate how to put the compost in one bowl, and the food in another.  While they’re doing this, ask follow up questions from the book:  who eats this food in the garden?  Would a bird eat this?  A worm?  A cat?
Once everyone has finished the garden task, show them the full bowl. Congratulate students on how much we did today!  

Closing ritual with SEL component (10 minutes):
Sanitize hands and distribute a tasting. “Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks!”
Finish with a goodbye song as you lead students out of the garden. 

Grade Level Variations–REQUIRED for COOKING LESSONS

Site Based Variations, if applicable

For groups that move quickly through the garden processing task, have a few easy backup tasks available (i.e. seed sorting or some herbs that they can separate between leaves and stems).

December Dessert Dumpling 

Summary: Students will smell and taste the flavors and aromas of the holiday season, while exploring the meaning of farm-to-table.

Objectives: Students will:
learn how to make a quick and easy apple-filled dessert dumpling.
understand the steps from farm-to-table.
be able to discuss the pros and cons of eating locally, for the environment, our health, and for farmers.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Hand washing
Pulse check: how are we feeling today? Thumbs up (good day), sideways (could get better)?
Opening circle/discussion:
3K: Hide apples (in plain view) for students to take turns going on an “apple hunt”! Have students place apples in individual baskets or one group basket.Try to have at least as many apples as there are students, so that each student can find an apple.
1st - 8th grades: culinary terms/gems 
Core - remove the tough central part and seeds from a fruit
Dumpling - pudding consisting of apples or other fruit enclosed in dough and baked
Holiday spices - traditional holiday seasonings, such as cinnamon, cloves, nutmeg, ginger, sage, anise, allspice, and cardamom

Engaging activity:
Prep / Cook tasks:
Core and dice apples
Measure all spices and mix well with apples
Cook apple mixture, stirring frequently
Assemble dumplings with pre-made apple mixture 

While baking dumplings, engage with apples:
Lower elementary: “Farm-to-Table Role Playing”
Have students role play farm-to-table roles (individually, by calling some to the front of the room or as a group). Act out different roles, use props if available, sing songs for each role, pass apples to each role player down the line, etc. For example, role play/pretend:
Farmer: mime planting a seed, watering, raking, etc. or have props available
Laborer: harvest and carry sack of apples to the “truck” or pass a sack of apples around the group
Truck driver: drive harvest to the store by following a designated “driver” around the room while carrying apples
Store keeper/cashier: place apples on shelves, ring up and bag groceries, etc.
Upper elementary and MS: 
Show a map of the United States and ask students to guess which state(s) grows the most apples. Washington and New York
Ask, “For NYers, which apple is better or healthier and why do you think that?” Write answers on board and/or encourage discussion on students’ ideas. Introduce and discuss concepts of eating locally:
Local foods are fresher
Local foods are seasonal
Local foods are better for environment
Local foods promote food safety
Local foods support the local economy
Local foods create community

Closing ritual:
“Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks!” 
Eat dumplings, discuss ingredients, tastes, textures, changes, improvements, etc. 
How are we feeling since class started? Thumbs up, sideways? 

Materials: 
Corers, knives, cutting boards
Measuring spoons
Oven safe trays
Apples
Optional props: baskets or sacks for carrying apples, farming tools (watering cans, rakes/hoes, etc.), steering wheel, cash register/play money
Map of the U.S.

Prep work and setup:
Set up tray for each table
Apple mixture tray(s): apples, corers, knives, spices, and measuring spoons
 Apple dumpling tray(s): oven safe trays/sheet pans, oil and oil brushes, dumpling wrappers, apple filling, spoons
Pre-make a batch of apple mixture ready to stuff dumplings (or use mixture made by a previous class)

Anticipated problems:
Timing: pre-make apple mixture and pre-bake dumplings if necessary.
Coring apples may be challenging for little hands. Pre-core apples if necessary.
Measure spices with younger students as a group to assure proper amounts.

What Grows in NY State
The Benefits of Eating Locally Grown Foods
National Agriculture in the Classroom
5 Holiday Spices That Could Help Boost Health

MS: Where Does My Food Come From?
MS: Who Grew My Soup?

Early elementary: farm-to-table worksheet
Elementary/MS: Food_Farm_Connection Matching Cards
Upper Elementary/MS: Food Fact Wheel
1st - 8th: Graphing Activity

body:
PK
All Seasons
Indoor/Outdoor
  

Roots



Summary: Students do a root dance, examine different kinds of roots, and pull roots out of the garden.
	

Objectives: Students will be able to explain what roots look like and what roots do. 
	

Cultural Responsiveness: incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles
	

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, responsible decision-making
	

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
1. Explain that today we are learning about a part of the plant, the root. 
2. Teach students a “root dance.”
   1. Have students all stand up and bend over, fingers pointed towards the ground. Tell students that they are plants, and you are drinking water from the soil through your roots. Have them imitate sucking water up into the plant. 
   2. Then have students stretch their arms up overhead like leaves. Explain that drinking all that water helps our plants grow big and strong! 
   3. Repeat this motion a few times. If you want to, you can also teach students a root song: “Roots down, leaves up!” 


Engaging activity:
1. Begin with a root exploration. 
   1. Put out trays with several roots in them, including edible roots such as beets or carrots. Give students time to explore the roots in the tray, using touch, smell, and sight.
   2. Model for students how these roots are usually hidden underground. Hold the root in your hand, with the attached leaves emerging from the closed hand. Show students how you would have to “pull” the plant from the ground to reveal the roots.
   3. Pass out blank paper colored pencils or crayons so that students can draw their edible root. Prompt them to include colors and shapes in their drawings. 
2. Pull roots in the garden, if possible.
   1. Find plants in the garden that need to be pulled. These can either be weeds that need to be pulled or root vegetables that need to be harvested.
   2. Demonstrate to students how to pull the plant out of the ground, using trowels if necessary. Give students the chance to observe the roots that they pull out. 


Closing ritual:
1. Finish with the “root dance” that you did at the beginning, reinforcing the role of roots in drinking water.
2. Finish with a tasting, ideally of an edible root.  
	

Materials: 
* Large trays
* Several roots with leaves intact, including recognizable edible roots (turnip, radish, carrot, beet, etc.)
* Blank paper 
* Colored pencils or crayons
* Plants needing to be pulled 
* Trowels
* Root tasting such as beet, carrot, or radish, or other tasting


Prep work and setup:
* Locate an area of the garden where plants can be pulled, if available. 
* If you are not able to do this lesson outdoors, you can plant seeds indoors in a variety of ways for students to pull up and observe during this lesson. 
   * Start sprouts in the classroom that can later be pulled up, as demonstrated in this lesson. Do this 10-14 days before you plan to teach the root lesson. 
   * Moisten paper towels and place them inside a Ziploc bag with a bean seed. In a few days, the bean will sprout and roots can be observed. 


Anticipated problems:
* Make sure there are ample plants for students to pull out. If not, you can pair students together to pull plants together. 


Background information for instructor: None.


Questions for students/extension activities: 
* Make a mural on butcher paper or poster board for students.  At the top of the mural draw in the tops of plants. Ask students what is missing from the picture: the roots!
