body:
3rd Grade
Fall/Thanksgiving time
Indoor/Outdoor


Haudenosaunee      
Thanksgiving Address


Summary: Students will read/listen to the Haudenosaunee Thanksgiving Address, partake in a garden job related to Native teachings, and do a gratitude activity related to the Thanksgiving address. 

Objectives: Students will learn about Indigenous peoples’ reciprocal relationship to the environment 

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson reshapes curriculum by positioning Native American people and cultures as leaders and models for environmental caretaking. 

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson promotes social awareness by helping students to understand the leadership of Native Americans on current environmental and social justice issues.

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
The teacher explains that today’s class will be about Native Americans, or Indigenous People. Explain that thousands of years ago, New York City looked very different. “There were no big buildings or subways or parks, but instead hills and forests. The land of New York City belonged to the Lenni Lenape people, and for thousands of years they cared for and honored this land.” Show image (p.1) from the Welikia Project.
“All across North America there were other tribes and Nations of Indigenous people. Today I want to talk about the Haudenosaunee People, an alliance of six tribes. The Haudenosaunee People’s traditional lands are in other parts of New York State.” Show image (p.2) of map of New York or the image from p.17 of this reading. “Each tribe has their own language and culture, but all live by The Great Law of Peace. This tells people how to treat others, the world, and how to live in community together.”
“Every year, many Americans celebrate Thanksgiving and take time to share what they are thankful for. The Haudenosaunee People practiced gratitude everyday, not just on Thanksgiving, through their Thanksgiving Address. This address tells us to appreciate and acknowledge all things.”
Before reading, explain that Native American people understand that people and nature have an “interconnected” relationship. In this understanding, the land provides for people, and people have a responsibility to care for the land in return. 
Discussion questions/prompts for after the reading:
What was your favorite part? 
What parts did you connect with most?
Why is it important to be thankful for nature’s gifts?
Do you think all people see themselves as interconnected with nature? Why or why not?

Engaging activity:
Lead students in a seasonal garden job.

Closing ritual:
At the end, have students partake in their own gratitude activity, sharing something in the natural world that they are thankful for and how they can take care of that thing.

Materials: 
Document with the excerpts we will be reading from the Haudenosaunee Thanksgiving Address
Photos
Materials for garden job
Seasonal tasting (potentially popcorn to relate to 3 sisters)

Prep work and setup:
Set up for garden jobs.
Set up for tasting. 

Anticipated problems:
Students may come with preconceived notions about Indigenous Peoples, the history behind Thanksgiving, and our general right to live on this land. Correct students and inform them of the truthful history and help them respect and appreciate Indigenous knowledge and tradition.

Background information for instructor:
A reading from the Museum of the American Indian provides much more context on the Haudenosaunee people and their lives.
An interactive map details ancestral homelands of different tribes and nations.

Photos: 
From the Welikia Project
Map of New York state that shows different tribal lands
Document with the two images to print before class

Back pocket activities: None.

Questions for students/extension activities: None.

Aim Students will identify different fast food logos. They will understand why advertising is very important and why are some more common to them than others. 

Materials

Parmesan Cheese 
Cayenne Pepper

PrepWork

Make sure to take out two bowls out 
Make sweet potato fries
Have two groups- one group will be making parmesan fries and the other group will be making cayenne/pepper fries 
Make sure that the fries are cut before hand
For the group with Parmesan fries make sure to they have graders to grade cheese 
Have all the peppers out and the 4 measuring spoons- 2 for each table 


Inquiry Activity (30 minutes)
Making Sweet Potato Fries 
Make sure you have fries cut all the students will need to do is put the topping and then wait for the fries to be ready. 

Opening to lesson plan: (5 mins)
Make a circle 
Does everyone know what is a logo? Talk a bit what a logos and what logos do? What about logos for food? What are the types of logos we see? 

Opening Circle (15 minutes)
Take out the cards you printed- Have them guess the logos for each picture and write down with dry erase marker 
Then ask them these questions and write down their answers on a piece of paper. 
What do you think is the purpose of this activity? (Logos are easy to recognize because they are everywhere.) Did you find it easy or difficult to identify the brands? If difficult, were there some that were easier than others? Why do you think so? (Some are more common.) If easy, why was identifying the brands so easy to do? (These logos are everywhere!) Where do we see these logos? (TV ads, the Internet, movies, billboards, advergames, people wearing brand paraphernalia, sporting events, school etc.) Does it make you want these products? Does it make you buy these products?  What do we know about these products are they good for you? 

Now have them compare these logos/advertising to the advertising they did last week. What were they promoting last week? Is it important we start promoting fast food? 

Closing Circle (10 mins) 

 Now after we have discuss this logo- show them a positive video a logo that promotes healthy eating- Appetite for Change 
While they are watching take out the sweet potato fries- Pass them out to the students 
Ask them what they think of these fries in comparison to Mcdonald fries.

Fattoush

Summary: Students will revisit the rules of the kitchen while preparing fattoush. They will also learn about the culture of several Middle Eastern countries while discussing their own family cultures.

Objectives: Students will be able to dice vegetables. Students will be able to understand the concept of food cultures.

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Where in the world do our classmate’s families come from? We come from all over! And when people travel, they bring ingredients and their food traditions with them. 
Look at the world map all together. Today we are making food from a region called the Middle East. It includes countries like Syria, Lebanon, Jordan, Israel and Palestine. (raise hands if you know anyone from here) 
Recipes from here have changed over time with people moving to and from the Middle East and with trade (ask for examples of trading).
For example, today we will make Fattoush. It is a bread salad with fresh summer vegetables from our garden like cucumbers and tomatoes. Did you know tomatoes originated in Peru? In ancient times, trade allowed for people to bring tomato seeds to Asia and eventually to the Middle East. This recipe relies on its neighbor countries and even far away countries to get ingredients.
Review Kitchen Pledge, Norms, and Consequences

Engaging activity:
Cucumber table: cut tomatoes, cucumber, parsley, mint, scallions, purslane using scissors and lettuce knives.
Corn table: 
2nd-5th grade: Mapping activity (have everyone put a sticker where they are from), culture box exploration of Middle Eastern Countries. 
K, 1st grade: taste test- sweet, sour, bitter, salty.
Carrot table: Make dressing and tear and season pita bread.

Closing ritual:
Clean up, set the table, “Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks,” eat together, clear tables.
Ask for suggestions on how students would make the recipe different next time.
Share shout-outs.

Materials: 
Map
Sticker dots
Recipe ingredients
Culture Box: photos, currency, spices, magnifying glass, etc.
Plastic knives, cutting boards, scissors, bowls
Jar, measuring spoons, citrus reamer
Teacher’s guide to cutting
Pencils
Tray set-up:
Tablespoons
Teaspoons

Prep work and setup:
Cut bell pepper into chunks.
Cut onion into chunks.
Reheat chili from the previous class. 

Anticipated problems:
Students who don’t know where they are from feeling left out. Make sure every student gets to put a sticker on the map-- they are from Brooklyn and that is something to be proud of if they don’t have connections to another culture.
Teachers may not feel confident to lead the cutting table.

Background information for instructor:
There are endless variations of fattoush and it is commonly eaten in Lebanon, Syria, Jordan, and Palestine. It is an essential salad for breaking the fast during the month of Ramadan in Lebanon and Syria especially.

Questions for students/extension activities:
What foods do you eat at home that are similar to fattoush?

Back pocket activities:
Extended culture box exploration

-Watch video about how to make fattoush.

Introduction to the Garden

Summary: Students go on a guided tour of the garden with stops to touch, smell, hear and see different things.   

Objectives: Students will be able to practice using their five senses as they explore and get to know the garden for the first time.

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, self-awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Welcome students to the garden.
Demonstrate and have students repeat your actions to warm up their five senses: shaking out their hands, wiggling their ears, blinking their eyes, etc.
Use this opportunity to introduce your norms for behavior in the garden: walking feet, asking before picking, calmness and kindness towards garden animals, etc. 

Engaging activity:
Lead students on a walk around the garden. Each stop along the route should use a different sense. At each station, model how you would like them to engage. Ask questions about what they experience.
If time allows, give out clipboards with blank paper and different colored crayons. Ask students to draw what they see that matches their crayon color. 

Closing ritual:
Have students share out their experiences of what they saw, heard, smelled, and touched in the garden today.
Introduce norms for tasting: waiting for all other students to receive the tasting, how to clean-up, etc.
Finish class with a tasting. 

Materials: 
Blank paper
Colored pencils or crayons
Clipboards
Tasting

Prep work and setup: None.

Anticipated problems:
If this is students’ first time in the garden, expect that the behavior norms will be new for them. Use this opportunity to reinforce norms and practice appropriate behavior, and highlight when students are doing a good job and meeting expectations.

All About Seeds

Summary: Students will sort beans according to different categories and make a bean salad.

Objectives: Students will be able to explain that seeds come in varying shapes and sizes and are also edible.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
(10-15 min) Seed Sorting game: have students sort a variety of seeds in different categories 
Have students sort seeds by shape, size, color, etc or have them sort seeds by their own choosing and a partner try to guess how they are sorted.
Close by explaining that seeds come in all different sizes, shapes, and colors.
(For older students, if time allows) Bean Dissection: students dissect a lima bean
Have students label each part of the seed diagram
Give each student 1-2 pre-soaked beans and a magnifying lens (soak beans overnight so they are easily dissected)
Instruct students how to peel off seed coat and dissect seed in half (being careful not to damage the baby plant inside)
Using the magnifying lens, have students find each part of the baby plant inside the seed (as per the seed diagram)
Close by explaining that a every single seed has everything inside itself to produce and feed a baby plant

Engaging activity:
Seed Salad: use a variety of different beans/seeds to make a salad
Place ingredients in individual ramekins/bowls for each student:
1-2 TBSP cooked black beans
1-2 TBSP cooked corn kernels
¼” slice of orange, yellow or red bell pepper
1-2 cherry tomatoes
1 green onion
1-2 stems fresh cilantro 
Have each student dice bell peppers, quarter cherry tomatoes, slice green onion, and tear cilantro leaves and add to bowl
Add seeds (corn and beans)to bowl
Pour pre-made dressing over each student’s bowl and have students toss their salad
DRESSING INGREDIENTS: 
⅓ cup fresh lime juice
½ cup olive oil 
1 clove garlic, minced
1 teaspoon salt
⅛ teaspoon ground cayenne pepper
(optional) honey to taste
Place lime juice, olive oil, garlic, salt, and cayenne pepper in a small jar. Cover with lid, and shake until ingredients are well mixed.
Closing ritual:
Tasting: Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks, thank you seeds!

Materials: 
Handful of dried beans in small cups/bowls for each student
Chopping mat & knife
Ramekin of beans
Ramekin of corn
1 bowl with a slice of bell pepper, 1-2 cherry tomatoes, 1 green onion, 1 cilantro stem
Fork for eating
If dissecting a bean: pre-soaked lima bean, magnifying lens, & seed diagram

Prep work and setup:
Soak lima beans
Print seed diagram
Slice bell pepper into ¼” slices
Prep ramekins of corn and beans for each student
Prep bowls of bell pepper, tomatoes, green onion, and cilantro for each student
Make salad dressing

Anticipated problems:
Younger students may have trouble understanding how to sort; may need to set specific categories for sorting instead of having them choose.
Removing seed coat or dissecting lima beans may cause damage to embryonic plant - tell students to dissect carefully
Students may have trouble locating baby plant 

Background information for instructor:
None.

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities:
Have students plant seeds if available.

Women’s History Month: 
Rachel Carson

Summary: Students will learn about Rachel Carson and how she inspired the environmental movement by her writing based on observations of nature. Spring After Spring will be read aloud.  Students will participate in either an indoor or outdoor gardening activity.

Objectives: Students will be able to identify ways that Rachel observed nature around her, and be able to understand Rachel’s perspective that all things in nature are connected. 
Indoors - Students will plant seeds in cow pots and take them home with them
Outdoors - Students will plant seeds in the garden & complete an observation worksheet

Cultural Responsiveness:[pick one or more] promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: [pick one or more]self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual (5 mins) :
Introduce topic of Women’s History Month in March
Introduce Rachel Carson. She was a marine biologist, an author, and an environmentalist. All of these words are then reviewed for vocabulary purposes.
Explain that we will read the story and then do a gardening  activity - weather permitting (outdoors or indoors)
Engaging activity (10 mins) :
Read aloud “Spring After Spring” 
Stop for discussion points throughout the book (post it notes)
Engaging activity #2 (10 mins) :
Tell students that they will be (depending on grade) either doing the biography graphic organizer worksheet/ is can has/ coloring/ word search. 
Call students up in groups of 5 to do the gardening activity.
For indoors (younger grades) planting seeds in cow pots
For outdoors - (younger grades) students get to plant seeds in the garden
For outdoors (older grades) students participate in an environment observation
Students will observe all plant, animal, bird, and insect life (possibly include the worm bin for this) and record their findings on the worksheet. 

Closing ritual (5 mins) :
Ask students what they know about nature and humans (they are connected).
Discuss why Rachel wanted to protect the environment.

Clipboards if students are outdoors

Plastic Trowels

 seeds
Seed spacers/rulers

pencil

                             Pond Ecology

Summary: Students will learn about pond ecology and make miniature water gardens.

Objectives: Students will be able to understand the ecology of a pond, identify biotic and abiotic factors that exist in nature and learn how to build a DIY pond garden.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, social awareness, respect for nature

Agenda/Class Flow:

“Today we are going to talk about ecosystems. Can anyone tell me what an ecosystem is?”
Take answers from students and write them on the board


Movement Activity:
Tell students that they must stand up and repeat after everything you say and do.
“This is a repeat after me song...” (wait for students to repeat)... “and this is a do as I do song...” (wait for students to repeat)...”an ecosystem is…” (wait for students to repeat) “a place..” (wait for students to repeat)... “where living...” (wait for students to repeat) “and nonliving things” (wait for students to repeat)...”come together…” (wait for students to repeat)... “and interact”

Ask students for examples of ecosystems where living and nonliving things come together and interact

Read about Pond Ecology on page 2 of the Food Chains and Webs book

Ask students to take turns reading out loud.
 Building Pond Gardens:
“Today we are going to make miniature pond gardens. What is one thing that lives in the garden that also grows in a pond?”
Take answers from students. Students should say “Plants!”

Instruct students to write their names first on the outside of their pond gardens prior to adding materials inside. 

Give students 15 minutes to assemble their pond gardens.

007 Dr. Carver Lotion-Making

Summary: Students will learn about an influential Black historical figure, Dr. GW Carver, then follow a recipe similar to the one he innovated to make lotion. Students will also assemble and eat Hoppin’ John burgers.  

Objectives: Students will be able to follow lotion making recipe to take home. Students will be able to collaboratively clean and set a table. Students will be able to assemble, eat, and describe a tasting. 

Core Competencies: Kitchen skills and related academic content, multicultural education, social-emotional learning.

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson encourages learning within the context of culture through a lesson with historical context. This topic also allows students to connect to and reflect on their own heritage and cultural experiences, or explore a new culture using historically relevant recipes. This lesson also communicates high expectations to students, letting individuals follow each recipe and assembly on their own. 


Social Emotional Skills: This lesson encourages social awareness by prompting students to empathize with others from diverse backgrounds and cultures as we learn about historical figures and ideas related to Black History Month.

Agenda/Class Flow: 
Opening ritual (10 minutes):
3K-1
Greet students in their room, sing good morning gardeners
Explain plan for the day
Buzz up to classroom
2-5
Wash Hands
Greet Students, welcome back to the classroom!
How is everyone feeling today (thumbs down to thumbs up)
Explain to students that they will get to learn about a new friend, George Washington Carver. Show picture. Dr. Carver was a scientist and a really smart man. He was an: *act out together for younger kids*
Artist	
Planter
Harvesting
Scientist
One of his creations was a cosmetic cream made from plants that he grew himself! In his honor and for Black History Month, we’re going to make our own cosmetics! At the end of class we’re also going to assemble some hoppin’ john burgers to eat together. 
Does anybody remember making Hoppin’ John Burgers a few weeks ago? 
The ingredients were: black eyed peas, rice, kale, seasonings 
We’re making Hoppin’ John burgers today also in honor of Black History month, because Hoppin’ John is a recipe created and popularized by Black people traditionally in the American south. The technique of cooking rice and beans together is from Africa and it has spread to every part of South, Central, and North America. Today we made Hoppin’ John burgers adapted from Marisa Moore, a dietician and food blogger from Atlanta, GA

Engaging activity #1 (15 minutes):
Lotion Making Activity listed in materials below
Label making 
Engaging activity #2 (15 minutes):
Clean and set table 
Assemble and eat Hoppin’ John Burgers/ Regular Hoppin’ John
Closing ritual (5 minutes):
Would anyone want to make this at home? How do you feel when you make something from scratch? 
Discuss
What did we learn today about Dr. Carver?
How do you feel following these recipes?
What do you like/ not like about the Hoppin’ John burger?
Shoutouts
Goodbye!

Family Meal Project

Summary: Students will work together on an end of semester project where they will plan and prepare a family meal. This is a multi-part lesson.

Objectives: Students will be able to work together as a class community to execute all the elements of planning and preparing a 3-course meal. 

Core Competencies: 
Garden skills and related academic content - students will harvest and use herbs/greens grown in the hydroponic lab
Kitchen skills and related academic content - students will learn and practice various cooking skills. Including: recipe reading, knife skills and menu planning. 
Multicultural education - students will prepare dishes from both their home culture and from other cultures.

Cultural Responsiveness: 
Encourages learning within the context of culture: students prepare recipes from both their own and other cultures
Communicates high expectations: students will read and complete recipes on their own, conveying to students your faith in their abilities, students must complete various tasks within a certain time frame 
Promotes student-centered instruction: students will plan a menu and select all the recipes they will prepare
Reshapes curriculum: students will work together in groups to complete their project
Positions teacher as facilitator: students will work semi-independently in small groups

Social-Emotional Skills: 
Self-management: students will take small risks by preparing and trying new foods
Relationship skills: students will work together, make connections to outside life and set the table for one another
Responsible decision-making: students will clean up after themselves and safely use sharp knives
Self-awareness: students will display joy, pride or ownership in their work; students will express their feelings and needs; students will ask for when needed and will help their classmates to complete tasks

This lesson was the final project for the Culinary Arts and Cooking Club electives during the 2022 school year. All classes performed all 5 parts of this lesson however on varying days due to the class schedules (ex. One class had an extra day for food prep).
Lesson written in an adapted format to include all 5 parts.
Lesson involved teacher ordering or purchasing decorations and other supplies

body:
2nd-5th GradeSpring
Indoor/Outdoor


Hummus and Pita


Summary: Students will read a story by a Palestinian author about olive trees in Palestine and how hummus is an important food in Palestinian culture since the main ingredients (chickpeas, olive oil, sesame) are native plants in that region and indigenous Palestinian people have stewarded this land, cared for these native plants and survived on this food for many generations. Students will then work in teams to follow a recipe for hummus. 

Objectives: Students will be able to work together to follow a recipe.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills, responsible decision-making, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Introduce Palestine & olive trees - We are going to be learning about foods that are native to Palestine and important to Palestinian culture and tradition (olives/olive oil, chickpeas) and then we will make hummus, a Palestinian food.
Emphasize that hummus is a food enjoyed in many different countries in the Middle East, like Lebanon, Saudi Arabia, Iran, Iraq, Egypt, and more. “Today we are going to focus on one country that eats hummus and grows the plants needed to make hummus - Palestine!”
“Do you remember how some plants only grow in certain areas?” Does anybody know what it means to be ‘indigenous’ or ‘native’ to land? Think about Native Americans.”
Connect this idea with today’s lesson - “We are learning about indigenous people today. Indigenous people have a very strong connection with the land and a lot of what we know about taking care of the earth and growing plants is because of indigenous people who passed down that knowledge!”
“You may have heard about Palestine in the news lately.”
Give a brief explanation of today - “there is a terrible conflict over sharing land and natural resources, including water, olive trees, chickpeas and lots of other plants that are important to making hummus - what we are making today - and other types of Middle Eastern foods. Both Palestinians and Israelis feel ancestral connections to the same land, and need this land and its natural resources to survive (food, water, shelter). However, Israel is forcefully occupying the land, which means displacing Palestinian people off their land (to Gaza, the West Bank, other surrounding Arab countries, to a diaspora which means to countries all over the world), even though Palestinians have lived in Palestine for many generations. Palestinian people getting pushed off of their land is very sad - it means Palestinian people cannot take care of their land, tend to their plants, make food from their crops, etc.” Emphasize that when people get pushed off their lnd it means they can’t care for the land, plants, and crops.
Name the oppressor here because the book does not.
“Today we are going to read a story about someone who grew up in Palestine a long time ago - in 1967. In order to understand what is happening there today, we have to understand what happened in the past.”
“We can’t talk about all the history of the Palestinian people today because we also want to cook, so if you have any questions at the end of our reading, we will give you a paper to write it down and we will make sure to give the answers to your teachers to give to you.”
“Today, the most important thing is understanding why Palestinian people feel connected to Palesitne, and generally understanding why anyone feels connected to their homeland.”
For 4&5 graders: Remember when we did our food memory activity? You may have written about an important cultural food to your family. 
For all: Food is a very powerful way to connect to where we come from, even if we are far away from that place.


First activity (reading)
Read These Olive Trees by Aya Ghanameh & discuss what the story means to students. Read the story. 
During reading, ask students if they know how long it takes for an olive seed to become a tree with olives?”
It takes about 3-10 years!
Ask students if they know how long it takes an olive tree to produce olives or grow?
It takes an olive tree 3 years to start producing olives and it takes a tree around 15 years to grow into its full size. Olive groves, areas where farmers cultivate lots of olive trees, take many years of care and tending to make sure they are healthy and produce food that people can eat.
Ask the class:
What are your initial reactions to this story? How did it make you feel? What did it make you think about?
Why has Oraib and her family taken care of this land over so many years? Why is it important for people to take care of the land they are living on? What are some ways you all take care of land - at home, school, in your community? What are some ways the land takes care of you?
Turn and talk
How does Oraib and her family feel when they have to leave the land? Why? What would be difficult about leaving land that you or your family lived on for many many years?
Do you all think it's important to share land and resources with people that are different from you and why/why not? How could we do a better job of sharing land with our neighbors in our lives, even if they are different from us? 
Pass out sticky notes and give students a few minutes to write down any questions about Palestine/Israel/These Olive Trees.
 Explain that we only have enough time to read this story and cook a recipe today but we know this is a big and confusing topic and we will do our best to answer any questions later and that they should keep asking questions at school and at home if they come up.

Second activity (cooking)
Make Hummus - Remind students about the importance of trying new foods and not yucking anybody’s yum, different families eating different foods, etc. Ask if anyone eats hummus at home or school or knows what it is (they serve it at lunch sometimes!). Describe hummus and introduce ingredients and directions. Choose group roles (upper grades).
4&5 graders get task sheets
2&3 graders do one job per table

Engaging activity:
Students work together to prepare the hummus at their table (Add pinches of salt, garlic, cumin and tahini/lemon juice, mash it)  
*K-2 students: Pass out supplies to each table group and introduce each ingredient one at a time, having each student take turns adding each ingredient to the bowl. At the end, everyone takes a turn mashing while singing the “mash it” song
Students slice cucumbers and carrots to dip in the hummus 

Closing ritual:
Students try the hummus with chopped vegetables and pita bread
Vote on how it tasted (loved/liked/tried it)
Presenters in each group share reflections about how their team worked together, what they did well and what was challenging.
Reflection questions: 
Where else can you get hummus or chickpeas to eat? (at lunch!)
What are other types of dips that people have made or tried?
Why are chickpeas and other beans good for our bodies? 


Materials: 
Reading packet
Task sheets
Trays with materials
Squeeze bottle of olive oil
¼ cup measure
1 lemon, cut in half
2 Cups (1 for lemon juice, 1 for mashing chickpeas)
Citrus reamer
Garlic press
1 clove of garlic
2 ¼ tsps
½ tsp
Salt ramekin
Black pepper ramekin
Cumin ramekin
Bowl of chickpeas
Bowl for combining ingredients

Prep work and setup:
Set up material/ingredient trays
Slice lemons in halves or wedges
Boil chickpeas
Put chickpeas in bowls 
Set up blender
Toast and cut pita bread
Put task sheets in sleeves
Print reading packets
Print and organize recipes

Anticipated problems:
Students might disagree on how much seasonings to put in and end up using too much or too little for other team members’ taste. Remind students to use “pincher fingers” and not pour the whole bowl of spices in. They should taste test as they work to know if they need more seasoning
Students get confused that the vegetables are for dipping and try to put them inside the hummus
This is a complex and emotional topic to discuss. Students may disagree on whose land it is and who is “right/wrong” -  the discussion and reading may take up more time than the cooking. Could be worth it to have a batch of hummus and pita ready to eat in case there is no time to cook

Background information for instructor:
-Ask if anyone in the class has heard of Palestine or what has been going on there recently? Explain that we all have different connections to Palestine - some of you may be from there or know someone that is from there, or you may have heard about it from your family/the news. Others of you might not have a connection to this place or know a lot about it. 

Palestine is a country in the Middle East, and Israel is a country that has been occupying Palestine - living on Palestinian land and pushing Palestinian people off - for the past 75 years, since 1948 (Israeli Independence Day/Nakba Day for Palestinians). This has created a serious conflict between the Palestinian and Israeli people for many generations and this conflict has escalated into wars at multiple times in history, including over the last 6-7 months since last October. 

Explain that in Palestine today, there is a terrible conflict over sharing land and natural resources, including water, olive trees, chickpeas and lots of other plants that are important to making hummus - what we are making today - and other types of Middle Eastern foods. Both Palestinians and Israelis feel ancestral connections to the same land, and need this land and its natural resources to survive (food, water, shelter). However, Israel is forcefully occupying the land, which means displacing Palestinian people off their land (to Gaza, the West Bank, other surrounding Arab countries, to a diaspora which means to countries all over the world), even though Palestinians have lived in Palestine for many generations. Palestinian people getting pushed off of their land is very sad - it means Palestinian people cannot take care of their land, tend to their plants, make food from their crops, etc. It is also leading to the death of many people in this region (mostly Palestinian people but also Israeli people and people in surrounding Middle Eastern countries - Lebanon, Egypt, Jordan, Syria, etc.), lots of peoples’ physical and emotional suffering, and the destruction of a lot of land and natural resources - water, olive trees, plants - that all people in the region depend on to make food that is important for their survival and to keep their cultural traditions alive. Emphasize that when countries fight in wars/use bombs, this is devastating to not just people, community and social infrastructure but the natural ecosystem.

Explain that today we are going to read a story about a Palestinian girl named Oraib who helps her family care for olive trees and groves (big areas of land/farms with many olive trees growing) that they have been taking care of for years, but then she and her family have to leave their land/olive trees during a war. Olive trees are very important to Palestinian people - as a plant, a food, a source of business and income, and as a part of their culture. We are making a recipe today, hummus and pita, that is a really important food in Palestine. It's also an important food to many different Middle Eastern countries. Hummus is made from chickpeas, which are a vegetable that are grown all over this region, because they thrive in that climate/environment. People who live in Palestine and in other Middle Eastern countries have taken care of and eaten chickpea plants, similarly to how they have taken care of olive trees, for many years.

Brainstorm notes
Introduce that we are learning ab indigenous people, palestinians indigenous to palestine. We learn ab indigenous people here bc they lived on our land/have taught us how to take care of our land and grow food, there are indigenous people in places all over the world w lots of different stories of how they lived on their land/what happened to them if they were kicked off etc/traditions they passed on (4th/5th learn ab lenape and algonquin, learning ab it in a different place)
We cant talk all ab the history of these people/this land/wars etc today because theres so much (and more resources we can send ur teachers / be around if u have questions) but today the most important thing is why do palestinians feel connected to palestine, why does anyone feel connected to their homeland. Today were focusing on learning about indigenous people being connected to their land, stewardship/experts of their land, knowing how to care for olive trees/groves over hundreds of years, make food.
This is a land where two people are fighting over the land and both feel connected to it - name the oppressor bc the book doesn’t 
Learning to share land/natural resources with neighbors so that we can all live together vs war and some people getting more than others 
Common experience of displacement/immigration - can be sad to not know your homeland, moving away from somewhere you know is hard but also land literally being destroyed/being separated from your plants/food that you rely on. You guys know about how long it takes for a seed to become food. Maybe ask them a q about how long does it take for a seed to become a plant, tree to grow, olive tree
Learning about a place through cultural memories and traditions is a way to connect with where we come from (remember our food memories?)
Tie this in to the end of the story when Oraib plants a seed and brings that knowledge with her


Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities:
Slamwich matching game (goes with Sandwich Swap book)

Task cards or handouts used: 
Group recipe sheet
Take-home recipes 
**Updated take home recipe

body:
K-1
Spring/Summer
Indoor/Outdoor
Year A

Edible Flower Collages & Salad

Summary: Students will collect a variety of natural textures and colors in the garden, explore the life cycles in plants and animals, and make an edible flower collage and salad.

Objectives: Students will be able to identify what plants and flowers are edible and in season.

Core Competencies: Environmental and community stewardship, garden skills and related academic content, cultural diversity

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson communicates high expectations of students to collect parts of the garden and construct them into both an art project and cooking activity.

Social-Emotional Skills: The Phenology Portrait aspect of this lesson encourages relationship building skills, as students will need to display calm, caution, or care for animals and plants they collect plant parts and take turns to assemble them into a portrait. The cooking element of this lesson encourages students to share tools and materials, work in groups, and practice making a recipe that they can replicate in their outside life.

Garden Connection: This lesson gives students an opportunity to use leaves, flower petals, seed pods, and other plant parts to make an art project and a recipe.

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
For all lessons: Spring flowers/bloom, Indigenous Heritage Month (native flowers)
If cooking: Chopping, Mixing, Salad Greens, Edible Flower Petals, Berries, Olive Oil, Vinegar, Lemon juice, Heating element: none
If gardening: Leaves, Petals, Seed Pods

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual: (10 min)
Welcome students back to garden and kitchen class and review class expectations, “Be kind, be safe, be respectful, be brave.” 
“Can everyone say the word ‘phenology’? Phenology is the timing of life cycle events in plants and animals. Plants, flowers, fruits and vegetables have different times of the year when they are planted, grown, harvested, die and go to seed - like the life cycle of a baby or any other living thing.”
Ask students what season we are in now. Ask them what flowers or plants they can see blooming and explain that we will be doing an art and cooking activity with these flowers. This will help us document our seasons and what was grown in our garden.

Create a Phenology Portrait: (15 min)
Assign students partners, and give them 5 minutes to explore the garden and collect as many natural textures and colors as they can. Remind them to be mindful of the plants and insects and try not to hurt anything living in the garden. 
Have students come back together and sit across from their partners. Tell them they will be making portraits (pictures) of each other, using the materials from the garden they just collected. What material reminds you of their eyes? Nose? Mouth? Maybe something is the same shape as a feature on their face. 
Pass out white paper and tell students to create an image of their partner’s face with the materials they have. Use tape to stick things to the paper.
Share portraits with the class. Ask students to tell the class about what natural materials are in their picture. Ask them how this picture would change throughout the seasons.

Make an Edible Flower Salad: (15 min)
Raise your hand if you’ve ever eaten a flower.
Give students a chance to share.
Tell students, “Some flowers are edible - we can eat them! We can eat them right off the plant, but they taste better if we put them into another food as a special secret ingredient, a garnish. Flowers taste good in salads, smoothies, on cakes and cookies. But you can’t just pick any flower from the street, you have to make sure it’s an edible flower.
Ask, “Does anyone know an edible flower?”
Give students a chance to share & then share marigolds, nasturtiums, pansies, orchids, roses, violets, zucchini/squash blossoms, chive blossoms, lavender, as examples (Show pictures or pass around real flowers).
We’re going to make a delicious salad today, with some of the flowers you all picked before.
Demonstrate how to use a knife safely (claw & saw) with a cutting board and pass out materials.
Distribute table jobs, prepare the ingredients, assemble the salad, then serve. “Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks!”

Closing ritual with SEL component: (5 min)
Taste the edible flower salad. Describe it with your senses.
“Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks!”

Grade Level Variations–REQUIRED for COOKING LESSONS
For younger students, prep salad ingredients and have students pour and mix the ingredients together.
For older students, add more herbs/ingredients from the garden into the recipe.

Site Based Variations, if applicable 
Add whatever is available to the portrait and salad, depending on your garden.

Back pocket activity–REQUIRED: 
Read about the marigold and look pictures

Materials: 
Paper
Clear tape
Clipboards
Flowers/plants
Bowl
Salad Greens
Berries
Cucumbers
Tomatoes
Burrata
Hemp Seeds
Chia Seeds
Coconut Flakes
Olive Oil
Agave Syrup
Salt
Pepper
Forks
Spoons
Bowls

Prep work and setup:
Split up & cut vegetables between table groups
Measure & prep condiments/dressing

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
Here are a few different edible flower salad recipes: (make it your own based on whats growing in your garden)
https://sharonpalmer.com/flower-power-salad-rose-vinaigrette-vegan-gluten-free/ 
https://athomewithrebecka.com/edible-flower-salad-with-basil-balsamic-vinaigrette/ 
https://artfulpalate.com/salads/edible-flower-salad/ 
“Phenology Portrait” by Paloma Jacqueline, in The Sonoran Desert School Gardeners 2022 Almanac

Tropical Seeds 

Summary: Students will continue sowing seeds in the garden and learn that some tropical seeds are used to make cookies.

Objectives: Students will learn that we use seeds not just to sow crops, but to also make sweet treats.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: (10-15 min) 
(FOR MS) Icebreaker: In a large circle, throw the beach ball globe around using different prompts:
One student starts with the ball and names where on the globe their family is from or where in the world they would like to visit.
Students raise hands if they share the student’s family origin or where they’d like to visit.
Student with ball throws to a student with a raised hand.
Continue the game until each student has had a chance to share.
Intro today’s lesson
What season is it now?
Spring time means it’s time to prep the garden and sow some seeds
We’ll also end our class by tasting some cookies and guessing which of the ingredients in the cookies come from seeds.

Engaging activity:
Planting: (10-15 min)
Add fertilizer or compost to soil
Mix in with cultivator
Smooth out soil
Plant seeds based on seed packet instructions
Label plantings with name and date
Prepping chocolate chip cookies: (10-15 min)
Distribute recipes and have students take turns reading aloud ingredients and instructions.
(If time allows) Demonstrate how to make a batch of chocolate chip cookies, as students are reading steps.
(If time allows) Split tasks and students into groups and have them make a batch of cookies (ex: dry ingredients, wet ingredients, spooning cookie batter onto baking trays)

Closing ritual: (5-10 min)
Tasting - distribute cookies to students
Thank you, gardeners. Thank you, cooks.
Changes, additions, improvements?
Class review
What ingredients in our cookies come from seeds or beans? [chocolate and vanilla]
Where in the world do these seeds come from? [hot places, tropical areas, Mexico - demonstrate on globe]
Show pictures of vanilla orchid and cacao pod. Ask students what plants they are. Hints:
They are both indigenous to Mexico. 
They are now grown all over the world in other hot/warm/tropical areas.
We primarily eat the seeds which are both considered beans.

Materials: 
Beach ball
Planting map

Prep work and setup:
Prepare/bake a batch of cookies
Set of dry ingredients, measuring and mixing tools, large bowl
Set of wet ingredients, measuring and mixing tools, large bowl
Pre-made batch of cookie dough, spoons, and sheet pans
Print out recipes and pics of vanilla and cacao

Anticipated problems:
Guide students carefully through ingredient measurements

Background information for instructor:

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities:
None.

Task cards or handouts used: 

Recipe and pictures of vanilla and cacao:

body:
Grade: :2nd-5th
Season:Winter
Indicate: Indoor



   The Three Sisters:
    A Lima Bean Lab

Summary: Students will read The 3 sisters story and do a lima bean activity.. 

Objectives:

Students will hear the story of The 3 sisters and learn the importance of interdependence and diversity. 

Students will become familiar with the different parts of a lima bean by doing a lima bean dissection.

As an additional activity, students will also plant lima beans in a ziploc bag. 

Cultural Responsiveness: 

Communicates high expectations by asking students to read along and demonstrates faith in students’ ability to do a scientific and gardening activity (ie. dissection and planting beans).


Social-Emotional Skills: 

Strengthens students’ self awareness by having students look closely at their role in their classroom, school and wider community as a unique individual. 

Also encourages students to be self aware by providing joy through movement and hands on activities.

Builds relationship skills by encouraging students to work together.

Requires students to self manage by encouraging them to do a new activity (dissection) that they may not have ever done before.  


Agenda/Class Flow: (45 minutes)

Intro: (10 minutes)
“Raise your hand if you have a brother, a sister or even a friend or a cousin who is like your brother and sister.”
Take answers from students
“Do we always get along with our siblings, friends or cousins?”
Sometimes we have to work with people who are close to us even if we don’t always get along. 
“Today we will hear the story of The 3 sisters. Raise your hand if you already know this story.”
The Three Sisters is a indigenous Lenape tale about the importance of plant diversity in growing food..
The Lenape are the first people to live in Harlem and the Bronx. They were here waaaay before we were and were here when this landscape looked much different. (ie. forests, streams, wetlands, deer, otters, etc.). 
Let’s learn more.

Reading: (20 minutes)

 Read The 3 Sisters story. 

Does anyone know what diversity is?

Diversity in gardening is growing many different crops (foods that we eat) together in the same space so that they can help each other grow.
In gardening we call this “Companion Planting”.
This is customary to do in Lenape agriculture.
Diversity is important in helping an ecosystem to be strong and healthy.
Without diversity many ecosystems would fail. ie. a forest without trees.
Other examples of Companion Planting: Growing marigolds in the garden next to tomatoes, peppers and eggplants to deter pests.

    2.  Are we all the same? Do we all look the same? Like the same things? Have the same   personality? Eat the same foods” Why not?

Because we are all very different. We come from different experiences and backgrounds and have different things to offer
. 
Without these differences the world would be a very boring place. 




Debrief story
Who are the Lenape
Who are the 3 sisters?
Why were they planted together at the same time in the same space?
How did each sister help the other?

Three Sisters Exercise: (5 minutes) 
Have students stand tall and straight like Corn sister.
Have students pretend to lay on the ground like Bean Sister.
Have students get low to the floor like Squash Sister.
Do this movement in combination and gradually increase the speed until students get tired and start laughing hysterically.

Lima Bean Observation & Dissection : (10 minutes) 


“We are now going to do an activity using Bean sister. Let’s put on our Scientist hats. What are some things that Scientists do?
Take answers from students.
Scientists explore, investigate, discover and record. 
“The most important thing scientists do is observe.” 
“What does it mean to make an observation?”
It means to look at something closely in order to find out information.
“We are going to do this same thing with Bean Sister.”
“Each of you will get a wet bean and dry bean.”
“Take a look at wet bean and dry bean. Observe the differences and similarities between them.”
 Are they the same shape?
 What is their texture?  
Which one is bigger?  
Which one is smaller?
Show students how to properly dissect a  lima bean. Point out all the different parts on the inside and explain the role of each part within the bean. 

Seed Coat- Outer skin like portion that protects the seed. The coat slowly comes off as the plant emerges from the seed. 
Cotyledon: Fleshy inner part that provides food for the baby plant on the inside until it is ready to emerge from the seed.
Embryo: The baby plant located inside the seed which can be seen with the naked eye if the bean seed is dissected properly. 




Additional Activity:

Lima Bean Planting: (10 Minutes)


“Has anyone ever planted a seed before? How do you plant a seed?’


You get a pot of soil, put in a seed, cover it, give it some water and put it in a sunny spot. 
“Today we are going to plant a lima bean seed just a little differently. Instead of using soil we will use cotton balls and instead of using a pot we will use a ziploc bag. Then, we will tape the ziploc bag to the window and in a few days we will get a lima bean plant.” 
Show students how to properly plant a lima bean in a bag. 
Pass out ziploc bags, cotton balls and markers. Have students write their names on the bags and their class number before planting their seeds in the bag. 







 








Materials: 
Lenape story
Lima beans 
soaked and dried
Potting soil
Pots
Ziploc bags
Spray bottles
Permanent markers

Prep work and setup:

Print out Three Sisters story

Anticipated problems:
Too long?

Background information for instructor:
Biodiversity, Ecology, Lima Bean anatomy

Questions for students/extension activities: 
See lesson

Back pocket activities:
Planting Lima beans in a ziploc bag

Task cards or handouts used: 
Lima Bean google slides

Insect Parts

Summary: Students become junior entomologists, examining insect parts and making clay insect models.

Objectives: Students will be able to explain insect characteristics.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Review previous lessons about pollinators and the role of insects in the ecosystem, food web, etc.
Ask students to describe characteristics of insects and draw those on the board.
3 body parts
6 legs
Antennae
Wings (for most insects)

Engaging activity:
Using clay and objects found in the garden, have students create insect models (following basic insect characteristics, but adding whatever additions they would like to their personal insect).
Have students share about their insect, for example: name, habitat, superpower?

Closing ritual:
Thank you, gardeners; thank you, cooks; thank you, insects!

Materials:

Clay
Prep work and setup:
Divide and distribute clay per table. 

Anticipated problems:
Modeling clay into shapes/insects may be challenging for small hands.
Students may need help collecting nature materials for their insect parts.

All gradesWinter
Indoor


Winter Fruit Salad (Seasonality)

Summary: Students will read a story about winter fruit and make a winter fruit salad. 

Objectives: Students will be able to understand the concept of seasonality and practice knife skills to make a fruit salad. 

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Opening discussion
3-5th: Discuss seasonality and the idea that fruits are limited in winter time because of the cold weather.
K-2th: Ask if students like to eat fruits and have them pair-share what their favorite fruits are. 
Ask if students think fruits prefer to grow in cold weather or warm weather
Introduce the idea of seasonality and that fruits in winter often come from warmer places (oranges) or have been in storage from the fall (apples/pears)
Read the book “An Orange in January,” stopping to explain unfamiliar vocabulary/phrases. 
Introduce recipe and review knife skills

Engaging activity:
Pass out one piece of each fruit to each student to chop them into bite-sized pieces
apples, pears, blood orange, mint
Groups can either make a group salad in a bowl, or individually depending on the class

Closing ritual:
K-2: coloring activity: if you were a fruit, what would you be? (see prompt cards below)
3-5th: If our class was a fruit salad (students write something they contribute to the class and put in a class chart)

Materials: 
Recipe
Tray Prep List
1 piece of each fruit per student (apple, pear, orange slice + mint leaf)
Mixing bowl 
Mixing spoon
1 Cutting board per student
1 Knife per student
1 Bowl for eating per student
1 fork for eating per student
1 laminated recipe

Prep work and setup:
Cut fruits into manageable portions
Set up trays
Write recipe and voting chart on board
Print take home recipes

Anticipated problems:

Background information for instructor:

Questions for students/extension activities: 

Back pocket activities:
Seasonal food wheel activity (Foodcorps)

Task cards or handouts used: 
Take home recipe
If our class was a fruit salad (Lower grades)
If our class was a fruit salad (Upper grades)

Women’s History Month: Michelle Obama

Summary: Students will learn about Michelle Obama and the White House garden. Students will participate in a garden activity (outdoors if weather permitting).

Objectives: Students will be able to identify main points of Michelle Obama’s life and time as First lady. Students will complete the worksheet (biography graphic organizer or is,can,has)

Cultural Responsiveness:[pick one or more] promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual (5 mins) :
Introduce topics for March - Women’s History Month & National Nutrition month
Important to mention WHY we celebrate women’s history month 
Ask students to think of way(s) they can stay healthy and share with the class
Introduce Michelle Obama & title of the story (explain that “First Lady” means President’s wife.
Explain that we will read the story and then do a gardening activity
Engaging activity (10 mins) :
Read aloud “Michelle’s Garden: How the First Lady Planted Seeds of Change
Stop for discussion points throughout the book (post it notes)
Engaging activity #2:
Pass out worksheet (according to grade) & explain directions (also have a sample on projector)
Call students up in groups to step away from the worksheet to help make potting mix ( have “recipe on board”

Closing ritual:
Ask students why planting vegetables is important
Ask students what “potting mix”  is made of

Gardening gloves

Summary: Students will participate in a garden scavenger hunt to review and wrap-up their thermal energy unit.

body:
K-5th GradeFall/Winter
Indoor



   Vegetarian Chili


Summary: Students will learn how to make a 3 bean chili that is served in their own cafeteria

Objectives: Students will be more likely to try the chili served in the school cafeteria.

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families: encourages learning within the context of culture

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Introduce the recipe and explain that it’s the same one that’s served in the cafeteria. 
Go over ingredients and make sure there are no allergies.

Engaging activity:
Pass around bowls of dried beans and pinch cups of spices (chili and cumin). Students can touch the beans - ask if they recognize any/all of them. 
For K, can have students pick out one black bean, one kidney, and one pinto and ask them : How many different beans are in our chili today? What colors are they? Which bean is the biggest, smallest, etc
Make the chili (each class makes chili that the following class will eat)
1 table preps garlic and onion: Peel garlic, use garlic press. Use mini food chopper to cut onion (with no tears!) The rest of the tables chop tomatoes and bell pepper.  All tables rip pieces of cilantro into a small bowl.
While (premade) chili heats up, K-3 students work on coloring sheet which reinforces the ingredients of the recipe
Upper grades: Review go, grow, glow and challenge each table to come up with a balanced meal that includes beans. Students share out/present as a table

Closing ritual:
Before they eat, students can add spoonfuls of the cilantro they previously tore into pieces to garnish their individual bowls (optional). 

Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks, bon appetit, let’s eat! Students vote with thumbs to show how they liked it.
Remind students that this exact chili is served in the cafeteria for Meatless Monday!

Materials: 
Cutting boards/knives
Bowls for chopped ingredients
Bowls/spoons to eat with
Optional but helpful: food chopper and garlic press
Coloring sheets
Pinch cups of spices
Bowls of dried beans 

Prep work and setup:
Cut bell pepper, tomatoes, onion into chunks
Portion dried beans/spices into small bowls for each table
Portion cilantro leaves into bowls to be torn
Pre-make chili (using ingredients the last class chopped) so it just has to be reheated

Anticipated problems:
Chili takes >40 minutes to cook, so you have to pre-make chili for each class using ingredients the previous class prepped. 

Background information for instructor: None


Questions for students/extension activities: 
Have you eaten chili before? Have you eaten the chili in the cafeteria before?
Ask if the beans and/or spices are familiar to students, prompt them to tell you how they like to eat beans at home
Older grades: talk more about meatless Mondays, how beans are a source of protein

Back pocket activities: None

Task cards or handouts used: None

Grade: KWinter 2022
Indoor - Kitchen


Lotion & Agar Soap  
 
Summary: Students will create two cosmetics recipes & make connections to Dr. George Washington Carver.

Objectives: Students will learn about some of Dr. Carver’s achievements, and create their own natural cosmetics recipes.

Cultural Responsiveness: 
Communicates high expectations:
expect students to read and complete a recipe on their own
Conveying to students your faith in their abilities
Expects students to complete various tasks within a certain time frame 
Encourages learning within the context of culture
Topic allows students to connect to and reflect on their own heritage and cultural experiences
Promotes student-centered instruction:
have students offer suggestions for recipes
ask students to cooperate/distribute tasks for each of their table members
Reshapes curriculum:
Incorporating viewpoints and perspectives that have historically been absent from education (i.e. centering people of color and the experiences of people of color throughout history; books highlighting people of color or with people of color as protagonists)

Social-Emotional Skills: 
Self-management
Take small risks in trying new foods and using new tools.
Relationship skills
Willingly take turns and/or share tools
Working well in groups
Make connections to outside life
Responsible decision-making
Cleaning up messes
Putting materials back where they belong
Using tools safely
Self-awareness
Displays sense of pride or ownership	

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual (5 minutes):
Good afternoon gardeners song.
Wash hands with jelly soap & let students know that they are about to make their own.
Hands & surfaces need to stay clean to avoid contamination.

Engaging activity (30 minutes):
Introduce the activity. 
Last week, we made agar jelly. This week, we are using similar ingredients to make soap. This is a vegan recipe (since many soaps contain animal fats) and is safe to use on our skin, but is NOT for eating.
Agar Soap
Instructors will boil a pitcher of water before the class. 
Mix agar with hot water in a large bowl.
Incorporate the rest of the ingredients - mix it song.
Instructor will pour the mixture into silicone molds to set in the refrigerator for at least 2 hours and tidy up, while:
The second instructor introduces the next activity.
Ask students what they know about Dr. George Washington Carver.
Though he was a brilliant scientist, he patented very few of his inventions. One of these is a pomade made from peanuts. We will create a lotion from natural ingredients.
Lotion
Mix 2 parts shea butter and 1 part coconut oil together.
Instructors will add ingredients ahead of time to ziploc bags for students to mush.
Ask students to smell the ingredients, and smell the mint oil. See if they would like 1 drop of peppermint oil added to their mixture.

Closing ritual (5 minutes):
Pack soap & lotion into a paper bag with the recipe card.
Thank students for joining us.
No need to wash hands - they can rub it into their skin as lotion.

Materials: 
Paper bags
Recipe cards
2oz containers
Popsicle sticks for mixing lotion
Lotion: 
Shea butter
Coconut oil
Peppermint oil
Soap:
Agar agar
Castille soap
Hot water
Glycerin
Peppermint oil

Prep work and setup:
Boil a pitcher of water
Pre-portion ingredients for soap.
Pre-portion ingredients inside ziploc bags for lotion.

Anticipated problems:
Hot mixture - make sure to cool enough for students to use.
Messy & greasy - could present tactile issues for some students.

Background information for instructor:
Read up on Dr. Carver!

Questions for students/extension activities: 
Students can create multiple mixtures. 

Back pocket activities:


Task cards or handouts used: 
recipe card

Eat the Rainbow: Orange 

Summary: Students will use their 5 senses to explore pumpkins and pumpkin seeds. Then they will practice measuring while making pumpkin pancakes.

Objectives: Students will be able to measure properly with adult assistance. Students will be able to taste new foods.

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Introduce pumpkins and the color orange
Sing the Eat the Rainbow song

Engaging activity:
Wash hands
Explore pumpkins
Each student gets a piece of pumpkin to explore. 
Students will remove the seeds and try to count how many they have in their pumpkin.
Wash hands again
With help, students will measure ingredients into a bowl for pancakes.
Introduce the “Mix it! Mix it! Mix it!” song.  

Closing ritual:
Set the tables.
Say “Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks!” And taste.
Give every student a tasting passport. If they taste, they get a sticker!
Clean up and line up.

A few raw pumpkins cut into pieces for each students
Trays with ingredients for pumpkins
Tasting Passports
Stickers

Roast and puree some pumpkin in advance
Set up trays with all ingredients

body:
All Grades
Spring
Indoor


Bean Dips

Summary:  Students will make hummus and learn about seeds and proteins.

Objectives: Students will be able to
Express their appreciation for eating seeds;
Explain how hummus is made;
Explain how to assemble the hummus pita; 
Explain the health benefits of eating seeds;
Describe increased confidence in eating seeds; and
State when, how, and who they will have hummus pita with.

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Explore the seeds we eat. 
Ask the students what is going on in the garden now. They might be aware that planting has started, but very little is ready to be harvested at this time. So, we are still eating food from previous harvests. Explain that what we are cooking today has seeds as its main ingredients. Seeds are very durable, and thus are terrific to eat as we await the new harvest.
Show the students several different grains and beans (have chick peas, sesame seeds and wheat berries in the mix) to remind them of the diversity of seeds we eat.
Remind the students that they had beans when they had the three sisters in January and whole grains during the whole grain tasting in February.
We are eating seeds today, but they will be in different forms than how we have had beans or grains in past lessons.
We are going to smash up the chickpeas (show mortar pestle). To make it more appealing to eat, we will add other ingredients. We will add grounded sesame seeds (called tahini) for a tart, nutty flavor, olive oil for flavor and to make it smoother, lemon juice to add a hint of sour, and salt to help bring all the flavors together. Together this makes hummus.
We will put the hummus on pita bread made from whole wheat flour (remind the student of when they made and observed whole wheat flour during the whole grain lesson)
We will add chopped parsley to the top for flavor and a garnish.

Engaging activity:
Make hummus:
Each side of the table gets a mortar and pestle. 
2 people – add garlic and a pinch of salt. Smash.
All – add chickpeas, and smash until smooth. 
2 people – add tahini. Stir with spatula. 
2 people – add lemon juice. Stir with spatula.
2 people- add olive oil. Stir with spatula. 
Assemble pita.
Hand out pita pieces to students.
All- spread hummus on pita. 
Rip up parsley and add for garnish.
Label parchment paper on a sheet tray with students' names and collect pitas. 

Closing ritual:
Clean up, set the table, eat together, clear tables.
Ask for suggestions on how students would make the recipe different next time.
Share shout-outs before lining up.

Materials: 
Recipe ingredients
Mortar and pestle for each table
Parchment paper and marker
Can opener
Measuring cups and spoons

Prep work and setup:
Trays set up

Anticipated problems: None.

Background information for instructor: None.

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 
K and 1st - Remind the students that today they are eating seeds! Seeds are what get plants started so they are good for giving us the energy we need to run and play. Seeds also help our heart, lungs, and muscles work well.
2nd and 3rd – Review the parts of the plant of the ingredients.
Leaves: parsley
Fruits: lemon, olives used to make the olive oil
Seeds: chick peas, sesame seeds, wheat
Explain that the seeds are the main ingredients. Since seeds have in them what plants need to grow, they give us energy to think, run, and play. Seeds also help our heart, lungs, and muscles work well.
4th and 5th – Introduce the parts of plants (as above). 
Discuss that the chickpeas and sesame seeds are good to help our heart, lungs, muscles and all the organs in our bodies work well (because they are rich in many minerals and some B vitamins). Remind the students that wheat is good for giving us energy to think, play, and do everything we want to be able to do (because it is high in complex carbohydrates). Also, the hummus and pita will help our poop be “just right” - not too hard or too soft, (because it is rich in fiber).

Back pocket activities:
Protein worksheet

Task cards or handouts used: 
Pre-k and k task cards
1st and 2nd grade task cards
3rd and 4th grade task cards
Protein worksheet
Images for protein game
Take home recipe

Spring

Expert’s Guide: Harvesting


Summary: Students will harvest the lettuce that they planted earlier in the spring, make it into a salad, and finish the last page of their books.

Objectives: Students will be able to describe and illustrate the steps for harvesting their lettuce.

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson communicates high expectations to students about their ability to grow plants on their own and complete a recipe on their own, positioning them as the experts on the content. 

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson promotes self-awareness as students display a sense of pride or ownership over the plants they’ve grown, the books they’ve written, and the recipe they’ve made. 

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Remind students that this is the last step in writing their Expert’s Guide book: harvesting and eating what they have grown.
Give students instructions for how to harvest their lettuce, cutting only a few outside leaves so that the lettuce can continue to grow.
Engaging activity:
Harvest lettuce with the students. 
Have students chop lettuce, along with other vegetables, to make a salad. Taste it all together.
Have students complete the last page of their book.
Closing ritual:
Clean up tasting dishes and their Expert’s Guide books. 
Congratulate students on doing all the steps! They grew this food themselves and cooked it themselves too!
Ask students what their favorite step in the Expert’s Guide book was. Share shout outs if time. 

Materials:
Scissors
Bowls
Hand sanitizer
Cutting boards
Plastic knives
Assorted vegetables, herbs, and fruits for salad (shredded carrot, apple, cabbage)
Trays
Pre-made salad dressing
Serving tongs
Forks and plates for tasting
Expert’s Guide Harvesting page
Colored pencils
Pencils

Prep work and setup:
Pre-cut vegetables and fruits so they will be easier to chop on the flat cutting board. Place vegetables, fruits, and herbs onto trays. 
Staple new pages into students’ books. 

Anticipated problems:
This lesson most likely will take 60 minutes to do in total. You can do the harvesting, cooking, and eating all in one 45-minute period and save the writing for another period.

Background information for instructor:
This lesson is the last part of the Expert’s Guide series. 

Questions for students/extension activities:
Students could harvest more than the lettuce for their salad.
Students could study all six major plant parts (roots, leaves, stem, flower, fruits, and seeds) and include all of them as part of a plant part salad.

Back pocket activities: None.

Task cards or handouts used: See above.

Three Sisters Tacos

Summary: Students will learn about Native American Indian Heritage Month and prepare three sisters tacos. 

Objectives: Students will be able to discuss Native American culture and influences, including the story of the three sisters. Students will understand what companion planting is. Students will be able to prepare a recipe for three sisters tacos. 

Core Competencies: 
Garden Skills: Students will learn about the practice of companion planting
Kitchen skills: Students will prepare a recipe
Multicultural Education: Students will learn about the Indigenous people of New York

Cultural Responsiveness: 
Communicates high expectations: students are expected to complete a recipe
Encourages learning within the context of culture: students will learn about indigenous tribes who practiced 3 sisters companion planting
Reshapes curriculum: students will work together in groups

Social-Emotional Skills: 
Relationship skills: working well in groups
Responsible decision making: using tools safely

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual:
Welcome back to class everyone! Last month we celebrated Hispanic/Latinx heritage month and made some dishes from Latin America. This month celebrates the heritage of another group. Does anyone know what group that is? 
This month we celebrate Native American Heritage Month. It’s a time to celebrate the rich histories, diverse cultures and important contributions of our nation’s first people - including their culture, traditions, music, crafts, dance, and ways and concepts of life.
There are over 500 recognized tribes throughout the United States
The land we are currently on is Lenape land. The Lenape are a Native tribe that lived all through New York. It is important for us to acknowledge the people who were on this land before us and the food/traditions we got from them. 
In addition to currently residing on Native land, we have all gotten a lot from those who lived here before us. This includes a lot of the food that we eat that is also native to this land. This includes corn and beans.
Corn and beans and squash are sometimes called the three sisters. The three sisters grow together in a practice called companion planting, which is something that was done by Native American tribes. 
Give quick overview of the Three Sisters Story 
Squash - grows along the ground, protects soil/roots
Corn - grows big and tall, supports the beans
Beans - climbs up the squash

Engaging activity:
Students will split into groups and prepare the recipe
Corn group
Squash group
Bean group
Toppings group(s)

Closing ritual:
Say thank yous and eat tacos as a class
Can you relate to the story of the three sisters? 


Materials: 
Baking sheet
Saute Pan
Cutting boards
Knives 
Graters
Measuring cups/spoons

Prep work and setup:
Pre-cut squash
Drain and rinse beans
Warm tortillas
Trays for each group
Beans - season and cook
Squash - season and cook 
Toppings - crema and cheese
Toppings - cabbage slaw or guacamole

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
Native Land Map - https://native-land.ca/
Legend of the Three Sisters 
May need to give some students some additional background information about Native/Indigenous people and how they were impacted by colonialism. 
Recipe Task Cards
