Welcome/ Draw a Garden



Summary: Students will be welcomed to ESYNYC programming, draw a garden, and go over class expectations.

Objectives: Students will be excited to start ESYNYC programming. Students will understand expectations in ESYNYC classes. Students will draw a garden.

Core Competencies: Environmental and community stewardship, social justice, garden skills and related academic content, multicultural education.  

Cultural Responsiveness: Promotes student-centered instruction

Social-Emotional Skills: Display sense of pride and ownership

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual: (10 mins)-- more than 10 
**Start inside, then move to garden after they draw
Introduce self, explain my role
Welcome students as they arrive and have them take seats. 
Welcome back to the kitchen and garden! I hope everyone had a wonderful summer. Is there anyone here who has never been in our garden before?  
Go around circle, introduce ourselves, icebreaker- 1 fruit or veggie that is the same color as something you’re wearing ** if they can't think of one if you could be a vegetable, which one 
Ask if they’ve sung Goodmorning Gardeners before. Teach/ sing song together
Remind/ explain classroom set up, where things are located, rituals when entering- washing hands
Explain plan for the day step by step
Engaging activity: (25 mins)
Introduce activity
Today we’re going to be drawing what we think a garden looks like, and then we’ll be going outside to your garden to look at what we have growing and try some of the plants you’ll be cooking with later this year
Start drawing and encourage students reflect on their knowledge and have pride in their drawings, write names on back
Have timer at front and remind when 2 mins, 1min, 30 seconds, and 10 seconds left
Collect when finished
Respect in garden conversation before we move outside
students establish their own guidelines using prompting questions, model respectful behavior for them in their own space
You all have worked hard in the garden and have cooked with the veg that you’ve grown there, let's make a “classroom agreements” list that we will all use to help us respect and be kind to each other and our space 
How do we walk when we’re in the garden
Walking on the paths, being kind to the bugs that live there, respecting the work we all have done by being gentle with the plants
Using those same principals with each other
 How do you feel when someone respects/ disrespects you
We can have one mic, and respect who is talking, help each other, take input from them 
Draw descriptive drawings of guidelines or write on board
Move outside, point out plants as we move to basil or spinach 
Tasting of basil or spinach, what sensory inputs do they notice
What can we use these plants for 
Look around ask 3 students things they observe about their garden

Closing ritual: (10 mins)
Recap what they learned in the garden
Ask for thoughts of what makes a garden before and after going to the garden
Remember that students may have prior knowledge/ have been there before 
Sing Goodbye song**
Thank you to the blossom
Thank you to the fruit
Thank you to the leaf and stem
Thank you to the roots
Thank you to the seed
Thank you all for your friendship!

Materials: 
Blank papers
Markers
Clipboards in shed
Papers pre-attached

Prep work and setup:
Place papers on tables at seats
Have marker bin at each table

Remind students of respect guidelines they established in beginning

African American Foodways:   Cornbread and Collard Greens 

Summary: Students will celebrate Black History Month and the differences between all of us by reading The Color of Us.  Then we will cook cornbread and collard greens.

Objectives: Students will be able to show each other respect no matter what they look like. Students will be able to measure ingredients (with assistance) and use scissors to cut greens.

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Read The Color of Us, asking for student participation at certain parts.
Ask students to compare their skin to a food and celebrate that we all look different!
Explain that we are cooking food invented by African American chefs- cornbread and collard greens.

Engaging activity:
Carrot table:
Make cornbread batter using task cards. 
Remind students about correct ways to measure ingredients.
Corn Table: use scissors to thinly cut collard greens and onions.
Cucumber table: cook the collard greens
Review the ingredients that we are using
Ask students to come up one by one to add ingredients and stir the pot.

Closing ritual:
Clean up, set the table, eat together, clear tables.
Ask for suggestions on how students would make the recipe different next time.
Review ways that we can show respect to everyone around us.
Share shout-outs before lining up.

Materials: 
The Color of Us
Recipe ingredients
Scissors 
Stove
Large saute pan
Wooden spoon
Oven
Baking pan 


Prep work and setup:
Wash collard greens and cut onions into slices and put on trays.
Prep cornbread tray
Cut vegetables for the first rotation

Anticipated problems: None.

Background information for instructor:
This lesson fits in well to Respect For All week that is celebrated school-wide

Questions for students/extension activities: 
After reading The Color of Us, ask students to use comparison to talk about their skin color in relationship to foods they know: “My skin is the color of honey/cinnamon/chocolate/cheerios”

Sweet Potato Fries: Lunch Club 


Summary: Students will be welcomed to ESYNYC programming, make sweet potato fries, learn about this healthy snack 

Objectives: Students will be excited to start ESYNYC programming. Students will understand expectations in ESYNYC classes. Students will learn how to make sweet potato fries and why this is healthier than regular fries. 

Core Competencies: List all that apply: kitchen skills and related academic content

Cultural Responsiveness: Reshapes curriculum by it promoting group work. 

Social-Emotional Skills: Display sense of pride and ownership by students making a recipe from scratch and trying it 

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual:
Welcome students to Lunch Club (5 Minutes )
Introduce myself, go around and ask people's names. Explain why students are there and what the plan is for today. 
Raise your hand if you have had French fries before? Where did you get them from? Today we will be making fries, something that you normally get from a fast food spot like McDonalds. We will use Sweet Potatoes because it's fall and sweet potatoes are a fall vegetable. Raise your hand if you’ve tried sweet potatoes? If you have had sweet potato fries? 
Go over sweet potatoes being a Fall vegetable. Can go over fall vegetables 
Engaging activity:
Make sweet potato fries 
Go over materials 
Go over ingredients 
Begin first step, ask for student to read the steps or have them in groups read the steps 
If time allowing and material allowing have students peel sweet potatoes 
Demonstrate how to cut potato into strips 
Hand out pans, oil, salt and pepper. Then demonstrate how to drizzle oil then seasonings and spread it onto the potatoes 
Collect pans with sweet potatoes and place into ovens 
2. Have students clean up while sweet potatoes are cooking 
3. Time allowing go over why sweet potatoes are a better version of regular fries. 4. Time allowing go over why making homemade ketchup would be better 
5. Ask students why cooking at home is better? Do you enjoy making food? 

Closing ritual:
Have students say “Thank you gardeners and thank you cooks” before trying sweet potato fries 
Would you make this again? Could you make this at home or with some help? 
Remind students that they made something very delicious today 
Give me a thumbs up if you made something you like? Give me thumbs up if you enjoyed making it? 

Materials: 
Sweet potatoes, pans, oil, salt and pepper 
Cutting board 
Knives 
Measuring cups and spoons 

Prep work and setup:
Peel and cut potatoes into  halves 
Prepare cutting boards, knives, 
Print Recipes

   Literary Sancocho

Summary: Students will compare the literary elements that make up a story to the ingredients that make up a recipe. 

Objectives: Students will be able to identify the different literary elements that make up a story.
                    Students will be able to put together different ingredients to create one final dish.
                    Students will be able to work together to make sancocho.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: 
Ask students to name the 7 literary elements they have been discussing in class:
Plot
Characterization
Mood
Setting
Theme
Imagery
conflict. 
All of these elements are a crucial part of creating a story. This is very similar to the ingredients needed to complete a recipe. 

What what a story be like if it did not have imagery? What would it be like if it did not have a plot?

Today we are going to make sancocho, which is a Latin American soup, that is made up of a variety of ingredients. The ingredients can be divided into three big categories: sofrito, broth and roasted vegetables. We’ll talk about what happens if any of the ingredients were to go missing.

Engaging activity: Students will work as a class to prepare sancocho - each table will have a different task that will be added to the final dish

Table 1: Sofrito → chopping vegetables and adding to the blender
Table 2: Soup base and herbs/spices → add premade sofrito and broth to pot; prepare herbs and spices to add 
Table 3: Chop the roasted vegetables

Give students a chance to observe and smell all the components of the recipe: sofrito, broth, vegetables. If it’s possible, they can taste them as well.

Closing ritual: Students will enjoy their sancocho as a class. At their tables, have them talk about what the soup would be like if it was missing one of its key ingredients: sofrito, broth or roasted vegetables.

NOTE: an easier, no heat alternative would be guacamole. In that case, you can ask students how it would be without: avocados? Salt? Limes? cilantro?

Materials:
Cutting boards
Knives
Measuring cups/spoons
Wooden spoon
Ladle 
Citrus reamer
Big pot
Scissors
Blender
Sancocho task cards
Immersion blender

Prep work and setup (include photos if possible):
If there is only 1 period - pre-make sofrito
Pre-make vegetable stock
Roast vegetables
Pre-chop vegetables 
Set up tables - 1) veggie prep 2) sofrito prep 3) seasonings/herbs

Anticipated problems: none

Tips and tricks: Make white rice to eat with sancocho

Double period ELA class

K-5
Winter
Indoor
Year B



Lucky 8 Stir Fry

Summary: Students will learn about and celebrate Lunar New Year through a vegetarian stir fry recipe.

Objectives: Students will be able to understand symbols and themes that are important during Chinese New Year. Students will also be able to use a knife safely.

Core Competencies: kitchen skills and related academic content, multicultural education, social-emotional learning.

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson encourages learning within the context of culture by highlighting a recipe by a Chinese cook and uses culturally relevant ingredients.

Social-Emotional Skills: Students will take small risks by trying new foods and will use responsible decision making by using tools safely.

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
For all lessons: Lunar New Year, China, single period
If cooking: chopping, cutting, knife skills, mixing; vegetarian, stir fry, noodles, bean sprouts, carrots, celery, mushroom, bok choy, snow peas;  Heating element: stove.


Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual (10 mins)
Welcome students back to kitchen class. “Tell students that this week is an important holiday in Chinese culture - Lunar New Year! This holiday is also celebrated in China, South Korea, and Vietnam. Does anyone in class today celebrate the Lunar New Year?”
If any students know about Lunar New Year, ask them to tell the class what they know, and correct any misconceptions.
“Lunar New Year is the start of the new year based on the start of the new full moon. Lunar New Year is a 15 day celebration where families come together to celebrate the start of spring, eat delicious food, and celebrate a new year of luck and fortune. People also decorate their houses with red paper cuttings and lanterns.”
“Eight is a lucky number in Chinese culture, especially around Lunar New Year. The word in Chinese for the number ‘eight’ is the same as the word for ‘prosperity’. Prosperity means having success and wealth. Today we are going to be making a Chinese dish that has eight vegetables in it so we can celebrate a prosperous new year!”
“Today we are going to focus on being brave as we try new foods and being safe with our tools”

Engaging activity (20 mins)
“We are making a vegetable stir fry. Each table will be responsible for cutting up and preparing our vegetables and making a sauce. Some vegetables we prepared before, but we are going to add them all into the pot together at the same time.”
There is some flexibility here, here are options:
Each table can be assigned to a vegetable to cut
Students at each table can be assigned to certain vegetables
One table can make the sauce while other tables cut veggies
Students can also make extra sauce to add on top for serving.
Teacher works to combine and cook ingredients at the stove while students clean tables and get ready to eat. Time permitting, take time to show students each ingredient being added to the wok.


Closing ritual with SEL component: (10 min)
Serve with precooked noodles or rice.
Remind students that today is about being brave and trying new foods. Ask students if they have a favorite vegetable in the stir fry!

Grade Level Variations
Younger students don't have to measure the sauce, they can just cut vegetables

Back pocket activity: 
Paper lantern making activity!
https://www.firstpalette.com/craft/paper-lantern.html

Cutting boards
Kid safe knives
A wok
Mixing spoon
A stove
Measuring tools

Scissors construction paper, glue/tape/stapler, markers/other materials to decorate

Prep work and setup:
Pre soak dried mushrooms and lily flowers (note: if any ingredients are difficult to obtain, you can substitute)
Wash all vegetables
Pre cook noodles or rice
Print and organize recipe cards

Rainbow Pizza


Summary: Students will discuss how eating foods of all colors is good for their health and then will make a pizza with different colored vegetables.

Objectives: Students will be able to explain how fruits and vegetables of different colors helps their bodies get all the nutrients it needs. 

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness

Review the different categories of macromolecules that give their bodies nutrients → protein, carbohydrates, lipids, vitamins/minerals

*Give worksheet if applicable*

Closing ritual: Discuss the different colors on our pizzas, and what macromolecules we get from the different ingredients.

Interconnection & Native American Traditions

Summary: Students will read about Native American history, traditions, and environmental stewardship, then practice environmental stewardship in the garden while being mindful of the interconnected relationships between people, plants, and animals. 

Objectives: Students will have a greater understanding and appreciation of the Native American worldview of the interconnection between nature and people. 

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson reshapes curriculum by positioning Native American people and cultures as leaders and models for environmental change. 

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson promotes social awareness by helping students to understand the present and past oppression experienced by Native Americans, as well as the leadership of Native Americans on current environmental and social justice issues. 

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
The teacher introduces that today the class will be focused on Native Americans. Students begin sharing what they already know about Native Americans. Teacher affirms and corrects students’ knowledge. 
Explain that in class, we will be talking about the Native Americans who lived in the past, but also about the experiences of Native Americans over time and in the present. Write the word “interconnected” on the board and ask students to define it. Explain that this word and concept will be especially important to understand the reading. Read aloud and have the students follow along.
After the reading, have students discuss what surprised them or was new information to them in the reading. Reinforce any new concepts with a particular focus on the Native American experience. Reinforce the idea that the interconnectedness between humans and nature is central to different Native American cultures. Emphasize that when we care for our garden, our neighborhood, and our community, we are continuing the tradition that Native Americans began on this land thousands of years ago. 

Engaging activity:
Lead students in a seasonal garden job. Ask students to notice interconnectedness between themselves, plants, animals, and other people while they are gardening. 

Closing ritual:
At the end, have students share what they noticed about interconnectedness in the garden today. If time allows, taste a seasonal ingredient (native to the Americas, if possible).  

Materials:
Interconnected: Understanding Native American Traditions reading
Materials for garden jobs
Seasonal tasting

Prep work and setup:
Set up garden jobs.
Set up a tasting.

Anticipated problems:
Students may have misconceptions or misunderstandings about Native Americans, past or present. Correct students’ understandings with a lens of respecting Native people and honoring the truth of history. 

Background information for instructor:
More information about Debby Tewa and Native Renewables can be found on their website. 

Questions for students/extension activities:
Students can continue to learn about the experience of Native Americans in the United States today, including the histories of oppression and resistance. Some topics could include Native American boarding schools, the reservation system, modern day Native American land ownership, and studies of contemporary Native American leaders, artists, musicians, and activists.

Area and Perimeter in the Garden
Summary: Students will complete garden-related problems using area and perimeter and start seeds indoors.

Objectives: Students will apply the principles of area and perimeter to garden-related problems.

Cultural Responsiveness: incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Begin with a rectangle drawn on the board as a grid (two squares tall by seven squares wide, for instance). 
I have a garden problem that I need help with. Let’s say that I want to build a bed that’s two feet tall and seven feet wide. I need enough length of wood to build all four sides. How would I figure out how much wood to buy?
Call on a student or students to explain how to figure out the amount of wood needed to build the bed. Write up the mathematical formula that they’ve used. 
Now let’s say that I know for each square on the grid, I will plant one plant. How many plants total could I plant in this bed? 
Again, have a student help you to explain how to figure out the number of plants that could go in the bed, and write up the mathematical formula they have used.
Engaging activity:
Pass out Seed Starting Math worksheets to students. Explain that you have given them a more challenging area and perimeter problem to solve. If necessary, help demonstrate to students how to fill out the different parts of the worksheet, especially how to color in the area and perimeter at the top. 
Circulate to help students as they work. 
Explain to students that they will be putting the concepts of area and perimeter into practice. Describe how they will be doing their seed starting garden job today: what they will be planting, how deep it needed to be buried, etc. If possible have students plant in cell trays that are divided into grids or other grid-shaped containers.
Lead students in seed starting job.
Closing ritual:
Connect the seed starting cell trays back to the area and perimeter lesson. What math equation can we use to figure out how many seeds we started today? How can we figure out the perimeter of the cell tray?
Finish with a tasting. 

Materials: 
Dry erase board & markers
Area and perimeter worksheet 
Red and blue colored pencils
Pencils
Seed starting materials, preferably cell trays or other grid-shaped trays
Seasonal tasting

Prep work and setup:
Set out soil, cell trays, and seeds for students to seed start with. 
Print out area and perimeter worksheets and put them on clipboards. 

Anticipated problems: 
Planting in cell trays with students can be tricky. Students will often lose track of which cells have been planted and which have not. Be sure to help direct students in how to keep track (for example you could make sure that in a group of students sharing one cell tray each student is in charge of planting one of the rows of the cell tray).

Background information for instructor: None. 

Questions for students/extension activities: 
This lesson was written to include an indoor seed starting activity, however you could also do an outdoor gardening job like direct sowing or transplanting to practice using area and perimeter in the garden. 

Back pocket activities: None. 

Task cards or handouts used: See above.

Following a Recipe

Summary: Students will put an out of sequence recipe in the correct order and follow it to make hot chocolate. 

Objectives: Students will be able to follow a recipe.
                    Students will be able to communicate with each other to work collaboratively.  

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: Today we are going to practice following a recipe. Can anyone explain why it is important to follow a recipe? When we are cooking it is important to follow a recipe because if we don’t our food will not taste as best as it can.  Another important part of cooking is to work together. In a restaurant everyone communicates to make sure that their customers have a great meal.  

Engaging activity: Students will be given a recipe that is out of order. They will first have to work together to put the recipe task cards in the correct order.  Once they have done that they can prepare the hot chocolate recipe.  Students should be taking turns with each step and communicating with each other to make sure they are using the proper tools and measurements.

Closing ritual: What did we learn from this activity? How did you work together as a group?

Materials:
Tools: pots, whisk, measuring spoons/cups
Ingredients: cocoa powder, chocolate chunks, milk, sugar, cinnamon, water

Prep work and setup (include photos if possible):
Pre chop chocolate into chunks
Print/Cut out recipe task cards - shuffle cards and clip together

Tips and tricks: none

HOT CHOCOLATE

Measure ¾ cup of water, add to the pot


Turn stove on to level 4


Wait for water to simmer before moving onto the next step.


Add 3 tablespoons of cocoa powder to the pot



Use a whisk to stir until there are no more lumps


Measure 3 cups of milk, add milk to the pot

7.
Add pieces of chopped chocolate to the pot

8.
Add 4 tablespoons of sugar to the pot

9.
Add ¼ teaspoon of cinnamon to the pot

10.
Take turns whisking until all the chocolate chunks melt and hot chocolate is smooth and creamy

Designing a Garden

Summary: Students will read a story, then come up with their own ideas for what to grow in the garden. 

Objectives: Students will be able to write, draw, and talk about their ideas for a garden. 

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles

Social-Emotional Skills:  relationship skills, self-awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Ask students if they know what season it is or what season is coming soon. Using student answers if possible, tell them it is spring (or soon will be). Explain that pring is the time when we will begin planting many things in the garden. Have students tell you what they already know about what happens outside in spring.
Tell the students that before we can plant the garden, we need to use our imagination to make a plan. Tell the students that gardeners use their imaginations to help them decide what they should grow in their garden and to visualize what their garden will look like. 
Explain that we will read a story to wake up our imaginations and to get ideas for what we should grow in the garden. Read your chosen story book. 

Engaging activity:
Explain that now it is time to use our imaginations to come up with our own ideas for what to grow in the garden and what our garden should look like. Ask prompting questions:
What fruits or vegetables should we grow in the garden?
What other plants should we grow in the garden?
What shapes should the garden beds be? 
What colors do you want us to have in our garden?
Hand out worksheets and coloring materials. Circulate and encourage the students, offering help as needed. Help students to write a sentence about their garden picture, prompting with a sentence starter if needed.

Closing ritual:
Have students share out their ideas with one another.
Finish with a tasting.

A read-aloud picture book related to gardening or food (i.e. Strega Nona’s Harvest,  Jack and the Beanstalk, Planting a Rainbow, Sunflower House, Growing Vegetable Soup, Pancakes, Pancakes)

body:
K-5th
Summer
Indoor
Year 1
A


Juneteenth 


Summary: Students will discuss Juneteenth, why many people eat red foods for Juneteenth, and make a red fruit salad (and fruit punch for upper grades)

Objectives: Students will be able to explain why eating red foods is associated with Juneteenth. Students will make and taste one or two red foods. Students will be able to prepare their own fruits and clean up after themselves.

Core Competencies: kitchen skills and related academic content, multicultural education, social-emotional learning. 

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson communicates high expectations by expecting students to complete a recipe on their own and expecting them to complete various tasks within a certain time frame. This lesson encourages learning within the context of culture because it is a lesson with historical context and it is a lesson that builds understanding of other cultures.

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson promotes self confidence by encouraging students to try new foods. This lesson promotes relationship skills by encouraging students to willingly take turns, work well in groups, and make connections to outside life. This lesson promotes responsible decision making by expecting students to use tools safely and clean up after themselves. This lesson promotes social awareness by discussing social justice issues.

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual with SEL component:
Welcome students to class. Engage them in a conversation about Juneteenth: Who knows what holiday is coming up? Who’s heard of Juneteenth? What does Juneteenth celebrate? Does anyone know where the practice of eating red foods on Juneteenth comes from?

On my whiteboard I had written: Juneteenth commemorates the day the last enslaved people in the United States (in Texas) were informed of their freedom. The practice of eating red foods on Juneteenth comes from the enslaved Yoruba (from West Africa) and Kongo (from central Africa) who were brought to Texas in the 1800s. For both of these and other West African cultures, red is a symbol of spiritual power, strength, and transformation.

For certain grades, I had a volunteer read it aloud to the class. For others, I read it to them and we had to do a few literacy checks along the way.

Engaging activity:
Have students prepare the fruit salad (and fruit punch for upper grades. For lower grades I gave a different ingredient to each table and then we combined everything at the end. For upper grades I had one table squeeze oranges and limes and measure juices for the punch and the other two or three tables prepared the fruit punch.

Closing ritual with SEL component:
Have students eat what they’ve made. Recap intro: why did we make a red fruit salad? What is significant about the color red? Have students clean up their tables before they leave.

Materials: 
Ingredients
Cutting boards and knives
Juicer
Something to put the fruit punch in
Bowls and spoons and cups
Recipe(s)

Prep work and setup:
Helpful to make syrup ahead of time
Write a message on the board or make a handout if you’d like.

body:
3K-2nd GradeFall/Winter
Indoor/Outdoor


Aloo Gobi

Summary: Students will learn to make Aloo Gobi, a potato and cauliflower dish from northern India. 

Objectives: Students will practice their knife skills, and following clear directions on the size the pieces should be cut. 

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual (10 minutes):
Welcome students back to class.  Go over our 4 rules for kitchen: we are kind, we are safe, we are brave, we are respectful.  
Explain that today, we are going to make a recipe from Northern India.  It is called Aloo Gobi.  It uses some flavors and ingredients that you may already know, and some flavors that are new.  
Show video.  Answer any clarifying questions.  

Engaging activity (20 minutes):
Sanitize hands and pass out cutting boards.  Introduce ingredients for chopping (onions), and how to be safe with onions (cutting slowly, hands away from face, and if you need a break put your knife down on your cutting board, look up and blink a few times to clear your eyes).  
Hand out knives and bowls with pre-sliced onions (you can also add garlic or ginger here if you want students to mince these as well). 
As students finish with onions, collect onions and introduce potatoes and cauliflower.  Explain that cauliflower is related to broccoli, and (if applicable) we grow this in the garden outside!  We can also grow potatoes in the garden.  Who here has had potatoes before?  Ask for a raise of hands and call on a few students to hear what dish they’ve tried with potatoes in it.  
Heat a large pan, and warm oil while students are finishing cutting.  
While students are chopping cauliflower and onions, call up individual students to help measure spices and supervise sliding them into the hot oil.  
Add onions to spices in the pan, then cauliflower and potatoes once they finish cutting (onions should sizzle for 2-3 minutes minimum before other veggies are added).  Saute occasionally so it doesn’t burn!  
Have students strip cilantro leaves for garnish.  Ask students if they have had cilantro before.  In what dishes have you had cilantro before?  
Have students help clean up tables to get ready for backpocket activity while recipe is cooking.  
Backpocket activity (10 minutes)
Give each table a naan bread to brush lightly with oil.  If there are more than 5 tables, have one table pick cilantro leaves off for garnish later.  If there are 2-4 tables, have all tables brush naan and then pick cilantro leaves for garnish.  
Put naan in oven to warm while you are transitioning and serving aloo gobi.  

Closing ritual (10 minutes):
Serve aloo gobi to students, and put a bowl of cilantro and/or limes in the middle of each table for students to add their own garnish. Distribute torn pieces of naan bread once curry is served.  
Wait until everyone has a plate, then: “Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks!”
Ask students what they think of the recipe, and what flavor or texture they would add more of if they were to make the recipe again.  
Thank everyone for coming to class today.  Discuss your petal system (this recipe works especially well for brave and/or respectful petals).  Give special shout outs to students or tables that worked well together, or were especially brave/or respectful in trying the recipe.

Materials: 
Set up: 
1 bowl for each table
2 trays lined with parchment paper for naan bread
1 pastry brush per table
Wooden spoon for stirring
Large deep frying pan with lid
Burner
Large chef knives, 1 per student (cauliflower is hard to cut)
Cutting boards, 1 per student
For serving
Plates
Forks
1-2 disposable plates per table for garnishes

Prep work and setup:
For the week:
Grate ginger
Quarter potatoes (par-boil and refrigerate for up to 2-3 days ahead of class)
Chunk cut cauliflower (about 6-8 pieces per class)
Slice limes
Slice onions 1-2 days ahead
Before class time:
Toast spices in oil for 1 minute before class arrives
Defrost naan the morning of, put on parchment paper
Preheat oven to 450-500F
Set up bowls with onions to cut first

Anticipated problems:
Students may have trouble cutting cauliflower.  Use larger knives, demonstrate safe cutting techniques.
Students may be bothered by the onions–explain how to be safe when cutting onions ahead of time!

Background information for instructor:
Aloo Gobi is a recipe that is oftentimes made in winter in India, when the cauliflower and potatoes are ready to be harvested.  

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities:
List your activities here.

Task cards or handouts used:

3K-8th GradeFall/Winter/Spring/Summer

Green Smoothie & 
Black Bean Brownies

Summary: Students will examine the role of sugar in both plant and human nutrition. These black bean brownies are delicious and sweet, but contain fiber and protein which balance out the sugar/energy rush we often get from eating sweets.

Objectives: Students will understand that sugar production in the chloroplasts of plant leaves provides both the plant and humans with glucose/energy to live and grow.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Hand washing
Pulse check: Show of hands, thumbs up//sideways for how we’re feeling
Opening circle/discussion:
How do we feel when we eat lots of sugar? 
Elicit: lots of energy, hyper, tired, sick, etc.
Discuss the role of sugar/insulin and what damage sugar can do in high amounts
Too much sugar will give us a sugar rush, followed by a sugar crash. When eaten often, this cycle of rush/crash could lead to diseases, such as Type II Diabetes.
For 3K start with coloring with spinach leaves activity below

Engaging activity:
Prep & Cook
Tables 1 & 2 - Green Smoothie: blend spinach, blueberries, bananas, and flax seeds/meal and distribute evenly into cups 
Tables 3 & 4 - Black Bean Brownie: measure out all ingredients into bowl and mash with masher before transferring to food processor; distribute into silicon trays and bake
Coloring with Spinach leaves (while brownies bake)
Crumble up spinach leaf and use it to “color” on paper
What makes this spinach leaf green/where is the green pigment coming from?
Discuss Chloroplasts
Discuss how plants use sunlight, water, and CO2 (the air we breathe out) to make their food which is sugar/glucose.
Chloroplast transforms the sun’s energy into glucose/sugar!
Glucose is then used by plants and our bodies for energy. 

Closing ritual:
Eat & Discuss
Thumbs up or sideways for smoothies and brownies. Suggestions, additions, changes?
Closing Pulse Check: How do we feel now that class is done? Thumbs up or sideways? 

Materials: 
Blender and food processor
Sheet pans or silicone brownie trays
Mixing bowls
Potato mashers
Measuring cups and spoons

Prep work and setup:
Bake a batch of brownies at the start of class
Measure out ingredients and set up on trays

Anticipated problems:
Keep an eye out for students using measuring tools appropriately. 
Provide back up activities as prep/cooking tasks may finish at different times.

Background information for instructor:
Chloroplasts
Sugar Recommendation Healthy Kids and Teens Infographic
What Does Sugar do to your Body?
Dietary Fiber

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities:
Provide a number of food labels and have students find how much sugar is in each serving of that food. Have them multiply the grams of sugar by the number of servings they would typically consume and compare it to the amount recommended by the infographic.
3-D chloroplast models: use 2-3 varying green colors of clay to create a chloroplast model  

Q-TIP PAINTINGS: Students will draw a tree trunk then dot paper with varying colors of green, yellow, orange, and red to depict changing leaf colors; Explain that during the Fall season, plants don’t make as much chlorophyll (because sunlight is less) and so other plant pigments are visible
LEAF PRINTS: Finger paint on the backside of leaves (preferably those with prominent veins) then carefully press leaf onto paper. Once the leaf is removed, the imprint should be left behind.
LEAF RUBBINGS: Please leaf under paper and rub the side of a peeled crayon on top of leaf to create an imprint.
LEAF SMASHING: using extra care (2 hands) holding a mallet, have students smash the chlorophyll out of a leaf. This may form a leaf imprint or just a green splash of color.

Task cards or handouts used: 
Spinach leaf coloring page
Recipe cards
Chloroplast diagram

body:
3K-8th GradeSpring
Indoor/Outdoor


Bees & Blueberries 

Summary: Students will learn about bees as pollinators and make blueberry cobbler, plant native plants in a pollinator garden, and/or make beeswax candles. 

Objectives: Students will be able to explain the importance of native bees in our ecosystem and colony collapse.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, responsible decision-making, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: (5-10 min)
Introduction to lesson: Today, we will make blueberry cobbler and learn about another pollinator, the bee! 
Wash hands and demo recipe.
(FOR MS STUDENTS)
How do we read a recipe? 
Explain recipe step-by-step, specifying instructions versus ingredient amounts.
Have students divide the recipe by 3 and write updated amounts on recipe cards.

Engaging activity:
Make blueberry cobbler (10-15 min)
(MS Students) Students will work together to complete the recipe (divide recipe and class into teams if necessary).
(ELEM Students) Have students take turns adding ingredients. 
While cobbler is baking, discuss bees and bee pollination. (5-10 min)
NATIVE BEES
4,000 native bee species in the United States (there were no honey bees in America before European settlers brought hives from Europe).
Native bees pollinate 80 percent of flowering plants and approximately 75 percent of the fruits, nuts, and vegetables grown in this country.
The southeastern blueberry bee, Habropoda laboriosa, capable of visiting as many as 50,000 blueberry flowers and pollinating enough of them to produce more than 6,000 ripe blueberries. At market those 6,000 blueberries are worth approximately $20 or more. 
Some species are solitary (living and raising their brood alone) and some are social (living together and sharing the rearing and provisioning).
The anthers of the blueberry flower are tubular with an opening pore only at one end. The southeastern blueberry bee attaches herself to the blueberry flower and vibrates her flight muscles very rapidly. Pollen comes out of the opening of the anther and is collected by the southeastern blueberry bee. When she moves on to the next blueberry flower, her buzz pollination not only shakes out the pollen but causes pollen clinging to her body to attach to the stigma and pollinates the flower.
HONEY BEES
10-80k bees per hive, depending on season
1 queen bee, worker bees are females and have stingers
Male bees have no stingers, are only for reproduction, and are kicked out of the hive during winter months
Produce honey by mixing nectar with bee “spit” (full of enzymes and antibacterial)
COLONY COLLAPSE
Cause is unknown, but may be due to a combination of factors including diseases, nutrition, stress, and pesticides. 
Many of the same factors affecting honey bee health are also affecting native bee species health as well.
Honey bees may be contributing to the extinction of some native bees. Honey bees can out-compete many native bees and extract vast amounts of pollen and nectar from every habitat in which they live.
Habitat loss and fragmentation are adversely affecting populations of native bees. Pesticides may also have an impact on many species including native bees. This became dramatically apparent in New Brunswick, Canada, years ago when the blueberry crop was nearly wiped out despite the fact that the plants appeared healthy.
It was determined that the program to control the spruce worm in nearby forests had almost eliminated the native bees. Blueberry farmers started litigation that went all the way to the Supreme Court of Canada. Eventually, they succeeded in having the Government place restrictions on the use of pesticides. It took a number of years before the local populations of native bees were restored, and blueberry crops became plentiful once again
(If time allows) Beeswax candle or Pollinator Garden planting (10-15 min)
Make beeswax candles
Support native bees by planting native plants 

Closing ritual: (5-10 min)
Tasting - Thank you, gardeners; thank you, cooks!

Materials: 
For each team, set up batter ingredients (divided by 3 teams):
6 (2) Tablespoons butter
1 (⅓) cup all-purpose flour
1 (⅓) cup granulated sugar
2 (.6) teaspoons baking powder
1/4 (.08) teaspoon salt
3/4 (¼) cup milk
ground cinnamon
Recipe cards and pencils (to adjust recipe by ⅓)

Prep work and setup:
Make a batch of blueberry cobbler
Print recipe cards & distribute pencils
Print pollinated foods & lesson plan
Trays for each team
Candle wax and wicks
Plants and trowels

Anticipated problems:
Adjust dialogue according to age. 
Students will need guidance on ingredient amounts.
To speed up the process, cook at a higher temperature for a shorter time.

Background information for instructor:
USDA Bee Pollination
USDA Bee Basics
Original recipe

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities:
None

Task cards or handouts used: 
Recipe Card_Blueberry Cobbler

2/3
Fall/Spring
Outdoor/Indoor
Year A

Bug Camouflage


Summary: Students will learn about camouflage, why it helps animals, and then create their own camouflage using paint and plastic animals

Objectives: Students will be able to understand how bugs and animals have adapted to protect themselves from predators.

Core Competencies: garden skills and related academic content

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson communicates high expectations by showing faith in students ability to be creative in their painting and create something that fits within the criteria for the project.

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson focuses on responsible decision making by asking students to clean up their messes and use the paint materials responsibly.

Garden Connection: Students will do this activity in the garden space to get camouflage inspiration, or even camouflage their bugs to a certain part of the garden

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
For all lessons: human and nature relationships, interconnectedness
If gardening: bugs, camouflage, animal adaptations, painting, art

Agenda/Class Flow: 
Opening ritual: 15 min
Welcome students back to garden class. Remind students that we have been learning about how animals and plants interact with the environment.
Explain that today we are learning about one way that animals protect themselves. The more we know about bugs and animals, the more we can help them survive.
Ask students to think about some ways that animals can protect themselves from a predator.
Solicit answers and lead them to the answer of camouflage. Ask for any specific examples they have of camouflage.
Pass out photos or project photos of camouflaging animals on the board. Ask students to try and find the animal in the picture (pictures included below)
Ask students why it is important for a small animal like a bug to be able to protect itself? 
Explain that today we are going to be learning more about camouflage by creating our own camouflaged bugs. 
Remind students that today we are going to be practicing respect for our tools and each other as we work on a creative project in a small space. Students are expected to clean up their work space before class is over.

Engaging activity: 20 min
Explain the different materials students will camouflage one bug to somewhere in the room, to a picture, or to somewhere made up!
Explain that the bugs will dry here and once they are dry the students will get their bugs back. 
Variation: you can keep all bugs to use as classroom decoration.
If students are keeping bugs: write names on a piece of paper and lay the bugs to dry atop each student's name so you know whose is whose.

Closing ritual with SEL component: 5 min
Have students clean up their materials.
End class with a seasonal tasting. Ask students to share something creative they saw a classmate doing.

Grade Level Variations:
For older kids you can have them match their bug and paint to a picture and make the bug blend into the picture. Or they can paint the bug to hide somewhere in the room.
For younger kids, they can just enjoy painting their own bugs whatever color.
You can have the bugs dry and redistribute them to students to take home, or you can make them into class decoration and put them on a string.
You can prepaint bugs to hide around the classroom and camouflage them before students paint their own.


Back pocket activity–REQUIRED: 

Materials: 
Paint brushes
Plastic bugs
Option 1
Option 2
Option 3
Option 4
Option 5
Option 6
Paint
Printed photos of example camouflage
Printed photos to match when painting
“Drying rack” - paper with student names
Plates to mix paint on 
Towels to wipe down tables

Prep work and setup:
Buy bugs and paints
Print any photos needed

Background info for educators:
This is a good lesson to do before or after this one to expand on how bugs/animals protect themselves in nature.

K-5th Any season
Indoor



  Chickpea Salad



Summary: Students will make a version of the chickpea salad that is served in the school cafeteria.

Objectives: Promote Meatless Mondays in the cafeteria.

Cultural Responsiveness: reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Go over ingredients.
If applicable, remind students of the hummus we made before, and refer back to that recipe to show that many ingredients are used in both recipes.

Engaging activity:
Students chop celery and bell peppers and rip parsley into small pieces.
Once they’re finished chopping/tearing, students collect ingredients in a bowl at the center of each teacher.
Students make a salad to go with the salad.
Students pass around a pinch bowl of tahini at each table to observe/smell. Have them add the tahini (pre-measured) to a jar with pre-measured lemon juice, olive oil, and salt. Once the lid is on tightly, students pass around the table and shake to make the dressing. *Make sure to demo shaking the jar using both hands.*
Option 1: Keep bowls of chopped celery/bell pepper/parsley at each table. While students are making dressing, add some chickpeas to each bowl. Put some dressing in each bowl and have students pass around the table to mix. Note: I did this for some classes and it worked fine - I went around to each table and poured some of their dressing into the bowl and let them mix (I erred on the side of caution and used light dressing, and let students add more only after they tasted it). The benefit of this method is that students get to do the mixing, although it does take up more time. Pass out plates/bowls and students can serve themselves.
Option 2: while students shake the dressing jars, collect all the bowls of chopped ingredients and bring to the front. Combine into one large bowl and mix in chickpeas. Add dressing to taste and mix together. The benefit of this method is that all the salad has the exact same amount of dressing. Students don’t get to mix which can be a positive or negative depending on how much time you have. Pass out bowls/plates and serve students from the master bowl.

Closing ritual:
Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks, bon appetit, let’s eat! Students vote with thumbs to show how they liked it.
Encourage them to try the chickpea salad that is served in the cafeteria on the salad bar!

Materials: 
Cutting bowls/knives
Large bowls to collect chopped ingredients
Jars with lids for dressing
Plates or bowls and spoons

Prep work and setup:
Prep celery and bell peppers into large chunks.
Pre measure olive oil, lemon juice, and salt in a jar
Pre measure tahini into a pinch jar (optional - otherwise, pre-measure tahini into the jar with the rest of the ingredients. I just think it’s likely an ingredient that would be unfamiliar to most students and is nice to let them observe it separately to see how creamy it is)

Anticipated problems:
You’ll end up with lots of oily dishes, might be worth using disposable tasting cups.

Background information for instructor: None

Questions for students/extension activities: 
Tell students that chickpeas have protein that helps your muscles grow (refer to Go, Grow, Glow chart)

Back pocket activities: None

Summary: Students will have a hearty oatmeal breakfast/snack consisting of various seed and fruit toppings, then prep the garden for spring planting by weeding, amending soil, and sowing seeds.

Objectives: Students will learn that seeds provide us with various health benefits and that we, in turn, can help seeds grow.

Who remembers what we made our pizza crust from last class? [chickpeas/garbanzo beans] This week, we will eat other seeds in oatmeal!
For older grades: Seeds provide different health benefits:
Contain good fats to help build our brains and keep us feeling full
Alternative protein to animal products
Keeps our blood sugar stable
Magnesium which builds bone and soothes muscle

The Garden in the Summer


Summary: Students will learn about different signs of the Summer season that can be seen in the school garden and some things that grow in the garden in the Summer.

Objectives: Students will be able to identify signs of summer in the garden.

Core Competencies: garden skills and related academic content, social-emotional learning.

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson reshapes curriculum by incorporating movement and group work. This lesson positions teacher as facilitator by executing students to work mostly independently.

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson promotes relationship skills by expecting students to work well in groups, Display calm, caution, or care for animals and plants, and Make connections to outside life.

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
For all lessons: Seasons, Summer
If gardening: Signs of Summer

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual with SEL component AND with emphasis on yearly theme/connection to previous lesson.
Welcome students to class. (5-8 mins)
How is everyone feeling today? Who can tell us what season we’re in right now? Spring is over… is it winter? Etc. How do we know it’s Summer? Explain that some signs of summer are: Hearing/seeing lots of birds, seeing lots of flowers, seeing bigger plants growing (remind them in the spring we learned that baby plants are called sprouts- does anyone remember how small the sprouts were in the spring? Are the plants bigger now?), much sunnier weather, and maybe even some fruits and/or vegetables starting to grow in the garden! See if anyone can repeat any of these back to you - say each a few times. 
Remind them how we move safely around the garden and why that is important.
Explain the activity. Check for comprehension. 
Today you’re going to be searching around the garden for signs of summer. I have pictures of different signs of summer that you can see around our garden. You will be given a picture of a sign of summer and you’re going to walk around with your picture and see if you can find that sign of summer.

Engaging activity:
Giving students laminated signs of summer and seeing if they can find it in the garden (flowers, bigger plants, birds, fruits/veg - whatever is actually in your garden).
Explain that when they find it they should call you over to show you, and if they’re right you will give them a new picture. Make sure you’ve printed out at least 7-10 different signs of summer (and 5ish copies of each) - as you hand pictures to students, remind them what the sign of summer is, i.e. can you find this green tomato in the garden? Can you find this flower? It works well if some of the pictures are very easy to find (boosts their confidence) and then some are a little more tricky - don’t give them the tricky ones first! You will want to walk around carrying the stack of pictures so you’re able to pass them out/collect them as students call you over to show you what they’ve found. 

Closing ritual with SEL component:
Gather students back up, collect the pictures
What were you searching for in the garden today? Signs of ___ ? Raise your hand if you found some big plants? (hold up a picture of one) Raise your hand if you found a flower? More than one flower? (hold up example) Raise your hand if you saw or heard a bird? (picture) Raise your hand if you moved around the garden safely? Does anyone want to share what was the most interesting or beautiful thing that you saw in the garden today?
Dismiss



Back pocket activity–REQUIRED:
Students can water the garden, you can have materials ready for students who may want to draw something they see in the garden, or have a worm or roly poly bin set up for students to observe. You could also do a guided tour of whatever you have growing in the garden.

Lenape Farming Techniques

Summary: Students read a story about the Lenape people, detailing the agricultural techniques they used, and do garden work using Native American methods. 

Objectives: Students will learn about the legacy of Native American agriculture.

Cultural Responsiveness: encourages learning within the context of culture, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Ask students to Imagine what New York was like hundreds of years ago before European colonists arrived. What would it have looked like? How would it have been different from what New York looks like today? Were there buildings? Were there animals? What people lived here? Have students turn and talk to a partner. 
Explain that today we’ll be learning about the people that lived on this land before anybody else. The Native Americans known as the Lenape people. 
Engaging activity:
Share the reading, “The Lenape People.” 
Have students share out what they learned or what surprised them. Take student answers.
Ask students to look back over the reading and find the 3 important farming techniques that the Lenape people used. Solicit student responses. On the board, write down the three techniques: fertilizer, mounds, and Three Sisters. 
Connect these techniques to things we currently do in our garden today. We still grow the three sisters, we use compost as fertilizer, and we plant in boxes or mounds too so the dirt doesn’t wash away. 
Lead students in a gardening job. Try to have it tie in with one of the Lenape techniques: amending soil, planting in mounds, companion planting, etc. 
Closing ritual:
Ask students to make connections between the Lenape techniques and the gardening job they did. When we care for our garden in this way, we are taking care of the land in some of the same ways as the Lenape did. 
End with a tasting. 

Materials: 
Lenape reading page 1 and Lenape reading page 2 
Materials for garden job

Prep work and setup:
Prep garden job of your choice.
Print out Lenape reading. 

Anticipated problems: None.

Background information for instructor:
List your info here.

Questions for students/extension activities:
This lesson could be extended so students practice all three farming techniques or cook a Three Sisters recipe together. 

Back pocket activities: None.

Task cards or handouts used: See above.

body:
3rd-5th GradeAll Seasons
Indoor


Trash Masters

Summary: Students will play a game to understand recycling, composting, and garbage.

Objectives: Students will be able to identify ways to produce less garbage.

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles by using tactile, kinesthetic games to encourage learning. 

Social-Emotional Skills: Students practice social awareness by learning about an environmental issue and identifying how they can positively contribute to the issue. 

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Pose a math problem to students: if every single person makes 4 pounds of garbage every day, how many pounds does each person make a week? Then ask students to figure out how much trash we make in one month (about four weeks).
Ask students if they know where all that trash goes. Write the word “landfill” on the board. Explain that a landfill is an area that is designated for trash to be dumped. Explain that in the landfill, trash doesn’t just disappear, but can stay there for a long time – maybe thousands or hundreds of thousands of years. 
Pass out landfill visuals. Ask students to talk in a partner or in small groups about what they see in the visual, and why that might pose a problem for plants, animals, people, and the environment. Share back student answers.

Engaging activity:
Explain that today we will be playing a game to learn about recycling and compost. Ask students to share their previous knowledge about recycling and compost and why these can help reduce the amount of trash we produce. 
Put students into small groups of 5-6 students each. Pass out Recycle & Compost guides and show students how to read it. 
Model one round of the game. Explain that you will be assigning one item at a time to discuss. Each group will have the same item. The group will discuss together, using their guides, to determine what bin the item goes into: trash, recycling, or compost. Either the teacher or the group will assign one student to be the “Trash Master” for the round. The “Trash Master” will be responsible for putting the item in the correct bin when the teacher calls for the end of the round.
Lead 5-6 rounds of the game. As items get called, indicate on the board whether they go in the trash, recycling bin, or compost.  

Closing ritual:
At the end, ask students to recall what items could go in each bin. Highlight that students’ choices and behaviors with regards to recycling can have positive impacts on our environment. “Now that you’ve learned this skill, you can help to make a positive impact and reduce trash in our world.” 
Finish with a tasting. 

Materials: 
Landfill visuals
Dry erase board and markers
Collection of 5-6 types of clean waste items, for each small group (such as newspaper, banana peel, plastic bottle), in trays or bins
Recycle & Compost guides
Three bins in the center of the room, labeled “compost,” “recycle,” and “garbage”
Tasting

Prep work and setup:
Gather items for each small group. If items are not available, cards can be substituted with the item name written on it. 

Anticipated problems:  None.

Background information for instructor:
Check your local jurisdiction for specific information about recycling rules in your area. If possible during the game, have students sort recycling into the correct bins, such as green for paper and blue for metal, plastic, and glass.

Questions for students/extension activities:
Students can make posters to teach others about how to correctly compost and recycle.

Back pocket activities: None.

Task cards or handouts used: See above.

Photos: None.

body:
3rd Grade 
All Seasons
Indoor


Rice Paddy Modeling


Summary: Students do a reading to learn about how rice is grown, then work together in small groups to build their own miniature rice paddy models using sand, playdough, popsicle sticks, water, and plants.  

Objectives: Students will be able to describe how rice is grown, strengthening their understanding of how terraces work and how they’re constructed by building their own miniature rice paddies.

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles by providing a hands-on, kinesthetic STEM activity to learn social studies content. 

Social-Emotional Skills: Students practice relationship skills by working in small groups to create their rice paddy designs. 

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Ask students to tell you what they already know about the ancient Chinese empire. 
Explain that today is all about one of the foods that first came from Ancient China: rice!
Hand out reading and read it aloud to the students as they follow along. Review definitions covered in the reading: “terraces” and “paddies.” Highlight that rice is planted in terraces to help prevent erosion during storms of rain and to prevent the plants from washing away down the hill, drawing to illustrate this, if necessary.

Engaging activity:
Tell students that we will be building our own miniature rice paddies today. Show students the materials they will be using. Set a challenge for the students to make a model rice paddy with at least three different level terraces. 
Break students into groups. Before students start building, have them take 2-3 minutes to discuss their ideas as a group to make sure everyone is included in the design process.
Give students time to build. If time allows, students can test their designs with a small cup of water to see if the plants erode.

Closing ritual:
Ask students to either share out their designs or to share a compliment or shout-out for a group or group member that they saw practicing teamwork or compassion.
End with a tasting. 

Materials:
Rice paddy reading
Play-Doh
Sand or coconut coir
Popsicle sticks
Baby plants, or something that can be used as plant models
Cups with water 
Trays to build in 
Tasting

Prep work and setup:
Purchase or make Play-Doh. Err on the side of having the playdough be drier than wetter. 
Spread sand or coconut coir into trays in a hill shape, so students have a hill to build into terrace.
Divide other building materials between groups.

Anticipated problems:
Don’t give students the water to test out their models until the very end. Otherwise, everything will be a soggy mess that’s hard to build with.
The building activity requires students to work together and help each other to complete it. You may want to highlight this before beginning the activity and have students offer their own suggestions about behaviors that will help them to work together during this activity. 

Tips and tricks:
You may substitute other building materials for these suggested materials. Do not use regular soil for this activity, as it will become very messy when water is added. 

Background information for instructor:
This lesson is meant to accompany the 3rd grade social studies world cultures case studies. It is also meant to follow this lesson on the foods, ideas, and inventions of the ancient Chinese empires.

Questions for students/extension activities:
Ask students to reflect on their designs:
What worked? What didn’t? 
Were there other supplies you would have liked to use to build your model rice farm?  
Today we made miniature models, but what do you think it would be like to build terraces on a hill on a farm? What would the challenges be? 

Back pocket activities:
None. 

Task cards or handouts used: See above.

Photos: 
Rice paddy modeling example photos are available here.

Holiday Snacks


Summary: Students will work together to make holiday treats that will be a part of the school wide celebrations. 

Objectives: Students will discuss their favorite holiday traditions and treats then work together to make snacks for the school community. 

Core Competencies: 
Kitchen skills and related academic content: students will work on recipe reading and their baking skills 
Social-emotional learning: students will work together to prepare something for the school community 

Social-Emotional Skills: 
Relationship skills - students will work together in groups to complete their recipes 
Self-awareness - students will express joy in making sweet treats and pride that they are making something for their school community

Cultural Responsiveness: 
Communicates high expectations - students must complete a recipe
Reshapes curriculum - students will work together in small groups

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Welcome back to the Green Room! Today we are going to do something special for our school community. When we had our Fall Festival, we made seasonal pumpkin muffins to share with our friends and teachers. This month there will be a special holiday celebration so we are going to make a fun holiday snack. 

Engaging activity:
Part 1: Recipe Reading Review (10 minutes) 
First we are going to review the different parts of a recipe and how to read it properly.
Have students answer the following questions:
What are the two parts of the recipe? (ingredients list and steps)
How are the ingredients listed? What order are they in? (in the order they need to be added) 
Recipe reading reminders:
Read your ingredients list and steps before starting!
Make sure you have all the ingredients and tools you need first (mise en place - everything in its place, is what chefs call your set up)
Now that we have reviewed and gone over some extra tips, how confident do you feel that you can work together to complete a recipe? 

Part 2: Preparing snacks
Wash hands
In their groups students will make:
Group 1 & 2: Sugar Cookies
Group 3: Rice Krispie Treats
Before starting, remind students to read through the recipe to make sure you have the correct ingredients and tools they need. 

Closing ritual:
Clean up.
Say thank yous and have a tasting. 
How well did you work in your groups? How do you feel about being able to share our  snacks with our school community?

Agar Jelly

Winter
Indoor
Year B



Monarch Migration 

Summary: Students will learn about monarch migration and play a team building game to understand the importance of preserving spaces that support the monarch lifestyle.

Objectives: Students will be able to understand the unique life cycle of monarch butterflies and how their garden is an important place to support monarchs and other pollinators.

Core Competencies: Environmental and community stewardship, garden skills and related academic content

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson reshapes curriculum by incorporating movement.

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson promotes relationship skills by having students work in a large group for a common goal.

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
For all lessons: Mexico, North America, 
If gardening: pollinators, monarch butterflies, migration, milkweed, life cycle
For 216: can be co or single taught, single period

Agenda/Class Flow: 
Welcome students back to garden class. Ask what they know about monarch butterflies.
Display a photo of a monarch butterfly for the students.
Explain that today we will be learning about the very unique life cycle of monarchs and how it relates to our garden. 
“We are going to watch a short video about monarchs, but before we do, it is important that we understand what the word ‘migration’ means. Can anyone tell me one it means? Why might a butterfly or other animal migrate?
Explain that monarchs are different from other butterflies because they migrate two ways, which we will see in the video. They migrate for 2 reasons - butterflies fly to Mexico before the winter because they cannot live in the cold. They fly back north because they do not have the plants they need to eat and lay their eggs in Mexico. 
“Let’s watch the video and learn more about it!” (watch until 3:49)
Ask students to share something that surprised them or something they learned.
“One thing that stood out to me about the monarch migration is how the butterflies need so many different stops along the way to get energy from flowers and to lay their eggs. The sad thing is, not every garden and green space has these flowers for monarch butterflies.”
“We are going to do an activity where we create a path from Mexico to NYC for the monarchs to travel. Remember - the monarch needs flowers to give them energy and also milkweed plants to lay their eggs. We need to work together as a class to figure out the best order for our monarch butterfly to travel. This means we need to work together and communicate in our activity”

Engaging activity:
 Every student will get a necklace/paper that has one of six plants (either a milkweed or a flower -  there are 5 diff flower cards). 
There will be multiple rounds of the game. 
Round 1: Students need to arrange themselves in an order that has 5 different flowers, then 1 milkweed plant, then repeat the order.
Round 2: Students arrange themselves in that same order, but no similar flowers can be next to each other.
Have students sit down. Ask students why it may be important for there to be lots of different flowers available to the butterflies.
Round 3: Students repeat the same pattern as round 2 but they need to be standing next to new people and they cannot talk.
Round 4: (optional) if you time round 3, do round 4 the same way but see if they can do it faster.


Closing ritual with SEL component:
Ask students what they learned from the lesson. “Why is it important for there to be lots of different flowers available? What happens when gardens or parks don’t have any flowers or milkweed for monarchs?”
“Just like you worked together as a class to create a good path for the monarch butterflies to take, gardeners and farmers all over America need to work together and do their part to make sure there are flowers and milkweed for monarchs. We do our part in our garden to have milkweed and lots of different flowers, but it’s important that gardens everywhere do the same.”
Finish with a seasonal tasting


Back pocket activity:
Monarch butterfly coloring page (at the bottom of this document)
You can also extend the activity, creating new rounds and challenges for students.

Materials: 
Video
Necklaces - print 6 copies so every kid can have a necklace  

Prep work and setup:
Make sure teaching space is equipt to watch video
Print cards/necklaces for the game

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
More in depth video on monarch migration 

Photos, if applicable:

Making Soil, Planting Pea Shoots


Keywords: coir, perlite, vermiculite, compost, pea shoots

Summary: Students will make potting soil, following a recipe and investigating the different components and their functions. 

Objectives: Students will be able to explain that different components of soil have different roles in helping plants grow. They will also gain a deeper understanding of the complexities of soil and why it is so important to conserve it properly.

Cultural Responsiveness:, communicates high expectations and reshapes curriculum.

Social-Emotional Skills: Self-awareness, relationship skills and  self-management

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:

Welcome back! Today we will be talking about soil, exploring its components, and planting seeds that you can take home when they’ve grown in a few weeks. 
What did you make it kitchen class last month? Ramen. One of your toppings was pea shoots, and today, we will be making our own soil from its components to plant pea shoots in. 
So, what is in soil? 
Write up a list of things that students have seen in the soil. Highlight that these are all ingredients that come from nature. Today, we are going to make our own soil. We are going to use ingredients from nature today to create our own soil. As I mentioned before, this is a special soil that is good for plants growing in pots, so it’s a little different from the soil you find outside. 
Introduce soil ingredients to students. Make comparisons between the ingredients for potting mix and the things they usually see in soil. You will use a worksheet to guide you in exploring the ingredients we will use today once we go downstairs. 
Perlite is a rock that comes from volcanoes. It has holes in it so it keeps air in the soil.
Coconut fiber comes from the outside of a coconut. It provides a soft, fluffy base where baby plants can grow. 
Vermiculite is a kind of shiny rock. It helps the soil to hold onto water for the baby plants.
Compost gives nutrients to the baby plants. 
Hand out Let’s Make Soil worksheets and pencils.  Walk students through how to do the worksheet. 

Engaging activity:
Let’s Make Soil worksheets
Simpler Let’s Make Soil worksheets 811

Closing ritual:
Review worksheet, focusing on the function of each ingredient
Taste pea shoots!



Materials:
Worksheet per student
Pencil per student, sharpened
Perlite bucket on each table
Vermiculite bucket on each table
Compost bucket on each table
Coir bucket on each table
Silicone cup per person
Trowel per group
Silver tin per person
Tablespoon per group
Pea seeds, presoaked, 1 tablespoon per person, in a container on each table
Spray bottles, pre-filled
1 piece newspaper per student
Water bucket per group
Label the table with sections for different classes
Word searches 

Prep work and setup (include photos if possible):
Bench circle
Pre set up tables
Soak chickpeas
Label table with class numbers

Anticipated problems:
Early finishers! Be sure to have word searches
We have a lot of classes! Make sure we have enough ingredients.

   Cellular Respiration

Summary: Students will do an experiment with yeast to visualize cellular respiration and make garlic bread. 

Objectives: Students will be able to conduct an experiment to observe how cellular respiration occurs. 

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: Based on what you all have been learning about in science class, can anyone tell us what connection you think there is between cellular respiration and rising yeast? When yeast rises it is because cellular respiration is occuring. Today we are going to perform an experiment to observe this happening. We will also be making garlic bread, this will also help us see how yeast rises in bread dough

Engaging activity: Each group will set up a bag with water, yeast and sugar. While the yeast rises in the bags they will be making the garlic bread. 

GROUP 1: 
Cut the packet of yeast open
Carefully pour the yeast into your bag
Add water to your bag
Use your fingers to mix the water and yeast together
Push the air out of the bag and close the seal tightly
Put bag on baking sheet on desk in the front of the room

GROUP 2: 
Cut the packet of yeast open
Carefully pour the yeast into your bag
Add 1 tablespoon of sugar to your bag
Add water to your bag
Use your fingers to mix the ingredients together
Push the air out of the bag and close the seal tightly
Put bag on baking sheet on desk in the front of the room

GROUP 3: 
Cut the packet of yeast open
Carefully pour the yeast into your bag
Add 3 tablespoons of sugar to your bag
Add water to your bag
Use your fingers to mix the ingredients together
Push the air out of the bag and close the seal tightly
Put bag on baking sheet on desk in the front of the room
*Recipe Task Cards below*

Closing ritual:
Clean up
Say thank yous
Eat together
Share thoughts about the meal and the experiment.
Did the experiment happen as you expected? 
What was your favorite part? 
How could we make it better?

Materials: 
For experiment: 
3 quart sized ziploc bags
Sharpie Markers
Yeast packets
Water
Sugar
Measuring spoons 

For recipe:
Pot
Measuring spoons
Premade pizza dough
Garlic (fresh or garlic powder)
Butter
Olive oil 
Parmesan cheese
Grater
spoon/pastry brush

Prep work and setup (include photos if possible):
Use sharpie Label bags with each group number and the amount of sugar
Set up a tray for each group with the supplies they need for the experiment
Defrost pizza dough 

Anticipated problems: none

Tips and tricks: none

Background information for instructor: none

Questions for students/extension activities: none

Back pocket activities: none

Recipe:  Garlic Breadsticks
1. In a small pot, add 1 stick of butter
2. Stir on low heat (level 1 or 2) to melt
3. When butter is melted take pot off of the stovetop
4. Add ½ teaspoon of garlic powder to butter
5. Add ½ teaspoon of oregano to butter
6. Add ½ teaspoon of parsley to butter
7. Add ¼ teaspoon of salt to the butter
8. Grate parmesan cheese and add to the pot
9. Using a spoon or the brush add the mixture to the top of your bread sticks
