Eat the Rainbow: Rainbow Finale! 

Summary: Students will practice measuring and knife skills to make a salad and salad dressing. They will review all of the colors that they have tasted this year.

Objectives: Students will be able to use safe knife skills (with assistance) to cut fruits and vegetables. Students will be able to measure ingredients (with assistance). Students will be able to taste new foods.

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, self-awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Take a walk in the garden on the way into the kitchen. Ask students to notice as many colors as they can as we walk through the garden.
Review all of the colors of the rainbow that we’ve eaten this year.
Sing the Eat the Rainbow song.

Engaging activity:
Introduce the fruits and veggies that we will use today to make our salad.
Demonstrate how to safely use lettuce knives and scissors.
Wash hands.
Students cut veggies/fruit and help the teacher measure dressing ingredients into a jar.
Mix it, mix it, mix it/shake it, shake it, shake it!

Closing ritual:
Set the tables.
Say “Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks!” And taste.
Give every student a tasting passport. If they taste, they get a sticker!
Clean up and line up.

Cutting boards
Knives, scissors
Measuring spoons

Recipe Reading

Summary: Students will learn about recipes, including the different parts of a recipe and how to read one.

Objectives: Students will be able to recognize and understand the format of a basic recipe. 
Students will be able to read a recipe to prepare a dish. 

Core Competencies:
Kitchen skills and related academic content: students will learn how to read a recipe 
Multicultural education - students will prepare hummus and pita chips, which are from the Middle East

Cultural Responsiveness: 
Communicates high expectations: students will read and complete a recipe on their own
Encourages learning within the context of culture: students will prepare a recipe from a different culture
Reshapes curriculum: students will work together in groups

Social-Emotional Skills: 
Self management - students will try a new food
Relationship skills - students will work collaboratively in groups

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual:
Welcome back to the Green Room! Today we are going to talk about how to read a recipe. Recipes are a really important part of cooking since they give us the information and instructions we need to prepare a dish. 
Learning how to read a recipe is a skill that you can definitely use outside of the Green Room! As you continue to practice doing this you will only get better and be able to do this in your daily life. 

Engaging activity:
Part 1: Cookbook Review (10 minutes) 
On your tables, there are 1 or 2 cookbooks. In your groups, pick one recipe from each book.
Look over the recipe and discuss the answers to the following questions:
What are the different parts of the recipe? (ingredients, cook time, servings, steps)
What additional things do you see in the ingredients list? (measurements, prep- i.e. sliced onions) 
What is important about the order the ingredients are listed in? 
Review the answers as a class.
Now that we have discussed the parts of a recipe, how confident do you feel about properly reading and following a recipe? We are going to practice by making chips and hummus!

Part 2: Preparing hummus and pita chips
Wash hands
In their groups students will do parts of the recipe for hummus and pita chips. 
Group 1: Pita chips (for larger class, 2 groups will make chips) 
Group 2: Prep for hummus
Group 3: Prep for hummus 
Before starting, read through the recipe to make sure you have the correct ingredients and tools they need. 

Closing ritual:
Clean up.
Say thank yous and have tasting. 
How confident do you feel in your ability to read a recipe?

Various cookbooks

2nd GradeWinter/Spring
Indoor/Outdoor


Expert’s Guide to Gardening: Compost

Summary: Students will examine the worm bin, discuss the importance of healthy soil, and complete the composting page of their Expert’s Guide books. 

Objectives: Students will demonstrate their knowledge of soil and compost.

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson communicates high expectations by having students be in charge of their own plants and by framing the students as the expert gardeners. 

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson promotes self-awareness as students display a sense of pride or ownership over their plants, books, and knowledge about gardening. This lesson also promotes relationship skills as students practice displaying calm and care towards garden animals. 

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Review with students the Expert’s Guide project. Ask them what they remember we did and wrote about last time. 
Today will be all about compost. Ask students what they already know about compost--where does it come from, what does it do for the plants. 
Tell students that they will be observing the compost and the worms as they make compost for our plants. When the compost is ready we can give the compost to our plants that we started last time. 
Review the rules for observing the worms (students can use popsicle sticks to stir and look for worms, students may hold worms, be gentle and kind to the worms, don’t walk around with the worms and keep them over the tray). 
Engaging activity:
Have students observe trays filled with material from the worm bins. Circulate and ask students prompting questions about what they see, what the worms are eating, and why we use compost in the garden. 
After observing the worms have students wash hands and return to their seats to complete the compost page in their books. 
Pass out workbooks to students. Have them turn to the pages that you would like them to complete. Read through the questions together with them.
Circulate to help students as they work. 
Closing ritual:
Congratulate students on their good work today. 
Explain that next month, you’ll be moving your plants outside.
Finish with a tasting. 

Materials: 
Dry erase board and markers
Expert’s Guide to Gardening workbooks with compost page 
Trays
Worm bin
Popsicle sticks
Pencils
Colored pencils
Seasonal tasting

Prep work and setup:
Add the compost page to student’s Expert Guide books. Leave out worms and worm bin materials in trays for students to observe. 

Anticipated problems: None. 

Background information for instructor:
This is the second lesson in a series of lessons called the Expert’s Guide to Gardening, after seed starting. 

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities:
If students finish early they can look back to see if they finished their seed starting page, or decorate the cover of the book. 

Task cards or handouts used: See above.

Indoor


Photosynthesis: Light Experiment Part 1 

Summary: Students set up an experiment to determine how sunshine affects plants.  

Objectives: Students will understand that plants use sunlight to make food and that other animals rely on plants for their food. 

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, by including visuals on the board, movement during the food chain game, and experimentation/observation with the light experiment. 

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson has students practice relationship skills as they have to work well in groups to organize the food chain cards. 

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Start by asking students what a plant needs. Today we are going to focus on one of those things, the sun. We are going to start with a game to prove to you that everyone connects back to the sun, everything needs sun!
Have a volunteer tell you one food. Draw up a food chain on the board, using student answers, eventually leading back to the sun (i.e. if a student says cheese draw on the board to illustrate that cheese comes from milk, milk comes from cows, cows eat grass, and grass needs sun to grow). Continue to elicit responses from students until you have shown that plants eat sun, animals eat plants, and we eat animals and plants. 
Engaging activity:
Next tell the students we are going to be building food chains like the one up on the board, where the teacher is the sun, and all of the students get a food chain card. 
Students are broken into groups of three. Each student gets a Food Chain card with their specific role on it. Students are instructed to grab hands with the person who they eat/are eaten by and to form a line. All lines should eventually connect to the sun in the center.
Ask students to describe who was in their food chain. What do all groups connect to? Everyone is connected to the sun. What would happen if there was no sun? All the energy on our planet originally comes from the sun.
Plants are special because they make their own food from the sun. The name for this is “photosynthesis.” Write “photosynthesis” on the board. Plants mix the air, water, and sunshine to make food. Photosynthesis happens in the plant’s leaves. Have students pretend to be plants soaking up the sun with leaves and turning it into food. 
Introduce the light experiment to the class. We will be seeing what happens when plants have sun and when they don’t. One plant will be out in the sun, one plant will be covered with a box. Both plants will have soil, water, and air. Ask students if they have any predictions. 
Each student gets a clipboard with a Light Experiment worksheet. Divide students into 6 groups. 3 groups get “sunshine plants” and 3 groups get “darkness plants.” At each of the stations, students will use the worksheet to write their predictions and draw the plants as they currently are.
Closing ritual:
Tell students the next time they come back we’ll get to see the results of our experiment.
Finish with a tasting. 

Materials: 
Dry erase boards and markers
Sun crown
Light experiment worksheets and food chain cards 
6 plants
Boxes to cover plants
Pencils
Seasonal tasting

Prep work and setup:
Print light experiment worksheets and put them on clipboards. 
Print food chain cards. 

Anticipated problems: None. 

Background information for instructor: None. 

Questions for students/extension activities: 
If time allows, have students share their predictions with a neighbor. Talk through the scientific process with students, during this lesson we made hypotheses, we will continue the experiment until the next time they come back, then we can make observations and conclusions. 

Back pocket activities: None. 

Task cards or handouts used: See above.

Kitchen Tool Identification

Summary: Students will match various kitchen tools to their proper name and description. 

Objectives: Students will be able to identify various kitchen tools we will use regularly. 

Core Competencies:
Kitchen skills and related academic content - students will become familiar with the different tools we use while cooking

Cultural Responsiveness: 
Communicates high expectations - students are expected to complete a task within a certain amount of time
Reshapes curriculum - incorporates movement and group work 
Positions teacher as facilitator - students will work in small independent groups

Social-Emotional Skills: 
Relationship skills - students will work together in groups
Relationship skills - students will make connections to outside life (items they have/use at home)
Responsible decision-making - students will learn about using tools safely

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual:
Hello everyone! Since you will be coming into the Green Room pretty often, it is important for you to be familiar with the tools we will be using.  So today we are going to play a game to help you learn what certain tools are and what they are for. 

Engaging activity:
Students will play the Tool ID game 
Students have to match the ID cards to the corresponding items on their tables
Students will break into 3 groups - one group per table
As a class go over the correct answers.

Closing ritual:
End of class discussion questions
Do you feel more comfortable in the kitchen?
Do you think you would be able to find any of the tools we may need while cooking?
Teamwork was important for this game. How did your group work as a team?

Students may take too long - set a timer (15-20 minutes to do game)

Black Bean Dip
Summary: Students will work in teams to practice following a recipe for black bean dip

Objectives: Students will be able to work together to follow a recipe and learn a basic technique for making a bean dip.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills, responsible decision-making, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Welcome students
Introduce yourself
Explain that we are going to be cooking something delicious and healthy
Go over class expectations to be safe, responsible, respectful and adventurous. Give all students hand sanitizer and remind them how to be clean and safe with their bodies and the tools.
Go over plan for the day  (5 minutes)
Today we are going to make black bean dip, a popular dish from Central and South America. Black bean dip is popular in Costa Rica, Mexico, Cuba, and also all over North America. Remind students that beans are a good source of plant protein (a “Grow” food) which helps to build muscles and keep us feeling full.
Raise your hand if you have ever eaten black beans. Raise your hands if you like black beans. If you haven’t had black bean dip before, raise your hand if you are ready to cook and try something new today.
Show students pictures of black bean dip.

Engaging activity:
Ingredients (5 minutes)
Show black bean dip task card picture instructions to review ingredients and instructions.
Safety and Materials (5 minutes)
Do a demo of handling knives/scissors and cutting as well as how to use the masher tool.
Go over safety and the materials we will be using.
Pass out cutting boards and knives/scissors.
Students will chop a few items - garlic, cilantro, carrots, cucumbers (10 minutes)
Pass out ingredients and show them how to cut it and how small you want it.
Use the task cards!
Take turns adding ingredients one by one into the bowl and mixing them up. (5 min)
Mix all the ingredients together with a food processor and transfer to a bowl. Serve the black bean dip and chips/carrot sticks/cucumbers.

  Closing ritual:
Have them wait for everyone to get black bean dip and cucumbers/carrots/chips. (5 minutes)
Before they taste it, have them say “Thank you gardeners, Thank you Cooks” 
As they’re eating, ask students to describe what they’re tasting.
You did a great job taking turns and working together! Without everyone’s help it wouldn’t have tasted so delicious! Did you have fun working together? 
Pass out recipes to take home.

Materials: 
Black beans
Garlic
Cilantro
Lime
Salt
Cumin
Jalapeno 
Cayenne
Sour Cream
Water
Measuring Cup
Tablespoons / teaspoons
Knives
Scissors
Cutting Boards
Mashers
Small cups for spices
Mixing Bowl
Mixing Spoon
Food Processor
Bowls
Spoons
Hand Sanitizer
Recipe Sheets
Coloring Pages
Word Searches
Task Card Picture Instructions
Black bean dip pictures

Prep work and setup:
Put seasonings in small cups
Cut limes into wedges and vegetables into manageable pieces
Put beans in bowls 
Set up food processor
Print out recipes, pictures, task card picture instructions, coloring sheets and word searches 

Anticipated problems:
Students might disagree on how much seasonings to put in and end up using too much or too little for other team members’ taste. Remind students to use “pincher fingers” and not pour the whole bowl of spices on. They should taste test as they work to know if they need more seasoning
Students get confused that the vegetables are for dipping and try to put them inside the bean dip. Remind them to cut carrots into sticks

3K-1 Indoor Garden Jobs

Summary: Students will engage in a garden job inside like soil mixing, seed saving, seed starting, or something else.

Objectives: Students will be able to understand that there are many ways to help the garden, both inside and outside.

Core Competencies: Environmental and community stewardship, garden skills and related academic content, social-emotional learning.

Cultural Responsiveness:This lesson communicates high expectations by conveying faith in students to complete the task in the allotted time. The lesson also reshapes curriculum by promoting groupwork and movement.

Social-Emotional Skills: Students will practice relationship skills by being calm and cautious with plants and animals. There is typically less room in indoor spaces so students will have to stay calm while doing a focused task like measuring soil or carefully planting a seeds.

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
All year, indoor, garden, soil, seeds, 

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual (5 mins)
Welcome students back to garden class. Remind students that all year, we’ve been learning what it means to be a gardener. Ask students some ways that they have learned to be a gardener. Solicit student responses. We’ve also learned WHY gardening is important and why we like to do it. Ask students about how gardening makes them feel (good, calm, happy, excited, etc)
Today we are inside because there are actually a lot of ways we can help the garden inside too! We are going to practice being calm for our garden job today as we work inside to help the garden get ready for spring and summer.

Engaging activity (30 minutes):
Explain the garden job. There are many indoor garden jobs to draw from - soil mixing, seed starting or seed saving. Pick one job and explain to students how the job is going to work.
For soil mixing: Ask students what plants need to survive. Explain that you will be making a soil recipe to plant seeds in. The soil needs to be the perfect mix of each ingredient to make the seeds happy and healthy.
Help students make the connection between measuring ingredients in kitchen class and measuring soil ingredients today.
Emphasize that in order to do the right mix of soil, we will have to be calm with our bodies as we focus to measure the right ingredients and keep our workspace clean.
For seed starting: Frame the lesson around the coming of spring and the need to start baby seeds inside where it is warmer so they can be big and strong when its time to put them in the ground.
Demonstrate how to fill the cup, make a hole, and bury the seed. Have students help show how this is done. 
Emphasize that we need to be calm as we put our seed in the soil. You can have students make wishes for their seeds as they tuck them away to grow!
For seed saving: Ask students where plants come from. Seeds! Explain that an important process in gardening is to save the seeds that our plants make so that we can grow them again. Some seeds are small and some are big, so we need to be calm so that we can save as many as possible!
You can put out seed envelopes and colored pencils for students to decorate or to take home!

Closing ritual with SEL component (10 mins)
Have students wash hands and come back together. 
Ask students how it felt to practice being calm. Ask again how doing garden activities makes students feel.
Do a seasonal tasting.


Back pocket activity: 
What a plant needs to survive worksheet

Materials: 
For soil mixing: ingredients to soil (perlite, vermiculite, compost, coir), small cups for measuring, trays to measure into, hand washing station
For seed starting: seed of choice, pre mixed soil, cups to start the seeds in, popsicle sticks and pencils to write the seed type, spray bottles to water afterwards
For seed saving: trays, seed envelopes, colored pencils

Three Sisters Enchiladas

Summary: .Students will learn about Mexican food culture by making enchilada. The class will also focus on the Three Sisters: corn, beans, and squash, and how these three plants grow symbiotically in the garden and fuel our bodies with everything that we need to grow.  

Objectives: Students will be able to measure ingredients properly, be shown the oven and be told how it works and how to behave around it, learn about the three sisters, learn about fresh vs preserved food. 

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Introduce today’s recipe.
This month we are making food from Mexico, this food and the ingredients that we are using today are ancient. The three sisters are ingredients that have existed and have been planted in Northern America for hundreds of years and people from different countries use them to make so many different recipes. 

Engaging activity:
Carrot Table:
Students will get all the ingredients needed to make the sauce that will go with the enchiladas. They will use a burner to heat up tomatoes, honey, and chipotle. 
Corn Table:
 Students will measure all of the ingredients that are found in the filling, including beans, squash and cheese. 
 All grades will do a tasting at the middle table. Students will be asked to taste fruits or vegetables and then be asked to taste their preserved counterpart. 
K - 2nd Grade - Students will talk about what they taste and be exposed to new tasting words
3rd grade- Students will write an opinion piece about the vegetables they just tasted. 
4th grade- Students will collect data based on students favorite vegetables, for a following activity. 
5th grade- Students will write a short description about the vegetables they just ate, comparing flavors. 
Cucumber Table:
Students will assemble all of the enchiladas, stuffing them, rolling them and placing them in a baking sheet where they will be baked. 

Closing ritual:
Clean up, set the table, eat together, clear tables.
Ask for suggestions on how students would make the recipe different next time.
Share shout-outs before lining up.

Measuring cups and spoons

Induction-ready pan
Zester 
Salt
Wooden spoon
Masher
Bowls
Spoons
scissors

Cut the first set of vegetables for the first class’s first rotation.
Pre-make one batch of curry powder.

body:
Smashed Cucumber Salad


Summary: Students will learn how to make a smashed cucumber salad and practice tasting norms together in kitchen class.

Objectives: Students will practice new kitchen skills, such as mixing and measuring, while also practicing using respectful language to share their opinions on food flavors, textures, and smells. 

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles by practicing fine motor, gross motor, and verbal skills. 

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson encourages self-management by encouraging students to develop their own opinions and practice sharing their opinions in a respectful manner. 

Agenda/Class Flow: (45 minutes)
Opening ritual (5 minutes):
Welcome students back to the kitchen. “Today is our last kitchen class of the year so we are making something very delicious from one of the biggest countries in the world! Does anyone have any guesses?” Take responses, then share that it is China.
“China is the 3rd biggest country in the world. Some people who live in China live near the ocean, while other people live in the desert or near mountains. The weather and food also changes in different areas of China. Today we are making a recipe from the Sichuan region, which is near the middle of China.” Show students China and the Sichuan region on the map.
Tell students that we are going to practice an important kitchen skill today, especially since we haven’t been eating in class together lately. “If we don’t like or don’t want the food, we don’t want to say that it’s yucky. If I like the food and someone else says it’s yucky, that might hurt my feelings. You can say, ‘I don’t like it,’ or ‘It’s not for me,’ but let’s practice doing it in a kind way.”
“Another thing I want us to practice today in kitchen class is trying something new. A food, a smell, or the way something feels might be new to you, and that’s okay! But just because it’s new doesn’t mean it’s bad. Let’s practice being brave and trying new things!”

Engaging activity (15 minutes):
Sanitize hands before beginning cooking.
Remind students of our safety norms: 
Touch only your own tools and ingredients. 
Always walk in the kitchen classroom.
Use a bear claw and a sawing motion when cutting.
Use all tools only for their intended job, not for playing. 
Be responsible for cleaning up your own space. 
Wait for everyone to eat all together. 
Express your opinions about the food using respectful language; don’t yuck anyone else’s yum. 
Keep an open mind and try new things!
“Our recipe today is called ‘pai huang gua’, or smashed cucumber salad. When we smash the cucumbers, it releases juices that help it absorb all the yummy flavors we will add. Plus, smashing it is very fun!”
Give each student the materials and ingredients needed to start the recipe. Give things out in sequence, explaining instructions to each table. Here is the recommended sequence:
Each table will have a large plastic bag with 4 servings of cucumber. Using one mallet/rolling pin per table, students will pass the smashing device around taking turns smashing the bag. 
Teachers collect the bag and add to a strainer and sprinkle it lightly with salt. Collect rolling pins as well.
While the cucumber strains, pass out containers for students to make the dressing in. The containers will already have sugar, garlic, and chili flakes (optional). 
Pass out a sprig of cilantro for students to rip.
While they rip the cilantro, pass out measuring spoon and squeeze bottles. Students will add soy sauce and rice vinegar to the container. 
Collect materials as they are no longer needed.
Put containers to the side and pass out safety knives and the drained, smashed cucumbers. Have students cut cucumbers into bite sized pieces. Add these to the container. During this, a teacher will go around and add sesame oil to each container.
Give them a top and let them shake, or let them stir using the measuring spoon.
For ease, we will be using reusable plastic containers that allow students to shake. However, plates or any other bowl can be used and students can mix.

Closing ritual (10 minutes):
Clean the area and have students get a table decoration. 
Students eat all together and share tasting words and feedback.
Practice using respectful language about likes and dislikes with the food. Model examples with students.
Clean up plates. 


Materials: 
World map
Cutting boards
Rolling pin or mallet
Safety knives
Plastic containers or plates 
Forks
Measuring spoons, in ½ and ⅛ tsp
Large plastic bags
Ingredients: cucumbers, garlic, salt, sugar, rice vinegar, soy sauce, sesame oil, red chili flakes, cilantro, sesame seeds
Take home recipes, one per student
Compost buckets
Hand sanitizer
Table decorations (optional)
China Bingo boards, laminated, one per student (for extension activity)
Bingo cards for the instructor, cut out from extra bingo boards (for extension activity)
Dry erase markers, one per student (for extension activity)

Prep work and setup:
Set up a bottle of hand sanitizer and a compost bucket at the center of each table. 
Arrange materials for each table. Each student will need a cutting board. Have knives, rolling pins, a container, ½ tsp measuring spoons, squeeze bottles with rice vinegar and soy sauce, and a plastic bag with the cucumber to the side ready to distribute. 
For the sauce, pre-fill the container with sugar, salt, garlic, and chili flakes.
Cut the cucumbers into two-inch pieces. Pre-bag the cucumbers, one per each small group. 
Print out and cut recipes to distribute.
Fill squeeze bottles with rice vinegar and soy sauce in separate bottles.
Print and laminate bingo cards.
Recommended quantities of ingredients per student are as follows:
¼ cucumber
¼ tsp sugar
½ tsp rice vinegar
½ tsp soy sauce
½ tsp sesame oil
⅛ tsp garlic
⅛ tsp chili flakes (optional)
1 sprig of cilantro

Anticipated problems:
It may be difficult for students to cut their cucumber once it is smashed. The squishiness of it may be hard for our sensory-sensitive students. Offer support or gloves, if needed.

Background information for instructor: None.

Questions for students/extension activities: None.

Back pocket activities:
If time allows, students can play China bingo.
“We wanted to play a little game to get to know China. Who has ever played bingo before? Today we are playing China bingo! Every tile on the bingo board is something that was invented in China, or a food that is from China.”
Explain that every student will receive a 3x3 bingo board. Show students how to use the dry erase marker to cross out the matching tile on their own board. Play until a few students have bingo. The game will look a little different for PK-K and 1-2.
For PK-K, there are additional cards along with the boards. The cards have the same images corresponding to the boards. The teacher will draw a card at random and walk around with it, showing students to cross off or color in the matching picture.
For grades 1-2, describe the different items listed with hints. See if students can guess which item on their board you are referring to. Confirm their answers, then have them cross off as they go. 

Task cards or handouts used: See above.

4th-5th Grades
Winter/Spring
Indoor
Year 1



Navdanya & The Importance of Seed Saving

Summary: Students will learn the history of the seed sovereignty movement in India, then will plant heritage seeds for our spring garden. 

Objectives: Students will be able to explain the importance of seed saving for human cultures. Students will be able to describe how to start a seed. 

Core Competencies: Environmental and community stewardship, social justice, multicultural education.

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson encourages learning within the context of culture by providing an example of leadership and resistance in the South Asian community.  

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson helps students to develop social awareness by learning about the problems that farmers have faced and how farmers have organized for their independence. 

Agenda/Class Flow: (45 minutes)
Opening ritual: (20 minutes)
“During garden and kitchen classes this spring, we are going to be doing a lot of activities connected to food. In cultures all across the world, people use food as a way to make the world more fair and better for everyone. We are going to be learning about some of these traditions and participating in activities that follow their example.” Explain that today we are going to be learning about a movement called Navdanya that began in India. 
Ask students to tell you about the plant life cycle: how a seed grows, flowers, and produces new seed for the next generation. “Throughout human history, farmers save these seeds for the next growing season. You might remember that we did this together in the fall when we were preserving the harvest.” Explain that this is important information to remember for our story.
Read the Navdanya text aloud. You may want to have prompting questions on the board to help them understand the reading:
Why do farmers have to know how to save seeds?
What were some of the new technologies that were invented 60 years ago?
What were some of the problems caused by these new technologies?
How did Vandana Shiva help solve these problems?
“Sharing seeds is a way to make sure that all farms and gardens can grow lots of different types of plants, fruits, and vegetables. Today, we are going to carry on this tradition by planting a few different kinds of seeds. These seeds were also saved by farmers long ago, like the Indian farmers in the Navdanya movement.”

Engaging activity: (15 minutes)
Lead students in a seed starting activity. If available, give students background on the history of the seeds they are starting today. Students can also plant different varieties in small groups to emphasize the importance of seed diversity.

Closing ritual: (10 minutes)
Share a seasonal tasting, ideally of an heirloom variety of fruit or vegetable. Emphasize that this fruit or vegetable was grown from a seed that was saved by a farmer in the past. “When we say, ‘Thank you, gardeners, thank you, cooks,’ we are saying thanks to the farmers in the past who saved these seeds for us.” 
During tasting, turn and talk to a neighbor: “If you could talk to one of the farmers from the past who saved these seeds for us, what would you say to them?”


Materials: 
Navdanya reading
Soil, pots, seeds, spray bottles, and plant labels for seed starting activity
Tasting 

Prep work and setup:
Set up materials for planting in small group stations, with soil in trays for easy scooping.
Prepare the tasting. 

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
The Navdanya website has a great deal of in-depth information on the seed sovereignty movement in India. 

Back pocket activities: None. 

Photos, if applicable: None.

body:
6th-8th GradeWinter




Cajun Black Eyed Peas sliders 
Summary: As a tribute to Black History month, students will learn how to make cajun black-eyed pea sliders . 

Objectives: Students will be able to learn about food related to black history month and learn how to make delicious healthy food. 

Cultural Responsiveness:
1. Communicates high expectations: understanding and reading recipe 
2. Encourages learning within the context of culture:lessons / recipe with historical 

Social-Emotional Skills: 
1.self-management: Taking risks making and trying new food. Self-confidence.
2.responsible decision-making: Using tools, being aware of safety of themselves and others, cleaning up
3.social awareness: learning and discussing social justice issues 

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual (10-15 min):
Hand washing.
Pulse check 
Opening circle/discussion: 
The black-eyed pea bean is a legume grown around the world for its medium-sized, edible bean. It is a subspecies of the cowpea, an Old World plant domesticated in Africa, and is sometimes simply called a cowpea.

George Washington Carver also used the black eyed pea to help black southern farmers build nitrogen in the soil.
Today we are cooking with black eye pea beans to make sliders .

Engaging activity (15-20 min):
Demonstrate prep tasks and tool safety
Prep ingredients
Table one - Chopping , slicing 
Table two - remoulade and Cajun seasoning 
Table three - making the sliders 
As a whole shaping the slides 

Closing ritual (10-15 min):
Clean and set tables
Put away prepped stuff for other classes

.
Eat, Discuss, Shout-outs & Clean up
Ask students what they thought of the food
Discuss the flavors of the sauce 
Did they enjoy making the sliders

Materials: 
Cutting board & knives
Measuring spoons , cup and knives
Mixing bowls 
Food processor 
Frying pan or bake and turner spatula 
Trays , mold shape round 
Mortar and pestle
Tray Set-Up:
Prep work and setup:
Soak beans overnight prior to class if using dried black eyed peas.
Cook beans 
Set up trays with ingredients 
Pre cut one batch of chips
ESYNYC teachers should divide tasks & groups before a class arrives
Create task cards with pictures (differentiate teaspoons and tablespoons, for example)
Anticipated problems:
Pre making / having back up 
Oven not working properly  ( use skillet or frying pan) 
Food waste - Can we cut things in half for picky students to taste?
Background information for instructor:
Black Eyed Pea
Black history Month

body:
All GradesFall/Winter
Indoor/Remote

 

Breakfast Banana Splits

Summary: Students will learn to follow a recipe for breakfast banana splits.

Objectives: Students will learn the concept of garnishing food and presenting it in a visually appealing way, and be able to work together to follow a recipe to make breakfast banana splits.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Welcome students
Introduce yourself
Explain that we are going to be cooking something delicious and healthy.
Go over class expectations to be safe, responsible, respectful and adventurous. Give all students hand sanitizer and remind them how to be clean and safe with their bodies and the tools.

Go over plan for the day  (5 minutes)
Today we are going to cook a yummy and healthy recipe with fruit and yogurt. This dish tastes yummy but also looks super good. Raise your hand if you think food tastes better if it looks good. (Give examples like ice cream with rainbow sprinkles, salad with cheese sprinkled on top, etc.). 
Raise your hand if you are an artist or like making art/being creative. What are your favorite colors? Raise your hand if you like colorful foods. What are they? If you are an artist or even if you just like colors or colorful food, this recipe and cooking in general is a way for you to be creative. You can cook to make delicious and healthy food, but also present it in a beautiful way. Some chefs call this “garnishing,” which means to decorate and present food in a beautiful way. (You can describe a cooking show as a reference, if they have seen the British Baking Show, etc.) 
Today we are going to be making breakfast banana splits. Traditional banana splits are a dessert food, but this is a version of the recipe that is healthy and refreshing to eat at the beginning of the day. A lot of people already eat yogurt/granola for breakfast, and this is an extra fun and special way to prepare that breakfast dish. This also could definitely be eaten as a snack or dessert. Show students pictures of breakfast banana splits.

Engaging activity:
Ingredients (5 minutes)
Show breakfast banana splits task card picture instructions to review ingredients and instructions.
Safety and Materials (5 minutes)
Do a demo of handling knives and measuring cups and spoons.
Go over safety and the materials we will be using.
Pass out cutting boards, knives and measuring cups and spoons.
Follow the step by step instructions to prepare all the ingredients and assemble them in a beautiful and creative way.
Demonstrate each step first and then allow the students to each take a turn. 
Use the task cards!
You can also assign one task to each student or pair of students so they can all do different tasks at the same time.
Set up all the ingredients, like a breakfast bar on a side table, and make sure they all have utensils in them.
Serve a banana to each student and have students go up to the table one by one, adding the ingredients they like to their banana split.
Encourage them to try the fruits, especially if they haven’t tried one before!

Closing ritual:
Have them wait for everyone to get their food and sit down before they eat (5 minutes)
Before they taste it, have them say “Thank you gardeners, Thank you Cooks” 
As they’re eating, ask students to describe what they’re tasting. Is there anything else you would add to make it better? How could you make it look more beautiful next time? What colors do you think this recipe is missing?
You did a great job trying new things, taking turns and working together! Without everyone’s help it wouldn’t have tasted so delicious! Did you have fun working together? 
Pass out recipes to take home.

Materials: 
Bananas
Blueberries
Grapes
Kiwi
Strawberries
Vanilla Yogurt
Granola with fruit and nuts
Maraschino Cherries
Knives
Spoons
Cutting Boards
Plates
Bowls
Measuring cups and spoons
Hand Sanitizer
Recipe Sheets
Coloring Pages
Word Searches
Task Card Picture Instructions
Breakfast Banana Splits Pictures

Prep work and setup:
Cut up fruits into manageable pieces.
Open and put yogurt and granola into bowls.
Print out recipes, pictures, task card picture instructions, handouts, coloring sheets and word searches 

Anticipated problems:
Some students may not want to eat fruits or eat them all mixed up together. Encourage them to try something new, even if it's separately on their plate.
Some students might have made this recipe with ice cream/fudge/sprinkles and ask for those toppings. Remind them that this is a different version of the recipe and these are the ingredients we have now, but they can make it with other ingredients at home, especially if they are making a dessert instead of a breakfast dish.

Questions for students/extension activities:

Breakfast banana splits word searches

All Grades

Shepherd Salad

Summary:  Students will learn how to make Shepherd Salad. Students will explore the bounty of late summer harvest and will get introduced to our year’s theme of exploring international food traditions. 

Objectives: Students will be able to behave safely in the kitchen by following the kitchen rules including safe knife skills. Students will be able to share a favorite food that they have made or eaten recently. 

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Welcome back to the kitchen! 
Ask students about their summer food experiences; what they ate, a new recipe they tried, if they cooked a meal for themselves, etc.
Review what is growing in the garden right now by asking students for ideas.
Introduce the first recipe of the year: Shepherd Salad from the Middle East. Introduce our garden ingredients.
Engaging activity:
Chopping Table: Review knife skills. Cut parsley, green onions, tomatoes, and cucumbers.
Bear claw: curl the fingertips of the hand not holding the knife so that they are safe. This hand will hold the food still while we chop.
Sawing motion: we move the blade back and forth, not up and down. There should be no chopping sounds, only quiet sawing back and forth.
Knife’s home: is at the top of the cutting board. It goes here whenever we are not cutting.
3-5th grade only: Chiffonade with parsley: stack two leaves neatly. Roll the stack into a caterpillar shape. Cut thin slices out of the roll. These turn into ribbons.
Middle Table activity: K- 2nd
Each student gets a paper plate. The teacher uses the talking points guide to instruct the students to draw and label a special, delicious food that they eat in their family. Students will have a few options to choose from. 
Option 1: Draw and label a special delicious food that they eat with their families
Option 2: Draw and label their favorite food that they made from last year
Option 3: Draw and label a food they would like to make during kitchen class
3rd-5th Middle Table Activity
Each student gets a paper plate. The teacher uses the talking points guide to instruct the students to pick a fruit or vegetable that grows in the garden that they want to cook with. Students will draw and label a recipe or dish they could make with that fruit or vegetable. 
Seasoning/ Dressing Table:
Distribute task cards (Pre-K through 3rd) or recipes (4-5th). Have each student identify the ingredients and tools necessary for their task on the table.
Mix it, Mix it, Mix it! Sing the Mix it song to combine ingredients.

Closing ritual:
Clean up, set the table, eat together, clear tables.
Ask for suggestions on how students would make the recipe different next time.
Review the kitchen pledge, ask about student’s paper plate drawings.
Share shout-outs before lining up.

Materials: 
Recipe ingredients
World map
Half sheet trays
3 large bowls
5 cutting boards
5 knives, either lettuce knives or paring knives
7-10 pairs safety scissors
3 citrus reamers for lemon
3 strainers (for lemon)
3 small bowls
3 whisks
serving spoons
Measuring cups and spoons
3 ramekins, for salt
3 fine graters, for garlic
paper plates, one per student, for middle table activity
colored pencils, crayons, markers
flags from the Middle East

Prep work and setup:
Cut tomatoes, onions, and cucumbers into strips for each tray.

Anticipated problems: None.

Background information for instructor: None.

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 
Read The Ugly Vegetables with younger grades. You can find neat activities to do from Grace Lin, the author of The Ugly Vegetables here :
http://www.gracelin.com/content.php?page=book_uglyveg&display=activities

Back pocket activities:
Pair and share foods from students’ paper plates

Task cards or handouts used: 
Middle table talking points for younger elementary and older elementary
Take home recipe
Yogurt task cards

1st Grade

Our Garden Community


Summary: Students will read a story about the members of a garden community, then make their own illustrations about what plants, animals, and people are part of a garden community.

Objectives: Students will be able to name plants, animals, and people that are part of a garden community and describe how they interact with one another. 

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Begin by introducing or reintroducing the word “community:” a group of people that work together. You may think of an example of another type of community, such as a school, and its members. Together with students, identify the people who are members of a school community and what jobs they do (i.e. the nurse takes care of sick kids, the students come to learn, the custodians clean the school building, etc.).
Explain that like a school or other kind of community, a garden is a community, too, in which plants, animals, and people work together. Explain that today in class, we will be reading a story about a garden community, and they will each act as members of the garden community. 

Engaging activity:
Pass out necklaces to students with names of plants, animals, and people in the garden community. Have students practice reading their necklaces. During the story, ask each student to listen for the name of their character and to do a special movement or dance when they hear their character’s name.
Read the story, helping students to listen for their character and highlighting the role of each character in the story. 
Collect the necklaces. Have students make their own illustration of a garden community, including a plant, an animal, and a bug. They can use the book for reference or can add other characters that weren’t included in the book. 

Closing ritual:
Have students share about what plants, animals, bugs, and people were in their garden community picture.
Finish with a tasting.

Materials:
Frankie and the Garden book
Necklaces with story characters	
Coloring pages 
Colored pencils
Tasting

Prep work and setup: None. 

Anticipated problems: None.

Background information for instructor: None.

Questions for students/extension activities:
This lesson makes sense as part of a larger unit on communities, but could be used as a stand-alone lesson as well. 

Back pocket activities: None. 

Task cards or handouts used: See above.

body:
Grade PK-5
Season Fall/ Spring
Indicate: Indoor/Outdoor
Indicate: Year 1


Paint our Garden


Summary: In the garden, students will observe and paint what they see. They will be introduced to different art styles and use their imaginations. This lesson can also be done indoors with a colorful plant or print out.

Objectives: Students will be able to represent components of our garden in their own way. Students will be able to explain what part of the garden they are painting.

Core Competencies: Garden skills and related academic, social-emotional learning.

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson reshapes curriculum by encouraging students to use their imaginations independently as they make paintings of the garden around them. 

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson encourages students to show self-confidence in their painting abilities, knowing that their imagination and how they feel are most important. This lesson encourages responsible decision-making as students use a material that will be messy if used improperly. 

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual (15 minutes):
PK-1
Greet students in their room, sing good morning gardeners
Today we are going to read a very special story about a friend of the garden called “The Very Hungry Caterpillar”(can substitute a relevant book about school garden). After we read our story, we're all going to put our artist hats on, because we’re going to be making paintings of our friend the caterpillar outside in the garden where caterpillars live!
2-5
Welcome students to class, explain plan for the day
Substitute reading for food web activity
Give each student a food chain card necklace. Have students look at their cards. Explain that this is who they will represent in the food chain. Ask students to read who eats them and who they eat, then to put on the necklace when they are finished reading. 
Model with another student how to read what is on the card and then decide on who else in the food web they are connected to. Model rolling the ball of yarn across to make that connection. Model how to hold the string and then pass it to the next student in the food web. 
Start with one student, holding the string. Have the student name another animal or plant that they are connected to. Unwind the string and connect the next student, starting to form the web. Facilitate moving the ball of string in the group until all students are included in the web. 
Now that the food web is complete, demonstrate what happens when a pesticide is introduced. Have the affected animals drop their section of the string, then have students who were connected to those animals drop their strings, until the entire web has fallen apart. “Even if just one or a few part(s) of the web are impacted, it hurts all animals, plants, and people involved.” 
Engaging activity (20 minutes):
Now we’re going to be painting our garden that is our part of the food web. 
Start by putting our names on the paper 
Then flip over
Show examples of different styles of art
Explain that students will look at their surroundings and paint what they see in our garden
Reminder to be respectful of each other and our materials
It's okay if you get a little paint on the table by accident- that's why we have the table cloths. But, it's not okay to paint on the table on purpose. We only paint on our papers.
Circulate and ask students to explain what they’re painting and give compliments on creativity/ other observations

Closing ritual with SEL component (10 minutes):
Wash hands if needed
How did you feel when you were painting?
This is your garden, do you feel proud making a painting of it?
You can show a friend or someone at home your own creative work, and the garden you take care of.











Materials: 

Food web necklaces


Prep work and setup:
Set up tables, table cloths, paint brushes outside
Weigh down table cloths if windy
Pre-distribute paint to pallets for easy transition

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
Needs good weather, but can easily be moved indoors with a physical example from greenhouse, printed picture, or slideshow. 

Back pocket activities:

February/March in the Kitchen: Dumplings


Summary: Students will make dumplings, learning how to properly fold them closed with the filling.

Objectives: Students will be able to fold dumplings, and saute vegetables, learning what the word “saute” is.

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Students will come in and we will talk about China and pocket foods. Show students where on the map China is and if anyone can name any other “pocket foods” which are any foods that have a filling or are stuffed. 

Engaging activity:
Cucumber table
 All students will work with age-appropriate knives to dice cabbage and mushrooms, 3rd - 5th grade will practice using a box grater to grate carrots using a protective glove; younger students will use scissors to cut ribbons of carrots and chives. The concept of sauteing will be reintroduced to all students. .
Carrot Table
 Students will be given a wonton wrapper and stuffing. They will each be allowed to assemble two dumplings. 3rd - 5th Grade will be given square wontons to create more elaborate dumplings; K - 2nd Grade will be given circular wontons to make semi-circle dumplings.  Students will then be presented with their very own chopsticks which they will use to practice picking up cotton balls. 
Corn Table
At the sauce making station, teachers will use this sauce making activity to reinforce measuring skills. Students will be given a placemat where they have to identify certain measuring utensils through common abbreviations. With extra time, we will informally quiz students to find the correct tool. After matching the tools to the abbreviations, students will use the correct tools to measure sauce.

Closing ritual:
Set the table, Eat, clean up as a class, shout outs if possible.




Materials needed:
Age appropriate knives (plastic lettuce knives or paring knives) and scissors 
Cutting boards
Pan for sauteing
zester 
4-5 cutting boards
1 induction burner
1 stirring utensil
Ramekins with water (4)
Tray for finished dumplings
Chopsticks
Cotton balls
Measuring spoons (TBSP and tsp of various sizes)
Measuring cups (for matching game)
Worksheets with measuring activities 

Prep work and setup:
Cut the first set of vegetables for the first class’s first rotation.

Anticipated problems:
Dumplings may stick to the steamers so make sure students make a few extra ones in case some of them rip open.

Background information for instructor: None

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities:
Culture box exploration

Task cards or handouts used: 
Sauce Task Cards
Dumpling and chopsticks activity

Summer Scavenger Hunt

Summary: Students will be welcomed to ESYNYC programming, go over garden expectations and get introduced to what is growing in the garden this summer. 

Objectives: Students will do an engaging group-based activity. Students will work as teams to complete a scavenger hunt in the garden. Students will learn more about their classmates through scavenger hunt prompts.

Core Competencies: 
Environmental Stewardship: students will do garden jobs as part of a scavenger hunt
SEL: students will have to ask questions and share with a partner
Garden Skills: students will do different garden jobs (incl. watering and weeding)

Cultural Responsiveness: 
Communicates high expectations: students must complete a scavenger hunt and perform different garden jobs
Positions teacher as facilitator: less teacher talk - students move through the garden independently
Reshapes curriculum: incorporating group work and movement

Social-Emotional Skills: 
Working well in groups - students will work together to complete the scavenger hunt
Students take small risks - try new things in the garden
Responsible decision making - students use tools properly and safely
Students express joy while working outside in the garden

Agenda/Class Flow: (45 mins)
Opening ritual: (10 mins)
Welcome to the garden at PS 109! (5 minutes)
Do a pulse check - how is everyone doing today?
Today we are going to go out to the garden to complete a scavenger hunt. This will give you an opportunity to explore the garden and learn more about your classmates.
Who has been to this garden before?  Or walked past it?  What have you seen growing  in the garden this past year?
Remind students of important rules for the garden. (5 minutes) 
Be Safe - we walk, not run, in the garden
Be Responsible - use your tools safely, leave the garden looking better than we found it (no tools left out, papers put away, no trash left behind)
Be Adventurous - try new things, participate!
Engaging activity (5-10 minutes): Facilitator will lead students in a quick garden tour.  Point out some exciting crops growing in the garden right now, where compost is, where trash goes, what’s inside shed, where watering cans are, etc.  

Engaging activity (20 minutes): Students will work together in pairs to complete a garden scavenger hunt. The prompts will be a combination of garden jobs and questions they must answer about their partner. 
Give scavenger hunt instructions:
Look around the garden for prompts, each emoji matches a line on your worksheet
Answer the question for the prompt on the correct line on your worksheet
If there is a task, do the task an put a check mark on the worksheet after you complete it
Students will have 15 minutes to go through the scavenger hunt. They should get to as many prompts as they can. 
Wrap up at the end - call out a few emojis and have students share answers (discussion)

Closing ritual: (5 minutes)
Questions for the class: Did you enjoy exploring the garden? What is one thing you learned about the garden or your scavenger hunt partner? 
Distribute tasting (remind about being adventurous, introduce don’t yuck my yum, and “thank you gardeners, thank you cooks!”)

Materials: 

Pencils
Clipboards
Markers for signage (washable if laminating after)
Watering cans, buckets for compost, twine and popsicle sticks to mark off area to weed
Garden staples or paint sticks and tape to attach scavenger hunt prompts

Prep work and setup:
Prepare tasting (seasonal fruit) - put in tasting cups 
Place scavenger hunt prompts throughout the garden
For watering prompt - fill up watering can/s
For compost prompt - get bucket of things to compost
Put scavenger hunt sheets on clipboards (one per pair of students)

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
Set timers for each activity to avoid going over time!

Ful Medames


Summary: Students will do a reading together about ful medames, then will cook and eat ful medames together as a tasting.


Objectives: Students will understand the origins of ful medames. 


Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Introduce today’s recipe.
This month we are making food from Egypt and we will be making a breakfast that people eat in Egypt all the time called ful medames. Locate where Egypt is on the map and show the class. This recipe is made with fava beans and we will be eating it with a bread that is also eaten with ful medames in Egypt. 

Engaging activity:
Carrot Table:
Mash beans, garlic, olive oil, herbs and seasoning and complete ful medames. 
Corn Table:
Students can either make the dough here or do the reading (see below) at this table with classroom teacher. 
Cucumber Table:

Closing ritual:
Clean up, set the table, eat together, clear tables.
Ask for suggestions on how students would make the recipe different next time.

Set up trays for small groups. Include all ingredients. Include microplanes for the garlic, juicers for the lemons, scissors for the parsley, and cup, tablespoon, and teaspoon measures. Include a mortar and pestle for each group.

Anticipated problems:

Tips and tricks:

Note that the portions on the task cards make a slightly smaller portion than listed on the recipe. The task cards are for a group of 8-10, with the assumption that you would have three groups working together to make enough for an entire class tasting.

body:
3K-7th GradeSpring
Indoor/Outdoor


Peach Sorbet

Summary: Students will learn about peaches and make a peach sorbet.

Objectives: Students will be able to explain certain characteristics of peaches and how to make a sorbet.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: 10-15 minutes
(Lower elementary and 3K): Fruit guessing game
Place fruit in a shoe box with holes so that students can feel each fruit and guess the fruit. [Alternative activity: provide pictures of different fruits and ask students to name the different fruits and their characteristics (color, taste, texture, etc.)]
Hand out slips of papers with numbers corresponding to the number of fruit boxes. 
Tell students to take turns walking around to each numbered shoe box and feel the fruit inside.
Have students spend a few moments at each shoe box to write down characteristics of each fruit (shape, texture, etc.) and to guess what fruit is inside. 
Once students have completed their guesses, ask them to name the characteristics of each fruit and what they think it is. Open the boxes to reveal what’s inside.
Discuss peach characteristics and What Am I? facts. 
(Upper elementary and MS students) What Am I?
Pass out each of the What Am I? clues to various students in the class.
In numerical order, ask each student to read their clue out loud.
After each clue has been read, pause and let students guess what the clue is referring to. Write each of the guesses on the board after each clue has been read aloud.
After all of the clues have been read, ask students to look at each of the guesses on the board and decide on a final answer.
Reveal the correct answer to the class—A peach!

Engaging activity: 20-30 minutes
Make peach sorbet.
Ask students what sorbet is - A frozen dessert, also called an italian ice, made from fruit and sugar.
Students break into groups to make a batch of sorbet. (For younger students, make sorbet together as a group, allowing students to take turns adding ingredients to blender.)
Measure and combine ingredients in high-speed blender:
5 medium peaches, sliced with skin on (about 5 cups or 750 grams)
½ tablespoon lemon juice (or lime juice)
½ cup granulated sugar 
Puree all the ingredients in blender for 1-2 minutes on high until smooth and peaches are completely liquified. 
Pour mixture into a 9-inch loaf pan, a shallow dish, or muffin tins (for individual servings). Cover and freeze for 6-7 hours, until fully set.
(If time allows) Students make some whip cream to top sorbet:
Whip 3 cups of heavy cream, 2 tablespoons sugar, ½ tsp vanilla, and pinch of nutmeg or cinnamon until stiff peaks form.
Students clean their work stations: gather tools and rinse in sink or place in wash bucket; wipe down tables.

Closing ritual: 5-10 minutes
Plate pre-frozen sorbet onto plates and distribute to students
Students to add whipped cream and sliced fruit if available 
Tasting
If time allows: Anatomy of a Peach

Materials: 
Blender for each group
Tray set-up for each group:
Cutting board and knives, measuring spoons/cups, muffin tin
5 medium peaches (or 5 cups of frozen peaches)
½ lemon, juicer, and ramekin (to squeeze and measure ½ tablespoon lemon juice)
Granulated sugar 
Liquid measuring cups and heavy cream
Vanilla extract, nutmeg and/or cinnamon
Whisk and bowl for whipped cream
Recipe cards

Prep work and setup:
Pre-freeze a batch of sorbet
Print recipe cards

Anticipated problems:
Students will need help following recipe measurements.
Students may want to blurt out fruit answers before naming characteristics. Prompt with, how do we know it’s an apple; what does it feel like; what is its shape, etc.?

Background information for instructor:
Peaches: What’s All the Fuzz About?

Questions for students/extension activities: Anatomy of a Peach

Anatomy of a Peach

body:
6th Grade
Fall/Winter
Indoor


   Llapingachos 
    (Ecuadorian potato pancakes)

Summary: Students will make llapingachos (Ecuadorian potato pancakes) and learn about the childhood of their classmates.

Objectives: Students will be able to read and complete a recipe
                    Students will be able to understand that childhood is a part of someone’s biography.

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: Today we are going to continue your biography unit. Last week you interviewed each other with questions to learn about one another’s childhoods and what kinds of foods you grew up eating. We also made mangu which is a dish from the Dominican Republic that many of you have been eating since you were very small. Today we are going to make llapingachos, which are little pancakes made from potatoes that come from Ecuador. 

Engaging activity: 
Students will work together to complete the llapingachos recipe.
Each student should assemble at least 1 potato pancake
Have a group make the sauce

Student writing activity:
After completing the recipe, students should sit down to create a food memory from their own childhood
Using the llapingachos, students should verbally describe what they see, hear, taste, smell, and touch to practice using sensory details
With Ms. Sterling and Ms. Paloma, students will read through Ms. Sterling’s example of a childhood sensory food memory. 
BRAIN STORM (graphic organizer)
See    	       big piles, happy people, shiny red sauce
Hear           laughing loud,  dad yelling
Taste          salty and sweet
Smell          warm bread
Touch         crunchy crust

Ms. Sterling’s Childhood:
Spaghetti and Meatballs on Sunday

Feel
Lots of happy people were around the table. 
          Hear
I hear them laughing loud.  
I hear my dad yell across the table. 
       See
The spaghetti is in big piles.
I eat a big bite with shiny, red sauce. 

      Taste
    Smell
It tastes salty and sweet.


Then I smell the warm bread. 
  Touch

The crust is rough and hard.  I rip off a big bite and it is crunchy. 
         Closing Statement:   This is spaghetti on Sundays. 


Closing ritual: Now that we have all made and tried these foods from Ecuador and the DR think about how they connect to your personal stories. Do they bring you comfort? Do they help you think of home or your families? What stories did you write about?

body:
1st Grade
All SeasonsIndoor


Seed Sorting, Greater 	Than or Less Than

Summary: Students will sort seeds into groups based on their physical type and will determine which group has a greater quantity. 

Objectives: Students will be able to categorize objects, count them in each group, and determine which category has a greater quantity using symbols >, <, and =. 

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Start out by asking students what they already know about seeds.
Explain to students that we are going to get to explore and see some new seeds today, but also that we will be practicing using the following math symbols. Draw the <, >, and = symbols on the board and ask students to explain to you how they are used.

Engaging activity:
Demonstrate to students how they will do the activity. 
Each student will get a sorting paper with two sides, as well as a cup with seeds. They should sort the seeds that they get onto the paper, putting one kind of seeds on one side and the other kind on the other. 
Then on each side they will count the number of seeds and write that in the box. Finally, they will decide what symbol to write in the center: >, <, or =. 
After they are done, they can put their seeds back into the cup and trade with another student to get some new seeds. 
Give out materials and have students do the activity, circulating to help students as needed.

Closing ritual:
Clean up materials. Ask students to describe what seeds they saw during the activity: colors, shape, textures, names. 
End with a tasting, preferably of seeds.

Materials:
Different varieties of seeds
Cups
Pencils or dry erase markers
Dry erasers (if using dry erase worksheets with students), 
Seed sorting worksheets for each student
Tasting

Prep work and setup:
Sort seeds into cups, with two types of seeds per cup. Make sure there is enough for one for each student, along with some extras. 
You can either give students a new copy of the seed sorting worksheet for each cup that they sort, or you can laminate the worksheets so that they can erase them and reuse them. 

Anticipated problems:
The activity will be more engaging and will last for longer if you offer many kinds of seeds for students to observe in the cups. Larger seeds are easier for students to handle and less likely to be lost. Some ideas include: multicolored corn, rice, different lentil varieties, peas, soybeans, sunflower seeds, pumpkin seeds, peach pits, different bean varieties, okra seeds, and large flower seeds such as four o’clocks. 

Background information for instructor:
This lesson plan does not introduce the concepts of greater than, less than, or equal to. It is meant to be taught after students have already been introduced to and practiced this skill in math class. 

Questions for students/extension activities:
Students can continually use this concept in garden or kitchen class, such when seeing which garden bed has more plants or when comparing quantities in a recipe.  

Back pocket activities: None. 

Task cards or handouts used: See above. 

Photos: None.

body:
3. Indoor Sprout Salad

Note: This is the third of three lessons, taking place on day ten. Teacher instructions here and here.

Summary: Students harvest their soil sprouts, and make and eat a salad.

Objectives: Students will be able to harvest sprouts, make salad dressing, and understand how their soil sprouts went from seed to table.

Core Competencies: Garden skills and related academic content. Kitchen skills and related academic content.

Cultural Responsiveness: Promotes student-centered instruction by giving students a hands-on way to engage with life sciences.

Social-Emotional Skills: Students will practice self-management by learning new garden and kitchen skills and showing care to their plants and other ingredients.

Agenda/Class Flow: 
Opening ritual with SEL component:
Tell students that ten days ago they planted seeds that they have been observing and caring for, and that today they get to harvest and eat their sprouts.
Tell students that at the end of this period, they will have mastered the important act of bringing food all the way from seed to table!

Engaging activity:
Distribute scissors and have students gently harvest their soil sprouts by cutting them at the base, near the soil.
Some students can collect the sprouts in the salad spinner and wash and dry them while other students get started on the salad. Once sprouts are washed and dried, they can be redistributed so that each small group of students has roughly the same amount of sprouts
In small groups, have some students cut the sprouts into smaller pieces with the scissors–about ¼-½ inch.
Other students can make the dressing.
When everyone is done, collect all the sprouts in a large bowl and supplement with other salad greens as needed.
Collect all the dressing. Make sure it is well stirred or shaken and add it to salad a little at a time, tossing the salad, until salad is lightly coated. Toss well.
Distribute salad to students asking them to wait until everyone is served.
Remind students not to “yuck” other people’s “yum.” It’s fine if they don’t like the salad, but they should keep their comments polite.
When everyone has their salad, ask students to join you in saying, “Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks,” and then everyone tastes their salad.

Closing ritual with SEL component:
Tell students that it’s good to think about the people who bring our food to us. That’s why saying, “Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks,” is a nice thing to do before you eat.
Ask students who the gardeners and cooks were for this salad. It was them, of course! Congratulate them on mastering the important art of growing and preparing food.

Materials: 
Soil sprouts from previous lessons
Scissors to harvest sprouts and cut them into smaller pieces–one per student or they can share
Salad spinner to wash and dry sprouts
Dressing recipe–one for every small group of students
Dressing ingredients:
Olive oil
Honey 
Tomato paste
Apple cider vinegar 
Dijon mustard 
Water 
Garlic
Salt 
Pepper 
Additional prewashed salad greens
Tablespoons and ½ Teaspoons–enough for every small group of students
One large bowl to serve salad.
One medium bowl for every small group of students to collect sprouts.
One jar or other container for every small group of students–to make dressing
Bowls or plates to eat salad–one per student
Forks to eat salad–one per student

Prep work and setup:
Get all of your ingredients and equipment organized and ready to distribute to small groups of students.








Adapted from Year-Round Indoor Salad Gardening by Peter Burke.

Varenyky

Summary:  Students will learn about Ukraine and vareniki and will discuss the similarities of pocket foods found in the cultures.

Objectives: Students will be able to compare and contrast different pocket foods from around the world. Students will be able to finely chop vegetables and fold dumplings. 

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Introduce today’s recipe.
Show the world map and ask students to identify Ukraine. If students volunteer, have them share about food in Ukraine. If not many students volunteer to talk about Ukraine, we will then prompt a conversation about pocket foods (dumplings), such as the pupusas made during the previous lesson. With this conversation in mind, students will be prepared to make comparisons in food cultures regarding pocket foods. 

Engaging activity:
Cucumber table: Making the dough. 
Students will make the dough for varenyky, knead it, and roll it out into a thin layer. 
Once the layer is thin, the students will cut out circles and place them on a floured pan.
Corn table: Assembling dumplings. 
With their classroom teachers, students will use the cut out circles of dough and assemble the varenyky to be cooked. 
Each student will be responsible for making 3 - 5 varenyky each. 
The finished varenyky will be placed on a floured sheet. 
Carrot table: Making the filling. 
Students will make the filling for varenyky by cutting and cooking onion and mushroom until the onion is caramelized. Once the onion and mushrooms are cooked, they will be added to mashed potatoes and mixed. This filling will be given to the assembly station. 

Closing ritual:
Clean up, set the table, eat together, clear tables.
Ask for suggestions on how students would make the recipe different next time.
Share shout-outs before lining up.

Materials: 
Map
Recipe ingredients
Large pot with lid
Mesh strainer
Circle cutter (water cups)
Potato masher
Bowls
Stove
Frying pan

Prep work and setup:
Make the first batch of filling, dough, and cut out dough circles for the first class.

Anticipated problems:
Some vareniki will explode in the boiling water, so make sure each student makes at least 3 so that there are plenty for the tasting.
Dust flour between the dough circles when transferring them to the next station

Background information for instructor: None.

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities:
Discuss whole wheat vs. all purpose flour.
Explore dumplings around the world.

Task cards or handouts used: 
Dumplings around the world cards (laminate)
Take home recipe
