Kitchen Measurements

Summary: Students will learn the differences between different kitchen measurements, learn how to properly measure using commonly used tools and make a recipe.

Objectives: Students will be able to identify various kitchen tools we will use regularly. 

Core Competencies:
Kitchen skills and related academic content - students will learn how to measure using the correct tools

Cultural Responsiveness: 
Communicates high expectations - students are expected to complete a task within a certain amount of time
Reshapes curriculum - incorporates group work 
Positions teacher as facilitator - students will work in small independent groups

Social-Emotional Skills: 
Relationship skills - students will work together in groups
Relationship skills - students will make connections to outside life (items they have/use at home)
Responsible decision-making - students will learn about using tools safely

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual:
A few weeks ago we played a game where you all became familiar with many of the tools we will use a lot in the Green Room. Today we are going to talk more about the tools we use for measuring and then prepare a recipe. Part of our goal for today is help you all learn and master a skill that is important for whenever you are cooking and that you will use outside school.
We will be making granola, a breakfast food that’s healthy for you and the planet. One of the things that makes it healthy for you is that it has whole grains, seeds and fruits in it, all of which give you energy. It also has much less sugar than most breakfast cereals you buy at the store.
One thing that makes it healthy for the planet is that we are eating lower on the food chain, so these foods take less energy and produce less carbon when you grow them.

Engaging activity:
Go over the following tools:
Dry measuring cups: what are some ingredients we would use this for?
Demonstrate how to properly measure 1 cup of rice - make sure you level!
Liquid measuring cup: : what are some ingredients we would use this for?
Demonstrate how to properly measure 1 cup of water - get at eye level to check you have the right amount 
Pay attention to what side you are looking at (cups vs. oz vs. mL)
Measuring spoons: : what are some ingredients we would use this for?
Demonstrate how to properly measure 1 tablespoon of flour - make sure you level
Explain common abbreviations - tsp = teaspoon, tbs/tbsp = tablespoon
Scale: what are some ingredients we would use this for?
Explain - we usually use for baking and it is the most accurate way to measure
Have students prepare a recipe for granola
Split into 3 or 4 groups
Wash hands
Reminders for the day: pay attention to the recipe, use teamwork, participate!
While granola is in the oven, have students work in pairs to complete the measurement worksheet
Say thank yous and have a tasting!

Closing ritual:
End of class discussion questions
What did you think of today’s recipe?
Do you feel confident that you would know how to use the right tools going forward?
Give a thumbs up if you feel confident that you used the right tools today/
Give a thumbs up if you feel confident that you will know the correct tools to use in the future.

Materials: 
Measuring cups - dry and liquid
Measuring spoons
Scale

Prep work and setup:
Preheat the oven
Set up trays of ingredients
Take out tools/supplies for 

Anticipated problems:
Granola may not be ready on time - bring to them at lunch; later classes can taste granola from prior classes

Questions for students/extension activities: 

Back pocket activities: none. 

Task cards or handouts used: 
Measurement worksheet
Recipe

  Green Room Survival Guide

Summary: Students will work together to make a soup and create a guide of Green Room tips for new ENL students. 

Objectives: Students will be able to explain the importance of having class in the Green Room and list rules, tools and other helpful information.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: Has anyone heard of a survival guide? If not, could someone tell us what it means to survive? A survival guide is something that gives us tips, tricks and other information that can help us do something. Today we are going to create a survival guide for the Green Room, to help teach future Evergreen students all about the Green Room. We are also going to make soup, which is a food a lot of people eat for survival because they are able to use whatever leftover vegetable scraps that are available.  

Engaging activity: Students will use a recipe to prepare creamy butternut squash and sweet potato soup. As the soup is blended and simmers, students will work in groups to complete a Green Room Survival Guide. 

Closing ritual: The class will eat their soup together and go over the answers they wrote on their worksheet in their groups.

2nd GradeFall/Spring
Outdoor


 Measurement Hunt 
 
Summary: Students go on a scavenger hunt to measure and compare the space between plants in the garden.

Objectives: Students will understand why plants need space and why different plants need different amounts of space. Students will practice using rulers. 

Cultural Responsiveness: reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Tell students we’re going to practice using rulers in the garden today.
Hand rulers out to students and ask them to find the side with inches and the side with centimeters. 
Draw a leaf up on the whiteboard. Have a student volunteer demonstrate how to measure how long the leaf is. 
Draw two plants up on the board. Have a student volunteer demonstrate how to measure the distance between the two plants. 
Engaging activity:
Pass out the Measurement Hunt worksheets. Read through the questions on the paper as a whole class. 
Explain that the shapes on the paper will match signs out in the garden. For example, when they find the heart sign in the garden, it means they should stop and measure the plant that is next to the heart sign. Then they write their answer down next to the heart shape on their paper. 
Send students out into the garden to complete the Measurement Hunt. Circulate to help students as they work. 
Closing ritual:
Have students return to the circle. Have them share out a few of their answers. 
Discuss what we learned from this hunt. Do all plants need the same amount of space? How do you know?
Finish with a tasting. 

Materials: 
Dry erase board and markers
Pencils
Clipboards
Rulers
Measurement Hunt worksheet 
Signs with shapes matching the scavenger hunt worksheets
Seasonal tasting

Prep work and setup:
Put scavenger hunt shape signs out in the garden. 
Print worksheets and put them on clipboards. 

Anticipated problems:
Circulate to make sure students are using the inches side of their ruler. 

Background information for instructor: None. 

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities: None. 

Task cards or handouts used: 
Here is an alternative version of a worksheet for the measurement hunt.

  	Go, Grow, Glow Grain Salad

Summary: Students will review how different types of foods can help our bodies and work together to make a salad using rice, beans, vegetables, and a cilantro-lime dressing

Objectives: students will be able to identify ways that different foods can help our bodies. Students will be able to work together to follow a recipe for a salad

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self reflection on team work

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
1. Review previous lesson on “Go, Grow, Glow” - ask students if they remember what these terms mean and call out a few examples and have students show the corresponding movement for Go, Grow, or Glow 
Go foods have carbohydrates to give us energy (bread, rice, grains)
Movement can be running in place or another quick move
Grow foods have protein to build muscle (protein-rich foods like meat, beans/legumes)
Movement can be flexing arm muscles
Glow foods are plants with nutrients to keep us healthy (fruits and vegetables)
Movement can be smiling and wiggling fingers by face like you are glowing
2. Introduce recipe: Today we will make a salad that has ingredients to help us go, grow, and glow!
3. Have students read the recipe in groups and try to identify the “go, grow, and glow” ingredients (Black beans are the Grow, rice is the Go, the rest are Glow)
4. Have students choose role cards in their groups to transition into the recipe

Engaging activity:
1. Students work together to chop vegetables (carrots, broccoli, bell peppers) and make the cilantro-lime vinaigrette, following the recipe.

Closing ritual:
Clean up areas and serve bowls within each table group
Taste together 
Presenters in each group share about how their team worked together and how their recipe came out
Reflection questions:
Point out ingredients and have students identify how each helps our body
What are other examples of grains you eat at home?
What other vegetables would you like to put in a grain salad?
Why do you think it’s important to eat a variety of food groups?


Materials:
Trays set up with:
Tray Prep List
Vegetables (carrots, broccoli, bell peppers)
Rice and beans in a bowl 
Pincher cups of coriander, salt, lime, cilantro, olive oil
Knives and cutting boards
Mixing spoon
Laminated recipes

Pre-cook rice
Cut vegetables into manageable portions, limes into wedges
Set up trays
print/laminate recipes for each group
Write recipe and voting chart on whiteboard

Background information for instructor:


Back pocket activities:
Students can create their own “Go, Grow, Glow” meal by writing or drawing a recipe that includes each component

Winter

Beet Ketchup

Summary: Students will learn about the importance of a root vegetable as a storage crop, they will practice knife skills by preparing a group recipe of Beet Ketchup.

Objectives: Students will be able to identify a root vegetable. Students will read and follow the recipe to learn how to make a ketchup using beets.

Cultural Responsiveness:[pick one or more] promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: [pick one or more]self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Introduce the Beet - explain that they will be making Beet Ketchup
For younger grades, you could have the students guess the name of the vegetable. Pass around the beet to students (or show it on a projector) so that they can see the root hairs on the beet. Explain that beets grow underneath the soil, and greens grow off the top (if you have these you can pass this around too)
Explain why root vegetables are an important vegetable at this time of year. Ask students what season it is (prompt a response for winter - no tomatoes are growing in the garden right now).Talk about storage crops, and how farmers depend on these crops during the winter. Root Cellar - this slideshow is helpful for photos of a root cellar if you wish to discuss how they are stored.

Engaging activity:
Read the Beet Ketchup Recipe together as a class (either handouts or written on the board)
Demonstrate how the onion should be cut, then have the students cut their piece of onion
Demonstrate how the cooked beets should be cut, then have the students cut their piece of beet and collect what they cut in the pot. 
Delegate remaining ingredients from the beet ketchup recipe to each student to then measure and put in the pot
Put the beet ketchup on the burner to cook
While beet ketchup is cooking, have students clean up (cutting board, knives, measuring utensils, etc.)
Older grades can write their own recipe card if time allows
Younger grades can complete a fill in the blank recipe card
When ketchup is ready, blend and season
Prepare tasting cups with beet ketchup and pre-prepared potatoes (students can help here too)

Closing ritual:
Ask students why root vegetables like beets are an important crop in the winter 
Ask students to name more examples of root vegetables
Pass out tasting cups 

Materials: 
Blender
Cutting mats
Plastic Knives
Liquid Measuring Cup
Tablespoon
Tasting cups
2 bowls
Pot
Induction burner

Prep work and setup:
Roast potatoes right before class
Cut potatoes into medium sized pieces, place into a bowl
Drizzle a small amount of olive oil over potatoes and toss the potatoes in the bowl so that all sides are evenly oiled
Season potatoes with salt, pepper, garlic powder, and paprika - toss again so that seasonings are evenly dispersed
Place potatoes on a tray
Roast in the oven at 425 until golden on the outsides and cooked through the center (about 30 minutes)
Roast & peel beets
Prepare pieces of onion & beet for each student to cut

Anticipated problems:
Making sure that there’s enough time to get through everything
Students having sensitivity to chopping onions
Students having sensitivity to the beet juice getting on their hands (needing to wash hands immediately after cutting beets because their hands are red)

Background information for instructor:
Beets can be roasted & peeled the day before if you have an early class
Students will produce more chopped onions than what is needed for the recipe so make sure to have a plan to use those cut onions that are leftover

Questions for students/extension activities: Compare label & ingredients of store bought ketchup vs ingredients in beet ketchup, tie in to another lesson about seasonality/winter in the garden


Back pocket activities:
Arts & crafts with beet juice?

Task cards or handouts used: 
Beet Ketchup Recipe

   Descriptive Language

Summary: Students will make a dish and practice using sensory details to create descriptive language. 

Objectives: Students will be able to follow a recipe.
                    Students will be able to describe the food they prepared using their senses.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: You all have been starting a unit on informational writing. Is informational writing fiction or nonfiction? Informal writing is nonfiction, which means that it is something that is true.  In order to give people information through writing, you must be able to describe what you are writing about. A great way to be descriptive is by using your 5 senses! By writing what you see, hear, feel, taste and smell helps describe it for others. Today we are going to practice this through cooking mangu. While you are cooking and eating write down your sensory observations in your note-catcher. 

Engaging activity: Students will split into 3 groups to make the different parts of their mangu 
Mangu - mashing boiled plantains
Pickled onions
Fried Cheese
Fried Eggs (optional 4th group if needed)
As students are eating their meal, students will complete their sensory details note catcher 

Closing ritual: Ask students to give 1 example of what they wrote in their note-catchers.

Sensory note catchers

           Food Logo Lesson

Summary: Students will identify different fast food logos and fruits and vegetables to understand the impact of advertising.

Objectives: Students will be able to explain why advertising is very important and why are some more common to them than others. 

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations,promotes student-centered instruction, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Today we are going to start talking about different food issues in our community. We are going to start by talking about food advertisements and logos.
Does everyone know what a logo is? Talk a bit about what logos are and what logos do? What about logos for food? What are the types of logos we see? 

Engaging activity:
You all are going to play a game where you will have to identify different food logos as well as different fruits and vegetables. 
Use your worksheet to write down your answers, you will have 15 minutes to do this.
Go over what students wrote on their worksheets
Was it easier to identify the food logos or the fruits/veggies?
Why do you think it was easier to identify the logos? 
Why do you think the fruits/veggies were more challenging than the logos?
Do you think people would eat more fruits/vegetables if there were logos and advertising for them? 
Other possible discussion questions: 
What do you think is the purpose of this activity?
Where do we see these logos? 
Does it make you want these products? What do we know about these products? Are they good for you? 
Closing ritual:
Give students a serving of popcorn and take turns going to the seasoning station.
Students will clean up and enjoy their snack together.

Worksheet

   Civil War Era Food

Summary: Students will prepare a recipe inspired by what was eaten during the Civil War.

Objectives: Students will be able to discuss and understand why certain foods were common during the Civil War Era and prepare a recipe. 

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: Ask students to take some guesses about what kind of food was eaten during the Civil War. After hearing some examples, ask students if they can think of why certain foods would be popular or common during this time period. Discuss farming, available crops. Talk about foods that would be good for soldiers, taking into account war conditions and food that provide bodies with strength and energy. Discuss rationing of certain ingredients/products for families during this time. 

Engaging activity: Students will work together to prepare the provided recipe (either drop biscuits or cornbread, greens and beans).

Closing ritual: While the class is tasting the recipe, ask students to put themselves in that time period. Do they think that this food would keep them full for a long time if they would have to wait a long time for their next meal? Do they think if they only had a small amount of ingredients to work with, that this meal made those ingredients last for a longer period of time? 


Materials:

cutting boards

Oven

Graters

   Thermal Energy Sort

Summary: Students will observe different food items and sort them based on how much thermal energy they have. 

Objectives: Students will be able to recognize that both hot and cold objects have thermal energy.
                    Students will be able to arrange objects based on how much thermal energy they have

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: We are going to continue talking about thermal energy. What do we already know about thermal energy? Do only hot items have thermal energy? No cold things can have thermal energy too! The only difference is that the molecules move slower whereas with hot items the molecules move really quickly. Today you all are going to an activity where you sort different foods based on how much thermal energy they have. 

Engaging activity: Each group will have a station set up at their table with 4 food items
Hot cocoa 2) Ice cube 3) Popsicle 4) Fresh Berries
With their index cards students will have to sort the items in order from Least thermal energy (1) to Most thermal energy (4) 

Closing ritual: Go over the correct answers to the activity. Ask students to explain why each item is ranked where. 

Materials:
Ice, popsicles
Hot cocoa
Berries
Index cards 

Prep work and setup (include photos if possible):
Freeze ice cubes
Make/freeze popsicles
Make hot chocolate 
Put berries (or other fruit) into bowls
Create index cards numbered 1-4 (one set for each group)

Garden on a Cracker

Summary: Students will discuss what can be found in a garden and assemble a “garden” on a cracker.

Objectives: Students will be able to work together to follow a recipe to make a simple bean dip and eat the dip with crackers and with veggie toppings.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Welcome students
Introduce yourself
Explain that we are going to be cooking something delicious and healthy.
Go over class expectations to be safe, responsible, respectful and adventurous. Give all students hand sanitizer and remind them how to be clean and safe with their bodies and the tools.

Go over plan for the day  (5 minutes)
Today we are going to cook a yummy recipe with black beans. Has anyone had black beans before?
Does anyone know where beans come from? (Seeds). Has anyone here ever planted a seed? What happens when you plant a seed in a garden? (It grows into a plant). What does a plant need to grow? (Soil, water and sun). 
We can eat some seeds, like beans, if we cook them. We also can eat plants, once they grow bigger and stronger and produce fruits and vegetables. Today our recipe is made with beans, which have protein in them and make us healthy and strong - some people eat meat for protein, but you can also eat vegetarian or plant-based foods, like beans, to get protein in your diet.
We are going to be making a recipe called “Garden on a Cracker,” a black bean dip that we will eat with crackers and veggie toppings. This recipe is called “Garden on a Cracker,” because the bean dip sort of looks like soil - they’re both brown/black. This is how we can remember that beans, which come from seeds, originally come from the soil, just like lots of other foods that we eat. 
Show students pictures of “Garden on a Cracker.”

Engaging activity:
Ingredients (5 minutes)
Show garden on a cracker task card picture instructions to review ingredients and instructions.
Safety and Materials (5 minutes)
Do a demo of handling knives and the masher tool.
Go over safety and the materials we will be using.
Pass out cutting boards, knives and measuring cups and spoons.
Follow the step by step instructions to make the black bean dip, or “soil” - measure and pour beans and spices into a bowl, mix it together and mash it up. Prepare veggie toppings - cut up tomatoes and green onions, and put the tomatoes, green onions and corn kernels into separate small serving bowls. 
Demonstrate each step first and then allow the students to each take a turn. 
Use the task cards!
You can also assign one task to each student or pair of students so they can all do different tasks at the same time.
Serve black bean dip, veggie toppings and a few crackers to each student.

Closing ritual:
Have them wait for everyone to get food before they eat (5 minutes)
Before they taste it, have them say “Thank you gardeners, Thank you Cooks” 
As they’re eating, ask students to describe what they’re tasting. Is there anything else you would add to make it better? How could we make it look more like a garden? (Add more colors, edible flowers, etc.) Remind students that if they make this again at home, they can use any other veggie toppings they like or have at home.
You did a great job trying new things, taking turns and working together! Without everyone’s help it wouldn’t have tasted so delicious! Did you have fun working together? 
Pass out recipes to take home.

Materials: 
Black Bean Cans
Cumin
Garlic Powder
Salt
Tomatoes
Green Onions
Corn Kernel Cans
Crackers
Mixing Bowl
Mixing Spoon
Knives
Spoons
Cutting Boards
Plates
Bowls
Masher
Measuring cups and spoons
Hand Sanitizer
Recipe Sheets
Coloring Pages
Word Searches
Task Card Picture Instructions
Garden on a Cracker pictures

Prep work and setup:
Open cans, drain out the liquid, and pour beans/corn into bowls.
Cut up vegetables into manageable pieces.
Measure out spices into bowls.
Print out recipes, pictures, task card picture instructions, handouts, coloring sheets and word searches 

Anticipated problems:
Remind students to mash up the beans carefully so that they don’t get the tables or themselves dirty.

Background information for instructor:

Questions for students/extension activities:

Soil Museum 


Summary: Students will read text to research the ways in which people today are conserving their soil and positively impacting themselves, their communities, and their environment.   

Objectives: Students will learn about how people around the world are working as stewards of the land to conserve the health of their soil. 

Cultural Responsiveness: reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, responsible decision-making, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
What does the word “conservation” mean? Define conservation on the board. 
Engaging activity:
We are going to be studying a Soil Museum today. We are going to be reading four stories about places in the world that were suffering from poor soil. As part of these stories, we are going to read about the efforts of people who are now working instead to conserve the soil – to keep it healthy and rich in nutrients.
Pass out clipboards with Soil Museum worksheets. Explain to students how you would like them to rotate through the stations in the Soil Museum, either independently or in groups. You may want them to read the questions for each station before going out to the Soil Museum. 
Let students circulate through the Soil Museum, researching from the readings and answering questions. 
Closing ritual:
Have students share out about what surprised them, what they learned, and about what people are doing now to protect soil health. 
Share a tasting. 

Materials: 
Dry erase board and markers
Soil Museum stations and worksheet 
Clipboard and pencils
Seasonal tasting

Prep work and setup:
Print out soil museum station papers, and hang them up around the room for a gallery walk.
Put soil museum worksheets on clipboards.

Anticipated problems: None. 

Background information for instructor: None. 

Questions for students/extension activities: 
Students may complete a garden job like cover cropping or composting to practice soil conservation in their school garden. 

Back pocket activities: None.

Task cards or handouts used: See above.

High School Indoor Spring African American Foodways: Black-Eyed Pea Salad Summary: Students will cook and eat black-eyed pea salad, an African American recipe with an ingredient that is Native to Northern Africa but was brought to the United States (through the transatlantic slave trade), and is now an important part of American farming and food traditions. Objectives: Students will be able to understand some of the ways that African American farmers and chefs have contributed to American food. They will be able to list ingredients, dishes, and cooking techniques that were innovated by African American farmers and cooks that have become an essential part of American cuisine. Students will be able to dice vegetables. Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness Agenda/Class Flow: Opening ritual: Explain that we are making food from America today, specifically highlighting foods and traditions invented and made popular by African American chefs. Explain that a lot of American food originates with Black Americans, just as a lot of American culture originates with Black Americans. Introduce today’s recipes- black-eyed pea salad. Explain that this is no-cook version of a dish called Hoppin’ John. Show pictures of black-eyed pea salad. Have students identify each ingredient in the recipe and then look at pictures of that ingredient chopped up. Engaging activity: Chop: celery, red onion, bell pepper. Make dressing. Toss together. Closing ritual: Clean up, set the table, “Thank you Gardeners, Thank you Cooks,” eat together, clear tables. Ask for suggestions on how students would make the recipe different next time. Share shout-outs before lining up. Materials: Pictures of Black-Eyed Pea Salad and Ingredients Recipes based on this recipe Back pocket activities: Watch video about how to make Hoppin’ Johns Black eyed peas coloring pages African American Farmers and Cooks reading Modern Black food and farming heros and worksheet Copyright Edible Schoolyard NYC. Sourdough & Fungi - K
 
Summary: Students will learn about fungi, then make sourdough & pasta.

Objectives: Students will begin to learn about fungi, and their edible uses!

Cultural Responsiveness: 
Promotes student-centered instruction
have students offer suggestions for recipes - students will build their own pasta bowl
Reshapes curriculum
Incorporating group work

Social-Emotional Skills: 
Self-management
Take small risks in trying new foods and using new tools.
Relationship skills
Willingly take turns and/or share tools
Working well in groups
Make connections to outside life
Responsible decision-making
Using tools safely
Cleaning up messes
Putting materials back where they belong
Self-awareness
Displays sense of pride or ownership	


Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual (10 minutes):
Thank students for joining, and have them wash hands before taking a seat. (5 minutes)
Introduce the activity - we’ll start with making sourdough to make bread next week, then we’ll cook up some pasta to enjoy together.

Engaging activity - Sourdough starter (5 minutes):
Explain that sourdough includes the ingredient yeast, which are suuuper tiny living things. This living ingredient helps our dough rise! We will also cook with a bigger fungus for our pasta recipe later: white button mushrooms.
Each instructor will make one sourdough starter with each table.

Engaging activity - Pasta (15 minutes):
Pasta should be cooked ahead of time.
Now, we’re going to make some pasta! Let’s get our toppings ready.
Students will chop vegetables:
Peppers
Broccoli
Mushrooms
One instructor will cook ingredients & set up the front table with a separate bowl for each ingredient (buffet style, like with the burritos).
In the meantime, the other instructor will read a story out loud to students.
Each student receives a scoop of pasta & can take turns letting the instructor know which ingredients they would like.
Enjoy!

Closing ritual (10 minutes):
Have students tidy up their space.
Stack materials into the dishwasher tray, wipe tables, and wash hands.
Thank students for their time, and remind students that we’ll be making our very own bread next week. We will also grow our own mushrooms next week!

Materials: 
Sourdough
Flour & water
Pasta
Pasta
Mushrooms, garlic, veggies, parmesan
Olive oil, salt, pepper, red pepper flakes
Tools
Cutting boards & knives
Hot plates & pans for each table
Quart containers for sourdough
Measuring cups (1 cup, ½ cup)

Prep work and setup:
Cook pasta ahead of time. 
Choose a storybook.

Anticipated problems:
Sourdough - contamination
Disinterest in sourdough? Maybe make sourdough croutons ahead of time, to eat with the pasta

Background information for instructor:
Recipe is found here.

Questions for students/extension activities: 
Explore pantry for additional spices for the pasta

Back pocket activities:
Mushroom spore prints

Pico de Gallo (Knife Skills)

Summary: Students will practice knife skills to make pico de gallo.

Objectives: Students will be able to use knives safely and effectively and identify different types of knife cuts. 

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Review previous class (setting agreements and expectations for the year)
Ask if any students have used a knife to cook before? Have them think-pair-share with a neighbor about what they cooked used the knife. Call on a few students to share out
Explain that we are going to practice our knife skills with a recipe that requires detailed cutting: Pico de Gallo
Salsa means sauce in spanish. It is a a mix of foods like tomatoes, onions, and peppers that can be blended (smooth), or cut into tiny pieces (chunky/pico de gallo)
Have students share ways that they have tried salsa before and what they ate it with
Introduce knife cuts: chopping and dicing
Use a visual aid to show chopping, dicing, and mincing knife cuts
Ask students to explain the difference between the cuts

Engaging activity:
Knife safety guidelines: how to hold a knife, what not to do with knives, bear claw and saw
Choose group roles & clean hands
In groups, students cut tomatoes, bell peppers, onions, cilantro; then squeeze lime juice and add salt in group mixing bowl. 
Pass out tortilla chips on plates and have students serve themselves some salsa
Taste it together and vote: tried it, liked it, loved it on a whiteboard

Closing ritual:
Clean up
Presenters share: how did it feel cooking as a team? What went well and what was difficult?
Reflection questions: 
What are ways that you practiced using knives safely today?
How confident do you feel in your knife skills (thumbs)? What is something you want to improve on?
What’s the difference between chopping and dicing? What other recipes might use dicing/chopping?
How would you describe the taste of this recipe? What would you add or change to make it even better? 
What foods can you eat salsa/tomatoes with?

Materials: 
Tray set-up:
Tray Prep list
Mixing bowl
Mixing spoon
Knives
Cutting boards
Tomato
Onion
Bell pepper
Lime wedges
Cilantro
Salt in pincher cup
Plates with tortilla chips for each student
Pictures of different knife cuts
Whole ingredients for display during intro 

Prep work and setup:
Cut tomato, onion, bell pepper into pieces
Cut limes into wedges
Set up whiteboard with knife cuts, recipe, and tried/liked/loved it 

Anticipated problems:
Leave onions in fridge to reduce crying

Background information for instructor:
Pico de gallo, also called salsa fresca or salsa cruda, is a type of salsa commonly used in Mexican cuisine. It is traditionally made from chopped tomato, onion, Serrano peppers, with salt, cumin, lime juice, and cilantro.

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities: None.

body:
3K-2nd GradeFall/Winter
Indoor


The Water Cycle 
  
Summary: Students will make a snow mosaic made from beans or seeds and/or a banana snowman, while touching on ideas of the water cycle and water in the form of snow.

Objectives: Students will celebrate winter and water with snowman theme activities.

Cultural Responsiveness: Communicates high expectations by encouraging students to create their own snowmen or mosaics.

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Talk about snow and what/how students play in snow.
For K-2, introduce the water cycle. Do a water cycle sing-along


Engaging activity:
Make banana snowman skewers using banana slices, raisins, seeds, fruit, chocolate chips, etc.
Have students slice bananas into ¼” thick slices (or pre-slice for younger students)
Insert skewers (pointed tops cut off for younger students) into 3 banana slices to form the shape of a snowman
Top with a strawberry slice or other fruit for a hat (ex: blueberry or grape)
Add optional ingredients for snowman parts: pretzel sticks (for arms), pumpkin seeds (for shoes), grapes or sliced strawberries (for hat), chocolate chips or raisins (for buttons)
Make snowman mosaics using seeds
Pre-cut cardboard or cardstock paper (minimum size 4x4 squares)
Glue colored paper to cardboard squares (if using cardboard versus cardstock)
Demonstrate drawing 1 glue circle on the cardboard/cardstock and sticking white beans to the glue circle. Add eyes, nose, buttons, hat, etc.
Repeat until all 3 snowman circles are complete (for 3k, may want to do just 1 circle for snowman face)
Allow each student to dole out their glue circles on their squares
Add glue and seeds/beans, strings, ribbons, etc. for eyes, nose, arms, hat, scarf, buttons, etc.
Allow mosaic to dry flat

Closing ritual:
Share our snowman mosaics
Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks! 
Banana snowman tasting.

Materials: 
Banana Snowmen
Skewers (pointed tops cut off for safety)
Bananas (pre-sliced into ½” segments if students are not slicing them)
Small paper plates (for each student’s banana slices)
Optional ingredients for snowman parts: pretzel sticks (for arms), pumpkin seeds (for shoes), grapes or sliced strawberries (for hat), chocolate chips or raisins (for buttons)
Snowman mosaics
Cardboard or cardstock squares for each student (adjust size according to how big you would like your mosaics)
Construction paper cut and glued to each cardboard square (if not using cardstock)
Elmer’s glue for each student (or glue cups with popsicle sticks or paint brushes to spread glue)
Cup of multi-colored and white beans for each student


Prep work and setup:
Prep either banana snowman and/or mosaic materials before class

Anticipated problems:
Little fingers may have trouble forming circles and glueing beans. May need to make glue circles for them and allow them to lay beans on glue. Alternatively, may want to just make 1 circle for a snowman face. Adjust accordingly.

Background information for instructor:
What is the Water Cycle?
Let it Snow, accompanying slide deck

Questions for students/extension activities: 
Additional snowman crafts in Let it Snow slide deck 

Back pocket activities:
None.

Task cards or handouts used: 
None.

   Corn and the Aztec Empire

Summary: Students will discuss the role of corn during the Aztec Empire and prepare atole.

Objectives: Students will be able to identify corn as a major food staple during the Aztec empire.
Students will be able to understand the connection between foods currently made in Mexico
Students will be able to work as a team to complete a recipe. 

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:  You all read an article about the Aztec Empire. Can anyone recall some facts from the article? Does anyone remember what food was very important during this time? Corn, which is a very versatile vegetable, was used for most meals. Today we will be making atole, which is a creamy dish made from corn flour that can be consumed as a drink or with a spoon. Atole is something that has been eaten since ancient times and is cited as a major source of calories for people living during that time period. 

Engaging activity: 
Students will work together to complete the recipe for atole. 

Closing ritual: Does atole remind you of anything you eat regularly? Can you think of other foods that are made with masa harina (corn flour)? 

Materials: 
Pot
Mixing bowl
Wooden spoon 
Whisk
Measuring cups
Measuring spoons

Prep work and setup (include photos if possible):
Set up a prep table with: stovetop, pot, liquid measuring cup, water, milk, brown sugar, molasses and wooden spoon
At nearby table: mixing bowl, whisk, (pre-warmed) water, masa harina, dry measuring cup
Set aside: cinnamon stick, vanilla bean and salt

Anticipated problems: none

Tips and tricks: none


Background information for instructor:
Lesson done for small pull-out speech groups with 3-5 students

Questions for students/extension activities: none

Back pocket activities: none

Task cards or handouts used: Recipe for atole below

ATOLE
Add 2 cups of water to the pot
Add 1 cup of milk to the pot
Add 1/4 cup brown sugar and 2 teaspoons molasses
Cook and stir until all the sugar is dissolved
In a bowl whisk together: ¼ cup of warm water and ⅓ cup of masa harina (corn flour)
Add the mixture to the pot
Add the cinnamon stick the pot
Use a knife to scrape the seeds from the vanilla bean and add them to the pot
Add 2 pinches of salt to the pot
Keep stirring the mixture until it is thick and creamy without clumps

body:
Grade 6-8
Fall
Indicate: Outdoor
Year 1


Teamwork in the Garden

Summary: Students will be welcomed to ESYNYC programming, go over class expectations and work collaboratively to brainstorm what they would like to do, learn and make this semester. 

Objectives: Students will do an engaging group-based activity. Students will work as teams to complete a scavenger hunt in the garden. Students will learn more about their classmates through scavenger hunt prompts.

Core Competencies: 
Environmental Stewardship: students will do garden jobs as part of a scavenger hunt
SEL: students will have to ask questions and share with a partner
Garden Skills: students will do different garden jobs (incl. Watering and composting)

Cultural Responsiveness: 
Communicates high expectations: students must complete a scavenger hunt and perform different garden jobs
Positions teacher as facilitator: less teacher talk - students move through the garden independently
Reshapes curriculum: incorporating group work and movement

Social-Emotional Skills: 
Working well in groups - students will work together to complete the scavenger hunt
Students take small risks - try new things in the garden
Responsible decision making - students use tools properly and safely
Students express joy while working outside in the garden

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual: (10 mins)
Welcome back to the Green Room! (5-10 minutes)
Do a pulse check - how is everyone doing today?
Today we are going to go out to the garden to complete a scavenger hunt. This will give you an opportunity to explore the garden and learn more about your classmates.
Why?
Gardening is a part of cooking. Growing your own food that you can cook is a very rewarding experience!
Cooking is all about teamwork. We will be together for several months so it is important to get to know each other and learn how to work together. 

Remind students of important rules for the garden. (5 minutes) 
Be Safe - no running in the garden 
Be Responsible - use your tools safely, 
Be Adventurous - try new things, participate!

Engaging activity: As a class students will discuss their goals Students will work together in pairs to complete a garden scavenger hunt. The prompts will be a combination of garden jobs and questions they must answer about their partner. 
Give scavenger hunt instructions:
Look around the garden for prompts, each emoji matches your worksheet
Answer the question for the prompt on your worksheet
If there is a task, do the task an put a check mark on the worksheet after you complete it
Students will have 15 minutes to go through the scavenger hunt. They should get to as many prompts as they can. 
Wrap up in the Green Room - go over scavenger hunt answers

Closing ritual: (5 minutes)
Questions for the class: Did you enjoy exploring the garden? What is one thing you learned about the garden or your scavenger hunt partner? 
Distribute tasting (say thank yous)

Materials: 
Scavenger Hunt Prompts

Prep work and setup:
Prepare tasting (seasonal fruit) - put in tasting cups 
Place scavenger hunt prompts throughout the garden
For watering prompt - fill up watering cans 
For compost prompt - get bucket of things to compost
Put scavenger hunt sheets on clipboards (one per pair of students)

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
Set timers for each activity to avoid going over time!

Back pocket activities:

5th Grade 
All Seasons
No Heat


Foods From Around the World: China

Summary: Students will do a reading and watch a video about the culture, geography, and typical ingredients from China, as well as how this food culture is present in New York City. Students then prepare and taste a quick recipe that is from this country.

Objectives: Students will be able to make connections between themselves, their culture, and other cultures. 

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Tell everyone that we are learning about China and the Chinese American community in New York City.
Hand out the reading. Read it aloud to students as they follow along. Take student questions and comments about what surprised them or what they connected to.
Watch the video. Again, have students share their questions or comments. 
Explain the recipe of the day. Use hand sanitizer and remind students about norms for health and safety while cooking. 

Engaging activity:
Prepare the recipe with students divided into small groups at tables. Each table will be following the same recipe. 
Fill tasting cups and sit down back as a large group.

Closing ritual: 
Finish by tasting what students have made together.
Have students share tasting words about the tasting.

Materials: 
Projector, Smartboard, or other means of showing video
Copies of China reading
Video
Take-home copies of smashed cucumber salad recipe
Hand sanitizer
Trays per each table with the following:
Ingredients from recipe
Plastic bag and rolling pin for smashing cucumbers
Cutting board and knives for cutting cucumbers
Scissors and plates for cutting herbs
Measuring spoons
Garlic press or grater
Jar or cup for mixing dressing
Bowls for ingredients
Stirring spoon
Recipe copy
Cups, plates, and utensils for tasting

Prep work and setup:
Set up trays with materials and ingredients. This recipe should be tripled for a class of 32. If you have three groups, each group can make the recipe as written. If not, divide materials and ingredients between your groups appropriately. 
Put cucumbers into plastic bags. 

Anticipated problems:
It is likely that you will not have an adult to lead each small group. Assign one student to be the table leader for each group. The table leader can give out jobs, pass out materials, and ensure the recipe is followed. 
Model for students how to smash the cucumber. The goal is to release the juices but not to mash it so much that it becomes soupy. You can have students give themselves 10 seconds to smash or sing a song while they smash, then pass to a neighbor. 
The cucumber will need time for the juice to drain. Have students who are making the dressing mix it in a jar or cup, then add it once the cucumber is done draining. 

Background information for instructor:
This lesson is the first in a larger unit about the food culture of New York City. The unit sequence is as follows:
Introductory lesson
Cooking and culture lessons (Uzbekistan, Mexico, Pakistan, China, Caribbean Islands, Ukraine)
Recipe design lesson
Voting lesson
An alternate recipe to make for this lesson is Chinese-style dumplings, although this recipe requires heat and pre-work. 
Notes from Winter 2025 classes at P.S. 216

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities: None.

Task cards or handouts used: See above.

1st-5nd GradeFall/Winter
Indoor/Outdoor


We All Need Trees 

Summary: Students will engage with various things that trees provide. 

Objectives: Students will learn that we get many things from trees.

Cultural Responsiveness: Communicates high expectations in using knives safely. Promotes student-centered instruction by having students name different things made from trees. Incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles and reshapes curriculum by using hand-ons activities and movement to learn about tree products.

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
(1st - 5th Grades) Exploration: 
Students will pick different objects from an assortment and talk about what we use them for. Lay objects on a table or have kids “hunt” for objects around the classroom.
Once all students have had an opportunity to talk about their found object, ask what is one thing all objects have in common. Where do all of these things come from? [All are made from trees.] If hunting for the objects, preface search with, “find something that came from a tree”.
Discuss things they have at home that might also come from trees.
Discuss things that trees do that may have not been mentioned. [Provide oxygen/clean air, shade, etc.]
(3k-K) Book about trees and/or fruits

Engaging activity:
Movement: demonstrate different tree parts and ask students the function of each part. Use our body parts to compare to tree parts. Play song for younger students: https://youtu.be/FJqy967xs1c 
Roots = absorb water
Trunk = delivers water to leaves
Bark = protects the tree
Branches = hold the leaves and grow towards direction of the sun
Leaves = make the food for trees
Make a fruit salad:	
Demonstrate how to cut safely using tunnel or claw to hold down fruits. 
WINTER FRUITS (sliced/diced) citrus, tropical fruits (bananas, pineapples, kiwi), persimmon, pear, apple
HONEY-MINT YOGURT DIP 
1 ½ cups plain yogurt
3 tablespoons honey
2 tablespoons fresh lime juice
3 tablespoons finely chopped fresh mint
Make recycled bird feeders (optional or instead of fruit salad): Ask students what animals (other than humans) depend on trees to survive? Explain that animals like insects, birds, chipmunks, squirrels, etc. use trees for shelter and food too. Make a bird feeder:
Use recycled materials such as milk cartons, pine cones, toilet paper or towel paper rolls, or half of an orange peel
Attach string or rope to bird feeder
Decorate milk cartons (if using)
Add suet to paper rolls or pine cones (if using) and roll in a bowl of seeds to allow seeds to stick
Add hole punches to orange peel to string rope
Add seeds when complete 
Hang outdoors on trees

Closing ritual:
Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks! Taste the fruit salad.
While eating, ask students one thing they learned or want to remember about trees.

Materials: 
Various objects made from trees: paper, napkins, wooden spoons/bowls, boxes, paper bags, wooden tools, newspaper, wooden furniture, etc.
For each student: cutting board, knife, fork, cup
Teacher cutting board and knife, bowl and spoon for mixing

Prep work and setup:
Lay out tree objects on a table or around classroom
Make holes in birdhouse materials and add rope for hanging

Anticipated problems:
Use easy to slice/cut fruits or pre-cut fruits for younger students to prevent injury 
Children may complain about sticky fingers when creating bird feeders

1st Grade
All Seasons
Indoor


  Sink or Float Experiment


Summary: Students will make predictions about whether common garden objects will sink or float. Students will then test their prediction and record their results. 

Objectives: Students will be able to compare their predictions with their results in an experiment.

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes student-centered instruction, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Define the terms “sink” and “float” with students, illustrating on the board if necessary.
Explain that we will be testing whether common objects from the garden sink or float. 

Engaging activity:
Show students the objects that they will be using in the experiment. Explain that before the experiment, they will be making a prediction, or a guess, about whether each item will sink or float. 
Pass out worksheets and have students make their predictions on the left-hand side of their worksheet.
In small groups, have students test whether each of the items sinks or floats. Have students record their observations as they go along for each item.  
At the end, have students complete their worksheets by answering what surprised them about the experiment. Provide sentence starters, if necessary. 

Closing ritual:
Ask students to share what they learned in their experiment.
End with a tasting.

Materials:
Buckets or trays with water
Items from experiment worksheet (or similar items)
Sink or Float Experiment worksheet
Pencils
Tasting 

Prep work and setup:
If you don’t have a water source in your classroom, fill buckets or trays ahead of time. 

Anticipated problems:
Have a plan to clean up water if there is a spill. 
Set clear guidelines for students about how to interact with the materials and the water before conducting the experiment. You may not want students to remove their objects after testing them, especially if that will get their arms, clothes, or tables wet. 

Background information for instructor: None. 

Questions for students/extension activities:
Students can select other garden, kitchen, or classroom items to test in the sink or float experiment. 
Students can discuss why they think certain objects floated and other objects did not. You can cut apart the squash to show the hole inside that would hold air to help it float.

Feedback Systems and Cooking

Summary: Students will make salad dressings to model how feedback systems in the body work. 

Objectives: Students will be able to identify how feedback systems work in the body to maintain homeostasis. 

Cultural Responsiveness:communicates high expectations, reshapes curriculum,

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: What do you all know about homeostasis? Homeostasis is the body’s ability to maintain an ideal environment for health and growth. The body has different systems, called feedback systems, that it uses to maintain homeostasis. A feedback system senses what is off in your body and either adds or takes away something to regulate it. An example is temperature, if your body temperature is too high and you are too hot, you will start to sweat as a way to cool you down. When your body temperature is lower and you are too cold, you shiver so that your body can generate heat to warm you up. The same can apply with food, if you are making lemonade and it tastes too sour you would add more water or sugar to fix it. Today we are going to use food to help you understand how these feedback systems work. 

Engaging activity: Students will be split into groups to make salad dressings. Recipe task cards below will tell them which version of the recipe they will make. After tasting the basic and their recipe, they will use the given ingredients to alter theirs to make it more like the basic dressing recipe.  Remind students that can only add ingredients but can not remove anything.

Closing ritual: Have someone from each group tell us what your original salad dressing tasted like. What steps did you take to alter it? How did it taste afterwards? Can anyone explain, in their own words, how they think is similar to what happens in your body? 

Materials: 
Bowls
Spoons
Microplane
Measuring spoons

Prep work and setup (include photos if possible):
Pre-make basic salad dressing recipe - one for each group
Wash and chop lettuce 
Set aside cups of water
Set up trays with ingredients and tools

Summary: Students will enjoy a DIY version of a popular drink.

Two Types of Potato Hash Browns & Kitchen Tool ID

Summary: Students will learn/review relevant kitchen tools’ names and purposes and make hash browns with two different types of potatoes.

Objectives: Students will know the names of the kitchen tools that they’re using and what the purpose of each tool is. Students will make, taste, and compare the difference between a sweet potato hash brown and a regular potato hash brown.

Core Competencies: kitchen skills and related academic content

Cultural Responsiveness: Reshapes curriculum by incorporating group work and using printed materials to match tools to their names and purposes. Communicates high expectations by expecting students to complete various tasks within a given time frame. 

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson will ideally build relationship skills by expecting students to take turns and share tools and to work well in their groups. This lesson should build responsible decision making skills by expecting students to use their tools safely and to clean up after themselves.

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual:
Welcome students to class: 5 mins
How are you all feeling today? Today we’re going to be making hashbrowns with two different types of potatoes: sweet potatoes and regular potatoes. But first, we’re going to go over all of the different kitchen tools that we’re going to need to make them. Who here is familiar with hash browns? Has anyone ever made them before? How do you feel about us making them today? Excited?
What are some kitchen tools that you already feel familiar with?

Engaging activity:
Do Kitchen Tool ID Matching Activity (10 minutes)
The tools should already be on tables. Pass out printed out cards with the names of all the tools, and separate cards with the functions of all the tools. Tell students we will spend about 5 minutes at our tables trying to match two cards (one name and one function) to each tool. Remind students they should be working together and that they should try their best to all agree on their answers.
Go over the answers, taking answers from students who want to share out, clarify any confusion and answer any questions anyone has about any of the tools.
Now, using the tools we just went over, we’re going to make the hash browns.
Make the Hashbrowns! (20-30 mins) 
Does anyone know what a potato plant looks like? Where do potatoes grow? Does anyone have any guesses how many different types of potatoes there are in the world? (5,000) Potatoes are very versatile (literacy check) and eaten all over the world. What are some different ways you have made or eaten potatoes before?
Students will take turns grating the potatoes: each type of potato will go in a separate bowl. Spices will be pre-measured on tables. When students have finished grating potatoes they will add spices to the bowls and take turns mixing everything together. 
While hashbrowns are cooking, students will clean/set their tables and get ready to taste.

Closing ritual: 10 mins
Taste Hashbrowns! Make sure each student has one of each type of potato.
Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks
Remind students that as they taste they should consider how the two potatoes may taste/feel texturally different. Do students have a preference? What (if anything) would they add to them? What kind of sauce could have been good with them? 
Go over points: Can someone share how they were safe today during class? Respectful? Adventurous? Kind? 

Materials: 
Printed Names of tools and their function for the following tools: Grater, Mixing Bowl, Mixing Spoon, Spatula, Peeler, Measuring cups (a couple different sizes).
I used this recipe 


Prep work and setup:
Could be helpful to have one batch of hash browns already cooking in the oven (since it takes longer for them in the oven and then have the students watch the ones they made being pan fried in class.

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
3K-K will not do the kitchen tool ID matching, focus lesson more on root vegetables and seasonality of potatoes.

Tortilla Española

Summary:  Students will get a chance to cook with eggs by making tortilla espanola. Focus will be on using seasonal spring vegetables. Students will also use eggs from chickens in our garden, and will learn more about our chickens and the eggs that they produce.

Objectives: Students will be able to practice cracking and whisking eggs, to learn how to safely saute vegetables and eggs in a pan, to practice chiffonading leafy greens, to identify seasonal vegetables from our garden, and to learn about the diversity of eggs from our chickens.

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Introduce today’s recipe: where it comes from and what our seasonal ingredients are. 

Engaging activity:
Table 1: Chop seasonal fresh vegetables.
First brainstorm vegetables that can grow in April. 
Students will dice asparagus and potatoes with age appropriate knives.
Students will use safety scissors to chop green onions and chiffonade the parsley leaves. 
Place each chopped vegetable in a different bowl.
Table 2: Crack and whisking eggs. 
All students will get a chance to help crack one egg and whisk the eggs together. 
Lead a discussion on why the eggs are different colors and sizes by explaining that different breeds of chickens produce different types of eggs. 
Have each student crack one egg into the bowl, promptly washing their hands immediately after. 
After cracking the eggs, have the students take turns whisking the eggs together while singing the “Mix It!” song. 
Table 3: K-2nd Grade: Eggs around the world.
Students will learn about the diverse ways that people cooked eggs around the world. 
Depending on reading level, have the teacher or the students read each of the readings about the different egg dishes. 
At the end of the readings, have students discuss the types of egg dishes they eat or help cook at home. 
Using the Family Egg worksheet, have students draw the types of eggs that they eat or cook at home, and if appropriate, have them write and describe the eggs they eat. 
Table 3: 3rd-5th Grade: Life Cycle of a Chicken. 
Students will learn about the life cycle of a chicken to better understand how chickens develop. 
Before the reading, each student or pair will receive a set of the Life Cycle of a Chicken photo cards. The photo cards depict the life cycle of a chicken and students will be responsible for putting the pictures in the correct order. 
As a group, in pairs, or individually students will read through the Life Cycle of a Chicken reading. 
When they finish the reading, if time allows, students will work through the Life Cycle of a Chicken worksheet. 
Stovetop and Final Activity
After they are done rotating through all three tables, students will be split into 1-2 groups to cook the vegetables on the stovetop.
Review what ‘sauteing’ is. Have students carefully add vegetables to the pan.
 When the vegetables are done cooking, add the eggs to the pan. 
Demonstrate to the students how to make the tortilla espanola by gently pushing in the sides of the eggs around the pan to allow the liquid on top to move to the bottom. Have students return to the tables and give each of them one job to help set the table as 1-2 adults finish cooking the tortilla espanola. 

Closing ritual:
Clean up, set the table, eat together, clear tables.
Ask for suggestions on how students would make the recipe different next time.
Share shout-outs before lining up.

Materials: 
Recipe ingredients
3 small bowls
4-5 age appropriate knives (plastic lettuce knives or metal paring knives)
4-5 age appropriate scissors
1 microplane grater 
Whole examples of each of the vegetables (asparagus, green onions, parsley, garlic, potato)
1 large bowl and whisk
1-2 towels
Example eggs from our chickens 
Eggs around the World reading
Family Egg worksheet
Colored pencils/crayons/markers/pencils 
Life Cycle of a Chicken reading and photo cards
pens/pencils 
1-2 saute pans
wooden spoons
oven mitt

Prep work and setup:
Set up herb trays with asparagus, parsley, green onions, and parboiled potatoes cut into strips.

Anticipated problems: None.

Questions for students/extension activities: None.

Sourdough & Fungi - MS
 
Summary: Students will learn about fungi, then make sourdough & pasta.

Objectives: Students will begin to learn about fungi, and their edible uses!

Cultural Responsiveness: 
Promotes student-centered instruction
have students offer suggestions for recipes - students will build their own pasta bowl
Reshapes curriculum
Incorporating group work

Social-Emotional Skills: 
Self-management
Take small risks in trying new foods and using new tools.
Relationship skills
Willingly take turns and/or share tools
Working well in groups
Make connections to outside life
Responsible decision-making
Using tools safely
Cleaning up messes
Putting materials back where they belong
Self-awareness
Displays sense of pride or ownership	


Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual (10 minutes):
Thank students for joining, and have them wash hands before taking a seat. (5 minutes)
Introduce the activity - we’ll start with making sourdough to make bread next week, then we’ll cook up some pasta to enjoy together.

Engaging activity - Sourdough starter (5 minutes):
Explain that sourdough includes the ingredient yeast, which are unicellular fungi. This living ingredient helps our dough rise! We will also cook with another fungus for our pasta recipe later: white button mushrooms.
Pass out ingredients & recipe cards to each team.
Each team will make one sourdough starter to bake with next week, which we’ll bake and enjoy with a dip.

Engaging activity - Pasta (15 minutes):
Pasta should be cooked ahead of time.
Students will chop vegetables & cook at each table.
Garlic (instructors can cut ahead of time)
Peppers
Broccoli
Mushrooms
Set up the front table with a separate bowl for each ingredient (buffet style, like with the burritos). Optional offerings: 
parmesan cheese
black pepper
red pepper flakes
Each student receives a scoop of pasta & can assemble their own plate.
Enjoy!

Closing ritual (10 minutes):
Have students tidy up their space.
Stack materials into the dishwasher tray, wipe tables, and wash hands.
Thank students for their time, and remind students that we’ll be making sourdough bread next week. We will also grow our own mushrooms next week, and talk more about fungi!

Materials: 
Sourdough
Flour & water
Pasta
Pasta
Mushrooms, garlic, veggies, parmesan
Olive oil, salt, pepper, red pepper flakes
Tools
Cutting boards & knives
Hot plates & pans for each table
Quart containers for sourdough
Measuring cups (1 cup, ½ cup)

Prep work and setup:
Cook pasta ahead of time. 

Anticipated problems:
Sourdough - contamination
Disinterest in sourdough? Maybe make sourdough croutons ahead of time, to eat with the pasta

Stewed Black Eyed Peas w/Plantains 
Summary: As a tribute to Black History month, students will learn how to make a black-eyed pea stew with fried plantains, a popular dish throughout western Africa. Also a traditional dish eaten throughout the Americas, it demonstrates the African Diaspora.

Objectives: Students will be able to:
Understand the difference between a soup and a stew.
Explore the concepts of seed and cultural dispersal through the lens of the trans-Atlantic slave trade.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, responsible decision-making, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual (10-15 min):
Hand washing.
Pulse check: thumbs up (good), thumbs sideways (could get better).
Opening circle/discussion: 
This dish is traditionally eaten for luck during the New Year in the American South. 
It originates in Africa, particularly Western Africa and Ghana, where it was brought to the Americas through the slave trade.
The African Diaspora is the spread of Africans (and consequently their cultures) throughout the Americas.
George Washington Carver also used the black eyed pea to help black southern farmers build nitrogen in the soil.
3K Class only: do a black eyed pea planting prior to hand-washing and cooking.

Engaging activity (15-20 min):
Demonstrate prep tasks and tool safety
Prep ingredients
Table 1: peel, slice, and fry plantains
Tables 2, 3, & 4 (distribute following tasks evenly): 
chop/dice onions, bell peppers, carrots, celery, and potatoes
grate ginger and garlic
tear/chiffonade greens (collard greens from garden or kale)
Closing ritual (10-15 min):
Clean and set tables
Eat, Discuss, Shout-outs & Clean up 
Eat stew that was made prior to class.
Save prepped ingredients to create stew for next class.

Materials: 
Cutting board & knives
Can opener
Measuring spoons and knives
Large pot and ladle 
Frying pan and turner spatula 
Tray Set-Up:
Table 1 - plantains, knives, cutting boards, large bowls for plantain slices
Other tables - 
Microplane with ginger and garlic
Cutting boards and knives for dicing onions, bell peppers, carrots, celery, and potatoes
Bowl for shredded greens 

Prep work and setup:
Soak beans overnight prior to class if using dried black eyed peas.
To ensure enough time to eat, cook a batch of stew prior to class or allot additional 20 minutes for cooking time. Vegetables prepared by class can be used to cook up stew for next class.

Anticipated problems:
Varying levels of understanding on the African Diaspora or geography based on age group. Adjust discussion accordingly.
Use extra caution with sharp tools such as graters.
Stew takes at least 20 minutes to cook. If not enough time to cook in class, cook beforehand.

Background information for instructor:
African Diaspora
Black Eyed Pea
Ghana, on the west coast, is one of the oldest civilizations in Africa.

body:
Grade: KSpring 2022
Outdoor - Garden


The Carrot Seed 
 
Summary: Students read The Carrot Seed and plant carrots outdoors.  

Objectives: Students draw connections between what they grow & what they eat. Students feel responsibility & accomplishment in the garden.

Cultural Responsiveness: 
Reshapes curriculum
Incorporating movement 
Incorporating group work

Social-Emotional Skills: 
Relationship skills
Willingly take turns and/or share tools
Working well in groups
Make connections to outside life
Responsible decision-making
Cleaning up messes
Putting materials back where they belong
Using tools safely
Self-awareness
Joy: Display a verbal or physical action of joy
Express their feelings and awareness of their needs 	
Displays sense of pride or ownership	
Asking for help when needed and conversely, taking the lead/helping others when able

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual (10 minutes):
Welcome students into the classroom. Today’s theme is all about carrots! We’ll start by reading a story about a little boy who plants carrots, then we’ll go outside and plant our very own carrot seeds! 
Read The Carrot Seed.
Now, who’s ready to plant some carrots?

Engaging activity (25 minutes):
Plant carrot seeds! (10 minutes)
Have students put on gloves before going outside, if they like.
Indicate where to plant with popsicle sticks:
¼” deep,  2 to 3 inches apart. Rows are 1 foot apart.
Students can wiggle the popsicle stick to make a hole, then drop in the seed. Remove the popsicle stick, then gently cover with soil.
Remind students not to pack the soil down too hard!
Since we have to wait a long time before we can harvest our carrots, let’s make some paper carrots that we can take home today! (15 minutes)
Color, then cut out carrot printouts.
Follow instructions for the craft here. Simplify by having students make just one tier of “soil” and only making carrots.

Closing ritual (10 minutes):
Have students tidy up their space before offering an opportunity to share their artwork.
Offer to collect artwork to hang on the pantry door, or to pack it in their bags to take home.
Thank students for joining, and we’ll see you next week!

Materials: 
Popsicle sticks
Carrot seeds
The Carrot Seed 
Crayons
Carrot printouts - try connecting to the printer, if not, students can draw & color in their own carrots.
Scissors - located where we keep the forks & spoons on the wire racks
Colored paper - in the blue coat closet or on the back hallway shelves
Double sided tape - to the right of the microwave

Prep work and setup:
Set up popsicle sticks to indicate where students should plant the seeds.
Make one example of the craft for students to see & understand.
Post work: water the carrots.

Anticipated problems:
The teachers are usually ok with going over time. But if you find that you are under time, you can grab a book from the rolling library and close the class with one more story.

Background information for instructor:
Carrots - Old Farmer’s Almanac

Chana Chawal and Raita

Summary: Students will learn about the spices that travel the world that are key flavors in this dish. Students will learn about making rice in the chana chawal and cut lots of veggies for the chana chawel and raita.

Objectives: Students will be able to have an understanding of spices and how they traveled. Students will also be aware of the interdependence that is needed for a cuisine to exist as it is. 


Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening Ritual 
Show our colored map in the room that highlights all of the countries that our students are from. Explain that we represent over 40 countries in our school! See how they respond and see if they have any follow up questions. “Today we are making food from Pakistan (raise hands if you know anyone from here). This country has delicious recipes with lots of flavor. Their recipes have changed over time with people moving to and from Pakistan.”
For example: Today we will cook chana chawal, chickpeas and rice, which is a form of rice and beans. Ask them to raise their hands if they have ever had rice and beans. Identify what kind of rice and beans they have and how are they made. 

Engaging Activity

Cucumber table: Sauteing - Cutting onion and tomato and sauteing those ingredients. Adding all of our spices and identifying what spices they are. 
Corn table: Sensory spice exploration - students will look into all of our spices and learn about them more in depth using their senses. Students will also be presented with a placemat that will give them more information about the spices. Students will answer a couple of questions on a worksheet for each spice to help retain some of the information that they read. 
Carrot table: Knife skills and Raita- cutting cucumber, radish, tomato, lettuce and adding this to yogurt and chaat masala.  Usually the teacher led class.

Closing Ritual
Clean up, set the table, eat together, clear tables.



Materials:
Middle table: spice place mats, guiding questions, actual spices, magnifying glasses
Food: Tomatoes, cucumbers, onions, ginger, radish, lettuce, yogurt, chaat masala, salt, cayenne, chili flakes, pepper, turmeric, cloves, cumin seed, cinnamon
Tools: Measuring cup, measuring spoons, knives, cutting boards, bowl, large pot, induction unit or stove top


Prep work and setup (include photos if possible):

Make chana chawal ahead of time (rice must cook for 30 min, each class will make chana chawal for the next class)

Anticipated problems:

Students and families may be disappointed that we are using brown rice and not basmati rice.

Eat the Rainbow: White 

Summary: Students will practice knife skills to cut up potatoes into “french fry” strips and cauliflower into small pieces. Students will be introduced to safety around using the oven.
