5th Grade
All Seasons
Indoor


Foods From Around the World: Final Vote

Summary: Students will vote on their recipe for their final class of the year and start seeds for that recipe.

Objectives: Students will be able to create their own recipe, using their personal knowledge and their learning in this unit. 

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Explain that you have narrowed down the recipe ideas from the previous class to a few options that they’ll get to vote on.  Explain that you narrowed it down based on what was popular in that class and what we thought would be feasible to make, given materials and time restrictions. 
Go through the slideshow of recipe options. This helps students familiarize themselves with the options in case there is something they haven’t heard of or tried before. Invite students who nominated those recipe ideas to speak about and promote their idea. 
Have students vote using ballots. Count the votes and announce the winning recipe.

Engaging activity:
Start seeds for the vegetable or herb required for the recipe.
Model for students how to plant their seeds.
Pass out materials. It is recommended that you pass out seeds only once students have filled their pots and made a small hole. 
Circulate to help students and to ensure sharing of materials.

Closing ritual:
Express excitement about students’ final recipe. Explain what steps come next, or ask students to predict what steps come next. 
Finish with a tasting. 


Materials:
Projector, Smartboard, or other means of showing video
Slideshow of student ideas (example here)
Small slips of paper for voting
Pencils
Pots
Soil
Seeds
Spray bottles or other method for watering
Tasting

Prep work and setup:
Read through students’ ideas from the previous lesson. Consolidate ideas into 5-7 final options that you would like students to choose from. Some ideas to consider when narrowing down your options:
Consider the popularity of the recipe and if multiple students nominated the same recipe idea. 
Consider recipe feasibility, including time needed to prepare and cook, materials and ingredients necessary, and opportunities for student involvement. 
Consider featuring recipe ideas that generated high levels of student excitement in the previous lesson.
Consider giving shy or less vocal students the opportunity to have their recipe idea highlighted.
Prepare a slideshow with the final options. 
Set up materials for planting: soil in trays, spray bottles for watering, etc. 

Anticipated problems:
If your students are deciding on their final recipe and then planting within the same day, you will need to have multiple varieties of seeds available depending on what their final vote is. For example, if students are voting between ramen with scallions or tacos with cilantro, you will need to have both scallion and cilantro seeds available. 

Background information for instructor:
This lesson is the first in a larger unit about the food culture of New York City. The unit sequence is as follows:
Introductory lesson
Cooking and culture lessons (Uzbekistan, Mexico, Pakistan, China, Caribbean Islands, Ukraine)
Recipe design lesson
Voting lesson

body:
4th GradeFall/Spring
Outdoor


Food Origins Scavenger Hunt

Summary: Students explore the garden using a scavenger hunt that directs them to find the geographic origins of different plants.

Objectives: Students will understand the historical geography of foods from the garden.

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles by providing a kinesthetic, active method of learning new information. 

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson has students practice responsible decision-making and self-awareness by working independently and being mindful of their physical space as they do the activity. 

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Welcome students. Go over norms for garden behavior, getting students’ input on what it means to show respect to all the elements of the garden—plants, animals, soil, water, tools, people. Remind students that during our activity today, we are going to practice being safe in the garden with our bodies and kind to living things. 
Start by asking students what they know about world maps (how many continents are there, what are the names of the continents etc.). Take ideas. 
Today we are going to be learning about where different foods first came from in the world. Before people started growing fruits and vegetables in their gardens, those plants came from somewhere out in nature. We will be using what you already know about world maps to help us find out where different fruits and vegetables first came from in the world. 

Engaging activity:
Hand out scavenger hunt sheets on clipboards and pencils.
Model and explain how students will do the scavenger hunt:
“You are going to walk through the garden looking for signs. They will tell you where the plants are from, along with other information.”
Hold up an example sign so students know what they’re looking for. Model how they should read the sign, and then copy down the name of the food onto the correct section of their worksheet. 
Once the students are walking around, circulate to help as needed.

Closing ritual:
Have students share some of their answers. Was anything a surprise?
Highlight examples of how students practiced strong self-management and responsible decision-making in the garden, such as showing kindness to living things, being safe with their bodies and with other students, and listening to one another.  
Finish with a tasting. 

Materials: 
Food origin scavenger worksheets: either version 1 or version 2 
Food origins scavenger hunt signs or there is a list of additional sign text  
Clipboards
Pencils
Tasting

Prep work and setup:
Put scavenger hunt signs out in the garden.
Print scavenger hunt worksheets and put them on clipboards.

Anticipated problems: None.

Background information for instructor:
This lesson was written as an outdoor scavenger hunt, but it could also be done as a gallery walk indoors. 
There are two versions of this worksheet. One has columns where students fill in the answer about what region of the world different foods come from. The other has a world map so students practice map reading and write the names of the food next to the continent that it comes from. 

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities: None.

School and Garden Communities

Summary: Students will sing a song about the different members of our garden community and do a garden job to understand that they as students are important members of the garden community.

Objectives: Students will be able to understand that the garden has many different people and animals who help it grow and thrive, just like the school community.

Core Competencies: Environmental and community stewardship, garden skills and related academic content

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson reshapes curriculum by incorporating movement and song. 

Social-Emotional Skills: Students will make connections to outside life by understanding that like the school community, the garden has many different creatures and people to help it thrive.

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
Community, garden, animals, school community


Agenda/Class Flow:
Introduction (10 min)
Welcome students back to the garden! Ask students what they know about the community. “What does community mean?”
Ask students to give an example of community - either their classroom or the entire school.
As a group, ask students who different members of the school community are?
Teachers, students, principals, school aides, custodians, kitchen staff, parents, etc
“You as students are members of the school community. You are also members of our garden community, because you come to help make sure the garden is healthy and happy”
“In the garden community, the members of the community are not just people, there are plants and animals too. There is a special song about the different members of our garden community I want to teach you! You may recognize it and we can sing along together”
Song lyrics at the bottom of the document - have students join for the repeating parts (the e-i-e-i-o part and the 3rd line of every verse)
Once you sing the song together, ask students if there are any other members of the garden community they can think of.

Engaging activity: (20 min)
Ask students to explain again how students are a part of the garden community. 
“Students are a part of the garden because they help keep the plants happy and healthy! Today we are going to do a garden job to help our garden community”
As a whole class or in small groups, lead students in a seasonal, age appropriate garden job.

Closing ritual with SEL component: (15 min)
Wash hands and come back together for ending and a taste test
Ask students how it feels to help in their community. 
Take student responses or have them pair and share with a partner. 
End with a seasonal tasting. 


Back pocket activity:
Picture matching activity - matching pictures of the member of the garden community to the job they do

Materials: 
Tools for garden jobs
Seasonal tasting

All Grades / Fall, Winter, Spring / Indoor


Bundle Dyeing with Natural Materials


Summary: Students will create their own dyed fabrics using natural materials from the garden and kitchen. 

Objectives: Students will understand how plant materials can be used to create dyes on fabrics. 

Core Competencies: Garden skills and related academic content, multicultural education

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson encourages learning within the context of culture by providing examples of how different cultures have used natural materials to make dye. 

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson helps students to practice responsible decision-making by sharing tools and materials with their classmates.

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual (10 minutes):
Begin by asking students what we use plants for. Students may say things like food, building materials, clothing, etc. 
Explain that another way plants have been used over time is to make color for things like clothing, hair, or makeup. Explain that today we will be using plants to dye some fabric. 
Pass around some example visuals.
Show students the different ingredient options that they have and what color they will become; some items are a different color when dry compared to the dye that they create! If possible, show students examples of dyed fabrics.
Tell them that today they are going to try something new–using plants to dye a piece of fabric.

Engaging activity (25 minutes):
Show students the procedure for creating a dye bundle:
Lay fabric out on a flat surface like a table. Then sprinkle your materials on top of the fabric. You can either do it along the entire length, or you can do it on half the length and then fold it in half on top of itself. 
Roll fabric up tightly, catching the dye materials inside the fabric. 
Write your name on a piece of cardstock to label your piece.
Use string to bind around the bundle. Leave one end loose and then go back and forth so the two ends of the string end up together. Tie your bundle with a bow tie so you can reuse the fabric. While doing this, tuck the cardstock label inside the bundle so you know whose piece is whose.
Explain that you will soak the bundles in water to extract the color after class. Then you will take them apart, wash them, and dry them. Then they will be ready to use! 
Have students create their bundles. Set them to the side, or if you are planning to dye them right away, put them into the pot to steep on the induction burner.

Closing ritual (10 minutes):
Ask students to turn and share with a partner: “How do people use plants in your family? Are there any special plants that are used in your family for specific holidays, celebrations, or purposes?”


Materials:
Dye visuals
Cotton fabric
String
Scissors
Dye materials (specific suggestions below)
Cups or ramekins for dye materials
Pencils
Cardstock
Stainless steel pot, 
Induction burner, tongs
Alum (optional)
Prep work and setup:
Cut up cotton fabric into squares for students. Cotton fabric should also be washed with a mild soap beforehand to remove any chemicals, like Dawn, Dove, or Dr. Bronner’s.
To make the dye last longer, you can prepare the fabric with a mordant. Alum is a safe mordant for use with kids. To do this, soak the fabric beforehand, then add to a hot water bath (not boiling) with the alum stirred in for about an hour. The amount of alum used should be 10% the weight of the fabric. This step is completely optional; students may not care as much if the fabric fades over time.
Set out trays of dye materials into cups or ramekins with labels.
Cut small pieces of cardstock for labels. 

Anticipated problems:
Students may need help tying their bundles, but otherwise should be self-sufficient with the activity. 

Tips and tricks:
Some easy, inexpensive, and kid-friendly dye materials are listed in this table. 
After students create their bundles, they should be dipped in water and soaked before going into the hot water bath to dye. This will allow the dye to absorb.
The bundles should stay in the water for at least an hour. The temperature should be between 140-180 F; any higher and the dyes will oxidize and change color. 
After the bundles are done, they should be rinsed of all plant material, rinsed in cold water, and then washed with a mild soap such as Dawn, Dove, or Dr. Bronner’s. They are then ready to be used. 

Background information for instructor: Additional, detailed information is available here and here. 

Questions for students/extension activities:
If time allows, you could also allow students to harvest some dye materials from the garden. 
This could also be altered into a science experiment, where students could predict the color of dye that each item will produce. Many of these dyes also react to pH, so you could do a controlled experiment in which you add a pH modifier (like lemon zest as an acid or baking soda as an alkaline) and compare that to a dye without pH modifier. 

Back pocket activities: None.

Task cards or handouts used: See above.

body:
KindergartenFall/Winter/Spring
Indoor


Seed Maps 

 
Summary: Students examine different kinds of edible seeds, dissect a lima bean, and color in a picture of all the parts of a seed.

Objectives: Students will understand different roles played by seeds, for making plants and as food. Students will identify different examples of edible seeds and different parts of the seed.

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles by including a turn and talk with visuals, an art activity, and a hands on seed dissection.

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson encourages students to practice self-management as students are responsible for coloring their seed map neatly and independently and managing their own coloring time. 

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Welcome students. Tell them that we are going to start by looking at mystery items in class. Hand out cups with a mix of seeds to each pair or small group of students. Ask them to turn and talk to their group about what they see. 
Have students share with the full class about what they saw. “Did you recognize any of the seeds?” Try to use a mix of edible seeds that will be recognizable to students (i.e. beans, corn, rice, sunflower seeds etc.).
Ask the students what we do with these seeds in garden or kitchen class. We can grow them or eat them!
Explain that when we’re learning about seeds today, we are going to practice being focused. Have students give examples of what it means to be focused: listening to the speaker, eyes on what we are doing, using coloring tools carefully. Explain that being focused will help us to learn about and then find all the parts of a seed. 

Engaging activity:
“We are going to look at a seed map.” Ask students what we use maps for. 
Hand out clipboards with seed maps to each student. Draw a seed map on the board as well to point to. 
Walk students through the three parts of a seed. The coat protects the seed, the baby plant is inside the seed ready to grow, and the food is the energy for the baby plant. Have students do motions to act out each of these three parts (i.e. making your arms grow up like a little plant and making leaves to act out the baby plant).
Next, have students color in the parts of the seed, one part at a time. Hand out only red colored pencils first and have everyone color in the seed coat together. Reinforce that the seed coat protects the seed. Collect the red colored pencils and hand out blue to color in the food on our seed maps. After coloring in the food, hand out green colored pencils and have everyone color in the baby plant.  
After coloring in the seed map have students place their clipboards down in front of them so they can reference it.
Show students how you can take apart a lima bean and find all three parts. Walk them around to show students the example. Give each student their own lima bean to dissect.
 
Closing ritual:
Clean up lima beans and seed map papers. 
Finish with a seed for a tasting. 
Highlight student behavior today that demonstrated focus. 

Materials: 
Dry lima bean seeds, soaked overnight
Seed map worksheets 
Crayons or colored pencils in three colors
A collection of edible seeds, in paper cups, enough to give each group of 2-4 kids in the class four different samples (i.e. popcorn, rice, sunflower seeds, pomegranate seeds, lima beans, caraway, anise, almonds 
Seed tasting (or other seasonal tasting)

Prep work and setup:
Soak lima beans overnight. 
Print out seed maps and put them on clipboards. 
Put a mix of seeds into cups for the opening circle. 
Sort out three colors of colored pencils for students to use on their seed maps. 

Anticipated problems: None. 

Background information for instructor: None. 

Questions for students/extension activities: Read a book about seeds (i.e. A Seed Is Sleepy).

Back pocket activities: None.

Whole Grains and Granola


Summary: Make granola gifts for holiday break and talk about the health benefits of whole grain foods compared to refined foods

Objectives: Students will understand the difference between whole grain and processed grains
Students will participate in gift giving (SEL)

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills (Make connections to outside life), responsible decision-making, self-management (Displays sense of pride or ownership, expressing feelings of gratitude)

Agenda/Class Flow:
 1-2 washing hands, do now: write a sentence about how it feels to give gifts
  K get hand sanitizer 

Opening ritual:

1. Welcome to class, go over expectations for cooking class
2. Hold up whole grain bread and white bread, ask students to identify differences. Explain that whole wheat foods (the brown breads, wraps, pastas, rice) usually still have the outside of the seed and a lot more nutrients than white bread and pasta which has many nutrients removed. Granola has a lot of oats which are a great type of whole grain for strong hearts. 


Engaging activity:
1. Make granola (each group makes a batch) with pre measured materials -- visual instructions
K and 1st
2.. Talk with your table about an adult in your life that you want to give a present to (someone in your family, or at school)
3. Write the name of that person and your own name, K (draw a small picture) 1 (choose a greeting from the board)
4. Peel the stickers and put them on a bag, when they’re done they will add 3 scoops of granola to the bag and use a twist tie to close it
2nd
1. OJ and types of bread activity to talk about the speed of energy that different foods give you
2. Worksheet for them to guess different types of grains as whole or processed
Etc.

Closing ritual:
1.  Ask “why is granola a good cereal to eat?” Go over whole grains and energy levels
2. As they have their gifts and are ready to have December break, why it’s important to show appreciation for people in your life
Etc.

All GradesFall
Indoor and Outdoor

Garlic Bread and Planting

Summary: Students will learn about garlic and other types of bulbs that are planted in the fall. They will learn about the uses of garlic, try some garlic bread, and then plant garlic in the garden. 

Objectives: Students will be able to describe bulbs such as garlic and how to plant them. 

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Discuss Fall: What season are we in? What happens in the fall? What happens to the weather? What happens in the garden?
Explain that in the Fall, it’s too cold to plant most things but we can plant bulbs. 
Using a visual aid of bulbs, show that bulbs are similar to seeds because they have a coat or skin on the outside to protect them. They store food and a plant on the inside. We plant bulbs in the fall so they can grow their roots before the soil gets too cold. In the spring, the plant will sprout up and grow. 
Highlight other bulb examples: onions, tulips, daffodils (if you have any on hand, cut open to show the layers on the inside)

Engaging activity:
Garlic as food/medicine:
Garlic has also been used as medicine for thousands of years. It’s antibacterial and can treat infections and protect against cold and flus. It’s also very popular in cooking.
Ask students if they have tried garlic or seen someone cooking with garlic. What types of foods does garlic taste good with? 
Garlic has a powerful onion-like smell and strong taste. It is used to add flavor to lots of foods included strews, sauces, and more.
Clean hands and do a taste test of the garlic bread. 
Ask students what words they would use to describe it and if it reminds them of any other familiar foods. 
Garlic planting:
Explain that we will be going outside to plant garlic. Each student will plant one garlic bulb. Transition outside.
Once outside, gather around the bed for planting. Point out the sticks and demonstrate how to plant: 
Pull up the stick for the spot you want to plant
Make a hole about the depth of your pointer finger
Put the unpeeled garlic clove in with the pointy side facing up and the flat side (roots) down. If the pointy side is not facing up, the garlic won’t be able to grow!
Cover the garlic back up with soil and give the stick to the teacher. 
Add dried leaves on top of the soil to protect garlic
Pass out garlic cloves and gloves (optional) and have students all point to which popsicle stick spot they will be planting. Once everyone has found one, start planting. 
As students finish, they can gather dried leaves from around the garden to cover up the bed. These will protect the garlic. 

Closing ritual:
Regroup at picnic tables
Discussion questions:
What is something you learned about garlic today? 
Why do we plant bulbs in the fall?
Do you notice any other changes in the garden now that it’s Fall?

Materials: 
Demonstration bulbs cut open if available: onion, daffodil/tulip
1 whole garlic bulb for demonstration
1 garlic clove per student (unpeeled)
Garlic bread:
Baguette cut in slices (1 slice per student)
Olive oil 
Garlic powder & minced garlic 
Plates (1 per table)
Garden gloves
Popsicle sticks 

Prep work and setup:
Make a visual aid of bulbs showing the layers of food, baby plant inside, and outer coat layer. Include names of other bulb examples
Break down garlic into cloves (1 per student)
Set up popsicle sticks (or other markers) in beds where garlic should be planted (a few inches apart)
Make garlic bread right before class so it’s warm for the tasting and the room smells garlicky when students come in

Anticipated problems:
Background information for instructor:
On garlic as medicine: 
Garlic has antibacterial properties and is used to treat infections, stave off colds and flus, lower cholesterol, promote heart health and more.
In ancient Egypt, it was given to workers to make them stronger. 
The Romans thought it could strengthen the heart, get rid of parasites, and treat the skin. 
During the Middle Ages, it was used (ineffectively) to ward off the plague. 
American colonists thought it could treat tapeworms and cure flu symptoms. 
During World War I, it was used to treat wounds.
Of all the garlic grown in the United States, 90% is grown in California. China produces more than 60% of the world’s garlic.

3rd GradeFall/Spring
Outdoor


Descriptive Word Hunt

Summary: Students will collect items in the garden that match a list of descriptive words. 

Objectives: Students will be able to practice using their descriptive word vocabulary to match objects found in the garden.

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson promotes student-centered instruction by giving students the opportunity to explore independently in the garden space. 

Social-Emotional Skills: Students practice self-management and responsible decision-making by practicing garden safety norms during their first visit to the garden. Students also practice relationship skills by working in pairs, and by listening and speaking in small groups.

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Welcome students back to the garden. Go over norms for garden behavior, getting students’ input on what it means to show respect to all the elements of the garden—plants, animals, soil, water, tools, people. 
Explain that today we will do a hunt around the garden using descriptive words, while also practicing being safe with our bodies and kind to living things in the garden.
Show an example of a leaf or other item. Ask students to give you some example words to describe or tell about that item. Take student answers. 

Engaging activity:
Show students the egg cartons with the descriptive words on the side or on the lid. Pass out egg cartons to pairs of students. Have them read through all the words, and go over any vocabulary words that are new. 
Point out the two boxes on the end with a question mark instead of a descriptive word. Explain that these are “mystery boxes.” Explain that students can put whatever they want in that box but they have to come up with their own descriptive word for it. 
Give students guidelines for how to pick things for our scavenger hunt in the garden. Tell students to take a piece of a plant rather than a whole plant, or a petal from a flower rather than the whole flower. Let students know that at the end of the class, they will not take any items from the scavenger hunt inside. All items will be returned back to the garden. 
Allow student pairs to go out in the garden to collect items. Supervise and assist as needed. If students find all 12 items quickly, they can find a second item for each category. 

Closing ritual:
Group two pairs of students together to make a group of four. Have students take turns sharing their favorite items with each other and explaining their descriptive words. 
If time allows, have a few groups show their “mystery items” to the whole class. The rest of the class can guess what descriptive word they chose to describe their item.
Share a seasonal tasting.
Highlight examples of how students practiced strong self-management and responsible decision-making in the garden, such as showing kindness to living things, being safe with their bodies and with other students, and listening to one another.  

Materials: 
Egg cartons, one for each pair of students
Tasting

Prep work and setup:
Prepare enough egg cartons for each pair of students. Write the following descriptive words on the lid or on the side of each egg compartment, or choose your own vocabulary words: rough, smooth, hard, soft, colorful, fragrant, big, small, heavy, light.

Anticipated problems:
Monitor students to ensure that they are picking items carefully and not taking out whole plants. Remind students that the items should be small enough to fit into their box, which should discourage them from picking whole plants. 

Background information for instructor: None.

Questions for students/extension activities:
This lesson would work well in conjunction with other lessons using descriptive words, such as writing garden poetry.  

Back pocket activities: None.

4th GradeFall/Winter
Indoor/Outdoor


Seed Saving and the Neolithic Revolution 

Summary: Students will learn about the origins of agriculture and seed saving and learn how this changed the way people lived.  Students will then practice seed saving out in the garden. 

Objectives: Students will understand the importance of seed saving throughout history, and they will learn how to save their own seeds from the garden.

Cultural Responsiveness: reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Welcome students. Explain that today they will be helping to save seeds from some of the plants in the garden. Ask students why we save seeds. 
People have been saving seeds for thousands of years, but before that they had to go find food out in nature. We are going to read a before and after story that shows how much life changed from a time when people got all of their food from out in nature to when people started saving their seeds and could grow most of their own food. 
Engaging activity:
Give out Seed Saver comics students to read, then turn and talk to their partners about three important changes that happened after people started saving seeds.
Have the below questions written on the white board:
When did people first start having seeds? 
What was life like before people started saving seeds? 
What was life like after people started saving seeds? 
Go over the questions with the students and tell them to look for the answers as they read and discuss with their partner.
Lead students in a seed saving job (either from plants out in the garden, or if indoors from dry flower heads or something that has been collected in advance).
Students take the seeds out of the flower/fruit.
Give students small envelopes to put their seeds in. 
If time students can decorate and label their seed envelopes with colored pencils. 
Closing ritual:
Thank students for their work. Let students take their seeds home or let them know that we’ll plant these seeds together in the spring once it’s warmer!
Finish with a tasting. 

Materials: 
Dry erase board and markers
Seeds for saving 
Small envelopes
Colored pencils
Small bowls for seeds and compost
Chalkboards or papers with seed saving instructions
Seed Savers Comic 
Seed tasting (or other seasonal tasting)

Prep work and setup:
Print out Seed Savers comic.
Set up a seed saving station. 

Anticipated problems: None.

Background information for instructor: None.

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities: None.

Task cards or handouts used: See above.

Year 1



Black Panther Party and the Free Breakfast Program


Summary: Students will learn about the Black Panther Party’s free breakfast initiative as a means of resistance and change.

Objectives: Students will learn about the Black Panther Party and make a breakfast dish.

Core Competencies: Environmental and community stewardship, social justice, multicultural education.

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson encourages learning within the context of culture by providing an example of leadership and resistance in the Black community.

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson helps students to develop social awareness by learning about a social justice issue, food insecurity, and how leaders in the Black community organized to make change. Students will also practice responsible decision-making as they use tools safely and clean up after themselves.

Agenda/Class Flow: (45 minutes)
Opening ritual: (20 minutes)
Welcome students to class. Wash hands and take seats. 
“We’ve been talking in garden and kitchen class about ways that people have used food and gardens to make the world a better place. Today we are talking about one of the ways that food was important in the Civil Right Movement.”
Solicit student knowledge about the Civil Rights Movement.
“In the 1960s, Black students weren’t allowed to go to the same schools as white students. A group during the Civil Rights Movement called the Black Panthers wanted to help Black students in schools. They got donations and started serving breakfast to students for free. Because of their great idea, eventually the federal government realized it should give free breakfast for all students.”
Watch video. Ask students beforehand to listen for surprising information or something new they learned.  

Engaging activity: (15 minutes)
“To celebrate the Black Panther Party’s achievement of bringing breakfast to all schools, we are going to be making a breakfast dish!”
Ask students if they have ever eaten granola before. 
“Granola is made with oats. It’s super fun to make because you can add whatever ingredients and spices you want.”
Explain that because the granola needs to cook in the oven, they will make the batch for the next class.
Pass out task sheets and have students read through their jobs.
Pass out trays with materials.
Once students make the granola mix, collect it.
Time permitting: give students cutting boards, knives, and bananas to cut into small pieces.
All bananas are added to a small bowl in the middle of the table.
Cooked granola is passed out. Students begin to make their own bowls with yogurt, granola, and cut fruit. 

Closing ritual: (10 minutes)
Once bowls are assembled, eat together.
Ask students how they feel when they forget to eat breakfast and why they think it is so important.


Materials: 
Task sheet
Take home recipe 
Projector or Smart Board to watch video
Ingredients: oats, sugar, oil, salt, cinnamon, pumpkin seeds, dried fruit, coconut
Sliced fruit and yogurt for serving
Measuring cups and spoons
Knives and cutting boards
Bowls and mixing spoons
Spoons and cups or bowls for eating

Prep work and setup:
Print task sheets and put in protective sleeves.
Print and organize recipes.
Print readings.
Set up one tray per table with oats, pumpkin seeds, sugar, salt, cinnamon, oil, and measuring spoons and cups.
Have bowls and mixing spoons ready for each table.
Prepare a batch of granola ahead of time for each class. 
Cut bananas or other fruit into small pieces ahead of class.

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
If you make small groups of 5-6 students, we recommend that each small group make one batch of granola in order to feed a whole class.
This article reports on the positive outcomes from eating breakfast. 
These two articles provide additional background on the Black Panther Party and the Free Breakfast Program:
https://www.blackpast.org/african-american-history/black-panther-partys-free-breakfast-program-1969-1980/
https://www.history.com/news/free-school-breakfast-black-panther-party
Can also do a different breakfast recipe, like fruit parfaits. Here is the lesson plan and recipe

Back pocket activities: 
If doing this lesson for more than 45 minutes, or if your students like to receive information through reading, this text shares much of the same information as the video in a little more detail.  
Visuals to pair with the reading

Baking Intro: Pumpkin Muffins

Summary: Students will be introduced to baking and follow a recipe for pumpkin muffins. They will do math and reading related to the recipe while they bake. 

Objectives: Students will be able to follow a baking recipe and describe the process of making muffins. 

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Ask students if they have ever baked anything before. What did they bake? Can anyone come up with their own definition of the word “baking”? 
Baking means cooking with dry heat such as an oven. Often things that are baked are breads or pastries, but many foods can be baked. 
Today we are baking muffins using an ingredient that is really popular in the Fall. Can anyone guess what ingredient it is? (Pumpkins!)
Show seasonings: cinnamon, ginger, nutmeg, cloves 
Distribute recipes and have students read them as a group
Review the recipe and explain baking terms:
Pre-heat
Show a Tablespoon vs. teaspoon
Show liquid vs dry measuring cups
Choose group roles and transition

Engaging activity:
In groups, students follow the recipe to make mini batches of muffin batter.
Collect the batter and bake in mini muffin tins. 
Clean up tables
While muffins bake, students complete a math worksheet. 
Optional: groups can use beans and measuring cups to visually solve the problems. 
After students finish math problems, flip to the back of the paper for a nonfiction reading about pumpkins. Tell students to read carefully because there will be trivia questions at the end. 

Closing ritual:
Taste muffins and vote tried/liked/loved it. 
Presenters share about how they worked together and descriptive tasting words
Ask trivia comprehension questions from the reading about pumpkins. Give out stickers for correct answers

Tray set-up:
Pumpkin
Oil
Soy milk
Brown sugar
Whole wheat flour / all purpose flour
Baking soda
Salt
Seasonings: cinnamon, ginger, nutmeg, cloves
Laminated group recipes
Mini muffin pan
Cooking spray

Pre-make batch of muffins as backup
Print worksheets (double-sided)

Friendship Pizza
(adapted from Learning for Justice)

Summary: Students will identify ways to promote friendship and acceptance by relating actionable items to ingredients they use to make a pizza.

Objectives: Students will be able to identify things that we can do or say to make a new friendship, or build up an existing friendship. Students will be able to identify ways to support other students when they need it. 

Cultural Responsiveness:[pick one or more] promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Opening ritual (5 mins) :
Explain to students that today they will be making a pizza together in groups. Have the classroom teacher divide them into groups of 4 before class begins.

Describe the idea of a “Friendship Pizza” where each ingredient would represent friendly and accepting words or actions.

      Students can taste the pizza that they made with their groups
Closing ritual (5 mins) :

Foods From Around the World: Intro Lesson

Summary: Students will watch a video and share their own food memory story to begin a unit studying the unique food cultures of New York City. 

Objectives: Students will be able to make connections between their personal experiences and others and will practice writing and speaking. 

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Explain that we are beginning a unit in which we are going to study the unique cultures and people that have come from all over the world to New York City. 
Show the video. Ask students to share what was surprising to them or what they had a connection to. (You can show the whole video, or just show the first half up to 2:38.)
Explain that in this unit, we will be cooking foods that have come to us from these different communities. We will also be reading and watching videos to learn about these communities and their countries of origin.  At the end, after learning about all these food traditions, the fifth grade will get to decide on what recipe they would like to cook for their final class at the end of the year. 

Engaging activity:
Explain that to begin our unit, we want to have an opportunity to share within the class about our own cultures, where we come from, and what’s important to us. Explain that we are going to be sharing a “food memory” that tells about a food that is important to us. 
Model a food memory story, sharing a personal story about an important food in your life. Include details about setting, sensory experiences, people present, and the food. 
Show the slideshow with prompting questions for the activity. Share a few tips for the students about their food memory:
It can be a food memory that happened just once, or it can be something that happens over and over again, like a traditional food or a food eaten on a special occasion. For example, one student might share that they go to the same restaurant every Friday night, and their food memory can be about that tradition.
Students should include as many sensory details as possible: sight, sound, smell, taste, touch.
Students can share their food memory in whatever format they want: a poem, a comic, a paragraph, etc. 
Model for students how they can write and illustrate their food memory on the worksheet. Pass out worksheets, pencils, and colored pencils. Circulate to help students as needed.

Closing ritual: 
If time allows, invite some students to share their food memories. Invite other students to make connections to the reader’s story.
Finish with a tasting.

Materials:
Projector, Smartboard, or other means of showing video
Video 
Food memory slideshow
Food memory worksheets
Pencils
Colored pencils
Tasting

Prep work and setup: None.

Anticipated problems:
Some students may have trouble thinking of their food memory and may need to talk through their ideas first. Circulate to help students who need this assistance. 

Background information for instructor:
This lesson is the first in a larger unit about the food culture of New York City. The unit sequence is as follows:
Introductory lesson 
Cooking and culture lessons (Uzbekistan, Mexico, Pakistan, China, Caribbean Islands, Ukraine)
Recipe design lesson
Voting lesson
The food memory activity is adapted from materials from the Edible Schoolyard Project.

Questions for students/extension activities: 
Students could expand on their food memory writings beyond this lesson. They could edit them, create a final draft, and publish them together as a class book.

Back pocket activities: None.

1st-2nd Grade

Soil Materials 
Exploration

Summary: Students will observe and describe the materials used in making soil, then will make a soil mix following a recipe. 

Objectives: Students will be able to describe soil materials using a variety of descriptive words and make predictions about how those materials help seeds to grow. 

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, self-awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Start by asking students why plants need soil. Some ideas might include: for a place for the roots to hold onto, to protect the seed, to hold water. 
Explain that today that we are going to be examining ingredients used to make soil. Explain that we are going to observe like scientists. Ask students how scientists observe.

Engaging activity:
Pass out the worksheet. You can have brief descriptions of each of the materials on the board for students. 
Perlite: a white rock
Vermiculite: a squishy mineral
Coconut shell: the outside of a coconut
Compost: made by the worms
Explain that in small groups, we will first be observing the different soil materials. Explain that they will be in little cups with tables. In each group, students should take turns passing around the cups and writing about the material they have. You can model for students some example words they might use: hard, soft, wet, dry, bumpy, rough, smooth. You can tell the students that they can think about how each material might be good for helping the plants grow and can include that in their description.
Divide students into small groups and lead them first through the materials exploration. Circulate to help students share materials and come up with descriptive words for each of the soil materials. 

Closing ritual:
Ask students to share back their observations and why they think each material was an important part of the soil recipe. 
End with a tasting, emphasizing how soil helped that food to grow so we can eat it.  

Materials:
Soil exploration worksheets
Pencils
Soil materials
Cups
Tasting

Prep work and setup:
For each small group, make small cups of each soil material, labeled with its name for the first soil observation activity.

Anticipated problems:
Students may need some scaffolding for the writing activity. First graders can write single words or simple sentences for their description. You may even want to amend the worksheet so it has a sentence starter on it. You could also provide sentence starters for students to reference on the board, such as, “This feels…” or “This looks…” to help them write a sentence. 

Background information for instructor:
These soil ingredients should be available at a local garden supply center. 

Questions for students/extension activities:
This lesson is intended as a precursor for a soil mixing activity. Students can mix soil ingredients next time to make a mix for planting seeds in pots. Once soil is made, it can be used for a planting activity in the classroom.
Students could do an experiment where they make the soil mix with different ratios, such as more coconut shell or less perlite, then test to see which one does a better job growing plants. 

Back pocket activities: None.

Beetles, Pests & Pollinators

Summary: Students will look at various types of beetles, discuss the benefits and harm of beetles, and make 

Objectives: Students will be able to explain the benefits and harm of beetles.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Quick Review: What was the pollinator/fruit we discussed in the last lesson? 
Students should be able to share:
Bees pollinate a large percentage of our fruit crops
The differences between bees in a hive (queen, worker, drone)
There are thousands of bee species (20K globally, 4K in the U.S.)
Bees pollinate blueberries, which grow all over NY state
Share pictures of different beetles (without revealing what they are). Ask students if they know what insect family they belong to.
Share beetle facts:
There are 350,000 different species of beetles (12,000 in the U.S), making them the largest group of animals on the Earth
Beetles represent 1/4 of all animals
Beetles have lived on Earth for about 300 million years
Beetles can be pests:
Eat all parts of plants: flowers, leaves, stems roots; burrowing into trees; fruits, and vegetables
The Asian long-horned beetle could cost NY billions of dollars from the destruction of wood (for furniture), maple syrup, and street trees
Benefits of Beetles: 
Beetles are decomposers, eating waste, rotten wood, and dead animals
Beetles are predators and can eat and therefore help control other insect pests
Beetles are pollinators-  Of the world’s almost 350,000 flowering plants, it is thought that beetles are responsible for pollinating close to 90 percent of them.

Tzatziki
Summary: Students will learn to follow a recipe for tzatziki, a traditional Greek yogurt and cucumber sauce with fresh herbs.

Objectives: Students will be able to work together to follow a recipe and learn a basic technique for making tzatziki. They will learn that tzatziki is a sauce that can be eaten in many different ways - with pita bread or chips as a dip, in a sandwich, or with rice, meat and vegetables.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills, responsible decision-making, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Welcome students
Introduce yourself
Explain that we are going to be cooking something delicious and healthy.
Go over class expectations to be safe, responsible, respectful and adventurous. Give all students hand sanitizer and remind them how to be clean and safe with their bodies and the tools.
Go over plan for the day  (5 minutes)
Today we are going to be making tzatziki, a traditional Greek yogurt and cucumber sauce with fresh herbs. This sauce is very popular in Greece as well as other countries in the Middle East. Many cultures around the world have similar yogurt sauces/dips, like Raita in India, Labneh in the Middle East, and Caesar/Ranch dressing in America. All these sauces are a little different but they are all made with yogurt, which is usually made from milk (usually from cows).
Raise your hand if you like yogurt. What type/flavor of yogurt do you like? Has anyone had Greek yogurt before? What about tzatziki? Does anyone want to share about a yogurt sauce or dip that your family makes at home? What is it called?
We are going to be making tzatziki today and then we will eat it as a dip with pita bread. Show students pictures of tzatziki.

Engaging activity:
Ingredients (5 minutes)
Show tzatziki task card picture instructions to review ingredients and instructions.
Safety and Materials (5 minutes)
Do a demo of handling knives, scissors, grater and the citrus reamer. 
Go over safety and the materials we will be using.
Pass out cutting boards, knives and scissors.
Follow the step by step instructions to make tzatziki - grate the cucumber, chop up the dill and parsley with scissors, cut up the garlic with a knife, measure out yogurt, olive oil, salt and pepper, put all the ingredients into the bowl and mix it up.
Demonstrate each step first and then allow the students to each take a turn. 
Use the task cards!
You can also assign one task to each student or pair of students so they can all do different tasks at the same time.
Serve tzatziki and a few pieces of pita bread  to each student. 

  Closing ritual:
Have them wait for everyone to get tzatziki and pita bread before they eat (5 minutes)
Before they taste it, have them say “Thank you gardeners, Thank you Cooks” 
As they’re eating, ask students to describe what they’re tasting. Does it taste sweet/salty? Is there anything else you would add to make it better? What would this sauce taste good with? (chicken, rice, etc.)
You did a great job trying new things, taking turns and working together! Without everyone’s help it wouldn’t have tasted so delicious! Did you have fun working together? 
Pass out recipes to take home.

Materials: 
Cucumbers
Dill
Parsley
Garlic
Greek Yogurt
Olive Oil
Salt
Pepper
Lemons
Pita Bread
Mixing Bowl
Mixing Spoon
Knives
Scissors
Spoons
Cutting Boards
Plates
Grater
Citrus Reamer
Hand Sanitizer
Recipe Sheets
Coloring Pages
Word Searches
Task Card Picture Instructions
Tzatziki pictures

Prep work and setup:
Cut up vegetables into manageable pieces.
Cut up the pita bread into 4-6 pieces per pita.
Print out recipes, pictures, task card picture instructions, handouts, coloring sheets and word searches 

Anticipated problems:
Make sure students are careful when using the graters. Feel free to give students gloves to protect their hands while grating. They also don’t have to grate the whole cucumber - only as much as they are comfortable with and then a teacher can help them with the rest as it gets closer to their fingers.

Background information for instructor:

Questions for students/extension activities: 

Back pocket activities: 
Tzatziki coloring pages
Tzatziki word searches

Task cards or handouts used: 
Take-home recipes
Task card picture instructions

Photos:
Tzatziki pictures

Cranberry Orange Oat Cookies: (First earned treat class of the year from BCCS point system)

Summary: Students will make a fun and healthy treat that they earned for demonstrating respect, kindness, adventurousness, and safety thus far in the school year during ESY.

Objectives: Students will feel proud of earning the opportunity to make cookies and will work together in order to complete the recipe. Students will taste the cookies. 

Core Competencies: Kitchen skills and related academic content.  

Cultural Responsiveness: Communicates high expectations by 1) showing that meeting high expectations brings reward and 2) expecting students to complete a recipe mostly on their own. Reshapes curriculum by incorporating group work.

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson ideally promotes relationship skills by having students take turns and share tools willingly and working well in groups. This lesson promotes responsible decision making when students use tools safely and clean up after themselves. Ideally this lesson will also promote self awareness by bringing students joy that they verbalize or physically express.

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual:
Welcome students to class, tell them that we are cooking a sweet and healthy treat today that they earned by getting enough points each class so far in the school year for showing respect, adventurousness, safety, and kindness. “I am so proud of how you guys have showed me all of those things every week when you come to Edible Schoolyard” 
Tell them that today we will be making cranberry orange oat cookies!

Make the cookies! Task Cards linked below for upper grades.

Closing ritual:

Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks
Ask students how it felt to earn this lesson, if they enjoyed making the cookies, what they would change (if anything), etc.

1st-2nd /October / Indoor


Plant Part Olympics

Keywords: plant parts, roots, stem, leaves, flowers, seeds, fruit,  soil, relay race, team work

Summary: Students will participate in a series of games and activities centered around the 6 plant parts. 

Objectives: Students will learn about the 6 plant parts, understand and be able to identify their functions and learn how to work together to accomplish a common goal.

Cultural Responsiveness:, communicates high expectations, reshapes curriculum. incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles

Social-Emotional Skills: Joy, fun, self-awareness, relationship skills and self-management

Standards Practiced:

Agenda/Class Flow: 

Intro:

“Have you ever looked at a plant and wondered how it is able to live? Have you ever wondered why plants are so green and healthy looking? Plants, like humans, have many different parts that help them live happily and be strong. We call these parts, plant parts. Can you say plant parts? Plant parts are the parts of the plant that help a plant to grow and survive. Name one part of your body that helps you to live? (Take some examples from the students; (nose, helps us to breathe and smell, mouth helps us to eat, hands to touch) Today we will play a few games to help us learn more about these parts.”

Will lead plant part dance (fast and slow, being sure to explain each function of each part)



Activity 1: Plant Part Relay
Split students into two teams. Have each group, form a single file line,  lining up one behind the other. In front of the first person in the line, place a tubtrug filled with soil. Tell students that buried within the buckets are pictures of the 6 different plants parts. Show an example of each. 



“As a team, you must dig for each plant part and get it to the back of the line and into the plastic bag. It’s almost like we are shopping for fruits and vegetables. Each person will get one chance to dig. Once you found 1 plant part picture, you must pass it to the person behind you but only by sending it under your legs. Once the last person in the line gets the picture and puts it into the plastic bag, the first person in line goes to the back of the line and the next person in line will begin digging again. Once your team has found all 6 plant parts and has them in the bag you must cross the yellow tape, reform that line and begin a potato sack race! These games are all for fun so there are no winners or losers!”

*Note: Since there are only 6 plant parts per group and there will most likely be more 12 students, here a few jobs other students can help with:

Holding the plastic bag(s) open
Holding up signs of encouragement (i.e. Go Team!)
Saying “On your mark, get set, go!”

Activity 2: Plant Part Scavenger Hunt

Students will work in groups to complete a scavenger hunt where they have to look for all the different plant parts. In groups, students will read clues and work together to find each spot that a plant part image may be hiding. Once they find each clue, students will collect the clues and try to put the image together!

Tasting: Plant Part Smoothie
Roots:  Carrot
Stem:   Cinnamon
Seeds:  Chia
Flower:  Honey
Leaves:  Kale
Fruit:      Apple/Banana/Blueberry

(*if time permits*) Debrief:

What does it mean to work as a team?
How did you all work as a team today?
Besides yourself, who do you think worked really well on their team today? Why?







Materials: 
Tubtrugs
Soil
Plant part pictures
Scavenger Hunt Worksheets
Pencils
Clipboards
Plastic bags
Potato Sacks


Prep work and setup (include photos if possible):

Set up clipboards, sharpen pencils
Put worksheets into clipboards
Set up relay
Put down tape to divide relay area from potato sack area

Anticipated problems:
Students might struggle with reading the worksheet


Task cards or handouts used: (These can be attached or linked to).

Plant Part Images (https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Ea8jA9ipd913tkQpvg5_2nvfq5RrcnKLcG-2LuPNx9A/edit)

(Scavenger Hunt Worksheet) https://docs.google.com/document/d/1_wFpujwnh4GuN8_z7-3q-oMFSeqowN5NDCAI6d8lzmo/edit

body:
Middle School Family Cooking Night: Callaloo	

Summary: Students will practice cooking greens and learn about the food geography of the Caribbean. They will share food memories that are special to their families and cultures.

Objectives: Students will be able to make connections between themselves, their culture, and other cultures. 

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction


Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills, self-awareness, social awareness


Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual (5 minutes):
Welcome students and parents to Family Cooking Night, tell the parents we are so excited to have them here and for the students to be able to show them what they have learned in cooking class this year. Introduce myself and Edible Schoolyard and give parents an overview of what we have been doing this year in class. Ask: Does anyone want to share their favorite recipe we have made?
Go over class rules (be safe, responsible, respectful, adventurous) and emphasize that today it is important to be extra respectful and adventurous because we’re making a Caribbean dish, Callaloo, and its important to a lot of peoples cultures and family traditions, maybe even some of you, so even if you don’t like it be respectful. Ask students, what is a kind way to say you don’t like something? (It’s not for me, not today, no thank you).
Pass around hand sanitizer and remind students to keep their hands clean while we are cooking and eating today.
Reminder about what we say before we eat (“Thank you Gardners, thank you cooks!”) Ask students if they can tell parents.

Introduce the theme for today’s lesson (10 minutes)
Today we will make a dish called Callaloo which comes from tropical Caribbean islands like Trinidad, Jamaica and Dominica. (Point to the countries on a world map).
Many people in this neighborhood and in this class come from or have family members that come from countries in the Caribbean. Every country, even every family, in the Caribbean makes this recipe differently, depending on what types of foods they have accessible to them/what grows well where they live, and depending on what is a family practice or tradition.Today we are going to make a Callaloo recipe that is Jamaican, but please feel free to share any other ways that you make this recipe. I would love to hear your suggestions, tips, and any special ingredients you add if you make this recipe that makes it special to you.
These are places that are warm for most of the year because they are close to the equator. The climate of a place affects what people are able to cook, because they use whatever foods can grow easily in that climate. 
Ask: What are some foods that grow well and are used in cooking in Caribbean countries? Lemons, coconuts, plantains, rice, beans, amaranth (which is a leafy green that we will be using today in our recipe). People also definitely use meat in Caribbean cooking, but there are so many vegetables and fruits that grow in those places, that it is also very easy to make vegetarian foods. We have been talking a lot this year about how to make healthy recipes with the foods that are either accessible to us in our neighborhood grocery stores or farmers markets or we are able to grow in our gardens at home, school, or in community gardens. Most ingredients in the Callaloo recipe are pretty easy to find in this neighborhood, especially since there are so many Caribbean people that live here and Caribbean grocery stores.
Optional: Show a photo of Brittney Williams (aka Chef Stikxz) picking Callaloo in her garden. Callaloo traditionally is made from a specific plant - amaranth. https://www.instagram.com/stikxz/p/CxDpPUIgV11/?hl=en&img_index=2 
Show the first minute of a video of her cooking callaloo with chicken and talking about the origins of the dish (she explains the different countries that Callaloo is cooked in). https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LOHEwYcekGg&t=3s 
Today we will first do a food memory activity to share about a recipe that we make in our families/cultures and a memory that we associate with that food. We will write and draw about a food memory, and share these back with the class when we are done. You can work on this independently, or with your family member that came with you tonight. 
Then we will cook and eat our Callaloo - I will pass out your materials and tools, as well as recipes and task cards with step by step directions to make this recipe. We will prepare the recipe at our tables, and then we will combine everyone's bowls into one big bowl and eat it together. 

Food Memory Activity (10 min)
Food is important in our lives because we all eat, but we all have different ways that we make and enjoy food. Passing down food memories, stories, and recipes is an important tradition for many communities. Writing and sharing about food is important, too!
A special thing about food is that smells/spices/ingredients can take us back to a memory and connect us to our past, our families, and places even if they are far away. I know that I have been walking down the street in Brooklyn and smelled a specific smell, like empanadas, that make me think of a specific memory where I was eating empanadas on Coney Island (or share another example). 
Show them the food memory worksheet and explain that they can do this activity about Callaloo, or any other dish or recipe that is important in their family. This could be a food or dish that you eat on holidays/birthdays/family events or a normal food you eat for breakfast or dinner that reminds you of a family member. Ask: can anyone think of a recipe or dish you will write about?
Get into groups - kids with their family members. If kids don’t have family members present, combine groups.
First, you will pick a food memory and brainstorm about why it is special or important to you. Then you can write and draw your memory on this worksheet. Tell them I will pass out some writing prompts to help them get started, but they also can write the story in their own way and don’t have to follow the prompts. Only use them if they are helpful!
Optional: Share a student/ teacher example of this activity.
Tell them that we will work on these food memory worksheets, and then cook our recipe, and we can continue working on them while the recipe is cooking and share them out with the class while and after we are eating.
Pass out Food Memory worksheets and pencils and colored pencils.

Engaging activity:
Knife/kitchen safety (2 min):
Demonstrate how to hold a knife; tunnel and claw techniques to hold down food. Remember that these are tools and not toys and we need to be safe with everything we are using.
Demonstrate how to slice, chop, and dice.
Show students the recipe task cards and review each step. Tell them that the pot at the front will be hot and we need to be careful around it. Tell them I will give them a chance to help stir but I will mostly be doing that part. Remind students to look at their task cards to see pictures for how big everything should be. Ask them to please move step by step slowly through the recipe and to ask for help if they need it.
Prep/Cook (20 min):
6th-8th grades: provide all ingredients to each table to make their own bowls of vegetables/spices and collard greens. Have them bring up their bowls to the front of the room to add to the big pot so we can cook it all together. 
Have one or two students help add the oil to the pot before we add in the vegetables and spices. Give students a chance to stir up the pot when they come up to add in their ingredients. Have one or two students measure out the coconut milk and add it to the pot when we are adding in the greens.
When the Callaloo is finished cooking, I will serve some out to each table. When we are finished cleaning up we will eat and then I will pass out recipe cards to take home.
Clean Up/Eat/Discuss (10 min):
Clean and set tables, wash tools/dishes
While food is cooking (and while eating): recall ingredients, ask for changes/improvements to recipe, ask if anyone wants to share how they cook or eat this recipe in their family. Share food memory worksheets.
“Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks!” and eat together. Continue sharing food memories with the class.

Closing Ritual (5 min): Thank parents/family members for coming today and for sharing so much about their families/cultures and the foods that they like to make. Tell students they did a great job today and thank them for their participation and energy.














Vocabulary:
Tradition: something people do that has been passed down from generation to generation
Callaloo: A Caribbean dish made with greens, vegetables, and sometimes meat with origins in West Africa.  Depending on the country, callaloo can be a stew, a steamed side dish, or a soup. 
Chiffonade: to cut into thin ribbons
Chop: to cut into small bite-sized pieces

Materials for a class of about 30 students: 
Food:
3 cups coconut milk
3 cups sweet potatoes (approximately 3 small sweet potatoes), peels on
24 cups (about 3 lbs) greens, either spinach, amaranth greens, or collard greens
Olive oil
salt
pepper mill with peppercorns
6 green onions
3 cloves of garlic
3 sprigs thyme
2 bell peppers or 3 jalapeno peppers

Tools and Equipment:
sauté pan (must be usable on an induction burner, if using)
wooden spoon or tongs
Pot holder
2 white ramekins
¼ teaspoon measuring spoon
5 cutting boards
5 knives, either paring knives or plastic lettuce knives
induction burner or stove
can opener
4 large bowls
3 4-cup liquid measuring cups
1 cup liquid measuring cup
microplane grater or garlic press
30 plates
30 forks
30 cups
Colored pencils
Pencils
Recipe and task cards
Map of Caribbean Islands

Task cards or handouts used: 
Recipe and Task Cards
Recipe based on https://www.foodandwine.com/recipes/jamaican-callaloo 
Food Memory Worksheets
Caribbean Islands Maps (see below)
Food Memory Activity Prompts (see below)

Back pocket activities:
Bingo sheet
Callaloo For You? Quiz and answer key
Coloring sheets
Show video about how to cook like a Caribbean grandma
Student Recipe Ideas Worksheet

Food/Materials Prep and Set up:
Par-boil 3 cups of sweet potatoes (about 3 small) and cut them into strips
Open a can of coconut milk and pour it into a 4-cup liquid measuring cup
Cut 1 ½ green peppers into thin strips

Prep Trays for Chopping and Seasoning (4-6 trays total):
	Food:
1lb of greens, stems on
½ green pepper’s worth of strips
2 green onions
1 cup’s worth of sweet potatoes
black pepper grinder
3 cloves of garlic (peel still on)
salt (in white ramekin)
3 sprigs thyme

	Tools and Equipment (1 tray per class):
4 large bowls
4-5 knives (plastic lettuce knives Pre-k-3rd, paring knives 4th and 5th)
4-5 cutting boards
1 cup liquid measuring cup
2 4-cup liquid measuring cups
¼ teaspoon
microplane
grade-appropriate task cards
small white ramekin

Prep tray for sauteing and set up the induction burner (1 per class):
	Food:
1 cup coconut milk (in liquid measuring cup)
Olive oil (in squirt bottle)
	Tools and Equipment:
high-sided large pan
wooden spoon
tongs
pot holder

Set up the videos on the smart board if you decide to show videos.


Anticipated problems:
Many of the families coming to this event are Caribbean, be sensitive to your own identity/ not being from this background. Try not to tell them how to cook something the right way/tell them about their cultural practices that you might not know a lot about, encourage them to share their own customs since they are the experts and listen deeply and treat them with respect. Thank them for sharing and teaching us about their cultural/family customs.

Background information for instructor: None.
Questions for students/extension activities: None. 
































































Food Memory Activity

First, think of a food memory that is special to you. 

Choose one of the prompts below to help get you started:

The first time I had my favorite food was…
A time that I ate a dish important to my family or culture was...
A time someone I loved made me something delicious was...
A time I ate or prepared something and it didn’t go as planned was...
The first time I cooked something really tasty on my own was...
A time when I tried a food I didn’t think I’d like and was surprised was…


Free write about your food memory. Include lots of details. The idea of a free write is to let the ideas flow! Try not to stop writing, and write whatever comes to mind about the memory. 


Some questions to get you thinking:
Who was there? Who cooked the food?
Where did you eat this food?
How old were you?
What was the occasion? 
What are the flavors and smells that you remember? 
What tasting words would you use to describe the food? 
What are the most important ingredients?
Why is this memory special to you? 


Descriptive tasting words:
Salty, Crunchy, Soft, Sweet, Crispy, Sour, Delicious, Bitter, Buttery, Spicy, Dry, Moist, Acidic

Food Memories

Summary: Students will share a food memory of either a time where they cooked a meal for someone else or a meal that was cooked for them. Students will share why this meal was important to them. Students will use sensory language to describe what they smelled, heard, felt and tasted?

Objectives: Students will be able to use sensory language to describe a food story/memory. Students will be able to work together to prepare a recipe. 

Core Competencies: 
Kitchen skills and related academic content - students will complete a recipe
Multicultural education - students will prepare a recipe based on Ms. Paloma’s food memory

Cultural Responsiveness: 
Reshapes curriculum - students participate in group work
Promotes positive perspectives on parents and families - students will share a personal story/memory that may be related to their home culture

Social-Emotional Skills: 
Self confidence - students share a personal story
Relationship skills - students are working together is groups

Agenda/Class Flow: 
Opening ritual: (5 minutes)
Welcome back to the Green Room!
Do a quick class pulse check/icebreaker - Ask everyone how they are doing (thumbs up/down/side)
The last time we were together, we did a mindful tasting activity and practiced using sensory language. Today we are going to practice this a little more by sharing memories or stories about foods that are important to us. 
Before we get started remember the Green Room Rules
Be Safe - keep your hands clean and use tools properly
Be Respectful - listen and be kind to your classmates as they are sharing
Be Adventurous - try new things and participate!

Engaging activity: (30 minutes)
Students will complete the food memory worksheet (15 minutes)
Directions: start with a list of foods that are important to you
Choose one food from that list and write a few sentences about a memory you have of that food. 
Things to think about: Why is it important to you? Does it remind you of a family member or friend? Was there a good experience connected to this food?
Share! Have a few volunteers share their food and their memory.
Lastly Ms. Paloma will share her food memory
As a class students will make sweet plantains

Closing ritual:
Share how we felt about class today?
How did it feel sharing your stories?
Do you feel more confident about talking about and describing food?
Discuss plans/expectations for the next class.

Knives, cutting boards
Chart paper
Food Memory Worksheet

Peel and slice plantains

8th Grade
Fall
Indoor


   Food Webs 

Summary: Students will create a food web to see how energy is transferred from plants to humans and then prepare a salad. 

Objectives: Students will be able to explain how plants get energy from the sun and that humans get that energy by consuming food. 

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations,incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: Can anyone tell the class how plants get their energy? Photosynthesis is how plants use energy from the sunlight to get food. Does anyone know why we eat plants, like fruits and vegetables? It is because when we eat plants we get their energy and other important nutrients. Animals like cows and pigs also eat plants so when we eat them we also still get the energy from the plants. Today we are going to create food webs to demonstrate the connections between us and the food we eat. 

Engaging activity: Students will work in groups to create food webs using the cards given to them. First they should sort them and make connections between the sun, plants and animals. After making their food webs, the class will work together to make a simple garden salad. 

Closing ritual: 
Clean up
Say thank yous
Eat together


Materials: 
Food web cards
String
chart paper
tape

Cut out food web cards
set out chart paper
cut strips of string for each group 

Anticipated problems: Not enough time to make salad

Tips and tricks: Show class an example of a food web before they make their own

Questions for students/extension activities: none
