body:
PK-5th Grade
All Seasons
Indoor
Year B



Mushroom Khao Soi


Summary: Students will make khao soi, a dish from Thailand, Myanmar, and Laos, and will practice cooking skills such as measuring and using a knife safely. 

Objectives: Students will be able to work together in small groups to make a recipe. Students will be able to make connections between Laotian food traditions and their own food traditions. 

Core Competencies: Kitchen skills and related academic content, multicultural education 

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson encourages learning within the context of culture. Students will watch a video to make connections between other cultural traditions and themselves. 

Social-Emotional Skills: Students will practice relationship skills by taking turns, working together to make a recipe, and making connections to other people’s cultures and traditions. 

Tags: Asian American Pacific Islander Heritage Month, coconut, mushroom, bok choy, measuring, knife skills, stovetop, blender, double period

Agenda/Class Flow: 
Opening ritual (10 minutes)
Welcome students to class. Explain that we are continuing this month to explore recipes that come from Asia and the Pacific Islands, as a way of celebrating AAPI Heritage Month.
Show a map and point out Thailand, Myanmar, and Laos. Explain that today we are making a recipe that originates from these countries, called khao soi. 
Explain that we are going to watch a video where Chef Ann, whose family is from Laos, shows us about some of the important ingredients in Laotian cooking. Ask students to think about any connections they have with what they see in the video. Watch the video together, then have students share in partners or small groups.
“I noticed in the video that Chef Ann said that sticky rice reminds her of her home and her family. While we are cooking, think about if there is a recipe or food that you eat that reminds you of your home or your family.”
Wash hands and get ready to cook. 

Engaging activity (25 minutes):
Divide students into small groups to make the mushroom khao soi paste. All ingredients can go into one bowl to then go into the blender. Student jobs can include: 
Chopping shallots and/or mushrooms with a knife
Using a garlic press to smash garlic
Measuring spices
Using scissors to chop cilantro or to cut lime leaves
Grating ginger with a microplane
If time allows, while the teacher is cooking at the stove, students can measure out the ingredients for the broth. Mushrooms can go into one bowl to be cooked first. All other ingredients can go into one bowl to then be added at once to the pot. Student jobs can include:
Measuring vegetable stock, soy sauce, and brown sugar
Pouring coconut milk and scraping the can with a spatula
Chopping mushrooms 
While the soup broth cooks, students can clean up their cooking area and set the table. 

Closing ritual (10 minutes)
Pass out tasting and try it all together.
“In the video, we saw that sticky rice reminded Chef Ann of her home and her family. Is there a recipe in your family that reminds you of your home and your family?” Have students discuss in pairs or small groups. 

Grade Level Variations:
To make this a 45-minute lesson, students will not have time to make both the khao soi paste and the broth in a 45-minute period. Choose one for them to make, and have the other pre-made. 
For PK and kindergarten students, you may want to have some of the ingredients prepped that will be more difficult for them to prepare, such as slicing the shallots or grating the ginger on the microplane. 
The lesson can be made into a 90-minute lesson by doing both cooking activities. 
For grades 3-5, students can likely do both cooking activities, then do the back pocket activity below in a 90-minute period. 

Back pocket activity:
For grades 3-5, watch this video about Chue and Tou Lee, Hmong farmers who originally came from Laos and who now live in North Carolina. Have students think about the following questions while they watch, then discuss in partners or small groups: 
Why do Chue and Tou think it is important to grow traditional Asian fruits and vegetables?
What connections do Chue and Tou make between their original home in Laos and their new home in North Carolina?
How do you think Chue and Tou are making a positive impact on their community?

Materials: 
Map, globe or other way of showing Southeast Asia on map
Video (watch from 7:00-11:07, or until 13:04 if you have time) 
Take home recipe
Ingredients as described in recipe

body:
3K-8th GradeFall/Winter
Heat


Seasonal Sides

Summary: Students will explore seasonality of crops and traditions by preparing a green bean casserole, mashed sweet potatoes, and cornbread. Middle school students will also take a closer look at our fungi ingredients.

Objectives: Students will be able to:
prepare these typical North American holiday side dishes.
understand the meaning of seasonality.
(for middle school) understand the role of fungi as a decomposer.

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Pulse check: How are we feeling? Thumbs up, sideways, down?
Wash hands
Opening circle:
What does seasonal mean? Seasonal can refer to the time of year or a repetitive pattern typically occurring at a certain time
Today our recipe will be 3 dishes that are traditionally eaten during the Thanksgiving holiday and fall/winter seasons in North America.
Culinary terms/”gems” that go with our recipe today: 
Roux - a mixture of fat (like butter) and flour to make a sauce
Mince - cut into very small pieces
Trim - remove all unwanted or inedible parts

Engaging activity:
Prep/cook green bean casserole
Trim green beans
Mince garlic
Chop mushrooms
Measure out flour and milk (for roux)
Cook /combine together as per recipe
Prep/cook mashed sweet potatoes
Peel pre-baked and cooled potatoes and add to large bowl
Add spices, milk, and melted butter and mash together
Optional if time allows: Prep/cook cornbread
Combine all dry ingredients in large bowl
In another bowl, combine all wet ingredients
Combine wet and dry bowls and mix
Add corn kernels 
Fill muffin tins and bake for 22-25 minutes
Optional lesson: Eye on Fungi 
Exploration Activity (1st-8th grades): distribute trays of different store bought mushrooms (or pictures) to each table.
Fold paper in half, then in  half again so you have 4 boxes. Draw a different type of mushroom in each box.
Presentation: (1st-8th grades) One of our recipe ingredients today is the ‘mighty mushroom’
Slide 1: What are these things on your tray/in the picture? Mushrooms
Slide 2: What kingdom do they belong to? Fungi
Slide 3: What is their ‘job’ or role in nature? Decomposers
Slide 4: What are their different parts called? Cap, stem, mycelium, gills, spores, etc. 
Slide 5: Did you know the biggest organism on the planet is a fungi? A fungi whose mycelium (root-like structure) span 2 square miles! Compare the size of Manhattan (or your local region) to the size of this fungi. 
Slide 6: Mycelium help plants by connecting to their roots and helping them grow.
Slide 7: Mushrooms are also part of a food chain. 
A food chain begins with plants as producers that gain energy through the process of photosynthesis.
The energy moves through the chain through different animals. At the end of the chain, the energy is transformed back into soil by Fungi! to re-start the cycle again.
The Law of Thermodynamics / Conservation and Energy: Energy cannot be destroyed or created (it must be transformed and fungi are the decomposers/transformers).
Slide 8: Mushrooms don’t only play a role in nature, they help people too! Mushrooms are packed with B-vitamins that help with energy levels, memory, blood cells, mood, and immune system. “So let’s eat our fungi!”

Closing ritual:
Eat and Discuss
“Thank you gardners, thank you cooks!” 
Which of the three do we like most?
Any improvements, additions, changes?
How have we changed since our initial pulse check? Thumbs up (better), sideways (same), down (worse)

Materials: 
Knives and/or scissors and cutting boards
Oven safe skillet
Measuring spoons and cups
Large  and small prep bowls 
Oven baking sheet or baking dish
Fork or mashing tool
Muffin trays

Prep work and setup:
Set up and distribute all materials on trays.
Cut in half, bake, and cool potatoes prior to class.
Pre-prep any ingredients or recipes that may be challenging for little hands.

Anticipated problems:
Completing all recipes under an hour may be challenging. Choose 1-3 recipes depending on age and time allotted. Alternatively, prepare most of the dishes prior to class and have students prep ingredients for use in a following class.
Measuring for younger grades.

Background information for instructor:
The Largest Living Thing on Earth is a Humongous Fungus
The Woman who Invented the Green Bean Casserole

Questions for students/extension activities: None.

Fungi worksheet
Explore different types of store bought mushrooms and make observations

Recipe cards (green bean casserole and mashed sweet potatoes)
Cornbread recipe
Fungi worksheet (use different parts according to age group)
Fungi presentation (for middle school classes)

body:
4th Grade
Spring
Outdoor


Pollinators, Our Garden, and Us

Summary: Students will review why pollinators are important and how to encourage their presence in urban spaces. Students will learn about a new type of activism, seed bombs, and make their own to plant in their communities. 

Objectives: Students will finish up the pollinator social justice curriculum by reviewing big ideas and by taking action in their community.

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles by giving students a kinesthetic, creative tool to be activists and changemakers in their communities.

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson teaches social awareness by teaching students about movements to revitalize urban communities using gardening. 

Agenda/Class Flow: (45 minutes)
Opening ritual (10 minutes):
“We’ve been learning a lot in the past few months about pollinators, like bees. We’ve been learning about some of the challenges that they face, and also the challenges that farm workers face.” Solicit student responses of what they remember from previous lessons. 
“Last time, we talked about how an activist is someone who makes change when they see something wrong or unfair happening. Last time, we did an art project to teach others about the connections between pollinators, farm workers, and us. What are some other things we can also do, right here in NYC, to help pollinators?” Solicit student answers. 

Engaging activity (25 minutes):
“We know from before that pollinators need a mix of lots of kinds of flowers, so we are going to make it our job to grow lots of flowers in lots of places for our pollinator friends. Even a small area of flowers can make a big difference for a migrating bird or butterfly!” 
Explain that today we are going to make “seed bombs” to help grow more and more flowers for pollinators around our neighborhood. 
“Seed bombs were first invented by a Japanese farmer and scientist named Masanobu Fukuoka. They were made popular by activists in New York City in the 1960s and 1970s, about fifty years ago. At the time, there were a lot of empty lots with no buildings and no parks. This land was abandoned.” 
“Activists started making seed bombs and throwing them into the abandoned lots. This would help wildlife, like pollinators, but it also made the neighborhood more beautiful and peaceful for the people who lived there.”
“A lot of these abandoned lots eventually became community gardens, where people from the neighborhood could spend time and grow gardens. We wouldn’t have our garden if it weren’t for the people back then who were activists and said it was important to have gardens in our city.”
Show students the steps for making seed bombs. Show students how to measure in correct ratios, how to mix, and how to shape the balls. 
Wash hands and clean up. Prepare for tasting. 

Closing ritual (10 minutes):
End the lesson with a seasonal tasting. Ask students if they can think of a place where they want to plant their seed bombs.
Ask: “How does it make you feel when you do something to take care of your neighborhood, pollinators, and other people?”


Materials: 
Seasonal tasting
Native wildflower seeds
Compost
Clay or clay powder
Water cups
Trays for mixing and drying
Plastic bags for students to take seed bombs home

Prep work and setup:
Prepare the day’s tasting. 
Prepare materials for seed bombs. 

Anticipated problems:
Seed bombs will need time to dry. You can have students make them during class, then bag them and distribute them later for students to take home and use. 

Background information for instructor:
How Radical Gardeners Took Back New York City
Seed Bombs
Recommended ratio and method is 5 parts clay or clay powder, 5 parts compost, 1 part seeds, and 1-2 parts water by volume. 

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities: None. 

Task cards or handouts used: None.

Latinx Heritage Month: Mexican Street Corn Salad (Esquites)


Summary: Students will be welcomed to ESYNYC programming, go over class expectations and…

Objectives: Students will be excited to start ESYNYC programming. Students will understand expectations in ESYNYC classes. Students will do a hands-on, engaging, seasonal activity.

Core Competencies: List all that apply: Environmental and community stewardship, social justice, garden skills and related academic content, kitchen skills and related academic content, multicultural education.  

Cultural Responsiveness: Communicates high expectations 

Social-Emotonal Skills: Display calm, caution, or care for animals and plants,Willingly take turns and/or share tools, Working well in groups

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual:
Welcome students (TIME)
Welcome to Edible! How are we feeling today? With your thumb you can say great, just okay, not that great.
Where are we today? Who hasn’t been to the kitchen classroom? With the person next to your right ask each other what do you hope to cook this year?  
Go over expectations (TIME)
In order for us to learn and have the opportunity to come to the kitchen classroom we have some rules to make it the best environment for everyone. 
What are some ways we can be safe? What are examples of not being safe? How can we be responsible? How can we be respectful in the kitchen classroom? 
We need to respect each other but we must also respect our tools, plants, etc. 
Green Room Expectations
PS 109 Kitchen Class Rules 2020
Fo
Engaging activity:
Recipe introduction (TIME)
Today we are celebrating Latinx Heritage Month by making Mexican street corn salad (also known as Esquites or Elote en Vaso). During September and October we honor the contributions of Latinx and Hispanic people, which includes anyone who speaks Spanish. 
Raise your hand if you or someone you know speaks Spanish. 
Recipe overview (TIME)
Many of the ingredients of Esquites are things that we can find growing in the school garden or on a farm during the summer.
Mexican Street Corn Salad (Esquites) Recipe
Activity overview (TIME)
To get you started, I have taken the corn kernels off the cob. You could also make this recipe by leaving the corn on the cob, which is called Elote instead of Esquites (show whole corn cob).
For the rest of the ingredients, you’ll either be cutting them up into small pieces, or mixing them together to make the dressing.
Show the items to cut; go over tool safety and use.
Peppers, scallions, cilantro
Show the items to mix.
Yogurt, sour cream, cotija, lime, spices

Recipe for one group of four within a class:
1 can of corn
¼ cup of cilantro
¼ cup of green onions
½ bell pepper
¼ cup parmesan cheese
1 tsp greek yogurt
1 tsp sour cream
½ tsp chili powder
Small pinch salt

One student chops pepper
One student cuts cilantro
One student cuts scallions
One student mixes spices, yogurt, sour cream, cheese in a bowl

Students who finish early can help other students.

Once yogurt mix is complete, add vegetables.
Activity (TIME)
Pass out trays of items to cut to each table/group. 
Adults should float to help students cut, collect food scraps or prepared ingredients, and answer questions.
As tables/groups finish the tray of items to cut, pass out the tray of items to mix.
Adults continue to float.
Clean-up/Prep for tasting (TIME)
As tables/groups finish the tray of items to mix, instruct them to clean their tables and provide cleaning supplies.
All used cutting boards, bowls, and tools should either be collected in dish bins and/or put in the sink to be washed.
Any used items with large amounts of food should be emptied into the compost/trash before being collected.
Tables should be wiped down.
While students are cleaning up, mix together the corn, cut ingredients, and dressing in a large mixing bowl.
Spoon the Esquites into tasting cups. Serve with either a utensil or a tortilla chip for students to eat it with.

Tasting and closing ritual:
Have students taste together (TIME) . Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks.
Ask students to share what they think, reminding them to be respectful (don’t yuck my yum).
If there’s time, ask students how they might change the recipe.
Reinforce one SEL practice (TIME) 
Did the student try something new? 
Did they accomplish something you can remind them of? 
Ask them how they feel after spending time in the kitchen.

Misir Wot 


Summary: Students will learn a little bit about Ethiopia and Ethiopian food, and make and taste Misir Wot.

Objectives: Students will learn about Ethiopia. Students will work together to make a yummy new food. Students will manage multiple instructions at once.

Core Competencies: kitchen skills and related academic content, multicultural education, social-emotional learning.

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson communicates high expectations by expecting students to complete various tasks within a certain time frame, encourages learning within the context of culture by building understanding about Ethiopian food culture, and reshapes curriculum by using worksheets and incorporating group work.

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson should demonstrate: self management by expecting students to take small risks by trying a new food, relationship skills by setting the table for each other and working well in groups, responsible decision making by using tools safely and cleaning up after themselves.

Agenda/Class Flow: 
Opening ritual with SEL component:
Welcome students to class
Tell them that today we will be making an Ethiopian dish called Misir Wot, has anyone ever heard of that before? Does anyone know where in the world Ethiopia is? 
Pass out the double sided recipe sheet and information sheet, point out the map of Africa to students and the picture of the plate of Ethiopian food.(For younger students, just print and hand out a nice big picture of a plate of Ethiopian food) Has anyone ever seen or eaten anything like that?
Talk about injera and how Ethiopians eat with their hands. What is injera similar to? (depending on the demographics of your students, many will probably say fufu because you eat it with your hands and it is a vessel for other foods)

Engaging activity:
Hand out pre-sliced onions to everyone and instruct them to cut them as small as they possibly can (5-10 minutes) Make sure when students are cutting onions they know not to touch their face or eyes and that they should wash their hands with soap when they’re finished. Students who finish early can cut garlic and/or tomatoes.
As students are done cutting their onions, they can bring them to the front and add to a deep pan or pot that has hot oil in it. Students should know not to come up to the hot pot unless they have been told to do so, have a maximum number of students that you’re comfortable with being by the burner and keep it below that. Students should also bring up tomatoes and garlic when ready, let 5 mixers add a tablespoon of tomato paste each, others can add bebere and salt and pre-cooked lentils a scoop or two at a time. You should have some leftover broth from cooking the lentils on hand to add if/when things start to get a little dry or to reach the desired consistency.  
Students can work on a word search or crossword puzzle when they are done with their onions but waiting for a turn to stir the ingredients in the pan. Let each student stir for at least a minute depending on how big the class is. CCrossword Puzzle (Grades 4 and 5)all students up to stir one or two at a time (the second student can be on deck and watch the first). The word search worked really well for my 1st-3rd graders, and the crossword for 4th and 5th graders, but take a look at them and assess what you think will work for your classes and their reading levels.

Closing ritual with SEL component:
Have students set the tables with bowls and spoons (whatever your table setting routine is)
Call students up one at a time to get a scoop. 
Do your tasting routine and clean up! As they’re eating, ask: are you guys enjoying this? What else do you think would taste good with or in this? How did you work well together? Did anyone show kindness to a classmate?


Materials: 
Deep pan or stove, something to sautee/mix with
Recipe
word search
crossword puzzle

Prep work and setup:
Pre-cook lentils in vegetable broth!!!!
Also-when you make your first batch with your first class taste after every tablespoon of Bebere, this is a spicy dish! Some classes may only be able to handle one tbsp, some may still be adding red pepper flakes to their own bowl after 3. Try to get a feel for the different spice levels so you can adjust to your class’ desires. For lower grades I only did like a teaspoon and a half of bebere.

3rd-5th gradeAny season

Sugar Shock / Fruit Flavored Water

Summary: Students will measure to find out how much sugar is in sweetened beverages, then make their own fruity flavored water drink. 

Objectives: Students will be able to understand how sugar affects their bodies, differentiate between natural and added sugars, and make a hydrating drink with natural sugar

Cultural Responsiveness:communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
By a show of hands, how many of us like to eat or drink things that are sweet?
Have students think-pair-share their favorite sweet foods & drinks
Ask, What is the ingredient that makes foods taste sweet? (Sugar!)
Discuss how sugar affects body (can introduce diabetes for 4th/5th grade classes) 
Discuss benefits of natural sugar (vs added) in fruits 
Introduce recipe: a drink that keeps us hydrated and tastes good because it’s made with natural sugar from fruits

Engaging activity:
Groups make fruity water by cutting/muddling strawberries (or other fruit) in pitcher, then adding citrus juice, herbs, and seltzer water
While water steeps, do sugar shock activity in group using soda/juice bottles (5-10 min)
Ask, how can we find out how much sugar is in a food? (nutrition labels)
Introduce activity: In groups, we are going to find out how much sugar is in a few different types of drinks and then share back with the class. Each group will get a drink, a bowl of sugar, a teaspoon to measure and a ziploc bag. You will read the label of your drink and the paper slip with information to find out how many teaspoons of sugar are in your drink. Then you will take turns adding teaspoons to the ziploc until you reach that number. 
Distribute a sugary beverage bottle, a teaspoon measure, and a bowl/ziploc of sugar, and math worksheet to each table. 
For students who may not be able to do the math, give them a paper that says how many grams and teaspoons are in their drink.
Groups share to the rest of the class “Our drink is __. It has __ g of sugar per bottle, which is __ teaspoons”
Share with the class the actual recommended daily limit for added sugar intake: 24g or 6 tsp sugar for children
Share how much sugar is in an orange / apple 
Ask what they think would happen if they had too much sugar every day for a long time, discuss how sugar affects body in the short-term

Closing ritual:
Taste fruit water and discuss feedback
Recap how to check the sugar content of drinks/snacks
What other ways could you make healthy drinks or increase water intake without added sugars?
Did anything surprise you today? Is there anything you learned that you want to share with your family?
Optional extension: Call out sugar facts/myths and have groups decide if they’re true or false (sugar facts included in this FC lesson). Condense as needed. Give out sticker prizes for participation

Materials: 
Cutting boards
Knives
Cups 
Empty bottles of sugary drinks (1 per group)
Pitcher (1 per group)
Teaspoons (1 per group)
Sugar shock math sheets to help find tsp of sugar
Sugar in bowls or ziploc bags (1 per group)
Sugar facts true/false sheet from FC (one printed for teacher)
Printed large example of a nutrition facts label (optional)

Prep work and setup:
Set up group trays: slice citrus into wedges, cut fruit into appropriate sizes, herbs, pitcher, cups, knives, cutting board

Anticipated problems:
Before sugar activity, remind students the sugar is for measuring, not eating!

Background information for instructor:

Questions for students/extension activities: 
True or False: Sugar facts & myths (Foodcorps)

Back pocket activities:

Task cards or handouts used:

Take-home recipes

Indoor


The Apple Story

Summary: Students read a story about apples and then illustrate the different steps of the apple’s journey to our table.

Objectives: Students will be able to explain some of the steps that food takes to get to us. 

Cultural Responsiveness: incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles

Social-Emotional Skills: social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Begin class with a tasting, either apple chips or apple slices.
Ask students to share what they have noticed about the garden and the outdoors. Ask: “Are plants growing right now? Why not?”
Show students an apple or draw an apple on the board. Ask students if they have seen apples at the store. Ask: “If I can get an apple from the store, where could that apple be coming from?” Students may answer that the apple was picked somewhere else, or that it grew when it was warm and has been saved since then. 
Explain that we are going to read a story today about where apples come from during the winter. 

Engaging activity:
Read the apple story. Have students act along with the story: pretend to plant seeds, pretend to pick apples, etc. 
Tell students that we are going to make our own apple story book. Distribute to each student one coloring page of the apple story. Students can work on coloring and labeling their picture. At the end, you can staple the drawings together in order as a book for the class. 

Closing ritual:
End with reading the completed story together once more, with students’ illustrations. Ask students to tell you what they learned from the story about some of the steps that an apple has to take to get to us from the farm. 

Materials: 
Tasting, either apple chips or apple slices
Apple story coloring sheets
Stapler or paper clips
Crayons or colored pencils

Prep work and setup:
Prepare apple chips, if desired, using the following recipe.
Prepare sufficient copies of your coloring sheets. Each class will end up assembling 2-3 copies of the book, depending on the number of students in your class.

Anticipated problems: None. 

Background information for instructor: None. 

Questions for students/extension activities: 
Students can write books narrating the events it takes for an apple to go from seed to table.
After discussing the path from seed to table of another food (peanut butter, cheese, a carrot), students can write and illustrate this food’s story.

Back pocket activities:
Have extra copies of the coloring sheet available if some students finish quickly and need a second coloring page. 

Task cards or handouts used: See above.

Kuku Sabzi

Summary:  Students will learn about Iran and make Kuku Sabzi, a Persian herb omelette, and Mast-o Khair, a cucumber yogurt sauce.

Objectives: Students will be able to compare and contrast different herb’s flavors. Students will be able to compare and contrast eggs from our school chickens with eggs from the grocery store.

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Introduce today’s recipe.
Show Iran on the world map. Explain that some people in Iran are Persian and that we are making a Persian herb omelette today called Kuku Sabzi. Talk about seasonal plants like the herbs we will be eating in class and how the chickens produce more eggs in the spring than in the summer.

Engaging activity:
Cucumber table: Mast-o Khair. 
Students will make a yogurt sauce by cutting cucumbers, shallots, garlic cloves, and dill. They will mix all ingredients and season with salt and pepper.
Corn table: Cut herbs.
With their teachers, students will use scissors to cut all the herbs that will be used in the kuku (chives, parsley, dill, cilantro, baby kale). Students will taste each herb and compare their flavors. 
Carrot table: Make the Kuku Sabzi. 
Students will study eggs from our garden chickens and compare them with eggs from the grocery store. 
Prompt them to hypothesize why they may have different colors and different shapes. 
Students will crack eggs into a bowl (wash hands right away!) and whisk the eggs together. 
They will add spices to the eggs, then mix the cut herbs into the eggs. 
Cook the kuku on a hot pan with olive oil. Cover and reduce heat to low while students help clean the table.  

Closing ritual:
Clean up, set the table, eat together, clear tables.
Ask for suggestions on how students would make the recipe different next time.
Share shout-outs before lining up.

Materials: 
Map
Recipe ingredients
Small pan with lid
Whisk
Wooden spoon
Bowls
Scissors
Zester
Knives
Cutting boards
Measuring cups and spoons

Prep work and setup:
Set up herb trays with dill, cilantro, parsley, baby kale, and chives.
Use the herbs cut from the last rotation of the last class for the first rotation of the next class

Anticipated problems: None.

Background information for instructor:
Nowruz, the Persian New Year, is celebrated in late March around the spring equinox. Kuku sabzi is a traditional dish of the Nowruz celebrations.

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities:
Talk about Nowruz and springtime. Brainstorm signs of spring together.

3K-2nd GradeWinter




Cajun Black Eyed Peas sliders 
Summary: As a tribute to Black History month, students will learn how to make cajun black-eyed pea sliders . 

Objectives: Students will be able to learn about food related to black history month and learn how to make delicious healthy food. 

Cultural Responsiveness:
1. Communicates high expectations: understanding and reading recipe 
2. Encourages learning within the context of culture:lessons / recipe with historical 

Social-Emotional Skills: 
1.self-management: Taking risks making and trying new food. Self-confidence 
2.responsible decision-making: Using tools , being aware of safety of themselves and others, cleaning up
3.social awareness: learning and discussing social justice issues 

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual (10-15 min):
Hand washing.
Pulse check 
Opening circle/discussion: 

Good morning gardeners / cooks song , hand washing  and taking a seat . Today we will be making delicious Bean burgers , with delicious sauce and CHIPS!!
Who remembers the book we read last week about George Washington Carver who used the black eyed pea to help black southern farmers build nitrogen in the soil.Today we are using the same bean to make burgers. 

Engaging activity (15-20 min):
Demonstrate prep tasks and tool safety

Phase Changes

Summary: Students will observe the three phases of matter by making hot chocolate. 

Objectives: Students will be able to explain the three phases of matter.
                    Students will be able to work together in small groups to complete a recipe.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Today we are going to continue discussing the three phases of matter. Can I have three volunteers to tell us each of the three phases and give an example? 
Solid - ex. book, flower, bread...
Liquid - ex. water, juice, milk...
Gas - ex. oxygen, steam...
Did you all know that there are some objects that can exist in all three of these phases? A great example is water! 
What phase of matter is a glass of water in? (Liquid)
If you freeze water, what happens? (It becomes ice, which is water in a solid phase)
Imagine you’re boiling a pot of water, what do you see? (Bubbles and steam, which is the gas phase)

Engaging activity:
Today we are going to prepare a hot chocolate recipe.  Something special about this recipe is that we are going to use ice cream!  By melting ice cream, we will be able to observe the three different phases of matter.
Students will break up into two smaller groups to complete the melted ice cream hot chocolate recipe.

Closing ritual:
Clean up
Say thank yous
Eat together
Share thoughts about the meal: What was your favorite part? How could we make it better?

Materials: 
Small saucepan (x2)
Whisk (x2)
Wooden Spoon (x2)
Liquid measuring cup (x2)
Dry measuring cups (x2)
Measuring spoons (x2)
Hot cups for drinking 

Prep work and setup:
Set up all materials listed above at two cooktops with the following ingredients:
Milk
Cocoa powder
Sugar
Salt
Vanilla ice cream (two 3.6 oz packages per group) 

Anticipated problems: None.

Background information for instructor:
This lesson was done with a small self-contained class.
Lesson should be done after the Phases of Matter lesson. 

Questions for students/extension activities: None.

body:
3rd Grade
All Seasons
Indoor


Fossil Fuels

Summary: Students will discuss visuals and play a game to learn about how fossil fuels are used.

Objectives: Students will be able to identify what fossil fuels are and how they are used. 

Cultural Responsiveness: incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Explain that today we are learning about fossil fuels. Define fossil fuels as an energy source that powers things that we use every day. Explain that there are different kinds of fossil fuels, such as coal, gasoline, and natural gas. Ask students to share what they already know about fossil fuels.
Explain that we are first going to look at some pictures of how fossil fuels are extracted. Ask students to look at the pictures and discuss the following questions with a partner:
Where are the fossil fuels in this picture?
How are the fossil fuels being taken out in this picture?
Have students share back. Next, explain that we will be looking at visuals of how fossil fuels are being used. Ask students to look at the pictures and discuss the following questions with a partner:
Where are the fossil fuels coming from in the picture?
What machines or electronics are using fossil fuels in this picture?
What is being produced by the fossil fuels being used or burned?
Have students share back. Emphasize that fossil fuels are used by machines and electronics such as lights, computers, and cars. Emphasize that when fossil fuels are used, they produce pollution. Ask students to share what they already know about pollution and how pollution affects plants, animals, and people.

Engaging activity:
Explain that now we are going to play a game to learn about what kinds of activities use fossil fuels or not.
Divide students into teams and have students choose a team name. Write a score chart on the board. 
Explain that one member of each team will choose a card and discuss together if that activity uses fossil fuels or not. Once all teams are ready, the teacher will call on a representative from each team, one at a time, to share what their activity was and if it uses fossil fuels. If the team gets the answer correct, they get 10 points.
Proceed with facilitating the game. Help students to discuss in their small groups when they get their cards. Ensure that students are listening to each other’s answers as each group shares back.

Closing ritual:
Finish by having students share their ideas about how to reduce their fossil fuel usage. 
End with a tasting.

Materials: 
Fossil fuel usage visuals
Game cards
Tasting

Prep work and setup: None. 

Anticipated problems:
The hot shower card is the most difficult one in the game. Students may need prompting in their small group to understand that the hot water would need to be heated by some sort of machine and would therefore use fossil fuels. 

Background information for instructor: None. 

Questions for students/extension activities: 
This lesson does not go in-depth to discuss how fossil fuel emissions affect the environment. It is meant to be an introduction to the topic. 
This lesson could exist as part of a larger unit on fossil fuel usage and climate change. 

Back pocket activities: None.

Task cards or handouts used:  See above.

Seaweed - Cooking 
 
Summary: Students will learn about nori and aquatic ecosystems, then make a sushi hand roll.  

Objectives: Students will make connections between aquatic plants and their dinner plate. This lesson will lead into a follow up lesson, using seaweed-based agar to create bioplastic.

Cultural Responsiveness: 
Encourages learning within the context of culture
Promotes student-centered instruction
Reshapes curriculum

Self-management
Take small risks in trying new foods and using new tools.
Relationship skills

Working well in groups

Using tools safely

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual (10 minutes):
Introduce a song to use as an opening & closing ritual each week:
Good Afternoon Gardeners song
Have students introduce themselves & favorite foods (or favorite ocean animal). Ask if any students have had sushi before. What about seafood? Ever swam in the ocean? Had a pet fish? Etc.
Have students act out their favorite animal, or pretend they are swimming or munching on their favorite snack.

Have students look, touch, and smell. Can they recognize what it is? Make a guess based on their observations?

Each student will make their own hand roll. Students will:
Add 2 spoons of rice.
Add pre-cut slices of veggies.
Use scissors to cut scallions.

Put in containers to take home.

Welcome & Garden Exploration:   
Gardening for All

Summary: Students will get an introduction to the school garden and learn about the many ways gardening benefits people.

Objectives: Students will be able to explore their school garden to experience and reflect on the benefits of gardening on an individual and community level.  

Core Competencies: Garden skills and related academic content, Social-emotional learning, Environmental and Community Stewardship

Cultural Responsiveness: 
Communicates high expectations: Conveying to students your faith in their abilities, Expects students to complete various tasks within a certain time frame 
Reshapes curriculum: incorporating movement, Incorporating group work

Social-Emotional Skills:
Self-management: Take small risks in using new tools.
Relationship skills: Working well in groups, Display calm, caution, or care for animals and plants
Responsible decision-making: Putting materials back where they belong, Using tools safely
Self-awareness: Display a verbal or physical action of joy

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
For all lessons: Garden, garden jobs, urban gardening, SEL, socioemotional health

Agenda/Class Flow:
Introduction (5 minutes): Welcome to the Green Room! Here is where we learn with and about food. Part of that includes going outside to the garden where you will learn how to grow and care for different plants. Has anyone ever gardened before? There are a lot of reasons why we garden. It is a great form of exercise, a way to relieve stress, and helps us have pride in something we grew ourselves. Today we are going to spend some time exploring our own school garden to get comfortable there and do some different garden jobs to help it look nice. We’ll also be doing something that benefits ourselves. We’ll check in at the end of class and see how being in the garden affected your mood.

Engaging activity (30 minutes): 
Go over garden rules:
Stay in the garden area
No running in the garden
Don’t pick/eat anything without permission
No throwing dirt or watering our friends.
Try new things & have fun!

Garden Job Matching: As a class let’s match each garden job to its correct description. 
Planting: putting seeds or seedlings (young plants) in the ground to grow
Weeding: removing any unwanted or troublesome plant
Harvesting: the process of gathering ripe crops
Composting: recycling leaves and food scraps, into fertilizer for soil and plants
Watering: adding water to plants so that they can grow

Now we are going to go out to the garden! There are stations throughout the garden where you will do each of the jobs that we just learned about. Try your best to make it to every station.
Garden jobs.

Closing ritual with SEL component (10 minutes):
Say thank yous and eat a tasting together as a class community
Go around the room and have each student share 1 word about describing how they felt working in the garden (use mood meter if available)

Back pocket activity: Garden Haiku
Have students write a short haiku poem about their favorite part of the garden
Remind students about the rules of a haiku: Haikus consist of just three lines (tercet); the first and third lines have five syllables, whereas the second has seven. Haikus don’t have to rhyme! 
Example of a garden haiku: 

I see the first leaf (5 syllables) 
On my dear favorite tree (7 syllables) 
Saying hello friend (5 syllables)
Materials: 
Printouts of garden jobs and definitions for matching game
Garden tools: watering cans, seeds, trowels, garden gloves, buckets for compost, scissors

Prep work and setup:
Make signs for garden job stations
Set up garden stations
Prep tasting - in season fruit

Eat the Rainbow: Red 

Summary: Students will participate in a skit about how plants grow, focusing on light and water. Then students will practice measuring to make a yogurt dip for fresh strawberries or apples. .

Objectives: Students will be able to measure ingredients (with assistance). Students will be able to taste new foods. Students will be able to name light and water as two things a plant needs to grow.

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, self-awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Introduce the color of the day: red.  
Sing the Eat the Rainbow song

Engaging activity:
Introduce strawberries or apples (whichever you decide to use for your tasting) and show the yogurt dip that you are going to make.
Plant growth skit:
Show the class a strawberry plant from the garden/greenhouse (or a photo of one if unavailable).
Tell each student to imagine that they are each a tiny strawberry plant. 
Ask them to stand up and then crouch down like a tiny plant.
Ask for two volunteers: one to be the sunshine and one to be the rain (give each of them a paper crown with a picture of what they represent).
Each time you give the cue to the sunshine or rain to come out, the tiny plants will grow a little bit until they are nice and tall.
Go through this activity a couple of times, doing some in slow motion and some super speed.
Wash hands.
Remind students how to properly measure ingredients and give them each a job to make yogurt dip.
Mix it, mix it, mix it!

Closing ritual:
Set the tables and serve food.
Say “Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks!” And taste.
Give every student a tasting passport. If they taste, they get a sticker!
Clean up and line up.

Measuring spoons
Bowl
Yogurt
Strawberries or apples
Honey
Lemon
Cinnamon (optional)
Wooden spoon
Paper crowns- one with sunshine and one with rain
Strawberry plant or picture of one
Tasting Passports
Stickers

Prep work and setup:
Have ingredients in easy-to-measure containers (squeeze bottles, small bowls)

Celebrating Georgia Gilmore: Pies from Nowhere

Summary: Students will learn about Georgia Gilmore’s contributions and the Montgomery Bus Boycott. Students will discuss her legacy and taste one of the foods Georgia Gilmore cooked. 

Objectives: students will be able to discuss how Georgia Gilmore used cooking as activism and was a leader in the Civil Rights movement.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Discuss what students have learned so far in their classes this Black History Month (5 min)
Ask if students know about the Montgomery Bus Boycott and Rosa Parks. If not, give some background info on segregation. Explain that Georgia Gilmore was another leader in the movement for civil rights, and she used her cooking talents to help (2 min)
Read Pies From Nowhere (10 min)

Engaging activity:
Cooking: Either do a demo of making southern style beans, or have students chop peppers and onions to make beans together. Pre-make cornbread to serve with the beans.
Upper grades: Give each group discussion cards with question(s). Everyone at the table should take a turn sharing and discuss their answers. Ideas for questions:
What did Georgia Gilmore believe in? 
What was Georgia’s talent? How did Georgia use her talents to help others? 
What are some obstacles that Georgia Gilmore faced? 
Why is the story of Georgia Gilmore significant to us today?
5 words to describe georgia gilmore
What would you say to Georgia if you met her? 
Georgia Gilmore believed all people should have equal rights. She fought for the rights of African Americans by baking pies and other foods to support the bus boycott. What beliefs would you be willing to go to fight for? What are some ways you can do that? 
Georgia’s mom said to think twice before doing anything you might regret, and never ever hate anyone. Do you think this is good advice? Why or why not?
Lower Grades: Georgia Gilmore character traits foldable: on the outside, students draw/write traits they can tell about Georgia by looking at her. On the inside, they write things they can tell by her thoughts and actions. 

Closing ritual:
Reflection questions:
What is one thing you will take away from Georgia Gilmore’s story?

Onions
White Beans
Liquid smoke

Chicken Spies 

  
Summary: Students will sing a song about the chickens, observe the chickens, and make a picture of their observations. 

Objectives: Students will learn more about the chickens, their habitat, and practice interacting with the chickens calmly and safely. 

Cultural Responsiveness: reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Tell students that they are going to meet one of the garden animals today, give them some clues and see if anyone has a guess. We’re going to meet the chickens!
Engaging activity:
Sing the chicken song (to the tune of head, shoulders, knees and toes) and talk about the different parts of a chicken's body.
Comb, wattle, wings and claws, wings and claws 
Comb, wattle, wings and claws, wings and claws
Beak and feathers and scaly legs 
Comb, wattle, wings and claws, wings and claws
Hand out chicken spies papers and explain the worksheet. Discuss the rules for interacting with the chickens (we want to have quiet voices and calm bodies so we don’t scare them away, we have to sneak up to them like we’re spies). 
Bring students over to the chicken coop and have them draw and write about their observations. Ask prompting questions about what the chickens are doing, eating, and what else the students see inside the coop. 
Closing ritual:
Bring students back to their seats. Ask them to turn and share their picture with a neighbor. Have a few students share out with the whole class about what they saw. 
Finish with a tasting. 

Materials: 
Chicken spies papers 
Clipboards
Pencils
Tasting 

Prep work and setup:
Print out the chicken spies papers and put them on clipboards. 
Arrange benches around the chicken coop, or clear some space so students can sit on the floor around the outside of the coop. 

Anticipated problems:
If it is too crowded to have the whole class around the chicken coop, split students into two groups and have one group doing a garden job while the other group observes the chickens. The garden jobs group could do a garden job related to the chickens like pulling weeds for them to eat.

   Go, Grow, Glow
Summary: Students will make a burrito bowl using ingredients that fall into each category. 
  Students will answer questions on their handouts.  

Objectives: Students will be able to apply their knowledge of molecules/macronutrients in order to prepare a healthy meal.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: The food that we can eat can be put into three categories that can help us understand what they do for our bodies. The first category are GO foods, they give us energy so that we can move. Carbohydrates are Go foods. Can anyone give an example of a Go food? THe next category are GROW foods, they help our bodies grow big and strong. Proteins are Grow foods.  Now can anyone give an example of a Grow food? The last category are GLOW foods, they help us look and feel good. Vitamins and minerals are Glow foods.  Can anyone give an example of a Glow food? Today we are going to make burrito bowls that are going to have Go, Grow and Glow foods!

Engaging activity: Students will work together to prepare the different components of burrito bowls
Go: Cilantro Lime Rice
Grow: Black beans
Glow: Tomato Corn Salsa

Microplastics & Sushi

Summary: Students will discuss and/or watch a video on plastic waste and make vegetarian sushi.  

Objectives: Students will learn the effects of plastic in our ecosystem, particularly as microplastics in the marine and human food chain, and discuss ways to minimize our plastic waste stream. They will also learn how to make vegetable sushi.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, responsible decision-making, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Review of previous lesson: Earth Day!
Last week we spoke about Earth Day and the need to protect our planet. We focused on what trees give us. Ask students, “Who remembers some things that trees give us?”
This week, we will continue our focus on protecting the Earth. Let’s take a look at plastic.
Take a look around the room from your seat. Can you find something that is made of plastic? [There is plastic all around us.]
Optional - show video: Is Recycling Worth It?
Optional - sort waste into what can/cannot go into the recycling bin.
What happens to plastic when we’re done with it? [Garbage/landfills or recycled]
Do math calculations of NYC waste.
⅓ of our garbage is recyclable.
Demonstrate pie charts of NYC waste streams.
Who takes our recyclable plastics? [China and/or developing countries]
What happens if other countries stop taking it? Where does it go? [It piles up!] 
Plastic doesn’t just disappear - it takes 500-1000 years for plastic to decompose. Lots of plastic ends up in the ocean, where it turns into microplastics. These microplastics have become part of both the marine and human food chain. 
“Matthias Rillig, a plant ecologist at Free University of Berlin, has shown how microplastics can affect organisms by altering their environments. In a recent study he co-authored, soil laden with polyester microfibers .. seemed to affect the activity of microbes that are crucial to the soil nutrient cycle.” 
“ A study published in April found particles and microfibers in packaged sea salt, beer, bottled water and tap water, making it virtually certain we are ingesting microplastics.”
“Humans could also be inhaling microfibers as they fall from the sky... Small airborne particles are known to lodge deep in the lungs where they can cause various diseases, including cancer. Factory workers who handle nylon and polyester have shown evidence of lung irritation and reduced capacity”
Should we continue to keep buying or using as much plastic as we do?
What can we do, instead? [Refuse, Reduce, Reuse!]

Engaging activity:
Today, we are going to make some vegetarian sushi to emphasize the importance of our oceans and marine life to our ecosystem! There are lots of nutrients in the seaweed wrapper that soak up minerals from the sea. We will leave marine animals out of our sushi to ensure we are not contributing (along with plastics) to their decline!
Sushi - demonstrate step-by-step sushi roll construction.
Sushi (Maki) Roll Ingredients
Rice: 1-1/2 cups short-grain sushi rice
1-1/2 cups water
1/3 cup rice vinegar
1 teaspoon salt
 
Filling Options (select 3 or 4):
1 cup sprouts (broccoli, soy, mung bean, etc)
2 scallions, sliced julienne
1 English cucumber, sliced julienne
1 carrot, sliced julienne
1 sweet potato, boiled to tender, sliced julienne
1 sweet bell pepper (red, orange, or yellow), sliced julienne
1 Hass avocado, sliced julienne
Nori sheets
soy sauce
pickled ginger
wasabi
Directions:
Place sushi mat with the slats running horizontally in front of you. Lay a sheet of nori, shiny side down, and lined up to the mat edge closest to you. Lightly moisten fingers with water and evenly press approximately 1/4-cup rice over the nori, with 1/2-inch at the far edge left uncovered (to seal later). Line the fillings of choice horizontally about 1-inch from the closest edge, across the rice.
Starting with the close edge, tightly roll the nori, rice, and filling into a cylinder, using the mat as a guide. As the sushi rolls and sticks, pull back the mat so you don’t roll it into the sushi. Wet the far edge with no rice and finish the roll to seal the sushi. 
Transfer to a cutting board. Cut each sushi into 8 to 9 pieces with a sharp knife dipped in water so the rice doesn't stick. Enjoy with soy sauce, pickled ginger, wasabi, or plain.

Closing ritual:
Tasting: Thank you, gardeners. Thank you, cooks!

Materials: 
For each student:
Sushi mats
Cutting board and knives
In individual plates:
Nori wrapper
¼ cup of rice
¼ of a bell pepper or 2-3 small peppers
¼ avocado
Ramekin of soy sauce
Chopsticks
Plastic waste (optional, sorting with students)

Prep work and setup:
Make rice
Set up individual work stations: cutting board, knife, sushi mat, ingredients
Projector and board if showing movie 
Various plastic items if sorting

Anticipated problems:
Rolling sushi may be challenging for students.

Background information for instructor:
From Fish to Humans, A Microplastic Invasion May be Taking a Toll
Whale poo is powering the world’s rainforests
Engineering A Fix For The Great Pacific Garbage Patch

1st GradeAll Seasons
Indoor/Outdoor


Leaf Study

Summary: Students rotate through stations to observe different leaves on plants, practicing their writing, drawing, and counting skills.

Objectives: Students will be able to explain the function of leaves.

Cultural Responsiveness: incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Ask students what they already know about plants and what they need to grow (light, water, soil, air). 
Tell students we are talking all about leaves today and why leaves are an important part of a plant’s body. Show students how to do a “leaf dance.”
Put your hands in the air as if they are your leaves. Take a deep breath in and out, and have students do the same. Explain that leaves also take air in and out for the plant.
Ask students: can a plant go to the grocery store? Can it eat a sandwich when it’s hungry? Explain that leaves have another job. Leaves also turn the energy from the sun into food for the plant. It’s called “photosynthesis.” Write “photosynthesis” on the board, and have students repeat the word. 
Have students mime with you soaking up the sun and turning it into food. 

Engaging activity:
Explain that students are going to examine the different leaves on different plants today. Tell them that they are going to be scientists who observe the plants, artists who draw the plants, and mathematicians who count the leaves. 
Model this with a potted plant as a whole group. Pass out worksheets, pencils, and clipboards, if using. Have them notice which station they should start at; different worksheets will have them start at different small groups. Remind students not to pick plants but to use their senses gently.
Send kids out in small groups to find the different stations and draw their leaves. Have students rotate between stations until they complete all four, or until time runs out. 

Closing ritual:
Have students share out their observations about how the leaves are the same or different. Ask students to teach back to you why leaves are an important part of the plant. 
End with a leaf tasting. 

Materials: 
Leaf study worksheets
Pencils
Boards or signs to designate stations
Four different plants, either potted or in the garden
Clipboards (optional)
Leaves for tasting, or other tasting

Prep work and setup:
Choose plants for each station. 
The plant at the blue station should have different or unusual colors.
The plant at the green station should have a scent. Herbs like mint would work well.
The plant at the orange station should have leaves that can be easily counted.
The plant at the yellow station should have an interesting texture. 

Anticipated problems: None. 

Background information for instructor:
This lesson can be done indoors or outdoors. Potted plants can be set up around a classroom in four separate areas, one for each group. Clipboard may not be necessary inside.This lesson can also be used to set up stations outside, using plants that are growing already in the garden. 

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities: None. 

Task cards or handouts used: See above.

   Apple Pie

Summary: Students will read How to Make an Apple Pie and See the World and make mini apple pies.

Objectives: Students will be able to follow a recipe to make apple pies.
                    Students will be able to understand where our food comes from.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: Can anyone tell us where the food we eat comes from? While for most of us food comes from the supermarket, did you know that many of the foods we eat come from near and far before they get to the supermarket and eventually our plates? Today we are going to make mini apple pies and while they are baking in the oven you will read about where the ingredients for apple pie come from.

Engaging activity: 
In groups students will make apple pies
While pies are baking they will read an excerpt from How to Make an Apple Pie and See the World 

Closing ritual: Students will eat together as a class. Ask students if they were surprised about the origins of the ingredients they used today. Do you think having ingredients from far away is good or bad? How does it affect our planet? 

Materials:
How to Make an Apple Pie and See the World book
Baking sheets
Silpat
Cutting boards
Knives
Measuring cups
Measuring spoons
Cookie cutter (or a cup)

Prep work and setup (include photos if possible):
Roll out pie dough
Pre-chop apples
Print out reading

Anticipated problems: none

Tips and tricks: none

Background information for instructor: none

Questions for students/extension activities:
Worksheet 

Back pocket activities: none

Task cards or handouts used: 
Mini Apple Pies Recipe

If book is not available: 
Making Apple Pie

Making an apple pie is really very easy. First, get all the ingredients at the market. Mix them well, bake and serve. Unless of course, the market is closed. In that case, go home and pack a suitcase. Take your shopping list and some walking shoes. Then catch a steamship bound for Europe. Use the six days on board to brush up on your Italian. If you time it right, you’ll arrive in Italy at harvest time. Find a farm deep in the countryside. Gather some superb semolina wheat. An armful or two will do. Then hop a train to France and locate a chicken. French chickens lay elegant eggs - and you want only the finest ingredients for your pie. Coax the chicken to give you an egg. Better yet, bring the chicken with you. There’s less chance of breaking the egg that way. 

Get to Sri Lanka any way you can. You can’t miss it. Sri Lanka is a pear-shaped island in the Indian Ocean. The best cinnamon in the world is made there, from the bark of the native kurundu tree. So go directly to the rain forest. Find a kurundu tree and peel off some bark. If a leopard is napping beneath the tree, be very quiet.

Hitch a ride to England. Make the acquaintance of a cow. You’ll know she’s an English cow from her good manners and charming accent. Ask if you can borrow a cup or two of milk. Even better, bring the whole cow with you for the freshest possible results. 

Stow away on a banana boat headed home to Jamaica. On your way there, you can pick up some salt. Fill a jar with salty seawater. When the boat docks in Jamaica, walk to the nearest sugar plantation. Introduce yourself to everyone. Tell them about the pie you’re making. Then go into the fields and cut a few stalks of sugar cane. Better fly home. You don’t want the ingredients to spoil. 

Wait a minute. Aren’t you forgetting something? WHAT ABOUT THE APPLES? Have the pilot drop you off in Vermont. You won’t have to go far to find an apple orchard. Pick eight rosy apples from the top of the tree. Give one to chicken, one to the cow, and eat one yourself. That leaves five for the pie. Then hurry home. 

Now all you have to do is mill the wheat into flour, grind the kurundu bark into cinnamon, evaporate the seawater from the salt, boil the sugar can, persuade the chicken to lay an egg, milk the cow, churn the milk into butter, slice the apples, mix the ingredients and bake the pie. While the pie is cooling, invite friends over to share it with you. Remember that apple pie is delicious topped with vanilla ice cream, which you can get at the market. But if the market happens to be closed. . . YOU CAN EAT IT PLAIN.

Compost

Summary: Students will listen to a read aloud of “Compost Stew”, watch a video about what happens to food scraps when they are composted, and play a sorting game of things that can and cannot be put into a compost pile.

Objectives: Students will be able to understand why it is better to compost our food scraps instead of putting them in the trash. Students will be able to identify items that can and cannot be composted.

Cultural Responsiveness:[pick one or more] promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: [pick one or more]self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Explain to students that today they will be learning about Compost.
Stand up if you’ve ever eaten an apple and threw away the core. Stand up if you’ve ever eaten a banana and threw away the peel. Stand up if you’ve ever eaten an orange and thrown away the peel. 
What if I told you that you could turn your apple core into dirt?  Let's read this story called “Compost Stew” and find out what happens if we put our food scraps into the compost instead of in the trash.

Engaging activity:
Read out loud “Compost Stew”
Ask students to recall one ingredient in compost stew
Show “Composting for Kids” video. Pause for discussion after each part.
Play Sorting game
Set up 3 bins in the classroom Trash, recycle, compost
Give each student 2 cards. Instruct students to read their cards and properly “dispose of” their cards in the correct bin. Students can work together to help each other. Circulate around the room while the students sort their cards into the correct bins.

Closing ritual:
Discuss again “why do we compost?”
Have students recall what was on their card and which bin they put it into

Materials: 
Compost Stew by Mary McKenna Siddals
Composting for Kids
Compost Coloring page

Create and cut out cards before students come to class
Set up 3 bins labeled Trash, Recycle, Compost
Queue up video

Lesson Title : 3 Sisters Dip & Pita chips 

Summary:  Students will learn to make homemade dip  from scratch based on the story of 3 sisters. They will cook with bean , squash and corn ( the 3 sisters ) .They will learn how these 3 ingredients work well together to make a delicious dip , just like the sisters worked to help each other . 

Objectives: 
Make dip from scratch using the main 3 ingredients (corn, beans and squash)
Learn how these 3 ingredients work well together just like the story of the 3 sisters
Discuss companion planting
Discuss food pairings (corn, beans and squash)
Discuss seasonal food 

Cultural Responsiveness: Promotes student-centered instructions and encourages learning within the context of culture :
students share what they know about a particular topic, country, culture, etc.Students generate ideas for lessons/ activities/project.

Social-Emotional Skills: 
Relationship skills:Volunteer to participate in gratitude activity
Show support to classmates
Willingly take turns and/or share tools
Working well in groups
Display calm, caution, or care for animals and plants
Make connections to outside life
Display compassion for classmates who are experiencing tough emotions 
Communicates clearly, listens actively, and negotiates conflict constructively
Social awareness:Demonstrates physical and emotional safekeeping
Empathizes with others from diverse backgrounds and cultures
Learning about and discussing social justice issues
Self management :Self-confidence: This lesson encourages students to show self-confidence when they share a story about their family’s food culture.

Agenda/Class Flow: (45 minutes )
Opening ritual: ( 10 minutes )
Welcome back to your kitchen, everyone! 
Today we are going to make a recipe using the Three Sisters story we learned last week. The Three Sisters are three types of vegetables that were grown by Indigenous people . What were they called ? The Lenape. Can someone name one of the three sisters? Take responses.
Introduce the three sisters: The three sisters are squash, corn and beans that are traditionally the three main agricultural crops of many Indigenous tribes. 
Today we are going to use the Three Sisters to make our own Homemade chips and the 3 Sister dip . 
 Explain how corn, beans, and squash will be used to make the dip and how these 3 ingredients work well together to make a simple , quick , nutritious and delicious snack.

Engaging activity: ( 25 minutes )
Making dip  
Making pita chips 
Knife skills ( cutting , chopping ) 
Mixing skills 
Working as a team 

Closing ritual:( 10 minutes )
Clean Up
Review the 3 sisters and discuss other recipes which use these ingredients
Talk about nutrition facts.

body:
K-1

Outdoor

Harvesting Friends / Cosechando Amigos

Summary: Students read a story about how a garden helps to build friendships, then do a garden job to practice working together and expressing their feelings.

Objectives: Students will be able to identify their own emotional experiences when working together in the garden. 

Core Competencies: Environmental and community stewardship, social justice, garden skills and related academic content, multicultural education

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson promotes student-centered instruction by focusing on students’ emotional responses during an activity. 

Social-Emotional Skills: Students practice self-management by identifying and speaking about their emotional experiences. 

Tags: garden community, social emotional learning, read-aloud

Agenda/Class Flow: 
Opening ritual (15 minutes):
Welcome students back to garden class. “We’ve been learning this year about how our garden is a community – a place where plants, animals, and people each work together to help the garden grow. Today we are going to read a story about how a garden helped people in their neighborhood to become friends.”
Gather students together to read the story. Before beginning, show the emoji board. “During the story, I’m going to ask you what some of the characters are feeling. These are some examples of feelings that the characters might have, although maybe you have other ideas, too.”
Begin reading the story, and pause at key points to ask how students think the characters are feeling. Invite them to come point up at the emoji board about what feelings they think the characters are experiencing. “How do you think Lupe and her mom feel when their tomatoes go missing?” “How do you think Antonio feels when Lupe catches him taking the tomatoes?” “How do you think Antonio and Lupe feel when they work in the garden together?”
“What do you think changes from the beginning of the story to the end? How does being in the garden together make the different characters feel? What I noticed is that at the beginning, Lupe was confused, and maybe even a little angry, because Antonio took her tomatoes. But she practiced being kind to him, and invited him in. And because of her kindness, the garden eventually became a place where people could celebrate, have parties, and make new friends.”
“When we work in the garden together, it helps out the plants and the animals, but maybe it also helps us to have good feelings sometimes. It’s okay if you have hard feelings, too. Today, we are going to do a job together, and then we are going to check in and see how we feel at the end.”

Engaging activity (20 minutes):
Choose a simple garden job to do together or in small groups. This could be seed saving, harvesting, amending with compost, watering, or weeding. Choose a job where students have the chance to work together and interact with each other. 

Closing ritual (10 minutes):
Model how students are going to share about their feelings in the garden today. “Wow, I feel really proud because I saw the way students were sharing the materials and being kind to one another. And I feel calm because looking at the flowers makes me feel less stressed out.”
Put students into pairs. Give each pair a copy of the emoji board worksheet. “Look at the different feelings on your feelings chart. How do you feel today after working together in the garden? Do you feel one of the feelings on the chart, or something else? What made you feel this way?” You can also model an example sentence starter on the board: “I feel __________ because….”
Give students the chance to practice talking with their partners about their experience today and their feelings after garden class.
“Thank you all for sharing your feelings.” 
Share a tasting, if time allows.


Back pocket activity:
If time allows, students can use either a piece of paper or a white paper plate to draw their own emoji of how they are feeling after garden class. They can turn and talk to a neighbor to share how they are feeling. 

Materials: 
Harvesting Friends/Cosechando Amigos by Kathleen Contreras, video
Harvesting Friends/Cosechando Amigos by Katheen Contreras, book
Emoji board, displayed at large in front of room
Copies of emoji board for each pair of students
Materials for simple garden job 
Tasting, if time allows

Prep work and setup:
Prep for your garden job of choice.
Prepare a tasting.

Grade 3K-PK

Garden Celebration: Culmination of Plant Parts and Seasons


Summary: Students will review their knowledge of seasons, plant parts, and celebration. Students will water the garden and enjoy a refreshing treat. 

Objectives: Students will be able to understand seasons/ what season they are currently in, students will be able to identify what a plant needs to grow, identify plant parts, responsibly use a garden tool

Core Competencies: List all that apply: Environmental and community stewardship, garden skills and related academic content, social-emotional learning.

Cultural Responsiveness: Promotes student-centered instruction: students will share what they know and have learned about the seasons

Social-Emotional Skills: Relationship skills: Display calm, caution, or care for animals and plants while doing an exciting activity with water. 
Self-awareness: Displays sense of pride or ownership as we reflect on our favorite plants in the garden

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
For all lessons: Garden celebration, end of year, summer
If gardening: Plant parts, seasons, water


Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual with SEL component AND with emphasis on yearly theme/connection to previous lesson.(10 mins)
Hello everyone, welcome back to garden class! I am so excited because today is going to be a celebration! Celebrating means that we are happy about something and want to enjoy and talk about it together. 
Raise your hand if you want to tell a friend or teacher when you feel happy about something!
Today we are celebrating the change of seasons from spring to summer in our garden. We are also celebrating all of the hard work we have done to take care of our garden this year!
Can anybody tell me what it feels like outside in the garden during the summer? 
Temperature: Do you wear your coat outside in the summer? 
Do you get thirsty outside because it's so warm? What do you drink? What do the plants drink?
Reminder of what seasons came before summer
The summer is an exciting time for our garden because the plants love the warm weather and we can grow so many more fruits and veggies!
But just like us our plants get thirsty and need water to grow. 
Today we are going to give the plants in our garden water to drink so they can grow!
Engaging activity: (20mins)
How do we water our plants? (have example from garden or printed diagram below)
Does this plant drink water from its leaves? Or from its roots underground?
Point to the leaves, point to the stem, point to the roots!
Now let's act it out- everyone reach your arms out far like a plant’s leaves. Reach up tall like a stem, make a circle with your arms like a flower, reach down like roots to drink water from the soil!
Do we pour all of the water in one spot or should we move as we water?
Water the garden with spray bottles, watering cans, or cups scooping from a tub of water. 
Wash hands
Because we are celebrating all of our hard work today, we are going to be trying a fruit that can be grown in our garden. (can swap out for lemonade, smoothie/ any easily available fruit ex trying 1 grape/ blueberry etc)
Wait for everyone to have food, thank you gardeners, thank you cooks, eat!

Closing ritual with SEL component: (10 mins)
We’re almost done with our garden class 
Who can share their favorite plant that you saw or would see in a garden?
I am so proud of how everyone took care of our plants today, and during all of the seasons this school year
Raise your hand if you feel proud of yourself, what about your classmates?
Thank you and goodbye!


Back pocket activity–REQUIRED: Draw/ color in a picture of yourself during your favorite season! Circle what season we are in right now

Materials: 
Watering can or spray bottle or cup 
Paper and drawing utensil

Prep work and setup:
Fill large tub with water for refilling watering cans
