Cajun Black Eyed Peas sliders 

Summary: As a tribute to Black History month, students will learn how to make cajun black-eyed pea sliders . 
	

Objectives: Students will be able to learn about food related to black history month and learn how to make delicious healthy food. 
	

Cultural Responsiveness:
1. Communicates high expectations: understanding and reading recipe 
2. Encourages learning within the context of culture:lessons / recipe with historical 
	

Social-Emotional Skills: 
1.self-management: Taking risks making and trying new food. Self-confidence 
2.responsible decision-making: Using tools , being aware of safety of themselves and others, cleaning up
3.social awareness: learning and discussing social justice issues 
	

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual (10-15 min):
1. Hand washing.
2. Pulse check 
3. Opening circle/discussion: 
   1. The black-eyed pea bean is a legume grown around the world for its medium-sized, edible bean. It is a subspecies of the cowpea, an Old World plant domesticated in Africa, and is sometimes simply called a cowpea.
   2. George Washington Carver also used the black eyed pea to help black southern farmers build nitrogen in the soil.
   3. Today we are cooking with black eye pea beans to make sliders .


Engaging activity (15-20 min):
1. Demonstrate prep tasks and tool safety
2. Prep ingredients
   1. Table one - Chopping , slicing 
   2. Table two - remoulade and Cajun seasoning 
   3. Table three - making the sliders 
   4. As a whole shaping the slides 


Closing ritual (10-15 min):
1. Clean and set tables
2. Put away prepped stuff for other class.
3. Eat, Discuss, Shout-outs & Clean up
	

Materials: 
* Cutting board & knives
* Measuring spoons , cup and knives
* Mixing bowls 
* Food processor 
* Frying pan or bake and turner spatula 
* Trays , mold shape round 
* Mortar and pestle
* Tray Set-Up:
Prep work and setup:
* Soak beans overnight prior to class if using dried black eyed peas.
* Cook beans 
* Set up trays with ingredients 
Anticipated problems:
* Pre making / having back up 
* Oven not working properly  ( use skillet or frying pan) 
Background information for instructor:
* Black Eyed Pea
* Black history Month 


Questions for students/extension activities: None. 


Back pocket activities:


Recipe: 
Black eyed pea sliders

The Ugly Vegetables

Summary: Students will read a story about a community that shares what they grow and do an art activity to share with their classmates.

Objectives: Students will be able to understand that part of the garden community is sharing what you grow with others.

Core Competencies: Environmental and community stewardship,multicultural education.  

Cultural Responsiveness:  encourages learning within the context of culture by reading a story that celebrates Chinese culture

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson emphasizes self awareness by students displaying pride in ownership in their art and showing support to other students.

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
For all lessons: garden, community, Chinese cooking, neighborhood, art
If gardening: garden theme  - community
For 216: can be single or co taught



Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual (5 mins)
Ask students to remind you what ‘community’ means. 
“Community is a group of people who come together. We’ve been learning about community this year, can you give an example of some types of communities?” - school, garden, neighborhood, classroom
“A big part of being in a community is helping others! We are going to read a story about a girl and her garden and why sharing what we grow is important. After the story, we are going to help others by making art to share with each other as a class community”

Engaging activity: (30 min)
Do a guided read aloud of “The Ugly Vegetables” by Grace Lin.
Once the reading is done, reiterate how the neighbors traded the flowers they grew for delicious vegetable soup. 
“The next spring when everyone started their gardens again, what changed?”
The neighbors grew vegetables AND flowers
“We are going to do an art activity that we share in our classroom community. Even if we are not growing vegetables or flowers, we can still make art to share with each other.”
Explain that students will not be keeping the art they make today. They will be sharing it with someone in their class. Talk about how it feels when we share or give a gift.
Have students decide to be in the flower group or the vegetable group. Students will color a coloring page of a flower or a vegetable from the story. 
At the end of class, students will pass their picture to the student on their right/left.

Closing ritual with SEL component:
Ask students how it felt to make something that they gave to a friend. Did it feel good to share your work?
Clean hands and do a seasonal taste test


Back pocket activity:
Instead of a backpocket, you can extend the art activity and give students more time to create their drawings

“The Ugly Vegetables” by Grace Lin
Paper and colored pencils for coloring
Clipboards to color on

Summary:  Students will get acclimated to the kitchen classroom, learn how to make fresh salsa and discuss the diversity of tastes and textures in the late summer harvest.

Objectives: Students will be able to understand the norms and expectations of the kitchen and garden space, become familiar with the indoor and outdoor spaces in the kitchen and garden, learn how to be safe in the garden and kitchen, and make a seasonal snack.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Opening ritual:
Welcome to the kitchen! Introduce today’s recipe: salsa. 
Explain the scavenger hunt that will help students get familiar with each part of the kitchen.
Engaging activity:
Break the class into small groups.
Hand one student a bowl for their table, instructing that this will be the bowl in which they combine their salsa.
Hand another student a clipboard containing the map/scavenger hunt tasks and another student a clipboard containing the recipe checklist.
You can label the stations with a star to make them easier to find.
Scavenger hunt stations:


Closing ritual:
Clean up, set the table, eat together, clear tables.
Review kitchen safety.
Ask for suggestions on how students would make the recipe different next time.

Summary: Students will hear “Go, Go Grapes,” a book about fruits and help to make pearsauce or applesauce.

Objectives: Students will be able to name their favorite fruits and follow along with a recipe. 

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles

Agenda/Class Flow:

(After thanksgiving break) Ask students if they ate anything really yummy for thanksgiving. 
Ask if anyone ate apple pie, or likes apples. Apples are a type of fruit! Today we are reading a story about fruits

Corn Celebration


Summary: Students will work together to prepare for a class party in celebration of their accomplishments in kitchen class so far this year.  

Objectives: Students will be able to work together in small groups to prepare for one element of the class party.  Students will learn about an important cultural crop, corn.   

Core Competencies: Environmental and community stewardship, social-emotional learning.

Cultural Responsiveness: Positions teacher as facilitator by having students work in small groups to complete tasks.

Social-Emotional Skills: Self awareness by encouraging students to take pride and ownership over their accomplishments in kitchen class so far.

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
For all lessons: celebration, Native American recipe, 
For 216: Indicate if lesson is co-taught and/or double period

Agenda/Class Flow: (50 mins)
Opening ritual with SEL component AND with emphasis on yearly theme/connection to previous lesson (10 minutes)
Welcome students back to class.  Show the class their petal chart (or whatever points system your class is familiar with).  Congratulate them on earning enough petals to earn a kitchen party!  Round of applause, snap fingers for each petal the class earned.  
“Today, we are going to have a party to celebrate your successes in kitchen class.  But parties take a lot of work!  We need your help today to set up the classroom for the party.  There will be different stations where you can help out.  (Decide whether you want to offer students some choice of which station, or just assigning tables tasks yourself).  
Introduce 3-4 stations:  these could be one or two “decorating” tables (coloring sheets, placemats, or a pennant banner), a menu writing table, a tea bag making table, etc.  

Engaging activity (20-25 minutes):
Assign students to stations to start, or allow students to pick their own stations, keeping in mind to limit certain stations based on available space.
Call 4-6 students up to help contribute to the recipe (cut cookies, flavor popcorn, etc).  Transition student groups in 5 minute rotations.  

Closing ritual with SEL component (15 minutes):
Clean up all tables and have students help put out tablecloths, wipe tables down, and add decorations and/or menus to their tables. 
“Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks!”
Students eat together.  If time allows, go around the room and ask what everyone’s favorite corn recipe is, or what they like about the recipe they tried today.  
Thank everyone for all their hard work in the kitchen this year!

Grade Level Variations:
3k/pk/k can contribute to a class mural (draw a flower or vegetable to add to the mural).  It may also make more sense to just have them do one task to help with the party, or coloring sheets once they finish with their mural.  
4th grade and up: students can help set tables and serve food
5th grade and up: students can help roll out dough (if doing cookies)

Oatmeal and the Power of Breakfast

Summary: 
Students will learn about the importance of breakfast and make and taste oatmeal.

Objectives: 
Students will be able to: 
Explain the importance of breakfast
Make their own oatmeal
Understand that oatmeal is a great breakfast food because it is a grain aka a Go Food
Identify some things that can happen if breakfast is missed (irritability, grogginess, crankiness, etc.)

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, 

Social-emotional skills: self-awareness, self-management, responsible decision-making should be shown by using tools appropriately and interacting with tablemates during the lesson in a kind and respectful manner

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:

In groups or pairs, students discuss: “What do you like to eat for breakfast, and why?”
As a class, discuss: “Why is eating breakfast so important?” 
A good breakfast should include some go, grow, and glow elements that keeps you feeling full and gives you energy to get through the day. (Review those terms and give examples for classes that are familiar, skip if they are not.)
Breakfast = Brain Power
Eating breakfast improves memory, concentration, Activity, and Creativity.
Not eating breakfast can make you feel very tired, have a hard time focusing, make you feel irritable/cranky, etc.
Briefly discuss high-sugar cereals (that they do not give you as much sustained energy, sugar rush/crash)
Today you are going to learn an important way to take care of yourself–by making yourself a breakfast that will help you have a good day!
Introduce recipe: oatmeal
Oatmeal is a quick and easy breakfast that can be customized to suit your tastes
Oatmeal is a great breakfast option because it is a GO food and gives your body and brain lots of energy to get through the day.


Engaging activity:

Demo making a batch of oatmeal for the class. Cal students up to take turns stirring the oatmeal.
Groups will get a shared bowls of coconut flakes, dried fruits, cinnamon, and anything else teacher has on hand or thinks would be a good addition


Closing ritual:
Clean up
Taste cooked oatmeal together 
Reflection questions:
What toppings would you like to try with oatmeal?
If you don’t like oatmeal, what are some other Go foods you could try for breakfast?
Why is breakfast important?

Red Drinks for Juneteenth


Summary: Students will discuss Juneteenth, the importance of red foods and drinks for Juneteenth celebrations, and work together in small groups to make their own red drink recipes.  

Objectives: Students will be able to explain why eating red foods or drinks is associated with Juneteenth. Students will work together as a team to create a recipe for a red drink and share their recipe with the class.  

Core Competencies: kitchen skills and related academic content, multicultural education, social-emotional learning. 

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson communicates high expectations by expecting students to develop a recipe as part of a team.  This lesson encourages learning within the context of culture because it is a lesson that gives historical context and that builds understanding of other cultures.

Social-Emotional Skills: Students practice relationships skills by working as a table to create their drink recipe.  This lesson promotes social awareness by discussing social justice issues.

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual with SEL component:
Welcome students to class. Engage them in a conversation about Juneteenth: Who knows what holiday is coming up? Who’s heard of Juneteenth? What does Juneteenth celebrate? 
Ask for a student to read from the board or printouts.
Juneteenth commemorates the day the last enslaved people in the United States (in Texas) were informed of their freedom, months after the Emancipation Proclamation was declared. The practice of drinking red drinks on Juneteenth comes from the enslaved Yoruba (West African) and Kongo (Central african) people, who were brought to Texas in the 1800s. For both of these and other West African cultures, red is a symbol of strength, power and resilience.  
OR, if possible watch this video.  Ask students to watch out for 2 things: What is this holiday about?  What foods do the people in this video eat to celebrate Juneteenth?:
The Best Foods to Celebrate Juneteenth (until 1:35)
Since this video was made, Juneteenth has become a national holiday! That happened in 2021. What did you learn from this video?  Ask for students to answer the above questions.  Prompt students with: This holiday is about a word that starts with an f.  They said it in the video–does anyone know what that word is?  That’s right, FREEDOM.  Write freedom on the board.  
Today we are going to make red drinks for Juneteenth.  Red foods and drinks are important for Juneteenth because it is a symbol of the strength, power, and resilience of black people in the face of enormous struggle.  We are going to make our red drinks with hibiscus tea and some red fruits.  


Engaging activity:
Pass out worksheets.  Explain to students that today, we are going to be working with our table groups to make a recipe for a red drink.  They will have to work together as a team to come up with their recipe.  Have a table leader write down what they plan to use on the recipe worksheet. Walk students through the options for fruit, sweetener, and add-ons.  
Once students have decided on their recipe in table groups, start handing out ingredients to each table.  Hand a bowl, masher and fruit to one student to start, and go around in a circle.   
Once they have finished mashing the ingredients, take mashers away and hand out cups.  Instruct students to take turns filling their cups with 3 scoops each.  

Closing ritual with SEL component:
Students try the recipe they made as a group.  Remind them that red is not only a color, but a flavor as well!  It doesn’t matter what fruit you use, as long as you end up with a red drink.  
Recap intro: why did we make red drinks today? What is significant about the color red? 

Materials: 
Ingredients
Cutting boards and knives
Juicer
Mixing bowls, mixing spoons
pitchers
cups


Prep work and setup:
Write a message on the board or make a handout if you’d like.

4th-5th Grades
Spring
Outdoor
Year 1


Fannie Lou Hamer


Summary: Students will learn about Fannie Lou Hamer’s Freedom Farm Cooperative and the importance of choice in garden work.

Objectives: Students will learn about self-determination as a means of activism and do a choice time garden job

Core Competencies: Environmental and community stewardship, social justice, multicultural education.

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson encourages learning within the context of culture by providing an example of leadership and resistance in the Black community

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson helps students to develop social awareness by learning about a Black leader and self awareness to take autonomy in the garden.

Agenda/Class Flow: (45 minutes)
Opening ritual: (20 minutes)
“This spring, we have been learning about lots of food and earth heroes whose passions for helping others and the planet make the world a better place. Does anyone remember who we have learned about so far?”
Take answers. Students have learned about Vandana Shiva creating a seed bank, Native Americans practicing interconnectedness, and the Black Panther Party creating the free breakfast program.
“Today we are going to be learning about another earth hero named Fannie Lou Hamer. Fannie Lou Hamer was an organizer and activist and fought for Black people in Mississippi and across the country to have the right to vote.”
“At this time, even though Black people were no longer slaves, they still worked on the farms of white people for very little money. They almost never made enough money to leave these jobs and start a farm of their own. Fannie Lou Hamer grew up on a farm like this one and had to drop out of school when she was in 6th grade to work.”
“We are very lucky to come into this garden, do fun jobs, and take breaks when we need to. We can do that because this garden is our space so we can make our own choices. How do you think that is different from how Fannie Lou Hamer grew up?”
Take student responses
“Fannie Lou Hamer believed in self-determination, which means giving people the resources they need to thrive without relying on someone else. So she started the Freedom Farm Cooperative.
“Fannie Lou Hamer saw a problem and decided to fix it. Thanks to a big donation, she purchased land in Mississippi and gave it to poor Black people. Families paid $1 a month to be a part of this community. They grew plants to sell for money and vegetables to eat. They also started a ‘pig bank’ where pigs were donated to families in need to raise and eat. This may sound gross or wrong to kill an animal, but people need food to survive and this was a very smart way to help people do that.”
“Fannie Lou Hamer shows us how gardening and farming can help us take care of ourselves and our community. Today we are going to celebrate her legacy by doing a choice time garden job. You will have three choices of ways to help in the garden. 
Have students sign up for different garden jobs
Prompt students to think of some way that they are grateful for the garden and also how it feels to have choice in their garden job.

Engaging activity: (15 minutes)
Students split up for garden jobs. 
Wash hands and gather again.

Closing ritual: (10 minutes)
Ask students to share how they feel grateful for and connected to the garden. Ask how it felt to be able to have self-determination over their garden jobs.

Materials: 

Prep work and setup:
Set up seasonal garden jobs
Create a board for students to sign up for their jobs

https://www.womenshistory.org/education-resources/biographies/fannie-lou-hamer
https://snccdigital.org/events/fannie-lou-hamer-founds-freedom-farm-cooperative/

Pre-K to 2nd GradeFall/Spring
Outdoor


Sensory Scavenger Hunt and 
Orientation

Summary: Students are welcomed to the garden and taught garden expectations. They explore the garden on their own, using a “Sensory Hunt” worksheet to guide them to identify things they can see, hear, touch, and smell.

Objectives: Students learn garden rules and reinforce their understanding of the five senses through guided exploration of the garden.

Core Competencies: garden skills and related academic content  

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles by giving students the chance to explore the garden with their five senses. 

Social-Emotional Skills: Students practice self-management by practicing safety in the garden and by gaining comfort in a new, unfamiliar environment. 

Agenda/Class Flow (45 minutes):

Opening ritual (15 minutes):
Welcome students. Ask students if they can name the five senses. 
Go over each of the five senses doing gestures to act out each one (shaking out your hands for touch, tapping your nose for smell etc.).
Ask students to make predictions for each of their senses about what they will see out in the garden (what things can we touch or feel in the garden, what things will we hear in the garden). 
Talk about how the garden is like the classroom even though it doesn’t look like the classroom! They will still walk instead of running and use one voice at a time.
In addition, the garden is full of special living things that need special care. Explain how students should walk on paths and not on beds, how they should not pick anything unless their teacher tells them to, and they should be kind and gentle to bugs, including bees. Talk about how to freeze when a bee is nearby.

Engaging activity (20 minutes):
Hand out the scavenger hunt papers. Read over the 4 boxes together as a class. Tell the students they won’t be tasting during the hunt but we will taste all together at the end. 
Before sending students to complete their sensory hunt, review the rules for the garden (how to be safe for ourselves, the plants, and the animals). 
Send students out to the garden to complete their sensory hunt. Circulate to point things out in the garden to students, and help remind them of the garden rules. 

Closing ritual (10 minutes):
Bring everyone back to their seats. Give students a few more minutes to finish their drawings and help them label their drawings if possible. 
Have students share out about what they saw in the garden. 
Finish with a tasting. 

Materials: 
Clipboards
Pencils
Sensory Scavenger Hunt papers
Tasting

Prep work and setup:
Print the sensory scavenger hunt papers and put them on clipboards.

4th GradeFall/Winter
Indoor


Food Miles Game 

Summary: Students will play a game in which they are assigned miles based on foods they choose.  They will evaluate whether the winner of the game should be the team with the most or least miles based on their discussion of the impact of each kind of food. 

Objectives: Students will identify parts of the world that their food comes from, understand that out-of-season food travels a great distance to get to them, and weigh the benefits of buying food locally.

Core Competencies: Garden skills and related academic content

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles by playing a kinesthetic game to understand content.

Social-Emotional Skills: Students practice social awareness by learning about and thinking critically about the impacts of how our food travels. 

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Pose a question to the students about a fruit or vegetable that is out of season and why it’s not in the garden right now. “For example, right now if I go look in the garden right now I won’t find any strawberries. Why not?” Take students' ideas. 
“The grocery store does have strawberries though! If it’s too cold to grow strawberries here then how does the grocery store have strawberries?” Take students’ ideas. 

Engaging activity:
Introduce the term “food miles” and hand out food miles maps. 
Read through the locations and how many miles away they are on the food miles map together as a class. 
Have students flip over the food miles map and read the back. The first column tells us when each food is available in New York, and the second tells us where the food comes from when it’s not available in New York. Read through a few examples of foods from the chart as a class. 
Explain the rules of the game:
There will be two teams. One member from each team will come up to draw a card. The card will tell you what food you are eating and what month you are eating it in. Teams will look at the food miles chart to figure out if that food came from New York or somewhere else and how many miles it traveled.
Put labels on the floor to represent the food coming from different places (i.e. a marker right at the front of the room to show New York--0 food miles, and one far away to show that it’s coming from Chile--5,000 food miles. When students pick a card and figure out where the food comes from, they go stand by that label on the floor and pretend to fly or drive the food back to the teacher in New York. 
Play several rounds of the game, keeping track of how many food miles each team has, as permits. 
When the game is over tally up the food miles for each team. Ask the students which team won. Is it better to have more food miles or less? Discuss the pros and cons with the students (more food miles means more pollution from transporting the food, less food miles means less variety--we wouldn’t have mangoes or bananas).

Closing ritual:
Challenge students to look at the stickers on fruits or vegetables the next time they are home or at the store to figure out where the different foods they eat are coming from. 
Finish with a tasting. 


Materials: 
Dry erase board and markers
Food Miles Map and Cards  
Food Miles markers
Seasonal tasting

Prep work and setup:
Print out food miles maps for all the students. 
Print out food miles cards and cut them up for students to draw during the game. 
Make food miles markers. 

Anticipated problems: None. 

Background information for instructor: None

Questions for students/extension activities: 
Have students write persuasive essays about if it is better to eat foods from far away, local foods, or a mix. 
Have students collect stickers from fruits or vegetables and bring them into class to share out about where their food comes from.

The Honeybee Man


Summary: Students will read a story, then do an art activity to learn about how bees help fruits to grow. 

Objectives: Students will be able to describe the relationships between honeybees, people, and flowering plants. 

Core Competencies: Environmental and community stewardship, garden skills and related academic content

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson encourages learning within the context of culture by using the story of an urban beekeeper to illustrate environmental concepts, providing a relatable narrative to New York City students. 

Social-Emotional Skills: Students practice relationship skills by learning to empathize with and understand the role of a common insect that may commonly provoke fear or anxiety.

Tags: pollinators, honeybees, arts and crafts, urban gardening, New York City

Agenda/Class Flow: 
Opening ritual (15 minutes):
“All year, we’ve been learning about how important relationships are in our school garden. Today we are going to learn about the relationship between people, plants, and a garden insect: bees.”
Have students turn and talk to a neighbor about how they feel about bees: scared, curious, nervous, happy? Empathize with students’ feelings, then explain that we are going to read a story that might change our ideas about bees.
Read The Honeybee Man by Lela Nargi and Kyrsten Brooker. Talk about the relationships shown in the book.
“How did the bees make the honey? Did they do it alone, or did they need to find something to make the honey?”
“How did Fred the beekeeper help the bees? How did the bees help him?” 
“What is Fred doing with all the extra honey? Is he keeping it all for himself?”

Engaging activity (20 minutes):
“In the book, we saw that the honey bees made honey that Fred could share with his neighbors. In turn, Fred gave the bees a home and took care of them and made sure they were safe. We are going to do a little art project to learn how bees help the plants, too.”
“Do you remember in the book when it said that the bees went to visit flowers on pea plants and blueberry bushes? When the bees drink from those flowers, it helps the flowers change into something else: a fruit!” Show one coloring page visual, with the flower on one side and the fruit on the other. “Without bees, plants wouldn’t be able to make fruits.” 
Have students pretend to be bees: buzzing, pretending to have wings, etc. Have students each “visit” a flower to pollinate it and make it into fruit. 
You can hang the coloring sheets, flower side out, around the classroom and have students go pick one in small groups. 
Or, students can come up in small groups to pick a flower from you as the teacher. 
Have students look on both sides of the coloring sheet. “What fruit does your flower turn into?” 
Have students color both sides of their coloring page. Older students can also label their drawings. 

Closing ritual (10 minutes):
Have students turn and talk to a neighbor again: “What is something new that you learned about bees today? Did your feelings about bees change today? How did they change?”
End with a honey tasting. 


Back pocket activity:
Roll out 1-3 pieces of mural paper, about 5 feet long each, depending on the size of your class. Tell students that we will be making a piece of artwork all together of the story that we read. Ask students to identify important parts of the story that they saw: the beekeeper, the neighbors, the bee, the beehives, the plants, the buildings, etc. Have students work together to draw a mural showing all these parts working together in the story. 

Materials: 
The Honeybee Man by Lela Nargi and Kyrsten Brooker
Coloring pages
Colored pencils, crayons, or other coloring materials
Honey for tasting, either in cups or using small disposable wooden spoons

Prep work and setup:
Make photocopies of coloring pages. Print double-sided, short-edge binding, so that the correct fruit and flower will be on both sides of the half-sheet of paper.
Set up honey tasting. 

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable: None. 

Photos, if applicable: None.

Foods From Around the World: Mexico

Summary: Students will do a reading and watch a video about the culture, geography, and typical ingredients from Mexico, as well as how this food culture is present in New York City. Students then prepare and taste a quick recipe that is from this country.

Objectives: Students will be able to make connections between themselves, their culture, and other cultures. 

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Tell everyone that we are learning about Mexico and the Mexican American community in New York City.
Hand out the reading. Read it aloud to students as they follow along. Take student questions and comments about what surprised them or what they connected to.
Watch the video. Again, have students share their questions or comments. 
Explain the recipe of the day. Use hand sanitizer and remind students about norms for health and safety while cooking. 

Engaging activity:
Prepare the recipe with students divided into small groups at tables. Each table will be following the same recipe. 
Fill tasting cups and sit down back as a large group.

Closing ritual: 
Finish by tasting what students have made together.
Have students share tasting words about the tasting.

Materials: 
Projector, Smartboard, or other means of showing video
Copies of Mexico reading
First video
Second video
Take-home copies of mango jicama salad
Hand sanitizer
Trays per each table with the following:
Ingredients from recipe
Cutting boards and knives
Citrus juicer
Measuring spoons
Bowl for ingredients
Recipe copy
Cups, plates, and utensils for tasting

Prep work and setup:
Set up trays with materials and ingredients. This recipe should be tripled for a class of 32 students, so divide up ingredients accordingly, depending on your number of small groups.

Anticipated problems:
It is likely that you will not have an adult to lead each small group. Assign one student to be the table leader for each group. The table leader can give out jobs, pass out materials, and ensure the recipe is followed. 
In small groups, have students mix all ingredients into a large bowl, then divide up into their individual cups.

Background information for instructor:
This lesson is the first in a larger unit about the food culture of New York City. The unit sequence is as follows:
Introductory lesson
Cooking and culture lessons (Uzbekistan, Mexico, Pakistan, China, Caribbean Islands, Ukraine)
Recipe design lesson
Voting lesson
Alternatively, you can make a bionico recipe with students. Student table instructions are here.

All Grades / Fall


Achik Chuchuk and Non

Summary: Students will share learn a little bit about Uzbekistan, make a achik chuk, non, and readjust to being in the kitchen.

Objectives: Students will use a world map to see where Uzbekistan is and identify a country where their family is from. They will cut and season tomatoes and form non bread.

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening Ritual:
 Where in the world do our classmate’s families come from? We come from all over! And when people travel, they bring ingredients and their food traditions with them. Look at the world map all together. Today we are making food from Uzbekistan (raise hands if you know anyone from here), and this country has delicious recipes. Their recipes have changed over time with people moving to and from Uzbekistan and with trade (ask for examples of trading).
For example: Today we will cook Achik Chuchuk and Non bread. Raise your hand if you like to eat non. Can anyone describe what it is like? Many of Uzbekistan’s neighbor’s eat non (Tajikistan, Afghanistan). Achik chuchuk is tomato salad but guess what?! Tomatoes originated in Peru! In ancient times, trade allowed for people to bring tomato seeds to Asia and eventually to Uzbekistan. This recipe relies on its neighbor countries and even far away countries to get ingredients
Review Kitchen Pledge and Norms:
Safety
Cleanliness
Kindness and Respect
Fun!


Engaging Activity

Cucumber table: knead bread and assemble loaves with classroom teacher and volunteer
Corn table: Find out where everyone comes from and map it! Ask a series of questions to get a sense of what countries students families come from. Write up a list and mark them on a map. Tell students that we will show how all of these countries and their food are related this year. 
Carrot table: Knife skills- making tomato salad with kitchen teacher

Closing Ritual
Clean up, set the table, eat together, clear tables.


Materials:
Recipe
Map for corn table, question to prompt, large world map, sticker dots to mark map
Tomatoes, cucumbers, red onions, dill, cilantro, salt, cayenne, AP flour, WW flour, yeast, salt
Baking sheets/pizza stones, fork/Uzbek bread stamp, measuring spoon, knives, cutting boards, spoon, bowls


Prep work and setup (include photos if possible):

Make non dough ahead of time (must rise for 2 hours)
Cut veggies into appropriate age group

Anticipated problems:
Teachers may not be comfortable taking on the bread forming. We will provide a pictorial guide for them to follow.
Helping students who don’t know where they are from feeling left out



Tips and tricks:

Make sure every student gets to put a sticker on the map-- they are from Brooklyn and that is something to be proud of if they don’t have connections to another culture.

Background information for instructor:
Uzbekistan is a country in Central Asia whose Ancient cities of Samarkand, bukhara, and Khiva were once at the crossroads of the Great Silk Road, connecting Asia and Europe. 
Its neighboring countries are Turkmenistan, Afghanistan, Tajikistan, and Kazakhstan
Uzbekistan is two thirds desert, and the rest is mountains and semi-arid plains. 
Uzbekistan’s fascinating mix of Mongol, Turkic, and Iranian ancestry and 150 years of Russian and Soviet rule inform its culture and cuisine (from Ken Albala’s “Food Cultures of the World” Encyclopedia)
Uzbekistan's largest festival and holiday is Eid al-Adha. It occurs 70 days after Ramadan, and it celebrates God’s mercy in providing the sacrificial lamb. Another important holiday is the Iranian folk holiday, Nauruz Bayram, which celebrates the vernal equinox.

Harvesting and Weight 

Summary: Students harvest and weigh food from the garden. 

Objectives: Students learn the basics of weight in relationship to the harvest.

Cultural Responsiveness: reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Welcome students. Ask students if they know what season it is. During the fall there are many fruits and vegetables growing in the garden. 
Write the word harvest on the board. Have students practice saying harvest out loud. Discuss what it means to harvest something. 
Engaging activity:
Pass around a few different items that have been harvested from the garden (or that could be harvested during the fall). Have students feel and smell them. Bring out a scale. Explain that we use scales to tell us how much something weighs because sometimes as gardeners we want to know how much food our garden has made. Pass around two different items harvested from the garden, this time one very light and one much heavier. Ask students as they’re passing them around to guess which one is heavier. 
Put the vegetables on the scale and compare their weight. You can practice simple math with the students by having them add how much they weigh all together (if this pumpkin is two pounds and this kale is 1 pound how much is that all together--holding up your fingers to model it).
Take students out into the garden to practice a harvest job. When finished harvesting weigh the vegetables and tell them how much they harvested. 
Closing ritual:
Thank students for harvesting today. Ask them what was the best fruit or vegetable they saw growing in the garden today. 
Finish with a tasting. 

Materials: 
Various fruits and vegetables already picked from the garden
Board and markers
Scale
Harvesting materials (bowls or buckets, scissors if needed etc.)
Tasting

Prep work and setup:
Harvest some things from the garden in advance.
Set out materials for a harvesting job. 

Anticipated problems: 
Have students harvest one vegetable or leaf at a time so you can watch their work as they go. Often students will want to take out a whole plant rather than harvesting just one part.

Lotion & Agar Soap  
 
Summary: Students will create two cosmetics recipes & make connections to Dr. George Washington Carver.

Objectives: Students will learn about some of Dr. Carver’s achievements, and create their own natural cosmetics recipes.

Cultural Responsiveness: 
Communicates high expectations:
expect students to read and complete a recipe on their own
Conveying to students your faith in their abilities
Expects students to complete various tasks within a certain time frame 
Encourages learning within the context of culture
Topic allows students to connect to and reflect on their own heritage and cultural experiences
Promotes student-centered instruction:
have students offer suggestions for recipes
ask students to cooperate/distribute tasks for each of their table members
Reshapes curriculum:
Incorporating viewpoints and perspectives that have historically been absent from education (i.e. centering people of color and the experiences of people of color throughout history; books highlighting people of color or with people of color as protagonists)

Social-Emotional Skills: 
Self-management
Take small risks in trying new foods and using new tools.
Relationship skills
Willingly take turns and/or share tools
Working well in groups
Make connections to outside life
Responsible decision-making
Cleaning up messes
Putting materials back where they belong
Using tools safely
Self-awareness
Displays sense of pride or ownership	

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual (5 minutes):
Wash hands with jelly soap & let students know that they are about to make their own.
Hands & surfaces need to stay clean to avoid contamination.

Engaging activity (30 minutes):
Introduce the activity. 
Last week, we made agar jelly. This week, we are using similar ingredients to make soap. This is a vegan recipe (since many soaps contain animal fats) and is safe to use on our skin, but is NOT for eating.
Agar Soap
Instructors will boil a pitcher of water before the class. 
Mix agar with hot water in a large bowl.
Incorporate the rest of the ingredients.
Pour the mixture into silicone molds.
Set in the refrigerator for at least 2 hours.
Tidy up. 
Introduce the next activity.
Ask students what they know about Dr. George Washington Carver.
Though he was a brilliant scientist, he patented very few of his inventions. One of these is a pomade made from peanuts. We will create a lotion from natural ingredients.
Lotion
Mix 2 parts shea butter, 1 part coconut oil, and 1 drop of peppermint oil together.
Students can use a popsicle stick to mix in a 2oz container.

Closing ritual (5 minutes):
Pack soap & lotion into a paper bag with the recipe card.
Have students help tidy up.

Materials: 
Paper bags
Recipe cards
2oz containers
Popsicle sticks for mixing lotion
Lotion: 
Shea butter
Coconut oil
Peppermint oil
Soap:
Agar agar
Castille soap
Hot water
Glycerin
Peppermint oil

Prep work and setup:
Boil a pitcher of water

Anticipated problems:
Hot mixture - make sure to cool enough for students to use.
Messy & greasy - could present tactile issues for some students.

Background information for instructor:
Read up on Dr. Carver!

Questions for students/extension activities: 
Students can create multiple mixtures.

body:
PreK-8th GradeFall/Winter
Heat


Lunar New Year and   
Vegetable Dumplings 

  
Summary: Students will learn about Lunar New Year and how to make dumplings.

Objectives: Students will be able to:
Discuss what Lunar New Year is
understand the difference between pan fried and steamed dumplings.
discuss the different types of dumplings made throughout various cultures.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations by expecting students to complete several tasks with minimum oversight, encourages learning within the context of culture and incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles by discussing Lunar New Year and Chinese food traditions, making dumplings.

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, social awareness should all be exemplified by students managing their own tasks, working well together at their tables, using their tools safely, and being kind and encouraging to their peers.

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual (10-15 min): 
Hand washing
Pulse check:How is everyone feeling today?
Opening Circle/Discussion: 
Lunar New year is coming up so we are going to be making dumplings! Has anyone ever heard of Lunar New Year? What is it? Where is it celebrated? People who celebrate Lunar New Year often celebrate by making and/or eating dumplings.
Has anyone ever heard of or tried a dumpling before? 
Dumplings are small pieces of dough wrapped around a filling of veggies, sweets, meats or fish. Dumplings can be made by boiling, steaming, simmering or frying. 
What types of different dumplings have you had or heard of?
Carribean/Latin America: empanada 
Puerto Rico: pasteles
Poland: pierogi 
Italy: ravioli and tortellini
Today we will be making Asian style dumplings, which are often made and eaten by Chinese families on the eve of the Lunar New Year which falls at the end of January or early February. 

Engaging activity (15-20 min): 
Cooking demonstration: show ingredients, tools, and how to safely use tools to prep vegetables
Dumpling Prep 
Depending on capabilities of classes: for lower grades (k-2 or 3) have a couple students mash tofu, have a table or 2 cutting scallions, and a table cutting cabbage if appropriate. (Teacher prepare everything else before that) Have students bring up the ingredients they prepared one table at a time and (again, depending on age of students) let students take turns mixing on the stove with the spatula. Let each table add an additional ingredient that was already prepared: first table that comes up can do garlic and/or ginger and soy sauce, next could do tofu and scallions and rice vinegar, then carrots and cabbage, etc. as appropriate. Older grades could definitely cut cabbage and grate carrots as well.
Once the filling has been prepared, demo how to fold the dumplings for the whole class:
Take one wonton wrapper and place ~1 tsp of filling in the center of the dough. 
Wet edges of wrapper and fold in half around the filling. Pinch and seal the edges.
Check for understanding and clarify any questions, Cook dumplings in whatever way makes sense for you. What I did was have students come up to the steamer whenever they had one that was ready to be steamed and fill up one layer of the steamer at a time. I made it very clear to students that they had to remember where in the steamer they put the dumpling and then when they were giving it to me I would tell them which layer of the steamer it was going in. Fill one layer at a time, students wait at their tables for that layer to be done and to be called back up to get them.


Closing ritual (10-15):
Clean and set tables
Eat, Discuss, Shout-outs & Clean up 

Materials: 
Tray set-up:
Knives & cutting boards
Box graters & shredders
Garlic press
Measuring spoons & cups
Frying pans
Steam baskets
Tongs
Bowls of water for dipping fingers
Bowls of soy sauce for tasting
Plates for students to fold their dumplings on and eat off of

Prep work and setup:
For 3K and Pre-K, create a batch of dumpling filling before class so students can just stuff dumpling wrappers

Anticipated problems:
Have students use extreme caution with box graters and shredders 
Younger grades may need help with tools and stuffing/sealing dumplings

Background information for instructor:
Symbolic foods eaten during the Lunar New Year
Dumplings are made all over the world.
Puerto Rican dumplings pasteles.
Italian dumplings: ravioli & tortellini.

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities:
None.

Task cards or handouts used: 
Recipe cards

Photos:


Other notes:
For 216 single period class flow:
Students will cut cabbage and scallion with scissors and/or mash tofu. These prepped vegetables will be sauted after class and used as filling for the next class.
Per class: ½ of cabbage; 1.5 cup shredded carrot, 1 bunch of scallions (5-6 scallions), 0.5-1 block of tofu
Before class:
Wash and prep cabbage and scallion
Pre-saute filling
Prep dumplings for steaming to do during class
Prep back pocket activities: coloring sheet for PK-1, dumplings around the world for 2-5

After students cut vegetables and mash tofu, distribute water cups, plates of filling, and dumpling wrappers. Explain how to wrap the dumpling  - not too much filling, not too much water.
Students can make as many dumplings as they want 
Prepare to eat. Optional to give students ramekins of soy sauce for the table.
Dumpling folding visuals!

body:
Grade 3K/PK
Season: Fall/Winter
Indicate: Indoor
Indicate: Year A/Year B

Roly Poly Lunch

Summary: Students will learn about roly polys (also known as pillbugs), and their role as decomposers in the garden.  

Objectives: Students will be able to identify the roly poly and the various kinds of food it eats from the garden.  

Core Competencies: Environmental and community stewardship, garden skills and related academic content.

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles by engaging students in a variety of ways–through puppets, an interactive activity, and song.

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson helps students practice relationship skills, by displaying calm, caution or care for animals and plants.  

Garden Connection: This lesson uses garden materials and explains the foundations of composting, an important function of the garden space.  

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
For all lessons: compost, winter lessons, indoor lessons

If gardening: compost, animals, stewardship, roly polys, worms

Agenda/Class Flow: (50 minutes)
Introduction: Welcome students back to class by sitting in a circle on the carpet or in the garden.  Sing an intro song, or greet students individually as they find their seats.  
Ask students:  what season is it?  Comment on the weather outside, and what the garden looks like.  (3 minutes)
Today, we’re going to learn about roly polys!  Make a rolling motion with your arms and ask the class to copy you.  Everyone say “Roly Poly!”
The roly poly lives in the garden.  Introduce the class to the roly poly finger puppet. 
Ask the class:  “What does the roly poly eat in the garden?”  Ask for some guesses (Does he eat carrots?  Peas?  Kitty cats?  Think of something stinky in the garden…)  
“That’s right!  The roly poly eats compost.  Can you say compost?”
“Ok, let’s feed the roly poly some compost lunch.”  Place the covered lunch tray in the middle of the circle and carefully unveil the roly poly’s lunch.
“Yum!  The roly poly is hungry!  What does the roly poly eat for lunch?”  Call on raised hands for guesses, or popcorn style, whatever works best for the group.  When a student identifies an item on the tray correctly, ask them if they want to help feed the roly poly today, and hand them this item from the tray.  Make a “yum yum yum” sound as the roly poly “eats” the food, and then place it back on the tray and ask again: “what else does the roly poly eat for lunch?”
Repeat until all the items have been guessed correctly from the tray.  “Wow, the roly poly had such a big lunch that it’s time for him to take a nap.  Say goodnight roly poly!”  Place the puppet back on the tray and cover with the lid as you put it away.

Engaging activity:
“Time to head to our seats for our next activity!  Walking feet to our seats.”  
Once students are seated, show a copy of the lunch tray sheet, and show students how to match the compost ingredients to the shapes on the tray.
Pass out lunch tray sheets to each student and table trays with compost ingredient stickers.  If students finish early, hand them roly poly stickers to “eat” the lunch.  
“Time to clean up!  Put all your stickers back on the tray.” Collect trays and worksheets.  
If there is time, ask students if they want to see the worms.  Explain the rules for looking at worms:  gentle hands, gentle sticks, quiet voices.  Remind them that worms are gentle too!  They do not bite.  Hand out worm bowls and sticks. 
“Time to say goodbye to worms, we need to clean up!  Goodbye worms.”  Collect worm bowls and hand out sanitizer. 
Closing ritual with SEL component: 
Give out fruit snack (bonus points if there is a peel or seed from it they can compost after).  All together:  “Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks!”
Bring students back to the rug or circle.  Ask students: “what did the roly poly eat for lunch today?”  
Finish with a goodbye song.

Grade Level Variations–REQUIRED for COOKING LESSONS
For 3K, they may not have time to look at worms.  To draw out the sticker portion of the activity, you can hand out stickers one at a time once they correctly identify them.

Site Based Variations, if applicable 
Site name: 

Back pocket activity–REQUIRED: 
Worms are the backpocket activity here!  Students in this age group tend to like to look at worms for a long time.  If you sense they are tiring of the activity, bring out some things they can feed the worms (sticks, leaves, or flowers).  
Materials: 
roly poly lunch worksheets
Sheet protectors, laminating paper
Double sided tape
roly poly puppet
Plastic or metal trays to put stickers on
Cafeteria lunch tray with a variety of compostable ingredients
Optional:  bowls with worms (enough for each student), and extra compost items to feed them

Prep work and setup:
Laminating the compost stickers and cutting them out takes time!  Plan accordingly.  The compost “trays” can be slipped into sheet protectors ahead of class.

Snowball Energy Bites


Summary: Students will make and taste energy bites rolled in coconut to resemble snowballs as a festive pre-holiday treat. Students will take some home and make holiday cards to accompany them for loved one(s) if there is time.

Objectives: Students will work together to make a healthy, energy-packed snack. 

Core Competencies: kitchen skills and related academic content

Cultural Responsiveness: Communicates high expectations by expecting students to complete tasks within a certain time frame without too much intervention and by conveying to students faith in their abilities.

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson should exemplify self management by expecting students to take small risks in trying new foods and regulating their emotions during class. This lesson should also exemplify relationship skills by expecting students to willingly take turns and share tools, and to work well together in groups.

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual with SEL component:
Welcome students to class.
Today we are going to be practicing working together to make a yummy snack packed with energy that look like little snowballs! 
Discuss how most of the ingredients we will be using are packed with energy - why does that make this a great snack?

Engaging activity:
Pass out trays of ingredients and mixing bowls and spoons.
First, have students take the pits out of dates, then put the dates in the food processor. After that, students can combine the rest of the ingredients in the bowl and mix.
Roll the balls in coconut either on a tray covered in coconut or by tossing the energy bites in a bowl of coconut. 
Have students taste the energy bites all at the same time.
If there is time after the energy bites have been made and tasted, pass out supplies for students to make holiday cards.
Closing ritual with SEL component:
Have students share out ways that they feel like they worked well together during class. Ask about the energy bites - if there are ingredients students think would have been yummy to add? Have they ever had anything like this before?

Materials: 
Energy Bites Recipe (I’m calling these “snowball” energy bites because we wanted to do something wintery/festive for the upcoming holidays - and the final step for students is to roll their energy balls in coconut so that they look like snowballs.)

   Phases of Matter


Summary: Students will use ingredients to demonstrate the three different phases of matter and bake oatmeal cookies.

Objectives: Students will be able to identify the three phases of matter (solid, liquid gas).

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: Can anyone tell us what the three phases of matter are? When we are cooking, especially baking, the phase of matter of our ingredients can be pretty important. If ingredients are not combined together correctly it can affect our final product. First you all are going to classify the ingredients we are going to use today according to the phase of matter they will be used for our recipe. Then we will bake oatmeal cookies.
Students will complete the first page of their worksheet

Engaging activity: Students will work in their groups to the prepare the Chewy Oatmeal Cookie Recipe
*Be sure to note the consistency of the ingredients*

Closing ritual: Students will eat their cookies as a group and complete their worksheet. Go over the answers and discuss how/why the ingredients changed phases.

   Pumpkin Pie Energy Bites


Summary:  Students work together to make a snack full of energizing seeds! Afterwards, students either sort seeds (K-1) or interview a classmate about their food preferences (2-5)

Objectives: Students understand that just like seeds have the energy needed for a plant to grow, seeds also give your body energy to grow and move.

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes student-centered instruction

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills, self-awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Go over what we’re making today, ask if there are any allergies. 

Engaging activity:
Students add energy bite ingredients to a big bowl and take turns mixing together with a spoon. (Note: this requires lots of preparation and creates extra dishes, so alternatively you could measure all the ingredients into one big bowl per table, and just have the students mix.)
Upper grades: Students can roll dough into balls and then roll in a plate of coconut flakes. Have buckets of water for students to wash their hands after (warning: this will be messy, but students didn’t seem to mind).
Lower grades: teacher can collect bowls and roll into balls with a clean, gloved hand, and then roll in coconut flakes.
Upper grades : Put energy bites in the fridge to set, and have students interview each other about food preferences. Eg, what is your favorite snack to eat? What is a food that you eat with your family? Etc. If time allows, let students share with the rest of the class
Lower grades: While energy bites set, pass out small cups of seeds to each student and have them sort into groups.

Closing ritual:
Remind students of tasting rules - we eat together as a family and don’t yuck anyone’s yum.
“Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks, bon appetit let’s eat!” After students have tasted, have them give a thumbs up/side/down to share how they liked it and ask for comments/feedback

Materials: 
Ingredients (See recipe below)
Small bowls for each ingredient 
Spoons to mix
Plates with coconut flakes for rolling
Trays lined with parchment for finished energy bites to go in fridge
Seeds to sort (for lower grades)

Prep work and setup:
Measure ingredients into small bowls (measure sticky ingredients like sun butter and honey directly in the big bowl where everything will be mixed. Everything else portioned into smaller bowls). Make sure there are enough bowls for each student at the table to add something!

Anticipated problems:
Lots of prep work and tiny bowls to clean - depending on class and other activities you have planned it might make more sense to make one big batch and have kids roll it out to avoid so much prep work and cleanup

Background information for instructor: None.

Questions for students/extension activities:
Extension for lower grades:
After seed sorting, have students choose one seed and give them crayons/paper to draw what plant they think would grow if they planted that seed in the ground. After they are finished put a dot of glue on the paper and glue the seed to the roots of the plant (make an example beforehand to show students)

Back pocket activities: None


Task cards or handouts used: None

Photos:




Pumpkin Pie Energy Bites
Makes 24
Ingredients
2 cups oats
½ cup peanut butter or other nut or sun butter
½ cup pumpkin puree
¼ cup chia seeds
¼ cup honey
1 teaspoon vanilla extract
½ teaspoon cinnamon or pumpkin pie spice
Optional: ½ cup mini chocolate chips and shredded coconut

Steps

Add all ingredients to a bowl and mix until combined.
Using your hands, form into balls.
Optional: Roll each ball in shredded coconut
Put in fridge until firm. 
Enjoy! Keep in the fridge for up to a week.

Women’s History Month: 
Alice Waters

Summary: Students will learn about Alice Waters and how she founded the Edible Schoolyard Project in 1995. Students will learn the importance behind an “edible education”. Students will write letters to Alice - highlighting their favorite things about the garden at their school. 

Objectives: Students will be able to identify some of Alice Waters’ achievements, and main reasons for founding the Edible Schoolyard project. 
Indoors - Students will prepare a seasonal tasting (garlic & honey roasted parsnips)
Outdoors - Students will plant peas in the garden

Cultural Responsiveness:[pick one or more] promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: [pick one or more]self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual (5 mins) :
Introduce topic of Women’s History Month in March
Introduce Alice Waters. She is the woman who created Edible Schoolyard. We will be learning about her life; why she feels that kids should have access to a garden and kitchen classroom, and what type(s) of learning happens through food. 
Explain that we will read the story and then do a gardening (or cooking) activity.
Engaging activity (5-7 mins) :
Read aloud “Alice Waters and the Trip to Delicious” 
Stop for discussion points throughout the book (post it notes)
Engaging activity #2 (10 mins) :
Tell students that they will be writing letters to Alice that she will be able to read. Have letter prompts on the board for older grades, or the written template for younger grades. Have the classroom teacher help with spelling and/or writing the letters
Call students up in groups of 5 to do gardening/cooking activity.
For cooking - students get to peel and cut a small piece of parsnip
For gardening - students get to plant pea seeds in the garden


Closing ritual (5 mins) :
Ask students what they like to learn about the most in classes with Edible Schoolyard.
Letter Prompts:

What I like most about the garden at my school is…
My favorite thing I have grown in the garden..
The most interesting insects I’ve seen in the garden are…
I think that having a school garden is important because…

Print worksheets

Foods From Around the World: Caribbean Islands

Summary: Students will do a reading and watch a video about the culture, geography, and typical ingredients from the Caribbean Islands, as well as how these food cultures are present in New York City. Students then prepare and taste a quick recipe that is from these countries.

Objectives: Students will be able to make connections between themselves, their culture, and other cultures. 

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Tell everyone that we are learning about the Caribbean Islands and the Caribbean community in New York City.
Hand out the reading. Read it aloud to students as they follow along. Take student questions and comments about what surprised them or what they connected to.
Watch the video. Again, have students share their questions or comments. 
Explain the recipe of the day. Use hand sanitizer and remind students about norms for health and safety while cooking. 

Engaging activity:
Prepare the recipe with students divided into small groups at tables. Each table will be following the same recipe, but groups may be sharing induction burners. 
Fill tasting cups and sit down back as a large group.

Closing ritual: 
Finish by tasting what students have made together.
Have students share tasting words about the tasting.

Materials: 
Projector, Smartboard, or other means of showing video
Copies of Caribbean Islands reading
First video (watch to 4:15)
Second video (watch to 2:15)
Take-home copies of callaloo recipe
Hand sanitizer
Induction burner
Large pot with stirring spoon
Trays per each table with the following:
Ingredients from recipe
Scissors and plates for cutting collard greens
Measuring spoons
Garlic press or grater
Can opener (optional)
Bowls for ingredients
Recipe copy
Cups, plates, and utensils for tasting

Prep work and setup:
Set up trays with materials and ingredients.This recipe should be doubled for a class of 32. If you have two groups, each group can make the recipe as written. If not, divide materials and ingredients between your groups appropriately. You can also add additional collard greens to the recipe, if desired.
Open cans of coconut milk (or put a can opener on the tray for students to do it). 
Recommended: pre-chop onion and bell pepper. 

Anticipated problems:
It is likely that you will not have an adult to lead each small group. Assign one student to be the table leader for each group. The table leader can give out jobs, pass out materials, and ensure the recipe is followed. 
This recipe uses an induction burner. An adult should always be nearby and closely monitoring the induction burner. Ensure that the cooking area is away from other groups for safety purposes and that students are aware of the norms around the stove. 
If there are multiple adults, each adult can be responsible for supervising an induction burner and more than one induction burner can be used. Tell each small group which induction burner will be theirs to put ingredients in so that ingredients are divided evenly. 
Only a few students at a time can be at the induction burner. Provide students with a place to put their prepped materials in their small groups, and then assign one student at a time to bring ingredients to the induction burner. For example, the students measuring spices can put them into a small bowl or container, and then one student can come and add it to the pot on the burner. 

Background information for instructor:
This lesson is the first in a larger unit about the food culture of New York City. The unit sequence is as follows:
Introductory lesson
Cooking and culture lessons (Uzbekistan, Mexico, Pakistan, China, Caribbean Islands, Ukraine)
Recipe design lesson
Voting lesson
An alternate recipe to make for this lesson is plantains with jerk seasoning.

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities: None.

Task cards or handouts used: See above.

Photos: None.

Middle School

                   

3. Environmental Impact of the Food System
  
Summary: Students will explore the environmental impact of different kinds of foods through a simulation.

Objectives: Students will familiarize themselves with the ecological footprint of different kinds of  foods and explore ways in which conventional agriculture can be made more sustainable.

Cultural Responsiveness:  
Promotes student-centered instruction by encouraging students to reconsider how their own personal choices may impact the natural environment.
Reshapes curriculum by providing visuals to help students make clearer connections. 

Social-Emotional Skills: 
Encourages social awareness by discussing social and environmental justice issues.

Opening: (5 mins)

We talk a lot about how food affects our own health. Can anyone give an example of a food that’s healthy for our bodies?
Today we are going to talk about how different kinds of food we eat affect the health of the environment.
Does anyone remember the cards we used to show how Takis get to our table? We talked about how water and other natural resources are used in the process.
Some foods use a lot of water and land and produce a lot of carbon and other pollution.
Other foods use fewer of these resources and are therefore better for the environment. 
Today we are going to look at three different foods and how healthy they are for the environment.

Engaging Activity (30 minutes):

Each table will get three bottles of water, a bottle of food coloring, and information about three different kinds of food.
As you read about each food, follow the instructions about your bottle of water. Sometimes you might take water out of your bottle. Sometimes you might add food coloring to your bottle.
When you are done, you will see which bottle of water looks the cleanest and which looks the dirtiest. This will show you which of the three foods is best for the environment and which is the worst.
Get students started on the simulation. Circulate and help as needed.
As students finish, hand out the worksheet and have them answer the questions and do the advertisement on the back.

Closing (10 mins):
Which food was the worst for the environment? Why?
Which food was best for the environment? Why?
What are some steps you can take to help the environment through your own food choices?
What are some ways you can talk to others about their food choices and the environment?
Pass out tasting and ask students if this is an example of a food choice that is good or bad for the environment and why.
Eat!

Materials: 
Environmental Impact Simulation Instructions
Environmental Simulation Questions
Three clear bottles or large glasses of water per table or per group of students
One bottle of food coloring per table or per group of students
One bowl per table or per group of students
One teaspoon per table or per group of students
One measuring cup per table or per group of students
Plant-based snack that is good for the environment: fresh or dried fruit, fresh vegetable, etc.

   Thermal Energy Experiment	

Summary: Students will learn about thermal energy and conduct an experiment to investigate what happens when things are hot or cold. 

Objectives: Students will be able to conduct an experiment in order to compare how quickly food coloring moves within hot and cold water.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: What is temperature?  What do you know about a thermometer? What is different when water is at a cold temperature? Is that different than when water is hot? Today we are going to do an experiment where we will observe how water acts at different temperatures. 

Engaging activity: Students will do the water experiment (they will complete the worksheet as they go) 
Each group will be given 2 cups of water → one hot and one cold, a thermometer, and food coloring
Students will add the thermometer to each cup one at a time for 15 seconds to record the temperature
Students will then add 1 drop of food coloring into each of their cups
Have students observe how the food coloring looks different in both of their cups

Closing ritual: The class will have a tasting and discuss the experiment.

Water

Thermometers

3rd-5th Grade
All Seasons
Outdoor
  

Biodiversity



Summary: Students will play a game to understand the importance and advantages of biodiversity. 
	

Objectives: Students will be able to explain what biodiversity is and how biodiversity provides protection against environmental risks. 
	

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes student-centered instruction
	

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills
	

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
1. Write up the word “diversity” on the board. Ask students to define the word. 
2. Explain that we will be starting out by learning about the diversity of our own classroom. Hand out Human Bingo sheets on clipboards with pencils. Model if necessary. Circulate to help and participate.
3. Ask students to share out what they learned about the diversity of their classmates.  


Engaging activity:
1. Define “biodiversity” as the diversity of life. Explain that we will be playing a game to learn about how biodiversity works in our garden.
   1. Hand out yellow necklaces to each student. Have these students stand in a circle. Give one student a worm sign to wear. Have the students with the worm sign stand in the center of the circle.
   2. Tell students that the person wearing the worm sign is a corn worm who eats yellow corn, and everyone else is the same variety of yellow corn. The corn worm will sing a song to count off students and choose the corn that it eats: “I am the corn worm! I’m gonna eat you!” If the worm points at a student, they die a quick corn death, represented by students sitting down.
   3. At the end of the round, ask students how quickly the whole corn crop was wiped out.
   4. Collect the yellow corn necklaces and pass out multi-colored corn. This time they are a field of mixed corn varieties. This time, when they are touched by the corn work, they will survive if they have white or red corn. Only the yellow corn will die. Have students predict how this round will be different than the one before.
   5. Play the game and time how long it takes the worm to infect the yellow corn. Ask students to explain how the second round was different from the first.
2. Ask students: “If you were a farmer, which scenario would you choose? Would you grow one variety of corn or several? Why?”
3. Have students brainstorm some other ways that biodiversity helps our garden: more new foods for us to learn about and try; growing more plants means we have different nutrients in our food; different foods allow us to grow food all year round; etc. 


Closing ritual:
1. End with a tasting to celebrate the biodiversity of our garden. 
	

Materials: 
* Human Bingo worksheets
* Pencils
* Clipboards
* Worm sign
* Yellow necklaces (made of yellow string) 
* Multi-colored necklaces (made of white or red string) 
* Seasonal tasting


Prep work and setup: None.


Anticipated problems: None.


Background information for instructor: None. 


Questions for students/extension activities: None. 


Back pocket activities: None.


Task cards or handouts used: 
* Biodiversity materials

Sri Lankan Curry



Summary:  Students will learn about curry and about some of the different countries that make curry. Students will go through the process of making curry, from cutting vegetables, making curry powder, to cooking curry.
	

Objectives: Students will be able to dice vegetables. Students will be able to describe the way that different spices look and smell. Students will be able to safely use the stove (with assistance).
	

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator
	

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness
	

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
1. Introduce today’s recipe.
   1. This month we are making food from Southeast Asia from a small country called Sri Lanka (show on map). Sri Lanka is close to India and has a lot of influences from other countries that have colonized them in the past (Britain, Portugal, Amterdam). This is a reason there are now many more countries that eat curry. One of the most important ingredients in our Sri Lankan curry is coconut milk! We will also be using eggplant and leeks with some more tomatoes from our garden. Curry also has a lot of spices, see if there are any spices that you recognize at the middle table, there might be some there that we have used before. 


Engaging activity:
1. Carrot Table:
   1. Cut tomatoes, eggplant, ginger, garlic, and leeks.
2. Corn Table:
   1.  Spice exploration - Students will be exposed to all the spices that are being used for curry powder. The students are encouraged to look at and smell all the spices, some of them can be touched to feel the texture. Then students will measure quantities of spices to place in a mortar and pestle to make the spice blend
3. Cucumber Table:
   1. Students will cook the curry at the stove.
      1. If time permits, we will make coconut milk using shredded coconut and a blender at the table.


Closing ritual:
1. Clean up, set the table, eat together, clear tables.
2. Ask for suggestions on how students would make the recipe different next time.
3. Share shout-outs before lining up.
	

Materials: 
* Map
* Recipe ingredients
* Mortar and pestle
* Culture Box: Photos, currency, spices, magnifying glass, etc.
* Plastic knives, cutting boards, scissors, bowls
* 2-3 large saucepans
* Spice guide for teachers
* Spice exploration placemats
* Spice study worksheets
* Trays prepped with tomato, eggplant, leeks, ginger, and garlic


Prep work and setup:
* Cut the first set of vegetables for the first class’s first rotation.
* Pre-make one batch of curry powder.


Anticipated problems:
* Some students may be nervous to try spicy food. Remind them that spices and spicy are two different things!


Background information for instructor:
* History of Curry


Questions for students/extension activities: None. 


Back pocket activities:
* Culture box exploration


Task cards or handouts used: 
* Spice exploration placemats
* Spice study worksheet
* Spice guide for teachers
* Take home recipe
