body:
Grade 3k-2nd
Season: Spring
Indicate: Indoor/Outdoor



The Very Hungry Pollinator

Summary: Students will create a butterfly craft & plant flowers.

Objectives: Students will understand the relationship between butterflies & flowers.

Cultural Responsiveness: 
Communicates high expectations:
Conveying to students your faith in their abilities
Expects students to complete various tasks within a certain time frame
Incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles:
Lesson incorporates individual students’ diverse learning styles  
Reshapes curriculum:
Incorporating movement

Social-Emotional Skills: 
Relationship skills:
Display calm, caution, or care for animals and plants
Make connections to outside life
Responsible decision-making:
Cleaning up messes
Putting materials back where they belong
Using tools safely
Considers safety and well-being of self and others
Self-awareness:
Displays sense of pride or ownership

Agenda/Class Flow: (include estimated time)
Opening ritual:
Hello friends! Welcome to garden class. Sing “Good Morning, Gardeners.”
(To the tune of “If you’re happy and you know it, clap your hands)
Good morning Gardeners, how are you? (clap clap)
Good morning Gardeners, how are you? (clap clap)
Oh, it’s garden day today, and that means it’s time to play,
So let’s all take a seat and listen, what do you say?
Today we are going to transform into butterflies that live in a garden. What do you know about butterflies?  Let’s learn a little more about how they grow.  

Engaging activity #1:
We’re going to hear about a Very Hungry Caterpillar. Why do you think we are reading a book about caterpillars when today is all about butterflies? Caterpillars turn into butterflies!
Read The Very Hungry Caterpillar.
Review life cycle of butterfly with picture cards: What is the beginning of a butterfly’s life? What does he become next? What does he become after that?

Engaging activity #2:
You all know so much about the life of a butterfly.  But what we haven’t talked about is just how beautiful butterflies are.  Today we are going to make a beautiful, colorful butterfly.  
Show students an example of the butterfly puppet.
Set out coffee filters for each student.  Students will decorate the coffee filters with crayons, making sure that the entire filter has been colored in.
Teachers should help finish putting together butterflies with clothespins and pipe cleaners.

Engaging activity #3
Now, let's do something special for the butterflies that visit our garden.  We are going to plant flowers, and butterflies will be able to drink the nectar from the flowers that we grow.  
Review garden norms if needed.
Give each student one nasturtium seed. Demonstrate how to plant them in the bed.

Closing ritual:
Students can fly their butterfly puppets around the space in search of flowers.

Materials:

body:
2. Observing Indoor Edible     
           Sprouts
Note: This is the second of three lessons taking place on day five. Lesson three should be done on day ten. Sprouts need to be watered once a day between days five and ten. Teacher instructions here and here.

Summary: Students learn about germination by dissecting lima beans. They care for and observe the soil sprouts they planted a few days earlier.

Objectives: Students will learn more about caring for plants and will understand how plants grow without light.

Core Competencies: Garden skills and related academic content

Cultural Responsiveness: Promotes student-centered instruction by giving students a hands-on way to engage with life sciences.

Social-Emotional Skills: Students will practice self-management by showing care with their lima bean seeds and soil sprouts

Agenda/Class Flow: 
Opening ritual with SEL component:
Write the following words on the board: rice, popcorn, almond, sunflower seed. (Or, have small bowls of each food available for students to see).
Ask students to raise their hands if they have eaten any of the foods listed on the board (or in front of them).
Ask students to guess what all of those foods have in common. The answer you are looking for is that they are all seeds! You can give them a hint that they are all the same part of the plant.
Tell students that seeds are very healthy for people and animals to eat. One reason for this is that they are full of all the nutrients a baby plant will need before it breaks through the soil to get its nutrients from the sun.
Write “germination” on the board and explain that germination is the time when a plant is growing underground.
Tell students they got their soil sprouts started on the process of germination five days ago, and that because of this, they helped the plants grow into something we can eat soon.

Engaging activity 1:
Hand each student a soaked lima bean and a copy of the seed dissection map. Explain that lima beans are seeds and that the students are going to look inside them to understand germination.
Show students how to gently peel the coat off of the lima bean. Have students look on the seed map to see the coat.
Show students how to gently split the lima bean in half lengthwise (so it looks like the seed map). Many lima beans should have a tiny sprout inside. If a student can’t see the sprout in their lima bean, have them look at a classmate’s bean or give them another lima bean to try. Explain that the sprout is the baby plant!
Show students that everything else–everything that is not the sprout or the coat–is food for the baby plant. For older students, you can teach them the scientific word for this: endosperm.
Explain that this food is what allows the baby plant to grow underground for many days before it breaks through the soil and can start getting its food from the sun. (For older students, you can explain that getting food from the sun is called photosynthesis).
Optional: have students color the three parts of the seed on their seed map

Engaging activity 2:
Tell students that now that they understand how a seed grows without light, they are going to take the newspaper off of the seeds they planted and see how much progress their seeds have made in five days in the dark.
Have students very gently remove newspaper from their loaf pans.
Hand students their Soil Sprouts Observation sheets from the previous lesson and have them fill in the section for “Day Five”

Closing ritual with SEL component:
Ask students to share their observations and predictions.
Tell students that they have continued their work as scientists and farmers, helping their seeds to germinate and learning more about what plants need.
Remind them that in five days, they will be harvesting and eating their sprouts.

Materials: 
Dried lima beans, soaked overnight, one per student.
Seed dissection maps, one per student plus extras.
Optional: coloring materials
Loaf pans with sprouts from previous lesson.
Soil Sprouts Observation sheet from previous lesson–Give each student their copy 
Optional: uncooked rice, uncooked popcorn, almonds, sunflower seeds. Enough to display to students.

Prep work and setup:
Soak lima bean seeds overnight
Loaf pans and Soil Sprout Observation sheets ready for distribution

Sweet and Sour Roots

Summary:  Students will explore root vegetables and why we eat them in the winter. They will cut carrots and make a sauce. Half of the carrots will be roasted and the other half served raw for comparison. 

Objectives: Students will be able to safely cut vegetables. Students will be able to identify roots. Students will be able to compare and contrast raw vs. roasted vegetables.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
 Welcome back to your kitchen, everyone! Ask the students to think about the weather outside. Through discussion bring to their awareness that it has been cold the last few months and very little has been growing in the garden. Show the students carrots, beets, parsnips, turnips, (and other roots, if available).
 “What part of the plant are all these vegetables?” 
Through discussion, help the students realize they are all roots and that roots are hardy enough that they can be harvested in the late fall and stored all winter. 

Engaging activity:
Table 1: Root comparison.
Have the students feel each of the roots.  Assign a root vegetable to each student then have them turn to the person next to them to describe the qualities of each of the roots.  For example: what color is it?  Is it round or long?  Is it hard or soft?  What kind of root vegetable is it?
Compare the roots to a strawberry on the tray.  Explain that strawberries grow in late spring so this strawberry had to come from very far away where it’s as warm as it is in the late spring.  Have the students turn to the person next to them to talk about the difference between the strawberry and the root vegetables.  Pose the following question to the students:  We cannot store strawberries throughout the winter because they will rot but these root vegetables will stay fresh for months.  Why do you think this is?
For Pre-K to 1st grade: Have the students pick a root vegetable to draw instead of the root comparison.  
Table 2: Cut Roots. 
Students will use cutting boards and age-appropriate knives to cut the vegetables.
Save half of the cut up roots to serve with the sauce as a comparison of roasted vs raw.
Table 3: Make dressing.
Students will follow the list of ingredients to add to the vegetables before they go in the oven.
Closing ritual:
Clean up, set the table, eat together.
Discuss the differences and similarities between the roasted and raw roots using all 5 senses.
Ask for suggestions on how students would make the recipe different next time.
Share shout-outs before lining up.

Materials: 
Recipe ingredients
2 medium bowls
tongs
sheet pan with parchment liner
2 additional sheet pans
jar with tight fitting lid
microplane or fine grater
3-1 Tablespoon measuring spoons
1 teaspoon measuring spoon
2 ramekins or small cups
Take Home Recipes for each student
Pre-K - 1st Grade task cards
2nd - 3rd Grade task cards
4-5th Grade recipe check list
Middle station teacher talking points

Prep work and setup:
Cut the root vegetables into strips so that the students simply perform the last cut.
Peel the ginger except the very top for an easier grip.
Preheat the oven to 425 degrees.
Root tray set up: 2 different carrots, 2 beets, rutabaga, 2 types of turnip, parsnip, labels for each root vegetable

Anticipated problems:
Make sure to let the roasted carrots cool slightly before serving.

Background information for instructor: None.

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities:

Task cards or handouts used: 
Take home recipe
2nd and 3rd task cards
Root exploration guiding questions
Dressing checklist for students

Photos: None.

All About Pumpkins


Summary: Students will learn all about Pumpkins and make and taste roasted pumpkin seeds!

Objectives: Students will learn the different parts of a pumpkin and learn that pumpkin is a squash. Students will observe pumpkins: what do they look like? What do they feel like? Students will season pumpkin seeds and taste them.

Core Competencies: List all that apply: garden skills and related academic content, kitchen skills and related academic content. 

Cultural Responsiveness: Communicates high expectations by expecting students to mix carefully and pass the bowl around the table fairly without needing too many reminders from teachers, conveying to students that teacher has faith in their ability to do this.

Social-Emotional Skills: Relationship skills and self management should be evident by students taking small risks and working well independently in groups and willingly taking turns mixing the pumpkin seeds with the spices and oil.

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)

Opening ritual: (5 mins)
Make sure students all sanitize their hands on the way into the classroom. Welcome students to class - how are you all feeling today? 
Today we will be learning about pumpkins and making and tasting pumpkin seeds! Who here has ever eaten anything pumpkin-y before? What do you all know about pumpkins?
Pumpkins and pumpkin products are very popular around Halloween because Pumpkins are a fall crop - which means they are harvested in the fall. Pumpkins are also squashes! There are many different kinds of squashes that we eat (show some pictures of examples)
Who thinks they might be trying something new today?

Engaging activity: Seasoning Pumpkin Seeds (10-15 mins)
Each table will receive a bowl of pumpkin seeds (without shells) and oil and seasonings. Ask students what they notice about the pumpkin seeds - shape? Color? Size? Familiarity?
Students will add oil and seasonings to the bowl and take turns mixing them all together. 
A student at each table (perhaps one who has been listening particularly well) can bring the bowl to the front of the room when its time and help Ms. Raili to pour seeds onto a baking sheet.
Pumpkin Exploration Activities (while seeds cook): 15 mins
Half the tables will get a pumpkin with the top cut out to explore the inside of the pumpkin: How do the insides feel? How are these seeds different from the ones we seasoned? How many seeds do you think are in there?
The other half of the tables will get a worksheet to color or label depending on the grade. Tables will switch activities after about 7-10 minutes, depending how much class time is left.

Closing ritual:
Students will re-wash hands since they have been in the pumpkins.
Taste the Pumpkin Seeds! 
“Thank you gardeners, Thank you cooks”
Tasting questions: what are some words that describe the flavor of the pumpkin seeds? What about the texture? Do they taste how you expected they would? What other seasonings do you think would be yummy to add if we made these again?
Closing: Raise your hand if you did/tried something for the first time today? Who is feeling proud of themselves? Who wants to share something they did during class that was helpful to someone else? 
Give any shoutouts that feel necessary - see you next week!

Materials: 
Labeling a Pumpkin Worksheet (thinking could work for K and 1)
Pumpkin Life Cycle Coloring Sheet

Read a book about Pumpkins!

Gardening & The Environment 

Summary: Students will get a (re)introduction to the school garden and learn about the various ways gardening is beneficial for the environment.

Objectives: Students will be able to explore their school garden to experience and reflect on the benefits of gardening on a community and environmental level.

Core Competencies: Garden skills and related academic content, Social-emotional learning, Environmental and Community Stewardship

Cultural Responsiveness: 
Communicates high expectations: Conveying to students your faith in their abilities, Expects students to complete various tasks within a certain time frame 
Reshapes curriculum: incorporating movement, Incorporating group work

Social-Emotional Skills:
Self-management: Take small risks in using new tools.
Relationship skills: Working well in groups, Display calm, caution, or care for animals and plants
Responsible decision-making: Putting materials back where they belong, Using tools safely
Self-awareness: Display a verbal or physical action of joy

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
For all lessons: Garden, garden jobs, urban gardening, SEL, socioemotional health

Agenda/Class Flow:
Introduction (10 minutes): Welcome to the Green Room! Does anyone remember what are the main things we do during class in the Green Room? Sometimes in the Green Room we will cook and other times we will head outside to our school garden. Today we will be talking more about gardening. Gardening can impact us in many ways, in smaller ways on an individual level but also in much bigger ways - like the entire planet. What are some ways you think gardening helps our environment and our planet?
improves the quality of air and soil
decreases carbon dioxide in the air to reduce effect of global warming
replenishes and protects the soil
protects local wildlife and pollinators
Makes the food system more local by growing our own food
reduces "heat islands" and make our cities cooler in the summer
Beautifies our planet

Today you’ll be doing your part to help the environment by helping our school garden.

Engaging activity (25 minutes): 
Go over garden rules:
Stay in the garden area
No running in the garden
Don’t pick/eat anything without permission
No throwing dirt or watering our friends.
Try new things & have fun!)

Garden Job Review: As a class let’s review each garden job 
(ask for volunteers to describe each job)
Planting: putting seeds or seedlings (young plants) in the ground to grow
Weeding: removing any unwanted or troublesome plant
Harvesting: the process of gathering ripe crops
Composting: recycling leaves and food scraps, into fertilizer for soil and plants
Watering: adding water to plants so that they can grow

Now we are going to go out to the garden! There are stations throughout the garden where you will do each of the jobs that we just learned about. Try your best to make it to every station.
Garden jobs.

Closing ritual with SEL component (10 minutes):
Say thank yous and eat a tasting together as a class community
Go around the room and have each student share 1 word about describing how they felt working in the garden (use mood meter if available)

Back pocket activity: 
Garden Alphabet Game

Materials: 
Garden tools: watering cans, seeds, trowels, garden gloves, buckets for compost, scissors
Chart paper or white board to review garden jobs

Prep work and setup:
Set up garden stations
Prep tasting - in season fruit

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable: 
https://www.greenmatters.com/p/how-gardening-helps-environment
https://www.buzzfeed.com/patrickbaker/ways-gardening-helps-the-environment

Summer Veg Saute (Slicing/Julienne/Chiffonade)

Summary: Students will learn a few more knife skills (slicing, julienne, chiffonade) to make a summer vegetable sauté.

Objectives: Students will be able to use knives safely and effectively and demonstrate different types of knife cuts. 

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Review previous class: What did we make last week? What types of knife cuts did we learn about? (Chopping, dicing, mincing)
Introduce new knife cuts: slicing, julienne, and chiffonade
Have a visual aid showing each type of cut and give examples 
Introduce recipe and demonstrate new techniques: slicing squash/onion, julienne carrots, and chiffonade basil

Engaging activity:
Reminder of knife safety rules & class expectations
Choose group roles & clean hands
In groups, students cut squash, carrots, and basil, then add to a group bowl to be collected 
Give a 2 min warning, then collect all group bowls and demonstrate sauté in the back
Add garlic, salt, and pepper. While it cooks, discuss sautéing 
Ask students if they’ve heard the word Sauté before and if they know what it means. Have students pair-share about foods they have cooked (or seen people cook) using this method
Tables clean up while food is being cooked/served
Taste it together and vote: tried it, liked it, loved it on a whiteboard

Closing ritual:
Clean up
Presenters share: how was it cooking as a team? Was there any difference from last week?
Reflection questions: 
What are ways that you practiced using knives safely today?
Does anyone feel more confident using a knife today than you did last week? 
How are the knife cuts we learned today different from the chopping and dicing we did last week?
How would you describe the taste of this recipe? What would you add or change to make it even better? 

Materials: 
Tray set-up:
Tray Prep List
Mixing bowl
Knives
Cutting boards
Summer squash/zucchini
Onion
Carrots
Basil
Plates 
Forks
Pictures of knife cuts
Whole ingredients for display during intro 
Garlic powder, salt, pepper, olive oil (for teacher to add)

Prep work and setup:
Cut vegetables into smaller pieces
Set up whiteboard with knife cuts, recipe, and tried/liked/loved it 

Anticipated problems:
Leave onions in fridge to reduce crying

Background information for instructor:
Pico de gallo, also called salsa fresca or salsa cruda, is a type of salsa commonly used in Mexican cuisine. It is traditionally made from chopped tomato, onion, Serrano peppers, with salt, cumin, lime juice, and cilantro.

Questions for students/extension activities: None.

Summary: Students will play a matching game using different food cognates they may find in the Green Room. 

Objectives: Students will be able to understand what a cognate is and identify some they may encounter in the kitchen. 

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: Today we will go over cognates. Does anyone remember what a cognate is? It is a  word that is similar in both English and Spanish. Today we are going to play a game where you will have to identify some common cognates that you will find here in the Green Room. 

Engaging activity: Students will work together in teams to match each photo with the correct English and Spanish word. When all groups finish, go over the answers as a class.

All Grades / Fall


Arroz con Gandules / Apple Jicama Slaw

Summary: Students will learn about the process of cooking rice and how water is absorbed by the rice. They will make sofrito and use it to season the rice and practice using correct measuring tools.

Objectives: Students will have an understanding of the process of cooking rice. Students will make the connection between chana chawal and arroz con gandules (they are both rice and beans!).


Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening Ritual 
Students come in and revisit the theme of the year, cooking from places around the world that are represented in our school community. Show them Puerto Rico on the map and let them know that it is part of the United States.  See how they respond and see if they have any follow up questions. 
Explain that arroz con gandules is rice and pigeon peas. Ask them if any of them like eating rice. Remind them that we ate rice and beans last month and ask them to pay attention to differences in making these two recipes.  
Engaging Activity
Cucumber table: Season and cook the rice - Students in 4th and 5th grade will be given the responsibility to lead the cooking table. With very little help from teachers, the students will find their ingredients, measure them, and put them into a pot to begin cooking them. Younger grades will have more guidance from the kitchen teacher and we will emphasize using the correct measuring tools.
Corn table: Sofrito station- students will be given a worksheet with age appropriate math. Students will be asked to solve some math equations in order to discover the recipe for the sofrito. Once the worksheet is completed, the students will move to the food processor where they will be shown all the ingredients for sofrito and they will be shown how the ingredients get blended together to make sofrito. 
Carrot table: Jicama Slaw - students will cut apples, cabbage, and cilantro and add vinegar and honey and mix it up. 

Closing Ritual
Clean up, set the table, eat together, clear tables.



Materials:
Food: Cabbage, carrots, apples, jicama olive oil, vinegar, salt
Bell pepper, aji dulce, culantro, onion, garlic
Rice, sofrito, pigeon peas, tomato sauce, salt, adobo, pepper, cilantro (opt.)

Tools: Pot for making rice, measuring spoons, measuring cups, box graters, gloves for protection from graters, mixing bowls, food processor

Prep work and setup (include photos if possible):
Use sofrito from the previous class for the first rotation of the next class
Deseed peppers in advance
Use par-boiled brown rice so that it is ready within the class period

Anticipated problems:



Background information for instructor:
Talk about how Puerto Rico is a part of the US and that it is a commonwealth / territory, and if you’re born there you are a US citizen.

Questions for students/extension activities:
What language do people speak in Puerto Rico?
In what other recipes have we used onions, peppers, and cilantro?

Philippines & Lumpia

Summary: Students will continue to learn about AAPI heritage month with a lesson focusing on the Philippines, a country that is a part of the Pacific Islands. Students will also prepare lumpia, which is a popular Filipino recipe.

Objectives: Students will be able to identify which countries are a part of the Pacific Islands, discuss the impact of other cultures and make comparisons to other Asian countries. 
Students will be able to work together to prepare Filipino lumpia (spring rolls). 

Core Competencies: Kitchen skills and related academic content, Multicultural education, Social-emotional learning

Cultural Responsiveness: 
Communicates high expectations: expect students to read and complete a recipe on their own and to complete various tasks within a certain time frame 
Encourages learning within the context of culture: creating lessons to build understanding about other cultures, lessons/recipes with historical context
Reshapes curriculum: Incorporates group work

Social-Emotional Skills:
Self-management: take small risks by trying a new food
Relationship skills: work well in groups
Self-awareness: take pride/joy in their work 

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
For all lessons: AAPI, AAPI Heritage Month, Asia, Pacific Islands, Philippines
If cooking: 
Cooking skill(s): cutting, dicing, recipe reading, seasoning
Main ingredient(s): spring roll, carrot, cabbage 
Heating element: stove (pan-fry) or oven

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)

Introduction (10 minutes): This month is AAPI Heritage month. Many of you might be familiar with many of the countries that are a part of Asia and we have already made some foods from those countries. Can anyone share something new they learned about AAPI Heritage Month or the countries we have learned about so far? But another part of the world whose cultures we celebrate this month are the Pacific Islands. The Pacific Islands are thousands of islands across the Central and South Pacific Ocean that make up a part of the larger region, Oceania (show map is one is available). There are about 15 islands/countries that make up the Pacific Islands, including the country of the Philippines. Because of both its location and history of colonization (especially by Spain), culture and food in the Philippines is a mixture of Asian and Spanish flavors. Does anyone have any examples from their own life of cultures mixing together? 

Engaging activity (25 minutes) : Today we are going to make a popular Filipino dish called lumpia. Lumpia are made from a rice paper wrapper that is filled, wrapped/rolled up and then fried. They are similar to egg rolls or spring rolls which are also popular throughout Asia.  

Go over rules for cooking
Follow instructions
Clean up after ourselves
Be safe with tools
Keep our hands clean → then wash hands

Before we get started we are going to go over instructions for wrapping our lumpia. (Use printouts of instructions or show video).

In their table groups, students will prep filling and each student will have a chance to wrap 1 or 2 of their own lumpia. 
Grate cabbage
Grate carrots
Finely dice mushrooms
Grate ginger
Add rice noodles
Add seasonings  

Teacher will lightly pan fry lumpia then finish them off in the oven (or cook in an air fryer for 7-10 minutes if available) while students clean up and set tables to eat. 

Closing ritual with SEL component (10 minutes):
Say thank yous and eat together as a class community
Ask for volunteers to share something new they learned in class today?
Was it something about a different culture or a new skill? 
Did the recipe remind them of anything from their own lives?
If there is time, have students shoutout someone in their group!

Grade Level Variations–REQUIRED for COOKING LESSONS:
For younger students, simplify the introduction, showing a map of the Phillipines and say that they are in the Pacific Islands, which are near Asia and have some cultural similarities.
For younger students, consider having some or all of  the filling pre-grated or pre-chopped, and give the students more time to make the dumplings.Young students probably cannot mince. The backpocket activity can be used if they finish too early.

Back pocket activity: Wrapper Origami
Using the wrapping instructions, have students practice wrapping lumpia by using tissue paper and making lumpia origami. 
Students can use cotton balls or tissues as the “filling”

Materials: 
Baking sheets
Saute pan
Oven
Spring roll wrappers
Veggie fillings: dry vermicelli rice noodles, firm tofu, cabbage, carrots, shiitake mushrooms, fresh ginger, all-purpose flour, cornstarch, seasonings, brown sugar, ground black pepper, soy sauce, sesame/vegetable oil

Prep work and setup:
Pre-make rice noodles by soaking in water
Print wrapping instructions

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable: 
Students will prep filling for following class but will have the opportunity to seasoning their own batch for their lumpia
Recipe

Butterfly Release Party
Summary: Students will go to the garden to release the butterflies they raised in science class.

Objectives: Students will be able to discuss some butterfly facts. 
Students will be able to understand that butterflies are pollinators which are beneficial for the garden. 

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: You all have spent the past few weeks raising caterpillars into butterflies and today we will be releasing them in the garden. Before we head outside, can a few volunteers share some facts they learned about butterflies in the process? A fact that I would like to share about butterflies is that they are pollinators. Does anyone know what a pollinator is and what they do? (Pollinators are any living thing that lands on a plant and carries parts of one plant to another plant, and they assist in this process by carrying pollen from one plant to another.) Pollinators are an important part of our environment, especially for our garden!

Engaging activity: Students will go outside to the garden to release their butterflies. After releasing the butterflies the class will enjoy snacks, plant seeds and other fun butterfly related activities (ex. Temporary butterfly tattoos). 

Closing ritual: Clean up

Snacks
Games
Temp tattoos

Three Sisters Pupusas and Curtido

Summary: Students will learn the legend of the three sisters. They will use the three sisters as ingredients in pupusas and make curtido to go alongside. 

Objectives: Students will be able to identify the three sisters and how they work together in the garden. Students will be able to identify a new type of dough made with masa harina.

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening Ritual
Narrate the legend of the Three sisters while 3 students act it out. Talk about Latin America and El Salvador (locate on the map, Spanish is spoken there). Explain that the Three Sisters have been used by ancient civilizations and Latinx cultures for centuries. The Three Sisters provide a complete food source.

Engaging Activity
Cucumber table: Making masa and assembling pupusas: Students first add masa harina and water to a bowl and mix it up to form the right consistency. Give each student a ball of masa and demonstrate how to form a pupusa. Use the filling from the corn table. When each pupusa is ready, transfer it to the hot griddle to cook. 
Corn table: Making the filling: Using task cards, students will add the correct amounts of beans, squash puree, cheese, and spices to the bowl. Then they will mash it and pass it around the table to combine the ingredients. They will do a reading comprehension activity with any remaining time.
Carrot table: Making the curtido: Curtido is a Salvadorian slaw, made with cabbage, carrots, oregano, vinegar, and onion. Students will cut the cabbage, onions and carrots then season with salt, pepper, vinegar, and oregano.



Closing ritual:
Set the tables together.
Say thank you gardeners, thank you cooks! Taste the food.
Ask for feedback-- what would you change about this recipe?
Clean up, give shout outs, line up.



Materials:
Food: Cabbage, carrots, onions, vinegar, dried oregano, salt, pepper,
Black beans, onion powder, garlic powder, squash puree, mozzarella cheese,
Masa harina, water, salt, canola oil 

Tools: cast iron griddle, measuring spoons, measuring cups, box graters (and gloves), mixing bowls, potato masher

Prep work and setup (include photos if possible):


Anticipated problems:
Remind students that the griddles are very hot and will be on throughout class
Younger students will need a lot more help forming the pupusas. Be ready to be more hands-on with them. 
If the pupusa filling mixes into the masa harina, it will be ok! You can add more filling and cook it anyway.

Background information for instructor:
America is all the land from Canada to Argentina. 
The Three Sisters plants originated in Central America and have been used for thousands of years by ancient civilizations to provide them with all the nutrients that they needed.

Welcome Back Chickpea Pizza

Summary: Students will make personal pizzas and review kitchen guidelines.

Objectives: Students will understand kitchen rules and guidelines and learn how to make an alternative pizza crust from chickpea flour.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
(5-10 min)
Opening ritual: Welcome back to the kitchen! 
Students sanitize hands as they enter the classroom and take seats.
Once hands are sanitized, students take a seat and fill out an entry ticket. Have students rotate washing hands and working on entry tickets. Place the ticket aside once complete.

Engaging activity: Assemble pizzas.
(10-15 min) 
Demonstrate writing names on parchment paper.
Distribute parchment paper and allow students time to write names. (Have teacher write names for younger students.)
Demonstrate ingredients/making chickpea crust and how to spread sauce, cheese, and toppings on cooked crust.
Distribute cooked pizza squares to each student, placing them on parchment paper, and have students assemble their individual pizzas.
Distribute spoon and bowl with shredded cheese, sauce, and toppings such as diced bell pepper, diced onions, basil, mushrooms, arugula, etc.

(10-15 min) Cook & Review
Collect all pizza parchments onto sheet pans and broil at 400 degrees for ~5-10 min. 
Demonstrate washing dishes and wiping down tables:
Have students take turns rinsing their own bowls and wiping down desks (provide wet wipes). 
Distribute water and clean plates to each desk.
Review entry ticket & kitchen guidelines, inviting students to offer their own suggestions:
Safety at all times:
Stay at their work stations/no moving around the room
Listen to instructions/keep voices at a safe level
Use appropriate tools and use them correctly; tools at rest when not in use (not waving them around; not walking around room with them)
Masks on and hands clean
Respect:
Listening to each other
Helping each other
Eating together and open to trying different foods 
Clean Up:
All responsible for clean up
Compost versus trash
(5-10 min)
Closing ritual: Tasting
Distribute finished pizza squares
Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks!

Materials: 
Hand sanitizer on yellow cubbies
Entry ticket, pencils, & markers/sharpies
Parchment paper
Wipes for cleaning desks

Prep work and setup:
Preheat oven to 450 degrees 
Sanitize work stations and writing utensils
Water and cups 
Print entry tickets
Pre-make and pre-cut chickpea crusts
For each student, prepare spoon and bowl:
shredded cheese, sauce, and assorted toppings: arugula, diced bell pepper, basil, etc.

Anticipated problems:
Timing may be tight for larger classes
Allowing students to choose toppings may leave a lot of food waste

Questions for students/extension activities:

Pasta 

Summary: Students will make fresh pasta from scratch, roll it out and shape it. Students will also make either a sauce to dress the noodles, or they will make a filling for ravioli. 

Objectives: Students will be able to list some of the ingredients of pasta dough. Students will be able to roll and cut pasta. Students will be able to distinguish what makes certain foods “preserved” and “fresh.”

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
K-3: “tickle the sky” breathing activity.
Introduce the recipe:
This month we are making food from Italy. Who has eaten pasta/noodles/spaghetti before?
K-3: Making pasta with mushroom and tomato sauce.
Talk about seasonality and preserving tomatoes from the summer months.
4-5: Making pumpkin ravioli! 
Compare ravioli to other dumplings we have made.
Talk about seasonality in relation to squash

Engaging activity:
Cucumber table: Roll out pasta dough using a pasta machine. Encourage teamwork!
K - 3rd Grade - Cut out pasta using a pasta machine to make fettuccine.
4th - 5th grade - Roll dough into long strips, add filling, cut out ravioli by hand.
Corn table: Make pasta dough using a pictorial guide. With extra time, students will play a fresh vs. preserved foods matching game
Carrot table: 
4-5th grade - Cut vegetables and make filling for ravioli 
K-3rd - Cut onion, garlic, and mushrooms. Saute and season to make tomato sauce. 

Closing ritual:
Clean up, set the table, eat together, clear tables.
Ask for suggestions on how students would make the recipe different next time.
Share shout-outs before lining up.

Materials: 
Map
Recipe ingredients
Culture Box: photos, flag, scavenger hunt cards 
Plastic knives, chefs knives, cutting boards, bowls
Large saute pan
Large pot with lid
Guide to making pasta dough: part 1 and part 2
Task cards for tomato sauce

Prep work and setup:
Make one batch of ravioli filling for the first group.

Anticipated problems:
Students may need extra help in assembling ravioli and in working as part of a team with the pasta machines. 

Background information for instructor:
Different regions make different sauces and types of pasta
One type of dough can make many shapes of pasta!

Questions for students/extension activities: 
When have you had pasta or noodles? How do you like to eat it?

All: Preservation matching game

body:
3rd-5th grades
All Seasons 
Indoor


Recycling Games


Summary: Students will play two different games to learn how to sort different items for trash, paper recycling, or mixed recycling.  

Objectives: Students will be able to sort items common to their classroom, cafeteria, and home into the different trash and recycling bins.  

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles by using tactile, kinesthetic games to encourage learning. 

Social-Emotional Skills: Students practice social awareness by learning about an environmental issue and identifying how they can positively contribute to the issue. 

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Students begin sharing what they already know about recycling. For example, have students share how recycling works in our homes or classrooms, why it is important, and what happens to the items after we recycle them. 
Highlight that reducing trash by recycling reduces the negative environmental impacts of waste in our world on water, wildlife, and people. 
The teacher writes the information on the board, leading students to the fact that there are three bins we sort into; green bin for paper, blue bin for mixed recycling, and trash.  Students share ideas about what things go in these three bins.
The teacher explains that today we will be playing a game to help us keep learning what items go in each bin. “By applying what we learn today in our homes or at school, we can help make a positive impact on the environment.”

Engaging activity:
Show the students the recycling cards, pointing out how there are red cards for trash, blue bin cards, and green bin cards.  
Teacher models how to play a memory game with the cards, laying out the deck of 16 cards into a 4x4 square.  Students will take turns flipping over two cards at a time trying to match two cards that go in the same bin.  Be sure to explain that the cards don’t have to be the exact same item, they must be two items that go in the same bin.  If a student finds a match they keep the cards; if not the cards are turned back over. 
The teacher will then model “Heads Up” with the recycling cards.  Hold one card up to your forehead and walk around the classroom so students can see what it is.  Tell the students you will be asking yes or no questions to try to figure out what your card is. Example questions are:
Does my item go in the blue bin? 
Is my item made of plastic?
Is my item something that we would use in the classroom?
Tell the students it is important to play memory first so they can familiarize themselves with the cards before attempting to play “Heads Up”. 
Put students into groups of 2-4 students for the games.  Give each group a set of cards, a handout with instructions for the games, and an area to play.  Circulate to help facilitate the game.
Bring the students back to their seats for the closing ritual. Have each group of students put their cards back into a pile for the teacher. 

Closing ritual:
Add to the list from the beginning of class about which items go in each of the three bins.  Ask the students what items they learned about from the card games and what bin that item goes in. Teacher will write answers on the board.
Thank the students for their answers and for being good teammates playing these games together today. “Now that you’ve learned this skill, you can help to make a positive impact and reduce trash in our world.” 
Finish with a tasting of your choosing. 

Materials:
Recycling cards
Recycling Game Instructions sheet
Tasting

Prep work and setup:
Sort recycling cards into 16 card sets, with an even mix of blue, red, and green cards in each deck. 

Anticipated problems:
Students may have a hard time coming up with yes or no questions, but they can reference the recycling game handout if needed. 
As the students are playing, circulate through the room making sure the students are noticing what items are on the cards, and how those items are sorted for recycling or trash.  
Help students with “Heads Up” by helping them generate yes or no questions or by giving them clues when needed. 

Background information for instructor:
Information and materials about NYC recycling can be found on the NYC DOS website or on the GrowNYC Recycling Website.  

Questions for students/extension activities:
Students can continue to practice recycling back in their classrooms.  Classroom visits can be conducted to see what classes are doing the best job recycling and can be offered a reward. 
Students could also play recycling jeopardy to keep practicing how to sort different items, or make informational posters for their classrooms or cafeteria about recycling. 

Back pocket activities: None. 

Task cards or handouts used:  See above.

Photos: None.

3k/Pre-K
Fall or Spring
Indoor
Year B


Butterflies

Summary: Students will learn about the Butterfly Life Cycle and a little bit about butterflies generally (what do they eat, where do they live), Students will learn (surface level) about Butterflies as pollinators, Students will make their own butterflies and fly them around the room looking for flowers.

Objectives: Students will be able to recall some facts about butterflies. Students will decorate their own butterflies. Students will reflect on what it means to be helpful.

Core Competencies: garden skills and related academic content, social-emotional learning.

Cultural Responsiveness:  Communicates high expectations by expecting students to complete an art project, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles by including an auditory element, a hands on art project, and a kinesthetic activity. Reshapes curriculum by incorporating movement.

Social-Emotional Skills: Promotes self management by using new (or unmastered) tools (clothespins, watercolors), Promotes relationship skills by working well at table groups and sharing tools (markers/paint/whatever they’re using) and listening actively. Promotes responsible decision making by having students clean up after themselves. Promotes Self awareness by having students feel pride and ownership over the butterflies that they made.

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
pollinators, animals

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual with SEL component AND with emphasis on yearly theme/connection to previous lesson.
5 mins: Welcome students to class - how is everyone feeling?
Today we’re going to be learning all about butterflies and how they grow - and you’ll all get to make a butterfly to keep! Butterflies are very helpful insects. They help all the plants in our garden to grow. Raise your hand if you think being helpful is a good thing … who wants to share an example of something you did recently that was helpful to someone else, maybe someone in your class, or your family, or your teacher? 

Engaging activity:
Read aloud: 10 Minutes
Read your choice of butterfly book, make sure it discusses butterfly life cycle. Be as engaging as possible!
After the book, use your life cycle visual to go over the life cycle again - does it seem like students are getting it? Ask questions like, Whats first? What’s next? Then what? Point to the different stages as you are describing them. Make comparisons to human life: First you’re a baby, a caterpillar is like a baby butterfly, etc.
Make butterflies! (10-20 minutes)
As we saw in the book, butterflies come in every different color combination you can imagine! What color butterflies have you seen before?
Show students your example of a completed butterfly. Explain that they will be making their own, tell them that they will color the coffee filter, choose their antennae color (if you bought fun color pipe cleaners) and then an adult will help them put the butterfly together. (If you didn’t buy colorful clothespins they can also color their clothespins however they’d like.) If you talked about parts of a butterfly during the read aloud, now is a good time to go over those again, hold your example in the air: the white paper that you’re coloring will be what part of the butterfly? What part is the clothespin? What about the colorful pipe cleaner? 
Hand out materials and coffee filters - you and whatever classroom teachers/paras are there should go around helping students choose pipe cleaner colors and put butterflies together when students are done decorating their coffee filters. 
When students have completed butterflies, explain to them that when butterflies visit flowers and travel around the garden, it helps all the plants to grow (which is great!!), let them fly their butterflies around the room visiting the different flower visuals that you’ve displayed.

Closing ritual with SEL component:
Clean up (5 minutes)
Provide clear instructions on how students can help to clean up the classroom, reminder that being helpful is so wonderful and you are so appreciative of them, they’re being helpful just like butterflies are helpful in the garden! 
Dismiss them in your own way - who learned something new about butterflies today? Who was helpful today? Who’s proud of themselves for making such beautiful butterflies? Who’s excited to show a loved one their butterfly and tell them what they learned today?

Back pocket activity: 
Have a different book about butterflies on hand to read to end class if there is enough time for it. 
Speedy students can make a second butterfly 
If you are doing this lesson in the garden in the spring students can look around the garden for butterflies or plant flowers for future butterflies to come and visit

Materials: 
Coffee filters 
Clothespins
Colorful pipe cleaners
Markers or Crayons or Watercolor paints and brushes (whichever you’re most comfortable with or happen to already have)
A book about Butterflies: The very hungry caterpillar is a great one, or there's a NatGeo KiDS book “Butterflies” that talks about the life cycle but also how butterflies survive (camouflage), what they eat, how many different beautiful color combinations they come in, etc.
A butterfly life cycle visual

Prep work and setup:
Cut pipe cleaners into 2-3 inch pieces.
Make or Print a Butterfly life cycle poster (mine pictured below)
Make your own clothespin coffee filter butterfly to show as an example
Hang some sort of flower visuals around the room (could be pictures, could be drawings, whatever you have or want to make)

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
I don’t know if other schools do this as well, but in the spring at BCCS all the 3K/Pre-K classes hatch butterflies from caterpillars in their classroom. If classes are doing this, this is a good lesson to do in advance of that.
This lesson could be done in the garden if you have a good space/external noise level for a read aloud and are comfortable doing a simple art project with kids in your garden space.

3K-8th GradeAny Season
Indoor/Outdoor


Blooming Trees & Fruit Salad

Summary: Students take a closer look at the blooming trees in our urban landscape.  

Objectives: Students will celebrate Earth Day by painting the natural landscape around our school and/or understanding the role of trees in cities, helping to create local ecosystems that provide habitat and food for birds and animals and also helping to keep humans healthy.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, responsible decision-making, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Welcome and Happy Earth Week! What is Earth Day, why do we celebrate it, what does it mean to you?
Review of previous lesson: What’s Inside a Flower
Last week, we spoke about flowers. Who remembers what is inside a flower?
The main parts of a flower are:
Petals: to attract pollinators by their colors and lines
Stamen/anthers: create pollen
Pistil/stigma: catches the pollen
Ovule or egg: pollen joins the egg and becomes a seed
Do we eat any flowers? (Yes, we ate broccoli last week!)
(MS) Today, for Earth Week, we’ll be taking a closer look at all the flowering trees around our city. Other than being pretty to look at, how do trees benefit us here in NYC and/or in urban landscapes? http://www.fao.org/zhc/detail-events/en/c/454543/ 
Trees can contribute to the increase of local food and nutrition security, providing food such as fruits, nuts and leaves… today we will be making a fruit salad! 
Trees provide  plants and animals with habitat, food and protection.
Trees in cities can help to cool the air between 2 and 8 degrees Celsius, thus reducing the urban “heat island” effect. For example, the correct placement of trees around buildings can reduce the need for air conditioning by 30 percent, and reduce winter heating bills by 20-50 percent.
Trees are excellent filters for urban pollutants and fine particulates. They absorb pollutant gases (such as carbon monoxide, nitrogen oxides, ozone and sulfur oxides) and filter fine particulates such as dust, dirt or smoke out of the air by trapping them on leaves and bark.
Research shows that living in close proximity of urban green spaces and having access to them, can improve physical and mental health, for example by decreasing high blood pressure and stress. This, in turn, contributes to the well-being of urban communities. Demonstrate pictures from Trees NYC.
Mature trees regulate water flow and play a key role in preventing floods and reducing the risk of natural disasters. A mature evergreen tree, for instance, can intercept more than 15 000 liters / 4000 gallons of water per year.
Engaging activity:
(MS Students) Trees provide us with fruit and today, we are going to make a fruit salad. Last week we talked about flowers and how seeds are made. Today, we will use what grows around seeds (the fruit!) to make a fruit salad.
What is the job of the fruit? To spread seeds.
How do fruits spread seeds? 
A seed cannot typically grow under the mother plant because it will not have enough space, sunlight, or water underneath it’s ‘mom’.
Animals eat the fruit and deliver the seed away from the mother plant. 
Demonstrate how to slice and dice fruits and mix together in a bowl.
Pour dip on top of fruit salad or in ramekins.
(Elementary) We’re going to observe the natural landscape surrounding our school. Let’s take a look at the blooming flowers and trees and paint what we see.

Closing ritual:
Tasting

Materials: 
(MS_Cooking) 
Tree maps
Cutting board and knife for each student
Cups or bowls and forks for fruit salad
Ramekins for dip
(ELEM) Clip boards and card stock paper, Q-tips, crayons, pink paint

Prep work and setup:
Divide fruit into individual plates.
Print out copies of tree maps.
Clipboards with paper, paint, and paint cups

Anticipated problems:
Outdoor elements while painting: wind, rain, sun, sounds, other students in yard.

Background information for instructor:
Flowering Trees in NYC
Benefits of urban trees

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities:
None.

Task cards or handouts used: 
Trees NYC

body:
3K-KFall/Winter/Spring
Indoor


Worm Study 

Summary: Students will study worms in an indoor worm bin, draw what they see, and do the worm dance. 

Objectives: Students will learn what worms need to thrive in a worm bin and will understand that worms play an important role in the garden. 

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles by allowing students to learn about worms through dance, hands on observation, drawing, and writing. 

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson promotes self-management as students take small risks in holding and interacting with worms. This lesson also promotes relationship skills as students display care and calm around garden animals. 

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Today we are going to be meeting one of the animals that lives in the garden. Give students clues about worms (i.e. this animal doesn’t have any legs, this animal is very wiggly). We are learning about worms!
Teach students with worm dance about what worms do in the garden. 
Munch, munch! (hand up, making eating gesture)
Wiggle, wiggle! (wiggle your whole body)
Poop, poop! (knees bending on the words)
Soil! (arms up, big happy gesture)
Talk to the students about the rules of meeting the worms. We have to be gentle with them, because they are animals. We can hold the worms in our hands or on our popsicle sticks, but be sure to dig slowly and carefully with your popsicle stick. 
Today we are going to practice kindness as we meet the worms. Ask students how they would feel if they were a worm - “would you like it if a big giant screamed at you, stepped on you, or took you away from your home?”
Engaging activity:
Divide class into small groups, assign each to a tray. Place a generous handful of worms, along with their food and bedding, in each tray.
Hand out paper, pencils, and popsicle sticks for stirring in the worm bin. 
Encourage students to touch or hold the worms, making sure they are being gentle. 
Can you draw what you see? What parts do you see on the worm? Which end is their head and which end is their tail? Do they have eyes? Why do you think there is garbage in their home? What do you think they’re eating? If you can, add labels to your drawing.
If you would like, hand out hand lenses. Now you can look at one part of your worm very closely with this hand lens and draw what you see.
Walk around and help students label their drawings.
Have the students write a sentence about what they think worms like to eat based on their observations.
Closing ritual:
Wash hands. Have students come back to their seats.
Allow students to share about what they saw in the worm trays. Write their answers on the board. 
Thank students for practicing kindness today and being gentle with the worms. Give shoutouts to students who were especially kind. Celebrate students who took a risk and went out of their comfort zone today.
Finish with a tasting. Remind students about the worm dance, worms eat out leftovers and make soil! If we don’t finish our tasting we can give the leftovers to the worms! Giving food to the worms is one way we can show our thanks and be kind to them!

Materials: 
Pencils
Paper
Clipboards
Worm bin
Trays
Hand lenses
Popsicle sticks
Tasting

Prep work and setup:
Put blank papers on clipboards for students to draw on. Put scoops from the worm bin in trays. 

Anticipated problems:
Some students might be nervous around the worms. Tell students worms don’t have any teeth, they can’t hurt us. Try to encourage students to hold the worms. Make sure students hold worms over the trays, tell them not to walk around with the worms so they don’t drop them. 

Background information for instructor: None.

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities:
If time allows, have students prepare scraps to put inside the worm bin. Students can use scissors to cut strips of newspaper in the worm bin, crush eggshells with a mortar and pestle, or cut up old leaves and weeds from the garden for the worms. 
For 3k-pk, you can also prepare “sensory trays” for students to extend the activity (as they probably will be less interested in observational drawings).  Before handing out worm bins, put trays of vegetables and flowers, seed packets, and other things students can touch with at their tables.  Show students how to use their popsicle sticks and magnifying glasses to explore the items, and once they’ve mastered the sensory trays, hand out worm bins.  
Task cards or handouts used: None.

Grades: K-5
Season: Any

Indicate: Year A

Roly Polys


Summary: Students will learn about roly polys and their role in our garden’s ecosystem. 

Objectives: Students will have greater familiarity with their garden’s ecosystem, making them better environmental stewards.

Core Competencies: List all that apply: Environmental and community stewardship, garden skills and related academic content

Cultural Responsiveness: Explain how this lesson incorporates the example of culturally responsive education you are choosing to promote. Promotes student-centered instruction by having students share their existing knowledge and encouraging student advocacy

Social-Emotional Skills: Pick one of the linked skills and explain how students will practice it during the class. Relationship skills: Display calm, caution, or care for animals and plants

Garden Connection: How does this lesson make use of the garden? This lesson focuses on a common organism in our garden and its role in keeping our garden healthy. Weather permitting, students will make their observations in the garden.

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
For all lessons: Earth month
If gardening: animals, decomposers, stewardship, respect

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual with SEL component AND with emphasis on yearly theme/connection to previous lesson. (5 min)
Can you remind me what our garden rules say about plants and animals? How do we interact with them and why?
We mostly leave the animals alone because we might hurt them and they are helping our garden grow. However, we DO play with two decomposers sometimes, who are they? Why are they called decomposers?
Today we’re going to become roly poly experts! What do you already know?

Engaging activity:
Go over some roly poly facts with pictures and/or video. (5 min)
Some important roly poly facts:
Aka pillbugs, potato bugs, doodle big, basketball bug, little pig
Crustaceans, molt, have gills (can’t breathe underwater or in very dry conditions), 7 pairs of legs, tiny hairs all over their bodies to sense
Nocturnal, prefer wet conditions, mostly eat dead plants but sometimes eat healthy plants if they are very wet. They love strawberries!
Females have a pouch that holds hundreds of eggs and carries her hatched babies for a few weeks
Predator defenses: hard shell, curls into a ball, produce chemicals like a skunk to repel predators (birds, ants, spiders, amphibians, etc), camouflage 
They can live in unhealthy soil with toxic heavy metals like lead and break it down in their gut. Scientists observe them to see how much metal is in the environment/how healthy the ecosystem is.
Observe roly polys in the garden or inside, pillbug supply source listed below. (30 min)
Fill out observation worksheet
For younger kids, skip worksheet and make craft

Closing ritual with SEL component: (5 min)
What are some things you learned about pillbugs today? Did it change how you see them?
What would you say to someone who is scared of the bugs or thinks we should smush them?’

Grade Level Variations–REQUIRED for COOKING LESSONS

3K/PK: explore roly polys in the garden and do a simple compost processing chore like tearing leaves or sifting finished compost
K-1: find roly polys based on their habitat, do the roly poly craft inside. (see craft below)
2-3: look at a roly poly powerpoint, add roly poly facts to body parts to the roly poly craft (see craft below), and observe roly polys on the roof if there is extra time
4-5: completes observation sheet


Site Based Variations, if applicable 
Site name: 

Back pocket activity–REQUIRED: Make a sign for the garden educating people on roly polys.

Materials: 
Pillbug supply if needed
Magnifying glasses
Clipboards & pencils
Powerpoint (2-3 grades)
Worksheet (4-5 grades)
Craft supplies: construction paper, crayons/markers, metal paper fastener, glue stick or tape, googly eyes (optional)


Prep work and setup:
Pre-cut some triangles and legs for craft
Locate roly polys for observation or set up indoor enclosures

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
https://animaldiversity.org/accounts/Armadillidium_vulgare/
https://www.treehugger.com/roly-poly-pill-bug-facts-important-environment-4864410

   Everybody Cooks Rice

Objectives: Students will understand that some foods are common among different cultures. 
                    Students will also practice using the Evergreen reader annotation guide. 

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: Ask students if they can think of a food that is very popular all around the world, after hearing some responses tell them that rice is a food that is featured in many different cultures's cuisines. Tell students that today we will be making Cuban-style rice with black beans, which is also called arroz congris because of it’s gray color (gris = gray in Spanish). After they work on the recipe they will be reading a story talking about other rice dishes around the world.

Engaging activity: Students will prepare the Cuban-style rice recipe. As the rice finishes cooking, the students will read an excerpt of the Everybody Cooks Rice book.

Closing ritual: The class will eat the rice they made together and discuss some of the rice dishes from other cultures mentioned in the book.

Materials: 
Rice and beans recipe
Everybody Cooks Rice book
Cooktop
Pot
Knives
Microplane
Scissors 
Measuring cups/spoons
Ingredients: 
Brown rice (cooked)
Black beans
Onions
Cumin
Oregano
Bay leaf
Olive oil
Lime
Green bell pepper
Cilantro
Vegetable stock

Black Bean Dip

Summary: Students will work in teams to practice following a recipe for black bean dip

Objectives: Students will be able to work together to follow a recipe and learn a basic technique for making a bean dip.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: relationship skills, responsible decision-making, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Review the hummus recipe if students have made it before. What ingredients did we use? What are other types of dips that students like to eat? (guacamole, salsa, etc)
Today we are making another type of bean dip! Remind students that beans are a good source of plant protein (a “Grow” food) which helps to build muscles and keep us feeling full
Introduce black bean dip ingredients and directions
Groups choose roles and transition, clean hands

Engaging activity:
Upper grade students: work together to prepare the black bean dip at their table (Add lime, cilantro, cumin, salt, and garlic to the black beans, mash it)  
*K-2 students: Pass out supplies to each table group and introduce each ingredient one at a time, having each student take turns adding each ingredient to the bowl. At the end, everyone takes a turn mashing while singing the “mash it” song
Upper grade students slice cucumbers and carrots to dip in the black bean dip

Closing ritual:
Taste the dip together with vegetables and/or tortilla chips
Vote on how it tasted (loved/liked/tried it)
Presenters in each group share reflections about how their team worked together, what they did well and what was challenging
Reflection questions:
Where else can you get black beans to eat? (at lunch!)
What are other types of dips or beans that people have made or tried?
Why are beans good for our bodies? 
Would you add or change anything about this recipe?

Materials: 
Tray Prep List
Bowl of black beans 
Mini masher
Pincher cups of olive oil, salt, garlic, cumin,
Limes cut in wedges
Hand sanitizer
Recipe sheets

Prep work and setup:
Put seasonings in pincher cups
Cut limes into wedges and vegetables into manageable pieces
Put beans in bowls 
Write recipe and voting chart on board

Anticipated problems:
Students might disagree on how much seasonings to put in and end up using too much or too little for other team members’ taste. Remind students to use “pincher fingers” and not pour the whole bowl of spices on. They should taste test as they work to know if they need more seasoning
Students get confused that the vegetables are for dipping and try to put them inside the bean dip. Remind them to cut carrots into sticks

Worm Art

Summary: Students will use imaginary play and painting to learn about worms, their home, and their food.

Objectives: Students will be able to identify where worms live and what worms eat.

Cultural Responsiveness: incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Show students a handful or tray full of soil. Begin by asking students to think of animals that live underground in the soil. 
Explain that today we will be learning about the worms in the soil. 

Engaging activity:
In small groups, have students observe worms in trays. 
Beforehand, remind students about how to treat worms respectfully: moving slowly, not harming the worms, not taking the worms out of the box. Ask students to quietly watch the animals for a few minutes. 
Ask students to describe what they see in the box: how the worms move, how they feel, etc. Ask students to pay particular attention to the foods that the worms are eating in their home. 
Students can use popsicle sticks to search for worms in the trays. 
Next, tell students that we are going to paint what we saw in the worms’ home.
Students will paint an underground scene with brown paint and cooked spaghetti. The spaghetti acts like a worm and substitutes for a paintbrush. Students are encouraged to think of the spaghetti as a worm and make it move like a worm on the paper. 
Circulate to help students make the worm move and assist with any painting problems that come up. 

Closing ritual:
Ask students to share what they saw in the worms’ home and what they learned about the worms. 
Finish with a tasting. Remind students that any food we don’t eat can be fed to the worms!

Materials: 
Active, healthy worm bin
Trays
Popsicle sticks
Paper
Paint
Cooked spaghetti
Smocks
Tasting

Prep work and setup:
Cook spaghetti. Lightly oil it to keep it from sticking together. 

Anticipated problems:
Have students wear smocks for the painting activity, if available. 
Have hand wipes, sinks, or other methods for cleaning up after painting. 

Background information for instructor:
You can find information on creating and caring for a worm bin here.

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities:
Students can also participate in activities to help feed or care for the worm bin, such as shredding newspaper, cutting up food scraps, and misting the worm bin with water.

body:
3K-8th GradeFall/Winter
Indoor


Bats & Banana Pancakes 

Summary: Students will discuss the importance of bananas, its bat pollinator, and make banana pancakes.

Objectives: Students will learn about the banana journey from the farm to our plates.

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes positive perspectives on parents and families, communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, responsible decision-making, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: (5-10 min)
Quick Review: Last week we spoke about marine ecosystems and microplastics. Who can remind me of some of the key points?
Lesson Introduction: This month, we will talk about another set of the Earth’s most important creatures - pollinators! From our lesson on flowers, what does a pollinator do?
This week’s focus will be on bananas and their pollinators. Does anyone know what animal pollinates bananas in the wild? [bats]
Bananas are an ancient group that has evolved along with bats over 50 million years.
Over 500 plants depend on bats for pollination, including mangoes, bananas, agave, and cocoa!  
They’re also important as pollinators, because they are nocturnal and tend to gravitate towards plants that other pollinators miss. The flowers that are visited by bats are typically:
Open at night
Large in size (1- 3.5 inches)
Pale or white in color
Very fragrant – sometimes musty or rotten odor
Produce a lot of nectar
Are there bats in NYC? [Here in NY, there are 9 different species of bats. Since there are no banana plants growing wild in NY, do bats provide any other services? During warmer months our local bats feed on a variety of insects, including mosquitoes.]
Banana Facts:
Bananas originated in Southeast Asia, in the jungles of Malaysia, Indonesia or the Philippines where many varieties of wild bananas still grow today. (demonstrate on map)
There are over 1000 different varieties of bananas growing around the world in tropical areas, but we mainly eat only 1 type.
Bananas are a staple food in many tropical countries and play a major role in terms of food security.
Today, bananas are grown in more than 150 countries, and 105 million tons of fruit are produced each year with a global export value estimated to be US $8 billion in 2016.
Bananas constitute a significant portion of the export revenues for many Latin American and Caribbean countries. For example, banana exports account for a quarter of Ecuador’s total non-petroleum exports, and Ecuador accounts for around 30% of world banana market supply.
According to the FAO and the Ecuador government, the banana industry generates about 2 million direct and indirect jobs in the country.They are an essential source of income and employment for many households, as well as being a source of nutrition and food security for more than 400 million people in producer countries.
Why is this important? What would happen if a disease wiped out the type of banana the world eats? 
Bananas that we eat from the market aren’t grown from seeds, so each banana is a twin of another banana. Even though there are over 1,000 banana types, the only one we eat is the Cavendish, which is threatened by Panama disease along with other diseases. That means that when one banana gets sick, every banana gets sick.
This would be a huge blow to food security, jobs, and income in many Latin American and Caribbean countries. Would this have an effect on the U.S.?

Engaging activity: (20-30 min)
Cooking: Banana pancakes
Ingredients
1 cup all-purpose flour
1 tablespoon white sugar
2 teaspoons baking powder
¼ teaspoon salt
1 egg, beaten
1 cup milk
2 tablespoons vegetable oil
2 ripe bananas, mashed
Directions
Step 1: Combine flour, white sugar, baking powder and salt. In a separate bowl, mix together egg, milk, vegetable oil and bananas.
Step 2: Stir flour mixture into banana mixture; batter will be slightly lumpy.
Step 3: Heat a lightly oiled griddle or frying pan over medium high heat. Pour or scoop the batter onto the griddle, using approximately 1/4 cup for each pancake. Cook until pancakes are golden brown on both sides; serve hot.

Closing ritual: (5-10 min)
Tasting

Materials: 
Globe/map 
Fork, cup, banana, and 2 eggs for each student
Griddles and spatulas
Plates and forks

Prep work and setup:
Set up griddle stations.
Trays of tools and ingredients for each station.

Anticipated problems:
Adjust discussion according to age/grade. 
Guide students through measuring ingredients.

Background information for instructor:
All About Bananas
Banana Infographic
Bats and Disappearing Wild Bananas
Bats: Misunderstood Pollinators
Wildlife NYC: Bats

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities:
None

None

All Grades

September Intro Lesson

Summary:  Students will get a chance to get introduced to the kitchen and garden space at P.S. 216, while making a healthy snack using seasonal garden produce. 

Objectives: Students will be able to understand the norms and expectations of the kitchen and garden space, become familiar with the indoor and outdoor spaces in the kitchen and garden, learn how to be safe in the garden and kitchen, and make a seasonal dip and snack.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Welcome to the garden and kitchen! Separate the class into four groups. Two groups will start in the garden and two in the kitchen.

Engaging activity:
Garden jobs: students will practice weeding, watering, compost sifting, and amending. 
Students will take turns “teaching” the rule to that garden to the other students.
Garden rules and harvesting: 
This group will go over the Respect in the Garden rules in the ramada. Rules will be written up for each category. 
Afterwards, students will go harvest an ingredient for their tasting. Harvest ingredients can include basil, cherry tomatoes, cukes, carrots, or kohlrabi.
Kitchen safety:
Students will be guided through how to be and stay safe in the kitchen through a series of either teacher led or student led safety skits. 
For grades K-1st, students will take part in a teacher lead scavenger hunt of the kitchen. Different parts of the kitchen will be numbered or labeled. Teachers will verbally provide them with a clue or question (i.e. “Where do we put our compost?”) and students will be responsible for finding the number or label that corresponds to that question in an allotted amount of time. 
For younger students (2nd-3rd), the teacher will be responsible for leading the students through the classroom, and then acting out ways to be unsafe in the kitchen (i.e. a teacher is acting out sauteing and getting too close to the pan, flipping ingredients too fast, etc.). It is the responsibility of the students to indicate what is being done wrong. After each demonstration, have one student come up to demonstrate how to be safe. 
For older students (4th-5th), divide the students into groups of 2-3 with skit prompts written out for the students to perform to the whole group. There are enough skits for four groups. While each student group is performing their skit, have the other students determine what is being done improperly. 
Dressing Making:
Focus on introducing students to the norms of cooking in a group and some of the common tools we use. 
For older students, pick a table leader to be responsible for ensuring that all parts of the recipe are done properly.

Closing ritual:
Clean up, set the table, eat together, clear tables.
Ask for suggestions on how students would make the recipe different next time.
Share shout-outs before lining up.

Materials: 
Recipe ingredients
4th-5th grade: 4-5 different prompts for skits, written on pieces of paper
K-1st grade: laminated numbers or labels 
K-1st grade: Scavenger hunt questions and prompts
Measuring tools
Medium bowl
Whisk/wooden spoon

Anticipated problems: None.

Background information for instructor: None.

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities: None.
