Scavenger hunt numbers and talking points
Skit prompts
Take home recipe

body:
Grades 4-5
Fall
Outdoor
Year A

Wangari Maathai

Summary: Students learn about Wangari Maathai, what it means to be an activist, and engage in caring for their school's street trees by picking up trash in the tree beds, cultivating the soil in the tree beds, and spreading mulch to protect the soil.

Objectives: Students will be able to:
Identify Wangari Maathai as an activist and explain her contributions.
Define the terms "activist" or “activism” and provide examples.
Demonstrate how to care for street trees by picking up trash, weeding, cultivating soil, and spreading mulch.

Core Competencies: 
Environmental and community stewardship
Social justice
Garden skills and related academic content

Cultural Responsiveness: Promotes student-centered instruction (encourages student activism)

Social-Emotional Skills: Relationship skills (display calm, caution, or care for plants)

Garden Connection: 
Being an outdoor activity, a garden classroom can be used for the beginning of the lesson before transitioning.
Can utilize the garden depending on whether there are trees in or near the space. 
If tree beds are near the garden, classes can be split into a passive garden activity with the classroom teacher while the ESY teacher takes the rest of the students to the tree beds (see below).

Tags: environmental heroes, community, activism, stewardship, trees, weeding, cultivating, mulching, fall

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual (with SEL component, emphasis on yearly theme):
Welcome the students and ask them to give you a thumbs up/down to show how they are feeling. (10 minutes)
Today we’re going to learn about a hero who helped her community and the Earth by planting trees.
Before we talk about this hero, I’m going to show you some pictures of different types of trees. Based on what you see and what you may already know about trees, think about why trees are important. (Be sure to show different types, such as trees with fruit, and in different environments, such as trees in a park and in a forest)
Why do you think trees are important for humans?
What about for other living things? Why might trees be important?
Introduce Wangari Maathai, either by showing a video, reading excerpts from Seeds of Change, or going over the poster board about her that you have created (see Materials and Background Information). Then check for understanding by having students answer reflection questions.
(10 minutes)
Example questions for understanding:
Why is Wangari Maathai famous?
What movement did Wangari start?
Why did she start this movement?
What happened as a result of this movement?
What award did Wangari win?
What were some challenges Wangari faced?
Based on what you have learned about Wangari, why do you think she is known as an activist? What do you think being an activist means?

Engaging activity:
Today each one of you is going to be an activist like Wangari by taking care of trees around the school so they grow big and strong. (25 minutes)
Review the different tasks students will be doing to care for the trees (e.g., trash pick up, weeding, cultivating, mulching), showing the different tools and supplies they’ll be doing for each. If possible, demonstrate the activities either in a garden tree bed or in a large pot of soil you have prepared to look like a tree bed. Be sure to review safety when using tools.
Have the classroom teacher(s) help you divide students into groups based on the number of tree beds (note: if there are too many students for the available tree beds, pull small groups to the trees from a stationary garden activity, outlined in number 2 below). Transition students to the tree beds. Ensure at least one adult has eyesight of each student and that no one is standing in or near the street.
Circulate while students are doing the different tasks in case there are questions or safety concerns.
Move students to different trees to help others or care for additional trees as needed and as time allows. See Backpocket activity if needed.
If it makes more sense logistically, pull groups of students to care for the tree beds while the rest of the class stays with the classroom teacher in the garden to complete a leaf rubbing activity. If possible, take a co-teacher or paraprofessional with you, or try to stay in line of sight of the teacher in the garden. (25 minutes - concurrent with tree bed activity)
Have the teacher hand out paper (on clipboards if needed) and fallen leaves you have collected, along with crayons or colored pencils.
Students will place the leaves under the sheet of paper and lightly color over the leaf, revealing its outline on the paper. Have one you have done to show as an example.
The results could be used as backgrounds for bulletin boards, cut into fall wreaths, or simply taken home by the students.
Be sure to have extra paper on hand for students who have time and want to do multiple sheets. Also consider having a tree or fall-themed word search available as a backpocket or alternative (see Backpocket).
If pulling students, choose only 1 or 2 tree care tasks since you’ll have less time with them.

Closing ritual (with SEL component) (5 minutes):
Bring everyone back together if you have separated them into groups. 
Raise your hand and tell me 1 thing you learned about Wangari Maathai.
Show me thumbs up/thumbs down if you think you were an activist today like Wangari.
What is 1 thing you did to care for trees today?
If there’s time, you could hand out a tasting that comes from a tree, like figs.

Back pocket activity:
Alternate Opening
Tree Yoga: Before we talk about this hero, we’re going to move like a tree! When I say go, you’re going to stand up, make sure you have space, and follow my directions as I show you how to move. (Reference video to show or adapt as needed)
For Engaging Activity
Sidewalk chalk: If some students working in the tree beds finish before others, or not all students can work on cleaning up a tree bed at once, the students that are waiting can draw on the sidewalk using sidewalk chalk.
Word search: If students finish the leaf rubbings, they can work on a fall-themed word search. Examples here - requires free account to download.
Rain plan
Indoors, the opening ritual could stay the same, the leaf rubbing activity could still be done, and students could create posters to put up in the school about why trees are important, or write a “tree memory” of a story from their life where a tree is a main character (e.g., Christmas, playing in a park or at recess, field trips, summer vacations, etc).

Materials: 
For Opening Ritual
Poster board with pictures and information about Wangari Maathai, or the book Seeds of Change
Alternatively, if starting indoors with access to a smartboard, you can use a video read aloud of Seeds of Change or this video interviewing Wangari Maathai.
For Engaging Activities
Tree bed activity
Gloves and trash bags - if cleaning up tree beds and weeding
Optional: trash grabbers
Hand cultivators or trowels -  if breaking up the tree bed soil
Mulch in bags, tub trugs, or wheelbarrows/garden carts - if mulching
Leaf rubbing activity
Tree leaves
White paper
Clipboards, if there isn’t a hard surface students can use
Crayons or colored pencils
Word search sheets, fall or tree themed

Prep work and setup:
Locate tree beds to be worked in and ensure any potential hazards are cleared ahead of time.
Create Wangari Maathai visual/poster, if not using other options listed above
Collect fallen tree leaves
Create leaf rubbing example
Put white paper on clipboards if needed

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
Depending on where the tree beds are located, it may be necessary to check with your school’s administration on whether the activity would be considered a “walking field trip”.
Background information
Why we need trees video
How do trees benefit people article
NYC Greenthumb Tree Care Clinic Handout
NYC Trees video
All About Wangari Maathai worksheet (create free account to view)

Photos, if applicable:

For recipes note: A, B, C, D


Cooking Skills - Recipe Reading Practice 

Summary: Students will continue learning about recipes and will practice reading and using a recipe to bake cookies.

Objectives: Students will be able to recognize and understand the format of a basic recipe. 
Students will be able to read a recipe to prepare a dish. 

Core Competencies:
Kitchen skills and related academic content: students will learn how to read a recipe 
Socio-emotional intelligence: students will be able to complete a task collaboratively and semi-independently from the teacher. 

Cultural Responsiveness: 
Communicates high expectations: students will read and complete a recipe on their own
Reshapes curriculum: students will work together in groups
Positions teacher as facilitator: there will be less teacher talk and students will work semi-independently. 

Social-Emotional Skills: 
Self management - students will try a new food
Relationship skills - students will work collaboratively in groups
Self-awareness - students will have a sense of pride and ownership of their finished product

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual:
Welcome back to the Green Room! In our last class we talked about recipes and what information they give us so that we can make delicious food. You also began learning how to read a recipe properly and had a little bit of practice. Today we are going to continue practicing and test your skills by working together in your groups to make oatmeal cookies!

Engaging activity:
Part 1: Recipe Reading Review (10 minutes) 
First we are going to review the different parts of a recipe and how to read it properly.
Have students answer the following questions:
What are the two parts of the recipe? (ingredients list and steps)
How are the ingredients listed? What order are they in? (in the order they need to be added) 
Recipe reading reminders:
Read your ingredients list and steps before starting!
Make sure you have all the ingredients and tools you need first (mise en place - everything in its place, is what chefs call your set up)
Now that we have reviewed and gone over some extra tips, how confident do you feel that you can work together to complete a recipe? 

Part 2: Preparing hummus and pita chips
Wash hands
In their groups students will make a batch of cookies using this recipe
Before starting, remind students to read through the recipe to make sure you have the correct ingredients and tools they need. 

Closing ritual:
Clean up.
Say thank yous and have a tasting. 
How confident did you feel in your recipe reading skills? How comfortable do you feel about reading recipes in the future?
How well did you work in your groups?

Materials:

   Good Mood Food

Summary: Students will discuss mood boosting foods and prepare recipes using those ingredients.

Objectives: Students will be able to identify foods that can help relieve stress and improve their mood.
                    Students will be able to work collaboratively to follow a recipe 

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations,reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: Today we are going to continue talking about dealing with stress. We have already made tea because there are certain herbs that can help us relieve stress. We also talked about comfort foods, which are foods that may make us feel less stressed because they bring good memories and feelings. Did you know that there are some foods that are considered mood boosters? These foods have nutrients that can help us feel happier and less stressed. Some examples are:
Avocados: have B vitamins and healthy fats that help the neurotransmitters in our brain that keep us happy functioning well
Berries: have antioxidants that keep us healthy and reduce stress that occurs within our body systems
Whole grains: have magnesium and zinc that are shown to help depression and other mood disorders
Water: helps our body absorb vitamins/minerals and helps give us energy and reduce stress

Engaging activity: Today we are going to make a few snacks using some of those ingredients. 
Guacamole with tortilla chips and also fruit infused water. 
Split students into groups to prepare the guacamole, chips and fruit water

Closing ritual: Ask students how they felt during this activity. 




Materials: 
cutting boards
Knives
Oven 
mixing bowls
measuring cups/spoons
water pitchers

Prep work and setup (include photos if possible): 
Preheat oven
Set up trays with necessary tools/ingredients
2 groups making water
1 orange
~½ cup strawberries
Small bunch of mint 
~¼ cup blueberries
Measuring cups (dry/liquid)
Water pitchers

1 group making guacamole
Avocados (cut in half/pitted)
Red onion - sliced
Cilantro - small bunch
Mixing bowl
Masher
Wooden spoon
Measuring spoons
Salt/pepper
Limes

1 group making tortilla chips
~10 tortillas
Olive oil
Salt, pepper, garlic powder, onion powder, nutritional yeast
Baking sheets - w/ silpat or foil
Knives or pizza cutters
Plastic bags

Fruit Infused Water
Cut oranges into slices
Add to the pitcher
Cut strawberries quarters
Add to the pitcher
Measure ¼ cup of blueberries
Add to the pitcher
Remove leaves from the mint 
Add to the pitcher
Measure 3 cups of water
Add to the pitcher 
Use the wooden spoon to stir the fruit
Try to smush the blueberries slightly 
Add more water if needed
Put pitcher in the fridge until ready to serve

Guacamole  (Makes 3-5 servings)

Ingredients 

2 medium ripe avocados
¼ white or red onion
1 lime, juiced
1 clove garlic, minced
¼ cup cilantro leaves
salt and pepper, to taste
¼ teaspoon cumin


Steps

Prep your vegetables: dice  onion and finely chop your cilantro. Put to the side once it’s prepped.
Mince garlic using grater, put to the side
Open your avocados and scoop out the inside of the avocado onto a big bowl.
Smash the avocado in the bowl with a masher or with the back of a fork to make sure it is smooth.
Add the lime juice, cumin, salt and pepper, and mix together with the other ingredients. Taste and adjust for seasoning.
Enjoy!

Tortilla Chips
 
Ingredients 						  Directions
                 

Tortillas


Cut tortillas into chip sized pieces
Add to bag

Olive Oil

Measure 3 tablespoons olive oil
Add to bag
Hold bag closed and shake to spread olive oil

Onion Powder


Add ½ teaspoon onion powder to chips

Salt & Pepper

Add ½ teaspoon salt to chips
Add ½ teaspoon pepper to chips


Once all ingredients are in the bag
Close the bag and shake it well
Make sure all the chips are covered
Spread chips onto baking sheet 
Leave some space between the chips 
Put into oven to bake! 
The chips are ready when they are crispy

 Introduction to Knife Skills

Summary: Students will learn and practice knife skills to prepare a fruit salad.

Objectives: Students will be able to demonstrate proper knife safety and identify a few basic knife cuts. Students will be able to work together to complete a recipe. 

Core Competencies: Kitchen skills and related academic content: students will practice knife skills and safety, students will complete a recipe

Cultural Responsiveness: 
Communicates high expectations - students will complete multiple tasks in a recipe
Reshapes curriculum - incorporating group work
Positions teacher as facilitator - students will work in small groups

Social-Emotional Skills: 
Self-management - taking small risks by using new tools
Relationship skills - working well in groups
Responsible decision making - using tools safely

Agenda/Class Flow: 
Opening ritual:
Pulse check: How is everyone doing today? Do we feel energized and ready for class? (If needed, play a game to get students moving!) 
We are going to continue learning and practicing how to use our kitchen tools properly. Today we are focusing on knife skills and will talk about how to use our knives safely and learn a few of the common/basic knife cuts.

Engaging activity:
Have students wash hands and split into groups of 4-5 students. Each student will start with a knife and cutting board. Where they will learn and demonstrate how to hold their knives correctly. 
Knife safety guidelines: how to hold a knife, what not to do with knives, bear claw and saw
Why is it important to practice knife safety? - prevent getting hurt, helps make it easier to use your knife
There are different ways to cut items with our knives. Today we are going to be slicing, chopping and dicing. (Each student should have at least one of each fruit to practice each cut)
As a class go through each cut using the fruit salad ingredients
Slice - grape or kiwi
Chop - apple
Dice - strawberry
Now that you have learned these three types of knife cuts you all will get extra practice by working in your groups to make a fruit salad. 

Closing ritual: Say thank yous and taste fruit salad together as a class. 

Reflection Questions:
How confident do you feel in your knife skills (thumbs)?
 What is something you want to improve on?

Cutting boards
Knives
Mixing bowls
Wooden spoons
Fruit Salad Recipe

Prep work and setup:
Write description of each type of cut on the whiteboard
Peel kiwis
Have one of each fruit available for each student to practice different cuts

The Three Sisters

Summary: Students will read a version of the Three Sisters legend to understand how corn, beans, and squash help each other grow in the garden. Students will then plant the Three Sisters together in the garden.  

Objectives: Students will be able to explain how Native American planting techniques are still used in our garden, and how corn, beans, and squash help one another to grow.

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Solicit information on what students know about the Three Sisters. 
Explain that we sometimes eat the Three Sisters, corn, beans, and squash, all together, but they are especially known for growing together in the garden. Explain that the Native Americans who lived here before were the ones who first knew how to plant the Three Sisters together.
Pass out copies of Three Sisters Legend worksheet.  Read it aloud as students follow along. Ask clarifying questions about which plant each sister represented and what happened in the story. Use this as an opportunity 

Engaging activity:

Closing ritual:
Ask students to remind you what they learned about the Three Sisters.
End with a tasting.

Summary: Students will think about what they’re thankful for and do a thankfulness tree craft reflecting those things, and finish with a tasting (dates)

Objectives: Students will reflect on the concept of gratitude and think about what they are grateful for in their own lives. 

Core Competencies: List all that apply: Environmental and community stewardship, garden skills and related academic content, SEL, multicultural education.  

Cultural Responsiveness: Ideally, this lesson promotes positive perspectives on parents and families (assuming that students feel thankful for someone in their family), Communicates high expectations by expecting students to understand the assignment after it is explained by the teacher and take the initiative to complete it on their own.

Social-Emotional Skills: Volunteer to participate in gratitude activity (this won’t really be voluntary but ideally they won’t be resistant to it and I will ask for friends to share out things that they’re grateful for), make connections to outside life (by reflecting on things in their outside life that they are thankful for), using tools safely and cleaning up messes (scissors, glue, etc.), and displaying sense of pride or ownership about the craft that they’ve created.

Welcome students to class. Talk about the upcoming holiday of Thanksgiving, how it is a time to reflect on all that we have to be grateful for. (Literacy check) What does it mean to be grateful?

Engaging activity:
Explain that today we are going to be doing a craft that reflects things we are thankful for. 3K/Pre-K will be coloring fruits that grow on trees and then the teacher will write on the fruit what the student says they are thankful for. These will all be attached to a large communal thankfulness tree. K/1st grade will be creating their own individual thankfulness tree attached below.

Have students who want to share with class share with class, then do a tasting. We will be doing dates with this lesson in 2022.

   Argumentative Writing

Summary: Students will prepare two different recipes and then write a convincing argument explaining why someone should choose one over another. 

Objectives: Students will be able to write a convincing argument regarding a food related topic.

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: Today we are going to be practicing writing an argument.  A good argument can convince or prevent someone from trying something. A very important part of writing a convincing argument is using descriptive language. Descriptive language includes words that help someone imagine something using their minds without seeing or tasting something for themselves. In class today you all are going to split into groups to make two different recipes, which we all try. After tasting our recipes you all will have to write an argument trying to convince a friend to try the recipe you liked best. 

Engaging activity: Students will be split into groups to prepare recipes. The recipes will be different in some way. After making their dish students will try both recipes and take notes about what they liked/disliked about each one. Students will then have to write a short argument explaining why someone should eat one recipe over the other. 

Closing ritual: Ask students to read what they wrote aloud. Take a vote to see which recipe students would prefer. 

Materials: chart paper, tasting words poster, recipes (see below for different options), paper, pencils

Prep work and setup (include photos if possible):
Anticipated problems: May be hard to do just 2 recipes with a large class

Tips and tricks: 
Pre-select groups so that students can not come in with a preference for certain ingredients
Do a blind taste test (so students won’t just choose the recipe they made)

Plant Life Cycle Exploration


Summary: Students will explore the early fall garden to look for plants in different stages of their life cycle. 

Objectives: Students will be able to describe the life cycle of a plant. 

Core Competencies: Garden skills and related academic content 

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson promotes student-centered instruction by having students apply what they’ve learned to independent work in the garden. 

Social-Emotional Skills: Students practice self-management by practicing garden safety rules during independent garden exploration. 

Tags: seed-to-table, plant life cycle, scavenger hunt

Agenda/Class Flow: 
Opening ritual (15 minutes):
Welcome students back to the garden. “This year, we are going to focus on becoming true garden experts. We are going to get really good at knowing how plants grow and how we, as gardeners, can help take care of them. Today we are going to focus on learning about an entire plant’s life cycle.” 
Ask if students know what the word “life cycle” means. “A cycle is made up of events which go in a circle. A cycle keeps going over and over again.” Point out that “cycle” looks like the word “circle.”
Using student answers, draw up a plant life cycle on the board from seed, to baby plant, to flower, to fruit, to seeds again. “What happens at the end? Does the cycle end? No, it repeats all over again!”
“What happens to the old plant when it dies and decomposes? What does it turn into? Where in the garden do we put it? In the compost!”
Explain that today, we are going to be exploring the garden to see the plant life cycle in action. Have students remind you of garden safety rules and expectations, emphasizing the importance of showing respect for plants, animals, and each other. Tell students you will be looking to see who has been an especially good leader today in being respectful in the garden. 

Engaging activity (20 minutes):
Pass out clipboards with scavenger hunt worksheets. Explain that there will be ten signs in the garden with a star on them. Each sign will be next to a plant in one stage of the life cycle. “Your job is to figure out which part of the life cycle it is in, and then draw a picture of it in the correct box.” Go over the worksheet and have students identify the life cycle step for each box, using the visuals if students are still developing reading skills. 
You can encourage students to label their drawings with descriptive text if that’s appropriate for your students. 
Have students go out into the garden to find the signs. Circulate and assist if needed. 

Closing ritual (10 minutes):
Call students back to the whole group. Have students share their drawings with a partner. 
Share a tasting. Have students identify what part of the life cycle the food comes from.
Have students give shout-outs to each other for being respectful in the garden of plants, animals, and each other. 

Grade Level Variations:
You can challenge students with stronger writing skills to write descriptive words for the plants they see at each life cycle station. 

Back pocket activity:
If time allows, use a second copy of the worksheet to allow students to draw their own complete version of the plant life cycle. Provide colored pencils or crayons so they can add detail to their drawing. 

Materials: 
White board and dry erase markers
Worksheets and star signs
Pencils
Clipboards
Colored pencils or crayons (optional)
Tasting

Prep work and setup:
Set up star signs around the garden. Choose plants where the life cycle is very apparent. For a seed station, you may want to have seeds set out in a cup or container. 
Optionally, you can set colored pencils or crayons in small buckets or cups at each station so students can add color to their drawings. 
Put worksheets on clipboards.
Prepare tasting.

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable: None.

Photos, if applicable: None.

4 Color Scavenger Hunt

  
Summary: Students walk around the garden looking for signs which instruct them in various tasks, mostly sensory observations. 

Objectives: Students will orient themselves in the garden through a sensory observation scavenger hunt.

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson positions teacher as facilitator and gives students independence to explore the garden on their own.

Social-Emotional Skills: Students practice self-management by working at their own pace and mindful of their own physical space during the scavenger hunt. 

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Welcome students. Explain that students will have a chance to use their five senses today to help them explore the garden on a scavenger hunt. 
Review garden rules and garden safety. 

Engaging activity:
Give each student a clipboard with the scavenger hunt paper – make sure to give out several versions with the order mixed up (so students go to different stations at different times)– and a pencil so that they can record their observations and/or answer questions as they go. 
Explain that students are looking for signs with shapes that match the ones on their worksheet. 
Model how you will find a sign that matches one of the shapes and colors on your paper (i.e. a red square) and write the answer next to the red square on your sheet.
Send students to start one color group at a time (i.e. send the students with the worksheets that start with green shapes to the area of the garden where all the green signs are first).
Circulate and help students read the cards and follow the instructions as they go. 

Closing ritual:
Ask students about their experience in the garden; what was their favorite station, what was the best thing they saw in the garden etc.
Finish with a tasting. 

Materials: 
Dry erase board and markers
20 laminated cards coded with shapes (triangle, square, circle, heart, star) in each of these colors: red, green, orange, purple.  See link below for examples.
Scavenger Hunt Worksheets and example signs 
Tape for attaching cards to stakes
Garden stakes
Clipboards
Pencils
Tasting

Prep work and setup:
Prepare signs for the scavenger hunt and place them out in the garden. Put the signs of each color in one area of the garden (so there is one area of the garden where all the green signs are and another part of the garden where all the red signs are etc.).
Print out scavenger hunt worksheets and put them on clipboards. 
Place out any materials needed for the signs of the scavenger hunt (i.e. if a sign says draw this tool, leave a tool at that station).

Anticipated problems:
Students will finish at different times, have students that are finished early help other students in finding and reading the signs.

3K-5th GradeAll Seasons

Garden Jobs

Summary: Students will participate in a garden job that contributes meaningfully to the health and growth of the garden. 

Objectives: Students will be able to explain the garden job that they did and why that job is important to the garden’s well-being. 

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Introduce students to the garden job that they will be doing today. Ask students to tell you about what information they already know about the garden job and why it’s important.
Explain clearly why the garden job helps the garden. 
Give students specific instructions about how to do the garden job, writing on the board if available. You can also demonstrate to students physically how they will move their bodies or use certain tools. Use this as an opportunity to also reinforce expectations for student behavior in the garden.

Engaging activity:
Lead students in the garden job. Repeat instructions as need be, or lead students through the job step-by-step.
Clean up tools and other materials. Wash hands if necessary.

Closing ritual:
Ask students to share back about their experience of doing that garden job. Ask prompting questions, such as:
How did our garden job today help the garden?
What was your favorite part of doing this garden job?
What did you see, hear, or feel while doing our garden job?
Who would you like to compliment or appreciate or give a shout-out for their work today? What did they do?
Finish with a tasting.

Materials: 
Tools and materials necessary for the specific job
Means for hand-washing or sanitizing
Tasting

Prep work and setup:
Assess what garden jobs need to be done and how many students can safely and effectively participate in the job. 
Set up tools and materials for the number of students.
Prepare and clear the area where students will be working, checking for any potential dangers or distractions. 

Anticipated problems:
Each garden job presents its own specific challenges; more detail is given in the background information section below. 
Scaffold jobs based on your students’ abilities and personalities, their prior experience, and their age. For instance, older students may be able to plant seeds or bulbs independently, but younger students may need step-by-step guidance through each part of the process. 
Like all of us, students are liable to make errors, especially if it's their first time doing a particular job! Be willing to accept students’ mistakes and learning as part of the process during the garden job. If you really need a job to be done without error,  leave it for another adult or teacher to do. 

Background information for instructor:
Find more details about how to facilitate specific garden jobs below:
Building Compost
Clearing
Harvesting
Mulching
Planting Seeds
Planting Starts
Saving Seeds
Seed Starting
Sifting Compost
Turning Compost
Watering
Weeding

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities:
Students who finish the garden job before others can be teacher’s assistants, responsible for tasks such as distributing or collecting tools or materials, cleaning up the space, cleaning tools, or helping with hand-washing or sanitizing.

body:
3-5th GradeAny season
Indoor

Whole Wheat Pancakes 
(Food Processing / Farm to Table)

Summary: Students will read Right This Very Minute to learn about the steps food goes through from farm to table. Students will make pancakes with whole wheat flour.

Objectives: Students will be able to describe how food gets from farm to our plate. Students will be able to follow a recipe for pancakes

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making, self-awareness, social awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Ask students to pair-share about what their favorite food is, and where they think it comes from
Tell students that most food has to go through a lot of steps before we get it on our plates. Read Right This Very Minute book (it’s lengthy so you can skip some pages for time)
Ask students to recall the steps that foods went through in the story. 
Those steps are called food processing. Usually foods that are less processed are the healthiest. When foods are processed, often they have a lot of sugar, salt, and fat added to make them taste better and last longer
Today we are making pancakes! What do you think is the main ingredient for pancakes? (Wheat)
Pass wheat berries around the room and show how wheat is processed using the grain mill
Explain the difference between white and whole wheat flour (white is more processed and has less nutrients) and remind that whole grains have more fiber and nutrients
A lot of foods are healthier when they are less processed. 
Introduce recipe, choose groups roles, transition

Engaging activity:
Students measure and mix pancake ingredients in a bowl with their groups 
Teachers collect pancake batter and scoop onto griddle to make mini pancakes 
While teacher cooks pancakes on a griddle:
Provide each group with a set of food source picture cards. Have groups work together to match the foods with where they came from (i.e. bread from wheat, jam from berries, etc)
Serve pancakes with maple syrup (distributed by teacher)

Closing ritual:
Groups present on how they worked together as a team to follow the recipe
Reflection questions:
Why might it be better to use local or less processed ingredients when possible?
Would you make this at home? 
Would you change anything about this recipe?
Why are whole grains good for our bodies?
What steps did the ingredients go through before they ended up on our plate as pancakes? 
Optional math extension: do math problems using a pancake recipe

Materials: 
Tray set-up:
Mixing bowl
Whisk
Flour (small bowl)
Salt (pinch cup)
Soy milk (measuring cup)
Oil (pinch cup or pre-mixed with milk)
Baking powder (pinch cup)
Cinnamon (pinch cup)
Honey (pinch cup sprayed with veg oil)
Materials:
Griddle
scoop for batter
Cooking spray
Plates
Forks
Squeeze bottle of syrup

Prep work and setup:
Pre-make some batter/pancakes as backup

Anticipated problems:

Background information for instructor:

Questions for students/extension activities: 

Task cards or handouts used: 
Food source matching cards (from get to the source foodcorps lesson)
Group recipe
Take-home recipes

2-5
Fall/Spring/Summer
Outdoor
Year A

Irrigation

Summary: Students will learn what irrigation is, different types of irrigation, and why we use it in the garden.

Objectives: Students will be able to understand how humans have adapted to be more efficient farmers in different environments

Core Competencies: Environmental and community stewardship, garden skills and related academic content, cultural diversity 

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson encourages learning within the context of culture because we will highlight ways different cultures have adapted irrigation techniques to their climates.

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson requires students to use responsible decision making when using their watering tools.

Garden Connection: This lesson happens in the garden space, will have students observe the irrigation mechanism in the garden, and have students hand water spaces.

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
For all lessons: human and nature relationships, interconnectedness
If gardening: water, farming adaptations

Agenda/Class Flow: 
Welcome students back to garden class. 
“Today we will be focusing on one very important thing a plant needs to survive. Before we start, can someone remind me what a plant needs to survive?”
Focus on water.
“Raise your hand for how you think the garden gets enough water: Do you think (1) the garden teacher waters it all? (2) the plants get water when it rains? (3) something else?”
“All of these answers are correct, but there is one main way our plants get water that you may not have heard of - it’s called irrigation.”
“We’ve talked before about how humans and nature are connected, and irrigation is a very important way that humans changed their behavior to better take care of plants.”

Engaging activity:
“To learn more about irrigation we are going to do a very quick reading and see some creative ways that people give water to their plants all over the world!”
Pass out reading.
“Even though our garden uses an irrigation system, sometimes as gardeners we need to check on our garden and make sure it has enough water. There are some spots in the garden that do not have any irrigation,so we have to water them by hand. Today we are going to be watering with watering cans, trying to find the areas that do not have any irrigation. You can find these by looking for the little signs”
GIve watering instructions and expectations

Closing ritual with SEL component:
Close with a seasonal tasting.
Ask students how they feel connected to the garden today?
“Even when humans create systems to help nature, the systems can’t do it all. It’s important that we work together to check on the garden and help out as humans. Humans can listen and respond to plants better than machines can.”

Back pocket activity–REQUIRED: 

Materials: 
Irrigation reading - adapted from the nat geo article

Prep work and setup:
Print readings
Make signs to mark unirrigated areas and place them around the garden

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
Nat geo article on irrigation
If you want to go more in depth, there is a lot of information about specific countries and how they are trying to improve their water use and irrigation techniques. I found info on Pakistan, India, Uzbekistan, Jordan, and there is definitely much more.

K-1
Spring/Summer

Indicate: Year B



Our Little Garden Harvest Lesson


Summary: Students will learn about harvesting and the importance of working together in the garden and kitchen.  Students will reflect on the tasks they’ve done this spring that helped get the garden ready for harvest, and participate in a garden tasting.  

Objectives: Students will understand the importance of harvesting as part of gardening. 

Core Competencies: Environmental and community stewardship, garden skills and related academic content, kitchen skills and related academic content, social-emotional learning.

Cultural Responsiveness: Communicates high expectations because students will be expected to harvest within a certain time frame, Reshapes curriculum because of incorporating group work, positions teacher as facilitator when students discuss in small groups to complete card game

Social-Emotional Skills: Relationship skills, students will complete a harvest task for their class community and share the harvest with their classmates

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
For all lessons: Spring garden jobs, spring recipes
If cooking: Peeling and/or shelling, peas and/or carrots Heating element: none
If gardening: harvest, stewardship, vegetables

Agenda/Class Flow: (50 minutes)
Opening ritual (15 minutes).
Welcome everyone!  Pulse check: how is everyone feeling today? Raise of hands for: excited, hungry, tired, bored, etc.  
Explain that today we are going to be learning about harvesting.  We’ll talk a little more about what that means later, but first let’s read a book about a neighborhood that comes together to make a meal using ingredients they harvested from their garden.
Have students gather in a circle to read Our Little Kitchen (or other harvest related book!).  
Reflection questions: What vegetables did they pick from the garden in this book?  What other ingredients did they use in their chili?  If we were to make a meal for our class, what would you make?
Engaging activity (10 minutes):
 Explain that today we are going to be learning about harvesting.  Who here has heard of the word harvest before?  (Raise of hands)
Harvesting is when we collect vegetables, fruits, herbs, and other plants that we have grown in the garden.  This spring, we’ve worked together in the garden to plant our seeds, spread our compost, water our plants, and weed our garden beds.  That was a lot of hard work!  (Round of applause, or pat our own backs).  Today, we are going to pick and gather the ripe crops that we’ve been taking care of this season.  
Hand out 5-6 picture cards of ripe and unripe fruits/vegetables (see below).
Ask students to work in their table groups to sort the ingredients into 2 piles: ripe (ready to eat), and unripe (not ready yet).  
After they’ve sorted their piles, show students the picture of a green, unripe fruit.  Is this ready to harvest yet?  (Take answers from tables)  You’re right, not yet!  Then show students the ripe fruit.  Is this ready to harvest?  Yes!  We can tell it is ready to harvest because of its COLOR.  
What about this?  Show students the picture of a very small carrot or other unripe vegetable.  Ask if it is ready to harvest yet.  Not yet!  You can tell it’s not ready yet because of its SIZE.
Engaging activity (10 minutes):
While students are working on card game, take one table group out at time to harvest one crop (peas, spinach, radish).  Remind students that today, we are going to be working together to harvest for our class, just like they did in Our Little Kitchen.  That means we will all harvest our vegetables into one bucket, and bring them back to share with each other.  
Demo harvest, have each student pick one vegetable and put it in harvest bucket. 
Engaging activity (10 minutes):
Help students wash vegetables in the bucket, or alternatively have a few already washed and prepped from previous class.  
Students wash hands and sit with their table groups. 
Hand out cutting boards, peas to shell (if using peas) or carrots to peel.  
Closing ritual with SEL component (5 minutes)
Can someone tell me a job we helped out with this spring that helped our garden grow?  (weeding, watering, composting, and planting the seeds)  Another applause or finger snaps for our class.  The garden was also doing a lot of work growing, taking in the water, and taking in the sun, so we could harvest these vegetables today (Finger snaps for the garden).  
Try tasting–thank you gardeners thank you cooks!



Back pocket activity: 

Materials:

Cutting boards
Bowls for collecting harvest
Our Little Kitchen book (or other harvest/community related book!)

Prep work and setup:
Fill bucket with water

Alternative Proteins	

Summary: Students will make a classic Italian dish of sausage and peppers with alternative Beyond Sausage protein.

Objectives: Students will be able to explain the benefits of eating alternative proteins. 

Cultural Responsiveness: communicates high expectations, encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: (10 min)
Quick Review: We’ve talked alot about pollinators these past few weeks. What were some of the pollinators  we discussed and the fruits they pollinated?
Students should be able to name:
Bats pollinate bananas.
Bees pollinate blueberries.
Flies pollinate mangoes.
Intro Lesson: Most of the pollinators we’ve discussed were insects. Can insects be food themselves? Share some of these insect eating facts:
There are more than 1,900 edible insect species on Earth, hundreds of which are already part of the diet in many countries.
Two billion people eat a wide variety of insects regularly, both cooked and raw.
36 African countries are "entomophagous," as are 23 in the Americas, 29 in Asia, and 11 in Europe.
Beetles are munched by people living in the Amazon basin, parts of Africa, and other heavily forested regions.
Butterflies and moths in their larval and pupal stages are full of protein and iron. They're very popular in African countries as an excellent supplement for children and pregnant women who may be deficient in these nutrients. 
Indigenous people in Asia, Africa, Australia, South America, and Mexico commonly eat bees and wasps.
Termites, with large colonies found in arid regions of Africa and Australia, are a popular food in those regions. 
In Democratic Republic of the Congo (Zaire), termites are lightly fried and sold in marketplaces to eat by hand.
Termites are high in protein, but also contain a high level of fat, and thus have a strong caloric value. 
Protein content of termites is on the same level as dried saltfish taken from the same area, and is much higher than in beef.
Ask students, what are some reasons people might eat insects? Elicit:
Healthier options: many insects are packed with protein, fiber, good fats, and vital minerals—as much or more than many other food sources.
Raising and harvesting insects requires much less land than raising cows, pigs, and sheep.
Insects convert food into protein much more efficiently than livestock do—meaning they need less food to produce more product. 
They also emit considerably fewer greenhouse gases than most livestock.

Engaging activity: (20-25 min)
Beyond Sausage and Peppers Recipe
We won’t be eating insects today, but we will be trying out another alternative protein - pea protein! 
Certain plants can be excellent sources of protein, often with fewer calories and fewer potentially harmful effects than animal products (ex: heart disease, cancer, diabetes, bone health, and weight control).

Closing ritual: (5 min)
Tasting: “Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks.”

Materials: 
Recipe cards and globe (or map)
Tray set-up for each group of 3-4 students

Prep work and setup:
Make italian seasoning
Tray set-up for each group of 3-4 students:
 small-medium onion
1 pepper medium red, yellow, or orange
2 beyond meat sausages
1 ½ tbsp all purpose flour
1 ½ cups canned tomatoes Italian style with onions, celery, and peppers
1 tsp Italian seasoning
Cutting boards and knives
1 tablespoon, ½ tablespoon, ½ cup, 1 teaspoon measuring tools
3 burner stations with: brown serving tray, spatula, 3 bowls for serving

Anticipated problems:
Yuck factor of discussing eating insects.
Lead students step-by-step through recipe measurements (demonstrate different measuring spoons/cups).

Background information for instructor:
Insects as Food for Humans
UN Urges Eating Insects
The Nutrition Source_Protein

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities:
None

Task cards or handouts used: 
Recipe card

body:
K-1
Spring/Summer
Outdoor
Year A

Sun Printing

Summary: Students will become familiar with what flowers, leaves and plant parts are in the garden, and use them to make cyanotypes or sun prints.

Objectives: Students will be able to walk carefully and safely around the garden, be respectful of living plants growing in the garden, and arrange their collected plant parts in an artistic and creative way. Students will be able to use natural sunlight to capture images, and understand this as an alternative to digital photography.

Core Competencies: Environmental and community stewardship, garden skills and related academic content.

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson communicates high expectations of students, by inviting students to walk freely around and collect natural elements safely and carefully, encouraging them to be stewards of the garden and treat the space gently and respectfully. This lesson also promotes student-centered instruction, because students are given the autonomy to pick out what objects they want to include in the project, arrange them creatively, and create diverse prints.

Social-Emotional Skills: Students will practice self-awareness, in the process of building unique projects that reflect their own creative art styles and feeling pride and confidence in their artistic abilities. The students will get to be playful and joyful in the garden and learn that they can make beautiful artistic creations without traditional art supplies.

Garden Connection: This lesson uses plant parts, flowers, leaves, sticks, etc., ideally things that have already fallen into the beds or on the ground, to create an art project. It also incorporates art in outdoor education, showing students that they can make art in the garden or in any outdoor space, using the sun and other natural elements.

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
For all lessons: Garden jobs, food preservation, Spring/summer/sunny days
If gardening: plants, roots, leaves, stems, flowers, petals, sticks, seeds, sun

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual: (10 min)
Welcome students back to garden and kitchen class and review class expectations, “Be kind, be safe, be respectful, be brave.” 
“Today we are going to be doing a really fun art project, called sun printing or cyanotyping. Can everyone repeat the word, cyanotype? This is an art project you can do in any outdoor space, like a park, the beach, or your backyard. The most important thing you need is a sunny day. Can someone share your favorite outdoor place, maybe your favorite park? What does nature look like in that place?”
Give students a chance to share. Provide an example, ex: “My favorite outdoor place is the beach. There’s ocean waves, sand, rocks, sticks, shells, grass, and bushes.”
“Can someone tell me how we can be respectful to living plants and animals in our garden?”
Give students a chance to share.
Remind students that plants and flowers are growing in the garden, and that we can’t pull off leaves, flowers, or vegetables, or we will hurt them and they won’t be in the garden for us and other students to enjoy anymore. Tell them that today we will be exploring the garden and collecting things, but we want to all be very gentle with the plants, because this is our garden and we are gardeners, and gardeners take care of their gardens with love and care.

Engaging activity: (25 min)
“Today we are going to walk around the garden for 10 minutes, collect some leaves, sticks, and other plant parts that we find, and then we will use them to make a print, which is like a picture. The sun will help create the image, instead of using a camera or phone to take a picture, or drawing the picture by hand. This is an art style that people have been doing for over 200 years! People all over the world, who live in different natural environments, use this art technique, and you can use whatever you have accessible to you. For example, if you are in the park and you only see grass and sticks, you can use that to make your sun print.”
Show students an example of a cyanotype print (maybe one with cyanotype paper and one with construction paper or with whatever materials you are using). 
“You can walk around by yourself or with a friend, and collect 5-10 things. Look for things with cool or interesting shapes or textures. If you collect small things, you can include more of them in the picture. If anyone needs help picking things out, or you are not sure if it is something that is alive and growing or not, ask me or another teacher and we can help you.”
Give students 10 minutes to walk around the garden and then have them come back to a central space and spread out and find their own space.
“I am going to pass out paper, and you can arrange your items in a cool design on the paper. Make sure each plant part has its own space on the paper - try not to pile things on top of each other. When you are done arranging your print, take a step back and don’t touch it. It needs a few minutes to sit in the sun, and the sun will print the image of your objects onto the paper. Does anyone have questions before we start?”
Pass out paper and help students arrange their prints. Help students tape down the objects with clear tape if they are not heavy objects and might fly away (leaves, stems, flowers, etc.)
Gather with students somewhere away from the prints so no one touches them.

Closing ritual: (5 min)
“You all were so respectful in the garden today, of each other and of the living plants and animals that are in our garden. Did anyone see any animals in the garden beds? How were you respectful of the animals? How were you respectful of the plants?” 
Give students a chance to share. 
“I saw a lot of students helping each other out today which is so kind. Can someone give a special shout out to a friend that helped you out, either in finding your plant parts or making your print?”
Give students a chance to share.
Tell students to walk carefully over to their prints and take everything off of the picture. Have them share their prints if there’s time.

Grade Level Variations
Older students can work together to make a 3-5 panel cyanotype, that you can string together and hang up in the classroom. You can encourage them to make a collaborative design that spans across multiple papers.
They can also learn more about the history of cyanotyping: https://mfastpete.org/art-break-with-the-mfa-sun-prints/  

Site Based Variations
If you have a shady area in the garden, you can arrange the cyanotypes there and then move them into the sun. If you are using cyanotype paper, the sun starts printing immediately when it is exposed to sunlight, so you have to make the design quickly. If you do it in a shady place, you have more time for students to arrange their designs and still come out nicely.

Back pocket activity:
Write observations about the plants they collected
Leaf tracing/rubbing
Split into groups and make fairy houses out of the natural materials they collected. Have them name the house and share back
Have students put all the compostable materials into the compost and turn/sift the compost

Materials: 
Cyanotype paper (hot press)
Construction paper (dark colors work best)
Plant parts
Clear tape
Cardboard / clipboards
Prep work and setup:
For younger students, you can collect materials / make them accessible or obvious so it’s easier for them to find.
You can tape cyanotype paper/construction paper to pieces of cardboard or clipboards before class (ideally inside or somewhere shady) so the papers don’t fly away, especially if it’s a windy day.

Background information for instructor or anticipated problems, if applicable:
Encourage students to place down their object and then not move it around or touch it too much, so that their print comes out clearly. You can also split up the group and print with half and have the other do leaf/rubbings or another back pocket activity so you can give the students printing more attention. 
Construction paper needs to sit out in the sun for longer periods of time than cyanotype paper. If you use construction paper, maybe have the students collect their prints next time they come to the class.
“Sonoran Desert Sun Prints” by Brit Palomarez in The Sonoran Desert School Gardener’s Almanac could be a way to tie in different geographic landscapes and how that affects culture and art.

Photos, if applicable:

Copyright Edible Schoolyard NYC. Attribute before sharing.

Photosynthesis: Light Experiment Part 2 

Summary: Students examine the results of their experiment to determine how sunshine affects plants. 

Objectives: Students will understand that plants use sunlight to photosynthesize and make food.

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles with a turn and talk introduction, a plant observation and writing activity, and a hands on art based activity. 

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson promotes relationship skills as students practice sharing materials during the plant observation and the leaf art activities. 

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Welcome students. Ask them what they remember about the experiment they started last time. Review the details of the experiment with them (all the plants get soil, water, and air, but some were covered with a box so they didn’t get any sunlight). 
Ask students to share their predictions with a neighbor. 
Engaging activity:
Pass back out students’ Light Experiment worksheets from the last class. Show students what they have to do on the worksheet to complete their study of the experiment. 
Send students back to their stations to make observations of their plants.
Bring students back to the large group. 
Summarize the results of the experiment with students. 
Write the word “chlorophyll” on the board. Practice saying it out loud with the students. Explain that this is what plants need to help them turn sunlight into food. It’s also what makes leaves green! We are going to do an investigation to see if we can get the chlorophyll out of some leaves. 
Demonstrate to students how you can rub a green leaf on blank paper to make a green mark. 
Pass out green leaves and blank paper for students. Let them experiment and make their own artwork from the green leaves. 
Closing ritual:
Clean up the leaf art.
Share a tasting. 

Materials: 
Dry erase boards and markers
Light Experiment worksheets 
Six plants (3 that have been in the light, 3 that have been in the dark)
Labels showing which plants have been in the sun and which have been in the dark
Boxes to cover plants
Pencils
Blank paper
Green leaves
Tasting

Prep work and setup:
After the light experiment part 1 you should have 3 plants that have been in the sun and 3 plants that have been in the dark for about a month. 
Collect leaves for chlorophyll art. 

Anticipated problems: None. 

Background information for instructor:
This lesson is part 2 of a light experiment. The plants need about a month before they clearly show the results of the experiment. 

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities: None. 

Task cards or handouts used: See above.

body:
3K-Pre-K
Spring
Outdoor
Year B



The Garden in the Spring


Summary: Students will learn about different signs of the Spring season that can be seen in the school garden and some things that grow in the garden in the Spring.

Objectives: Students will be able to identify signs of spring in the garden.

Core Competencies: garden skills and related academic content, social-emotional learning.

Cultural Responsiveness:This lesson reshapes curriculum by incorporating movement and group work. This lesson positions teacher as facilitator by expecting students to work mostly independently.

Social-Emotional Skills: This lesson promotes relationship skills by expecting students to work well in groups, Display calm, caution, or care for animals and plants, and Make connections to outside life.

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
For all lessons: Seasons, Spring
If gardening: Signs of Spring

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual with SEL component AND with emphasis on yearly theme/connection to previous lesson.
Welcome students to class. (5-8 mins)
How is everyone feeling today? Who can tell us what season we’re in right now? Winter is over… is it fall? Etc. How do we know it’s Spring? Explain that some signs of spring are: Hearing/seeing more birds than in the winter, seeing lots of baby plants (which we call sprouts), seeing more flowers starting to grow, and seeing more insects out and about! See if anyone can repeat any of these back to you - say each a few times. 
Remind them how we move safely around the garden and why that is important.
Explain the activity. Check for comprehension. 
Today you’re going to be searching around the garden for signs of spring. I have pictures of different signs of spring that you can see around our garden. You will be given a picture of a sign of spring and you’re going to walk around with your picture and see if you can find the sign of spring.
Engaging activity:
Giving students laminated signs of spring and seeing if they can find it in the garden (flowers, sprouts, birds, insects).
Explain that when they find it they should call you over to show you, and if they’re right you will give them a new picture. Make sure you’ve printed out at least 7-10 different signs of spring (and 5ish copies of each) - as you hand pictures to students, remind them what the sign of spring is, i.e. can you find this sprout in the garden? Can you find this flower? It works well if some of the pictures are very easy to find (boosts their confidence) and then some are a little more tricky - don’t give them the tricky ones first! You will want to walk around carrying the stack of pictures so you’re able to pass them out/collect them as students call you over to show you what they’ve found. 

Closing ritual with SEL component:
Gather students back up, collect the pictures
What were you searching for in the garden today? Signs of ___ ? Raise your hand if you found a baby plant - a sprout? (hold up a picture of one) Raise your hand if you found a flower? More than one flower? (hold up example) Raise your hand if you saw or heard a bird? (picture) Raise your hand if you saw a bug somewhere in the garden? (picture) Raise your hand if you moved around the garden safely? Does anyone want to share what was the most interesting or beautiful thing that you saw in the garden today?
Dismiss


Back pocket activity–REQUIRED: 
Students can water the garden or you could have a worm or bug bin set up for them to look around in.
Alternatively, if there is time: You could talk to students about specifically what plants grow in the garden in the spring, explain that since it is getting warmer it is time to start planting seeds, because plants will be able to survive better when its warm - in the winter most plants cannot grow as well because it is too cold and there isn't as much sunlight. Lead a guided tour of the garden and show students what has already been planted in the garden, are there sprouts yet or just seeds? Why can’t we see the seeds? 

Materials: 
This lesson requires you to take some pictures of signs of spring in your garden and then print them out (and laminate if possible). When I’ve done it I took about 10 pictures (3 or 4 flowers, 2 or 3 distinct looking sprouts, a roly poly, maybe a bee or flying insect if you can get a picture, and one picture of a bird) and then made 5 copies of each one so some students could be looking for the same picture at the same time. 

Prep work and setup:
Print, laminate, and cut photos.

Latinx Heritage Month: Mexican Street Corn Salad (Esquites)


Summary: Students will be welcomed to ESYNYC programming, go over class expectations and…

Objectives: Students will be excited to start ESYNYC programming. Students will understand expectations in ESYNYC classes. Students will do a hands-on, engaging, seasonal activity.

Core Competencies: List all that apply: Environmental and community stewardship, social justice, garden skills and related academic content, kitchen skills and related academic content, multicultural education.  

Cultural Responsiveness: Communicates high expectations 

Social-Emotional Skills: Display calm, caution, or care for animals and plants,Willingly take turns and/or share tools, Working well in groups

Agenda/Class Flow: (link to relevant hand-outs and include estimated time)
Opening ritual:
Welcome students (TIME)
Welcome to Edible! How are we feeling today? With your thumb you can say great, just okay, not that great.
Where are we today? Who hasn’t been to the kitchen classroom? With the person next to your right ask each other what do you hope to cook this year?  
Go over expectations (TIME)
In order for us to learn and have the opportunity to come to the kitchen classroom we have some rules to make it the best environment for everyone. 
What are some ways we can be safe? What are examples of not being safe? How can we be responsible? How can we be respectful in the kitchen classroom? 
We need to respect each other but we must also respect our tools, plants, etc. 
Green Room Expectations
PS 109 Kitchen Class Rules 2020

Engaging activity:
Recipe introduction (TIME)
Today we are celebrating Latinx Heritage Month by making Mexican street corn salad (also known as Esquites or Elote en Vaso). During September and October we honor the contributions of Latinx and Hispanic people, which includes anyone who speaks Spanish. 
Raise your hand if you or someone you know speaks Spanish. 
Recipe overview (TIME)
Many of the ingredients of Esquites are things that we can find growing in the school garden or on a farm during the summer.
Mexican Street Corn Salad (Esquites) Recipe
Activity overview (TIME)
To get you started, I have taken the corn kernels off the cob. You could also make this recipe by leaving the corn on the cob, which is called Elote instead of Esquites (show whole corn cob).
For the rest of the ingredients, you’ll either be cutting them up into small pieces, or mixing them together to make the dressing.
Show the items to cut; go over tool safety and use.
Peppers, scallions, cilantro
Show the items to mix.
Yogurt, sour cream, cotija, lime, spices
Activity (TIME)
Pass out trays of items to cut to each table/group. 
Adults should float to help students cut, collect food scraps or prepared ingredients, and answer questions.
As tables/groups finish the tray of items to cut, pass out the tray of items to mix.
Adults continue to float.
Clean-up/Prep for tasting (TIME)
As tables/groups finish the tray of items to mix, instruct them to clean their tables and provide cleaning supplies.
All used cutting boards, bowls, and tools should either be collected in dish bins and/or put in the sink to be washed.
Any used items with large amounts of food should be emptied into the compost/trash before being collected.
Tables should be wiped down.
While students are cleaning up, mix together the corn, cut ingredients, and dressing in a large mixing bowl.
Spoon the Esquites into tasting cups. Serve with either a utensil or a tortilla chip for students to eat it with.

Tasting and closing ritual:
Have students taste together (TIME) 
Ask students to share what they think, reminding them to be respectful (don’t yuck my yum).
If there’s time, ask students how they might change the recipe.
Reinforce one SEL practice (TIME) 
Did the student try something new? 
Did they accomplish something you can remind them of? 
Ask them how they feel after spending time in the kitchen.

Evergreen Introduction 
Scavenger Hunt

Summary: Students will use their senses and vocabularies to find objects/spaces in the garden.

Objectives: Students will be able to...
Learn the rules of the Green Room and the garden 
Become familiar with the garden
Become familiar with garden tools

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, self-awareness

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Hello everyone, welcome to the Green Room! This may be your first time or your hundredth time in this space, so what we are going to do today is go over the rules and later we will go outside for a fun activity in the garden.
Go over the Green Room rules. Give students the 4 main rules, have them fill in what that means
Be Safe (Knife/tool safety, listening to instructions)
Be Responsible (CLEAN UP)
Be Respectful (Don’t yuck my yum)
Be Adventurous (Try everything/try new things) 

Engaging activity:
Hand out clipboards/pencils

Answer the question on the prompt on your worksheet
If there is a task, do the task an put a check mark on the worksheet after you complete it

Closing ritual:
Wash hands
Go over scavenger hunt answers/responses
Say thank you’s
Enjoy a tasting!

Pencils
Clip boards

body:
3K/PK
Fall
Indicate: Indoor/Outdoor
Indicate: Year A/Year B

What does a gardener do? 

Summary: Students are introduced to the concept of a gardener, listen to a read-aloud about someone working in the garden, and then experience themselves as gardeners by doing a garden job.

Objectives: Students will be able to understand the definition of a gardener and complete a light gardening task that builds on their skills. 

Core Competencies: Environmental and community stewardship, garden skills and related academic content

Cultural Responsiveness: This lesson communicates high expectations as students use tools to garden independently with little help from the teachers, and conveying to students your faith in their abilities.  

Social-Emotional Skills: Students will practice relationship skills when using the shakers to aim seeds on the dirt for planting, and practice caution around each other when using these tools.  

Garden Connection: How does this lesson make use of the garden?  This lesson takes place in the garden.  Students will complete light garden processing tasks. 

Tags–Pick a tag from each category: 
For all lessons: monthly theme, holiday or heritage month (if applicable), region of the world (if applicable)
If cooking: Cooking skill(s), Main ingredient(s). Heating element: none, stove or oven.
If gardening: garden theme (ie pollinators, soil, seeds, compost, animals, stewardship, vegetables, water, etc)
For 216: Indicate if lesson is co-taught and/or double period

Agenda/Class Flow: (45 mins)
Welcome students back to class.  The garden looks a little different than last time we visited, let’s go check it out to see what’s new.  (15 minutes)
Remind students that in the garden, we use our walking feet.  Make sure you have both of your walking feet on!
In the garden, we use our eyes to see.  Show me how you use your eyes (demonstrate by holding your hands up to your eyes like binoculars).  Good!  We are going to use our eyes to see today, but we are not going to touch the plants just yet.  
If you see a bee or a bug today and you are feeling nervous, give yourself a hug tight–That’s how we are safe around bees in the garden.  
Walk students to an area where they can sit in a circle.  Explain that today, we are going to learn about gardeners.  Raise your hand if you are a gardener!  If you take care of a garden, that means that you are a gardener.
Let’s read a book first to learn what gardeners do.  
This book is about a very tiny gardener.  Gardening can be a lot of work, and this gardener has to ask for help from a friend to take care of his garden.  
Reflect:  what did this gardener grow in his garden?  Was it a vegetable? A fruit?


Engaging activity (15 minutes):
Today, we are going to work together to take care of the garden.  Gardening is easier when we do it together!
Complete a garden task with students.  If it is fall, you can plant cover crops with students.  Explain that when we plant seeds in fall, the plants can cover the soil like a little blanket to keep the soil safe and warm all winter long.  Cover crops can help our plants grow big and strong next spring, just like the plants in the book.  
Show students how to gently shake seeds onto empty beds.  Sing a song about shaking (“Shake it shake it shake it, shake it all you can, shake it like a shaker, and shake it once again!”).
Show students how much hard work they did for the garden.  Raise your hand if you are a gardener!  Today, we are all gardeners.  

Closing ritual with SEL component (10 minutes):
Sanitize hands and distribute a tasting. “Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks!”
Finish with a goodbye song.

Grade Level Variations–REQUIRED for COOKING LESSONS

Site Based Variations, if applicable 
Site name: 

Back pocket activity–REQUIRED: 
For groups that move quickly through the garden processing task, have a few easy backup tasks available (i.e. seed sorting or some herbs that they can separate between leaves and stems).

Parmesan shakers or empty spice shakers for planting cover crop

Some classes will be too squirmy to sit for a book.  If this happens, you can sing a song instead, to the tune of : 
Sleeping little peapods sleep all winter long
Should i come and wake them with a little song
They’re so still
Are they ill?
Wake up little peapods!  Climb little peapods climb climb climb
Climb little peapods climb climb climb
Climb little peapods climb climb climb
Climb all springtime long
You can also sing this for other cover crops–clover, buckwheat, radishes, etc. and instead of “climb”, ask students to “root”, “sway” or “grow”.

Guerilla Gardening for Birds


Summary: Students make seed bursts to grow bird-friendly plants in their neighborhood, learning about the importance of habitat and the role they can play to make New York City a better home for birds.

Objectives: 
Students understand how native plants are good for both people and birds.
Students learn which native plants and trees can support birds in New York City.
Students understand the importance of increasing bird habitat and other green spaces in New York City.
Students learn about historical figures who created green spaces in their New York City neighborhoods
Students learn how to create more bird-friendly habitats in their neighborhoods.

Cultural Responsiveness: Encourages learning within the context of culture–plants and birds that are local to New York City.

Social-Emotional Skills: Social awareness: learning about and discussing social justice issues.

Agenda/Class Flow: (include estimated time)

Show picture of guerilla gardening. What’s happening here?
Show guerilla gardening logo. What do you think this means?
Explain guerilla gardening: taking over abandoned or neglected parts of your city to grow plants.
How does this work make the city better for people?
Elaborate more on guerilla gardening. 
One important New York City guerilla gardener was Hattie Carthan.
Hattie Carthan moved to Bed-Stuy, a predominantly Black neighborhood in Brooklyn, in the 1950s. She noticed that there were very few trees, particularly compared to wealthier parts of the city that were predominantly white.
Ms. Carthan knew that trees were important because they made the air cleaner and the temperatures cooler.
In response, Ms. Carthan raised money from her neighbors and created an organization called Tree Corps, organizing the children of Bed-Stuy to plant over 1500 trees in the area.
Here is a mural of Hattie Carthan, who became known as the Tree Lady of Brooklyn.
Another important New York City guerilla gardener was Liz Christy, who used seed bursts to transform the many vacant lots in her neighborhood, the Lower East Side of Manhattan. Seed bursts are balls of clay, soil and seeds that you can toss wherever you hope to see flowers grow.
Ms. Christy was inspired to clean up a vacant lot in her neighborhood that was full of garbage.
Believe it or not, the government of New York City, which owned the lot, at first refused to let Ms. Christy improve this abandoned piece of land.
But Ms. Christy kept fighting, and eventually the city gave in. They rented to lot to Ms. Christy for $1.00 a month, and she turned it into the Bowery-Houston Community Farm and Garden. Following her example, community gardens sprang up all over the city.
What do you think of this way of making our city better?
We’ve talked about how plants are important for the people who live in a city. Did you know they are also important for birds? How are they important?
Plants provide food for birds
Did you know that birds are also important for plants? How are they important?
Some birds can pollinate plants, helping them grow.
The National Audubon Society protects birds and the places they need, and they have an amazing resource that will show you what plants you can grow here in New York City to attract birds. Here’s a list of plants and the birds that like to eat them.
You are going to be guerilla gardeners today! You are going to make seed bursts.
You can toss your seed bursts in a tree bed or in an abandoned lot and you can take care of the seeds as they grow.
Today you are going to make seed bursts that are full of seeds for plants that New York City birds like to eat.
Let’s brainstorm a list of places in your neighborhood where you could plant your seed bursts.
Here’s a great video from the National Audubon Society that tells you how to make seed bursts.
And here’s a list of some public parks in New York City that have pollinator gardens

Materials: 
Model magic or clay or clay powder
Seeds for plants that New York City birds want to eat.

Visuals of dirty and clean/flourishing gardens - to show the process of guerilla gardening overtime with images that they recognize
