Habitat Hunt and Orientation

Summary: Students will be welcomed back to the garden, review garden expectations, and explore the garden on a scavenger hunt looking for the basic necessities of life for animals.

Objectives: Students will be able to understand and observe that the garden is a home for many living things.

Cultural Responsiveness: promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, positions teacher as facilitator

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, responsible decision-making 

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
“Welcome back to the garden! This year we are going to be learning about the plants, animals and people who make are garden so special.” 
“Because the garden is a special home, we need to make sure we keep ourselves and the garden safe. Let’s talk about how we can do that: walk on paths not on the bed, don’t pick anything unless your teacher asks you to, be gentle with all the bugs you see.”
“Our garden is a habitat for lots of special animals. Write the word “habitat” and its definition on the board: a place where an animal lives and grows.
Help students generate a list of the things that animals all need (food, water, shelter, air) by asking leading questions, writing answers on the board.
Teach students the “Habitat Song,” to the tune of “Lollipop, Lollipop,” with hand motions for the third line:
“Habitat, habitat, have to have a habitat
Habitat, habitat, have to have a habitat
Food to eat, water to drink, a place to sleep”

Engaging activity:
Explain that our garden is a good habitat for many animals because it gives them food, water, and a place to sleep. Tell the class they are going to go out on their own and go on a habitat hunt.
Pass out worksheets. Explain that we are going to look for animals in the garden, but also for important parts of their habitat: food, water, a place to sleep. Model how students can draw on their worksheet, as well as how they can label their drawing or write a sentence at the bottom about what they observe.
Ask students to predict what animals they might find in the garden today: bugs, worms, birds, etc. Remind students about expectations for interacting with animals in the garden. 
Send students out into the garden. Circulate to help students draw and write what they see. 

Closing ritual:
Ask students to share what animals and animal habitats they observed in the garden today. 
End with a tasting. 

Materials: 
Clipboards
Pencils
Habitat Hunt worksheet
Tasting

Prep work and setup: None.

Anticipated problems: 
Students may have trouble finding animals in the garden, especially if they are only looking for larger animals like birds or squirrels. Remind them that the hunt is also for habitat. Students can draw areas in the garden where they think animals might like to visit, or they can draw sources of food or water from animals. Model this for them in the beginning of the activity.

Background information for instructor: None. 

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities: None.

Task cards or handouts used: See above.

Photos: None.

Three Sisters Soup
Summary: Students will explore the food culture of Indigenous Peoples of the Americas by creating this Three Sisters soup. The soup also highlights the agricultural method of companion planting

Objectives: Students will be able to:
Create a soup by combining and pureeing the three sisters: corn, beans, and squash
Understand the difference between agriculture and foraging as food sources
Discover some foods that grow in NY state

Cultural Responsiveness: encourages learning within the context of culture, promotes student-centered instruction, incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills, responsible decision-making

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual: 20 mins for 3k-K 
3k-K First 10 mins: meet in classroom, sing goodmorning gardeners, and go over plan for the day
Hand washing
Pulse check: How is everyone today? thumbs up, thumbs sideways
Welcome back to kitchen class! Introduce new friends. 
Today is going to be a lot of fun, we’re going to learn about the Three Sisters and make their soup!!
Has anyone heard of the Three Sisters before?
Let’s all get introduced to them!
Excitedly introduce each sister in puppet show + start
5 Mins 3K - PreK: Three sisters puppet show
1-5
Lesson intro:
Intro Indigenous Peoples month
Who were the first people to ever live here? What does the word Indigenous mean
Is anyone here Indigenous, do you have friends or family who are? 
Recipe for today is a Three Sisters Soup
What are the three sisters- does anyone here have siblings?
Explain ingredients being used today. What are the three sisters, what vegetables, has anyone cooked with them before?
Explain information from puppet shows in more detail. How does each plant grow and benefit each other? Discuss companion planting: growing crops in a way that they help each other
Do you see how each plant is different, but they need each other’s help to grow
Do you ever work together with your siblings or friends? 
How do you feel when you work together on something?
Let’s get started using the Three Sisters to make our soup!
Engaging activity:
Prepare soup 
Peel and dice garlic
Dice onion
Dice bell peppers
Measure spices
Saute squash (and other ingredients)
Once cooked, add to blender and puree
Add corn and beans to squash puree and serve
Closing ritual:
“Thank you gardeners, thank you cooks.”
Eat and discuss: how many thumbs up? Changes/improvements?
Was this your first time having squash in a soup? 
What is something your proud of yourself or your table for doing today?
Clean up

Materials: 
Graters and microplanes
Knives or scissors and cutting boards
Measuring spoons and cups
Immersion blender

Prep work and setup:
Prepare and distribute tools and ingredients on trays 
Prep for younger grades that may need help:
Cut bell pepper into chunks
Cut onion into chunks
Combine and saute ingredients then blend
Core apples
Measure and combine dressing ingredients

Anticipated problems:
Finishing all tasks at the same time
Younger groups may need help finishing the entire recipe; adjust by pre-prepping ingredients or only doing one of the recipes
Different levels of understanding/interest in regards to companion planting and nitrogen/protein → provide visuals!

Nitrogen Cycle

In Search of Essential Nutrients

body:
2nd GradeFall/Spring
Indoor/Outdoor


Water Cycle
 
Summary: Students observe the difference between water on blacktop and water in the garden, play a game simulating the water cycle, and water plants in the garden.

Objectives: Students will understand that water goes in a cycle, identify the different parts of the water cycle, and understand the role of plants and soil in the water cycle.

Cultural Responsiveness: incorporates different individual and cultural learning styles, reshapes curriculum

Social-Emotional Skills: self-management, relationship skills

Agenda/Class Flow:
Opening ritual:
Welcome students. Tell students today we will be learning about water. Ask why water is important in the garden.
We’re going to tell the story of how water travels, everyone is going to help us act out the steps in the story. 
Engaging activity:
Hang up water cycle poster on the board (or draw an equivalent diagram). Talk through the cycle with the students, going over all the vocabulary words (condensation, precipitation, infiltration, transpiration, evaporation, absorption). Make up hand motions for each of these steps (i.e. pretending to be the roots of a plant drinking up water for absorption, pretending to be leaves breathing out water for transpiration, etc.). 
After going through all the steps, pass out necklaces in order so the students can practice repeating and acting out all the steps. Tell the students they will pass our raindrop around the circle. Students have to help make the motions and explain how our rain drop can get to the next step. For example, if a student is wearing a cloud necklace they would act out and say precipitation so they can pass the raindrop to the person with the next necklace in order (the ground). Help the students and have them keep practicing and acting out the water cycle steps as a class. 
Have students go out into the garden to water plants with watering cans. Review garden rules before sending students. 
Closing ritual:
After watering, have students return to the circle.
Ask students to review what steps of the water cycle they were helping with by watering in the garden. 
Finish with a tasting. 

Materials: 
Water cycle necklaces 
Water Cycle Poster and Water droplet cutout 
Watering cans
Seasonal tasting

Prep work and setup:
Prepare water cycle necklaces (one for each student)
Fill and set out watering cans for watering job.

Anticipated problems:
Help students remember the vocabulary words and motions as they act out the water cycle by passing the water droplet around--it may take a few rounds of this game before they are able to do it without help. 

Background information for instructor: None. 

Questions for students/extension activities: None. 

Back pocket activities: None. 

Task cards or handouts used: See above.
